Lára àwọn ojúṣe àti iṣẹ́ Ibùdó Sànpọ̀nná Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ni: Ìwádìí fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà àti ìdàgbàsókè àwọn òté agbógunti. ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìpàdé ọdọọdún Àjọ Agbani-nímọ̀ràn lórí Ìwádìí Fáírọ́ọ̀sì Fàríólà ti Àjọ Elétò Àgbáyé (ACVVR). àkóso àgbéyẹ̀wò àwọn àlàkalẹ̀ ìwádìí fún iṣẹ́ lórí fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà nípasẹ̀ ACVVR àkóso àwọn ìbáṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àkójọpọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà méjì (tí wọ́n tún jé àwọn Ibùdó Àjọṣepọ̀ WHO) àkásílẹ̀, ṣíṣe iṣẹ́ àti àmójútó àwọn ìbéèrè láti ọwọ́ àwọn aṣèwádìí lórí DNA fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà láti inú àkójọpọ̀ 2 lábẹ́ àwọn pàtó. ṣíṣe àmójútó ààbò-ẹ̀dá àti ìdáàbòbò-ẹ̀dá èèkàn ní ọdún méjì àwọn àkójọpọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà tí wọ́n káríayé 2. ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwùjọ lórí ìwádìí lórí DNA fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà àti àwọn ọ̀ràn tí wọ́n jẹmọ́ ara wọn. Ìgbáradì àti ìfèsì pàjáwìrì àkóso àti ìgbáradì ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwùjọ lórí ìdàgbàsókè àti ìlò àwọn òté agbógunti fún àwọn fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn. ìgbáradì ìdàgbàsókè àti ọgbọ́n ìfèsì, àti irinṣẹ́ láti ṣàmúlò àwọn òté ìgbáradì àkóso àti àmójútó àkójọ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára sànpọ̀nná lágbàáyé. àkóso ìdàgbàsókè ìṣàṣepọ̀ láàbù fún ìdámọ̀ àti àyẹ̀wò àwọn fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn. ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwùjọ àti àwọn ọgbọ́n ìfèsì fún olóde-ẹdun ènìyàn àti àwọn fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn mìíràn. àtìlẹ́yìn agbára ìṣẹ̀dá àti ìṣẹ̀dá àwọn èròjà ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́. ìnípa lórí àwọn ètò ààbò ìlera àgbáyé Ìpamọ́ ìtàn sànpọ̀nná lágbàáyé àmójútó àwọn àká Ètò Ìfòpinsí Sànpọ̀nná ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àká Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé àti ilé ìyáwèékàwé ìdarí lórí àwọn ìjíròrò ìlànà àti ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ mánigbàgbé tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìfòpinsí sànpọ̀nná ìjábọ̀ lọ́dọọdún fún Ìgbìmọ̀ Adarí Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé àti Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé Ibùdó iṣẹ́ náà ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àti àkóso owó ìrànwọ́ fún àwọn iṣẹ́ wọ̀nyí àti fún àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ nígbà tí ó yẹ nígbà tí ó nílò gẹ́gẹ́ bí igbaninímọ̀ràn àti ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ ìmọ̀.
translations.reference: Since the eradication era, safer vaccines and specific treatments have been developed for smallpox and related diseases such as monkeypox. The Secretariat is also responsible for emergency preparedness and manages a smallpox vaccine emergency stockpile in the unlikely event that the disease re-emerges. The Secretariat manages the biosafety and biosecurity inspections of the authorized variola virus repositories as mandated by the World Health Assembly, and ensures annual reporting to WHO Governing Bodies. The Smallpox Secretariat supports WHO Archives in preserving the global history of smallpox and documenting the lessons learned from successful eradication of smallpox. The WHO Smallpox Secretariat's current roles and responsibilities include: Variola virus research and development of countermeasures organizing annual meetings of the WHO Advisory Committee on Variola Virus Research (ACVVR) coordinating review of research proposals for work with variola virus through the ACVVR managing relationships with the two variola virus repositories (which are also WHO Collaborating Centres) recording, processing and monitoring requests from researchers for variola virus DNA from the 2 repositories under specific rules conducting biennial biosafety and biosecurity inspections of the 2 global variola virus repositories developing normative guidance regarding research on variola virus DNA and related matters Emergency preparedness and response coordinating and preparing normative guidance on development and use of medical countermeasures for orthopoxviruses developing preparedness and response strategies, and tools to implement preparedness measures maintaining and managing the smallpox global vaccine stockpile coordinating development of a laboratory network for orthopoxvirus detection and diagnostics developing normative guidance and response strategies for human monkeypox and other orthopoxviruses supporting capacity-building and developing training materials contributing to global health security initiatives Preserving global smallpox history overseeing the Smallpox Eradication Programme archives in collaboration with WHO Archives and the WHO Library leading on policy discussions and commemorative events regarding smallpox eradication reporting annually to the WHO Executive Board and the World Health Assembly The Secretariat also mobilizes and manages funding for these activities and for ad hoc events as required such as technical consultations and training.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"From the perspective of public health, specialized and specific interventions have been established for prevention and the treatment of diseases, particularly measles. This focus also extends to the strengthening and coordination of specialized measles prevention interventions during times when people are not vigilant and the disease resurges. The focus is also on strengthening disease surveillance and outbreak investigation of priority diseases, as recommended by the World Health Organization, and a strong mandate exists for the World Health Organization's Advisory Committee on Health. The Measles Hub supports the World Health Organization's technical departments in conducting global measles surveillance and documenting lessons learned from successful experiences in measles elimination. Among the activities and functions of the World Health Organization's Measles Hub are:  Maintaining a priority disease surveillance database and the assessment of high-risk countries. Organizing annual meetings of the Global Outbreak and Response Network on Priority Disease Surveillance (ACVVR). Coordinating the review of surveillance data for priority disease surveillance through ACVVR. Coordinating partnerships with two priority disease surveillance organizations (which are also WHO collaborating centers).  Analyzing and responding to requests from epidemiologists regarding measles virus DNA from the two organizations. Conducting outbreak investigation and retrospective investigation of measles cases over a two-year period in two priority disease surveillance organizations globally. Conducting social research on surveillance data on measles virus DNA and related strains. Strengthening and improving the coordination and surveillance of high-risk populations for priority diseases. Strengthening surveillance and expertise, and providing guidance to standardize the control and investigation of specialized measles interventions globally. Coordinating the sharing of data for the detection and verification of priority diseases. Conducting social research and expertise for human health and other priority diseases. Supporting the development and innovation of diagnostic products. Focusing on global health programs, global measles surveillance history, and the response of the World Health Organization's technical departments and the nursing management on the challenges and emergencies related to measles prevention. A strong mandate exists for the World Health Organization's Executive Board and the World Health Report. The work unit coordinates and manages funding for these activities and for emergencies when needed, such as outbreaks and epidemics of knowledge."

translations.source: Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò Ìsọnísókí: Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó léwu fún ẹ̀mí tí àwọn oró ẹnu ejò tí ó lóró ń fà. Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò tún lè wáyé nípasẹ̀ oró ejò tí ó fọ́n sí ojú láti ọwọ́ àwọn ẹ̀yà ejò kan tí wọ́n ní agbára láti tu oró gẹ́gẹ́ bí òté ààbò. Àwọn akitiyan àtijọ́ láti ṣàkóso àìsàn láti ara oró ejò ti pèsè àwọn ìwífún-àlàyé ìmọ̀ àrùn tí ó fọ́n ká, tí kò pé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn afaragbá kì í lọ sí ibùdó ìlera tàbí ilé ìwòsàn àti dípò bẹ́ẹ̀ wọ́n máa ń gbáralé àwọn ìtọ́jú abẹ́lé. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé, àwọn ìwífún-àlàyé tí ó wà fi hàn pé àwọn ènìyàn 4.5-5.4 ni ejò bù jẹ lọ́dọọdún. Nínú èyí, mílíọ̀nù 1.8-2.7 ní àìsàn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni 81 000 sí 138 000 kú láti ara àwọn àìsàn. Lára àwọn ọ̀wọ́ tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu jùlọ ni àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ iṣẹ́ àgbẹ̀, darandaran, ọdẹ, àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́, àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní ilé tí wọ́n kò kọ́ dáradára àti àwọn tí wọn kò ní àǹfààní sí ẹ̀kọ́ àti ìtọ́jú ìlera. Òjòjò àti ikú máa ń sáábà wáyé láàárín àwọn ọ̀dọ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọmọdé ní ìṣòro ọ̀ràn ikú púpọ̀. Síwájú sí i, àwọn obìnrin máa ń ní ìrírí àwọn ìdènà sí ìṣàmúlò ìtọ́jú ìṣègùn ní àwọn àwùjọ kan bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn aláboyún wà nínú ewu. Ìṣòro kan tí ó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ tí ó ń dènà àǹfààní sí ìtọ́jú òògùn agbógunti-oró ejò tí ó láàbò, tí ó sì múnádóko ní àwọn ọ̀pọ̀ agbègbè, àti pàápàá jùlọ ní àyíká àgbègbè Sahara Afíríkà, jẹ́ ìdí kan tí ó nípa lórí ewu wíwá ìrànlọ́wọ́ nípasẹ̀ òògùn ìbílẹ̀. Àmì àìsàn: Ẹnu tàbí oró fífọ́n láti ara àwọn ejò olóró lè fa onírúurú àwọn pàjáwìrì ìṣègùn líle tí ó lágbára. Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò láti ara àwọn oríṣìí ejò lè fa onírúurú àmì àìsàn, àwọn kan lágbára jù àwọn mìíràn lọ. Èyí mú kí ìpèsè òògùn agbógunti-oró ejò tí ó tọ́ jẹ́ ìṣòro tí ó ń lọ lọ́wọ́. Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò lè fa rọpárọsẹ̀ líle tí ó lè dènà mímí, èyí tí yóò mú kí ìtọ́jú ìṣègùn ayákánkán ṣe pàtàkì. Àwọn ènìyàn lè ní ìrírí ìṣòro ẹ̀jẹ̀ sísun tí ó lè yọrí sí ẹ̀jẹ̀ àsunsínú tàbí akùdé kíndìnrín tí kò ṣe é wò. Ìbàjẹ́ ìṣù ara líle tún lè wáyé, èyí tí ó lè yọrí sí ààbọ̀ ara títí ayé àti ọwọ́ gígé pẹ̀lú. Àwọn ọmọdé nínú ipa ewu ńlá nítorí ara wọn tí ó kéré jù tí àwọn àgbàlagbà lọ. Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ikú àti àbájáde láti ara ẹnu ejò ṣe é dènà dáadáa nípa pípèsè àwọn òògùn aporó ejò tí wọ́n wà káàkiri tí wọ́n sì ṣe é ṣàmúlò, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí ewu pọ̀ sí. Àwọn òògùn aporó ejò alágbára nìkan ni ìtọ́jú pípéye láti dènà tàbí ṣe ìwòsàn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ipa àwọn oró ejò. Ìtọ́jú: Àwọn òògùn aporó ejò jẹ́ ìtọ́jú pípé láti dènà tàbí ṣe ìwòsàn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ipa burúkú oró ejò bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n wà nínú àlàkalẹ̀ àwọn òògùn pàtàkì WHO. Wíwà àti níní àǹfààní àwọn òògùn aporó wọ̀nyí, pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe ìpolongo lórí àwọn ìlànà ìdènà abẹ́lé láàrin àwọn àwùjọ àti àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera, ni àwọn ọ̀nà tí wọ́n dára jùlọ láti ṣe àdínkù àwọn ìṣòro ńlá àti ikú láti ara oró ejò. Lẹ́yìn tí ejò tí ó ṣe é ṣe kí ó lóró bá sán ènìyàn, ẹ tẹ́lẹ̀ àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ wọ̀nyí: Ní kété ẹ kúrò ní agbègbè tí sísán náà ti waye. Ẹ yọ ohunkóhun tí ó bá fún kúrò ní ẹ̀yà ara tí ejò tí náà sán láti yàgò fún ewu bí wíwú bá wáyé.
translations.reference: Snakebite envenoming Overview: Snakebite envenoming is a potentially life-threatening disease caused by toxins in the bite of a venomous snake. Envenoming can also be caused by having venom sprayed into the eyes by certain species of snakes that have the ability to spit venom as a defence measure. Inadequate past efforts to control snakebite envenoming has produced fragmented, inaccurate epidemiological data. Many victims do not attend health centres or hospitals and instead rely on traditional treatments. However, available data show 4.5–5.4 million people get bitten by snakes annually. Of this, 1.8–2.7 million develop clinical illness and 81 000 to 138 000 die from complications. High-risk groups include rural agricultural workers, herders, fishermen, hunters, working children, people living in poorly constructed houses and those with limited access to education and healthcare. Morbidity and mortality occur most frequently among young people and children suffer higher case fatality. Furthermore, women experience increased barriers to accessing medical care in some cultures and pregnant women are extremely vulnerable. An ongoing crisis restricting access to safe, effective antivenom treatment in many regions, and particularly sub-Saharan Africa, is one factor that contributes to the predisposition for seeking help through traditional medicine. Symptoms: Bites or sprayed venom from venomous snakes can cause a range of acute and serious medical emergencies. Envenoming from different types of snakes can cause different symptoms, some more serious than others. This makes the preparation of correct antivenoms an ongoing problem. Envenoming can cause severe paralysis that may prevent breathing, making immediate medical attention critical. People may also experience bleeding disorders that can lead to fatal haemorrhages or irreversible kidney failure. Severe local tissue destruction may also occur, which can lead to permanent disability and even limb amputation. Children are at higher risk of severe effects due to lower body mass than adults. Most deaths and serious consequences from snake bites are entirely preventable by making safe and effective antivenoms more widely available and accessible, particularly in high-risk areas. High quality snake antivenoms are the only effective treatment to prevent or reverse most of the venomous effects of snake bites. Treatment: Snake antivenoms are effective treatments to prevent or reverse most of the harmful effects of snakebite envenoming and are included in the WHO list of essential medicines. The availability and accessibility of these antivenoms, along with raising awareness on primary prevention methods among communities and health workers, are the best ways to limit serious consequences and deaths from snakebite envenoming. After a bite by a snake suspected of being venomous, follow these steps: Immediately move away from the area where the bite occurred. Remove anything tight from around the bitten part of the body to avoid harm if swelling occurs.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Disease from Mosquito Bites (Malaria):** Disease from mosquito bites is a disease that poses a threat to life caused by the bites of female mosquitoes. Disease from mosquito bites can also be transmitted through mosquitoes that have bitten people with high fever. Historical interventions to control disease from mosquito bites have been hampered by forecasting models that have not been accurate. A large number of people do not go to health centers or hospitals and instead rely on traditional healers. However, the available forecasts indicate that 4.5-5.4 million people are infected with malaria annually. Of these, 1.8-2.7 million have malaria disease, and 81,000 to 138,000 die from malaria-related illnesses. Among those at greatest risk are agricultural workers, pregnant women, children who work, people who live in homes without proper sanitation, and those without access to education and healthcare. Malaria and death are common among children, and children are among those with the highest mortality rates. Furthermore, women experience difficulties accessing reproductive health services in some communities, and expectant mothers are at risk. A trend that limits access to effective malaria prevention drugs, which is prevalent in many regions and particularly in the Sahel region of Africa, is a factor contributing to the risk of a humanitarian crisis through local drugs.

**Symptoms:** Bites from mosquitoes or mosquito larvae can cause various health complications. Disease from mosquito bites can cause a variety of symptoms, some severe and others mild. This makes effective malaria prevention drugs a necessary intervention. Disease from mosquito bites can cause fever, which can lead to severe anemia, which will make intensive treatment essential. People may experience severe chills that can lead to pneumonia or kidney failure. Damage to the spleen can also occur, which can lead to chronic illness and limb paralysis. Children are at greater risk due to their smaller size compared to adults. A large number of deaths and disabilities from mosquito bites have hindered the availability of mosquito repellent drugs, making them scarce, especially in areas with high risk. Ineffective mosquito repellent drugs are the only treatment to prevent or treat many of the effects of malaria.

**Treatment:** Mosquito repellent drugs are a key treatment to prevent or treat many of the harmful effects of malaria and are included in the WHO’s list of essential medicines. Improving the availability and accessibility of these mosquito repellent drugs, along with implementing strategies for proper home treatment among communities and healthcare workers, are the best ways to reduce severe illness and death from malaria. After a mosquito bites someone, follow these steps: Leave the area where the bite occurred. Remove any items that may have come into contact with the mosquito’s saliva to prevent infection if a reaction occurs.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ẹ fi ọkàn afaragbá balẹ̀, nítorí pé kì í ṣe gbogbo oró ejò ni ó ń ikú kíákíá. Ẹ jẹ́ kí ẹni náà wà láìmira pátápátá kí ẹ sì gbé ẹni náà lọ sí ibùdó ìlera ní kíákíá. Fífi ọ̀ǹtẹ̀ sí ojú sísán náà pẹ̀lú èèfún lè jẹ́ ohun tí ó dára ní àwọn ọ̀ràn kan. Ẹ yàgò fún àwọn ìlànà ìtọ́jú ìbílẹ̀ àkọ́gbà tàbí àwọn òògùn ìbílẹ̀. Ẹ lè fún ẹni náà ní Parasitamọ́ fún ìrora abẹ́lé (èyí tí ó lè lágbára). Èébì lè wáyé, nítorí náà ẹ mú kí ẹni náà sún pẹ̀lú ẹ̀gbẹ́ rẹ̀ òsì ní ipò ìmúláradá. Ẹ mójútó káà ìmí àti ìmí rẹ̀ kí ẹ sì gbáradì láti jí i bí ó bá ṣe pàtàkì.
translations.reference: Reassure the victim, as most venomous snake bites do not cause immediate death. Immobilize the person completely and transport the person to a health facility as soon as possible Applying pressure at the bite site with a pressure pad may be suitable in some cases. Avoid traditional first aid methods or herbal medicines. Paracetamol may be given for local pain (which can be severe). Vomiting may occur, so place the person on their left side in the recovery position. Closely monitor airway and breathing and be ready to resuscitate if necessary
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Lower the fever with a cold compress, because it's not every illness that causes fever that leads to death repeatedly. It is advisable to keep the person calm and take them to a health facility frequently. Applying a cold compress to the forehead with a cloth can be beneficial in some cases. Give traditional healing methods or herbal remedies. You can give the person Paracetamol for a high fever (which can be potent). Shivering may occur, therefore, make the person sweat with their clothes in a ventilated position. Monitor their breathing and pulse, and be ready to assist if it becomes critical."

**Notes on the translation:**

*   **Cultural Context:** The text references "traditional healing methods or herbal remedies," which is important to acknowledge as part of the cultural approach to healthcare in Yoruba communities.
*   **Specificity:** I've tried to maintain the level of detail present in the original Yoruba.
*   **"Kíákíá":** This word appears multiple times and signifies frequent or repeated action. I've translated it as "repeatedly" or "frequently" depending on the context.
*   **"Ìlànà ìtọ́jú ìbílẹ̀ àkọ́gbà tàbí àwọn òògùn ìbílẹ̀":** This refers to traditional healing practices and herbal medicines.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Àwọn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe ìkóràn Ìsọnísókí: Àwọn àìsàn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn wà lára àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ káàkiri àgbáyé tí wọ́n sì ń nípa lórí àwọn àwùjọ tí wọ́n tòṣì jùlọ tí wọ́n kò sì ní àǹfààní jùlọ. Wọ́n máa ń ràn nípa àwọn ẹyin tí ó wà nínú ìgbẹ́ ènìyàn èyí tí ó ń sọ ilẹ̀ di àìmọ́ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí kò ti sí ìmọ́tótó. Àwọn ẹ̀yà gbòógì tí wọ́n máa ń mú ènìyàn ni aràn roboto (Ascaris lumbricoides), aràn kòbókò (Trichuris trichuria) àti kán-ànyàn (Necator americanus àti Ancylostoma duodenale). Aràn inú máa ń pèsè àwọn onírúurú àmì àìsàn tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ìṣòro inú bí (ìgbẹ́ gbuuru, inú rírun), ìrora àti àìlera gbogbogbò. Kán-ànyàn ń fa àdánù ẹ̀jẹ̀ inú líle èyí tí ó lè fa àìlẹ́jẹ̀tó. Àwọn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn máa ń ràn nípa àwọn ẹyin tí wọ́n máa ń jáde láti ara ìgbẹ́ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n jẹ́ afaragbá. Àwọn àgbà aràn máa ń gbé nínú ìfun níbi tí wọ́n ti máa ń pèsè àwọn ẹgbẹẹgbẹ̀rún ẹyin lójoojúmọ́. Ní àwọn agbègbè tí wọn kò ní ìmọ́tótó tó tó, àwọn ẹyin wọ̀nyí maat ń sọ ilẹ̀ di àìmọ́. Èyí lè ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àwọn onírúurú ọ̀nà: àwọn ẹyin tí wọ́n lẹ̀ mọ́ ẹ̀fọ́ tí ènìyàn jẹ nígbà tí wọn kò bá ṣe, fọ̀ tàbí bó ẹ̀fọ́ náà dáadáa.; wọ́n máa ń jẹ ẹyin láti ara àwọn orísun omi tí kò mọ́; àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n ń ṣeré ní orí ilẹ̀ tí kò mọ́ máa ń kó ẹyin be sì ni wọ́n yóò fi àwọn ọwọ́ wọn sí ẹnu wọn láìfọ wọ́n. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Àìsàn máa ń ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú iye àwọn aràn tí àgọ̀ gbà. Àwọn ènìyàn tí àìsàn wọn jẹ́ àwọn aràn tí kò pọ̀ (aràn níwọ̀n) kì í sáábà jìyà láti ara àìsàn yìí. Àwọn àìsàn líle lè fa onírúurú àwọn àmì àìsàn tó fi mọ́ àwọn ìfarahàn nínú ìfun (ìgbẹ́ gbuuru àti inú rírun), àìjẹunrekánú, ìrora àti àìlera gbogbogbò, àti àìdàgbàbóṣeyẹ̀ àti àìgbèrú. Àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n le lè fa ìdíwọ́ inú tí ènìyàn gbọ́dọ̀ tọ́jú pẹ̀lú iṣẹ́ abẹ. S. stercolaris lè fa àìsàn awọ ara àti òpónà ọ̀fun bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìjẹunkánú láàrin àwọn ọmọdé. Ní ti àdínkù àjẹsára ẹni tí ó gbàlejò wọn, ajọ̀fẹ́ náà lè fa òòrèkóòrè àìsàn/àpẹẹrẹ àpínká tí ó lágbára bí wọn kò bá tètè tọ́jú wọn bí ó ṣe tọ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó máa léwu bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ìtọ́jú wà. Ní 2001, àwọn aṣojú ní Àpérò Àgbáyé fẹnukò papọ̀ lórí ọ̀nà àbáyọ (WHA54. 19) tí ó ń rọ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ní àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn láti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ní kojú aràn, pàápàá jùlọ sikosomíásì àti àwọn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn. Ọgbọ́n láti ṣàkóso àwọn àìsàn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn ni láti ṣàkóso ewu àìsàn nípasẹ̀ ìtọ́jú òòrèkóòrè àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ní àwọn agbègbè àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ni: àwọn ọmọ jéléósinmi àwọn ọmọ ọjọ́ orí wọn lọ ilé ẹ̀kọ́ àwọn obìnrin tí ọjọ́ orí wọn ti tó bímọ (tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn aláboyún ní ìpele kejì Tai ìkẹta oyún àti obìnrin tí wọ́n ń fún ọmọ lọ́yàn) Àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn ń ṣe àwọn iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n léwu bí àwọn olùkórè tíì tàbí awakùsà.
translations.reference: Soil-transmitted helminthiases Overview: Soil-transmitted helminth (STH) infections are among the most common infections worldwide and affect the poorest and most deprived communities. They are transmitted by eggs present in human faeces which in turn contaminate soil in areas where sanitation is poor. The main species that infect people are the roundworm (Ascaris lumbricoides), the whipworm (Trichuris trichiura) and the hookworms (Necator americanus and Ancylostoma duodenale). Intestinal worms produce a wide range of symptoms including intestinal manifestations (diarrhoea, abdominal pain), general malaise and weakness. Hookworms cause chronic intestinal blood loss that result in anaemia. Soil-transmitted helminths are transmitted by eggs that are passed in the faeces of infected people. Adult worms live in the intestine where they produce thousands of eggs each day. In areas that lack adequate sanitation, these eggs contaminate the soil. This can happen in several ways: eggs that are attached to vegetables are ingested when the vegetables are not carefully cooked, washed or peeled; eggs are ingested from contaminated water sources; eggs are ingested by children who play in the contaminated soil and then put their hands in their mouths without washing them. Symptoms: Morbidity is related to the number of worms harboured. People with infections of light intensity (few worms) usually do not suffer from the infection. Heavier infections can cause a range of symptoms including intestinal manifestations (diarrhoea and abdominal pain), malnutrition, general malaise and weakness, and impaired growth and physical development. Infections of very high intensity can cause intestinal obstruction that should be treated surgically. S. stercoralis may cause dermatological and gastro-intestinal morbidity and is also known to be associated with chronic malnutrition in children. In case of reduced host immunity, the parasite can cause the hyperinfection/dissemination syndrome that is invariably fatal if not promptly and properly cured and is often fatal despite the treatment. In 2001, delegates at the World Health Assembly unanimously endorsed a resolution (WHA54.19) urging endemic countries to start seriously tackling worms, specifically schistosomiasis and soil-transmitted helminths. The strategy for control of soil-transmitted helminth infections is to control morbidity through the periodic treatment of at-risk people living in endemic areas. People at risk are: preschool children school-age children women of reproductive age (including pregnant women in the second and third trimesters and breastfeeding women) adults in certain high-risk occupations such as tea-pickers or miners.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Parasitic Diseases Causing Public Health Concerns: Parasitic diseases are among the most prevalent diseases worldwide, particularly affecting the most vulnerable populations who lack adequate sanitation. They are caused by eggs found in the human environment, leading to illness in areas with poor hygiene. The most common parasites affecting people are roundworm (Ascaris lumbricoides), whipworm (Trichuris trichuria), and hookworm (Necator americanus and Ancylostoma duodenale). Intestinal worms cause various disease symptoms, including diarrhea, abdominal pain, vomiting, and general weakness. Hookworm can cause anemia due to blood loss, which can lead to fatigue. Parasitic diseases are caused by eggs that are released from the human digestive system, especially from those with poor hygiene. Older parasites live in the soil, releasing countless eggs daily. In areas with poor sanitation, these eggs can contaminate the environment. This can happen in several ways: eggs contaminate food when it is not properly washed or cooked, or when the food is poorly prepared; people consume eggs from contaminated water sources; and children playing on the ground who are unaware may pick up eggs and put them in their mouths.

Symptoms: Illness is associated with the number of parasites ingested. People with light worm infections (minor worms) are not always symptomatic of this disease. Intestinal illnesses can cause various symptoms associated with disturbances in the intestines (diarrhea and abdominal pain), weakness, general illness, and stunted growth and development. Parasitic infections can cause internal bleeding, which requires iron treatment. *S. stercolaris* can cause skin lesions and anal fissures, along with constipation in children. In severe cases, infection can lead to severe illness/complications if not treated properly. In 2001, the World Health Assembly (WHA54.19) unanimously resolved that countries with a high prevalence of parasitic diseases should begin to control parasitic diseases, especially helminths and diseases that affect the soil. The key to controlling parasitic diseases is to reduce the risk of illness through the treatment of people at risk in areas with high disease prevalence. People at risk include: preschool children, school-age children, women who are pregnant (including breastfeeding mothers), and older adults engaged in risky occupations such as teachers or farmers.**

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Specificity:** I've tried to maintain the specificity of the Yoruba terms, especially when referring to specific parasites.
*   **Clarity:** I've rephrased some sentences to improve clarity and flow in English.
*   **Cultural Context:** I've attempted to convey the cultural context where appropriate (e.g., mentioning breastfeeding mothers).
*   **Technical Terms:** I've used standard English medical/scientific terms where possible.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú Àjọ WHO dábàá àwọn òògùn - albendazole (400mg) àti mebendazole (500mg) - kájúẹ̀, kò wọ́n bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n rọrùn láti lo láti ọwọ́ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọn kì í ṣe òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera (b.a àwọn olùkọ́). Wọ́n ti là ìdánwò ààbò gbòòrò kọjá bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ènìyàn ti lò wọ́n pẹ̀lú ipa ẹ̀yìn níwọ̀n àti èyí tí kò tó nǹkan. Àti albendazole àti mebendazole ni wọ́n máa ń fún àwọn àjọ ìlera ìjọba orílẹ̀ èdè nípasẹ̀ Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ní gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ní àrùn fún ìtọ́jú gbogbo àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n wà ní ọjọ́ orí láti lọ sí ilé ẹ̀kọ́. Ifamẹkitíìnì fún àkóso S. stercolaris ni ìrètí wà pé yóò wà ní iye tí kò jara lọ láti ọdún 2021.
translations.reference: TreatmentThe WHO recommended medicines –albendazole (400 mg) and mebendazole (500 mg) – are effective, inexpensive and easy to administer by non-medical personnel (e.g. teachers). They have been through extensive safety testing and have been used in millions of people with few and minor side-effects. Both albendazole and mebendazole are donated to national ministries of health through WHO in all endemic countries for the treatment of all children of school age. Ivermectin for the control of S. stercoralis is expected to be available at affordable price from 2021.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

The WHO recommends medications – albendazole (400mg) and mebendazole (500mg) – stating that they are easy to use by individuals who are not healthcare workers (e.g., teachers). They have been extensively tested across various regions, and the side effects experienced by people who have used them are minimal. Both albendazole and mebendazole will be provided to government health agencies through the World Health Organization for all countries that need them to treat all children from their first day of school. The cost of controlling *Schistosoma stercolaris* is expected to be relatively low from 2021 onwards.

translations.source: Àbíkú Ìsọnísókí: Ọmọ tí ó kú lẹ́yìn ọ̀sẹ̀ méjìdínlọ́gbọ̀n oyún, ṣùgbọ́n síwájú tàbí lẹ́yìn ìbí, ni a mọ̀ sí àbíkú. Ó tó mílíọ̀nù 2 àwọn àbíkú ni ó wà lọ́dọọdún - ọ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan ní ìṣẹ́jú àáyá 16 kan. Ó lé ní ìdá 40% gbogbo àwọn àbíkú ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àsìkò ìbímọ - àdánù tí ó ṣe é dènà pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú dídára tí ó sì pọ́nnilé lásìkò ìbímọ tí ó fi mọ́ ètò àmójútó àti àǹfààní àsìkò sí ìtọ́jú aláboyún pàjáwìrì nígbà tí ó nílò. Níní ìrírí àbíkú lásìkò ìlóyún tàbí ìbímọ jẹ́ àjálù tí wọ́n kò ṣe àmójútó tí ó tó nínú àwọn ètò, ìlànà àti ìrànwọ́ àgbáyé. Àwọn ìdíyelé wà, pàápàá jùlọ fún àwọn obìnrin àti ẹbí wọn, bí ìbànújẹ́ alábiamọ, ìṣòro owó àti àwọn àbájáde ọrọ̀ ajé, àti ìdẹ́yẹsí àti èèwọ̀ pẹ̀lú. Ní 2014, Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé fọwọ́sí Ètò Ọmọ tuntun Kọ̀ọ̀kan, èyí tí a ti rí àfojúsùn àgbáyé 12 tàbí àwọn àbíkú onípele kẹta (pípẹ́) díẹ̀ nínú àpapọ̀ ìbí 1, 000 ní orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan ní 2030. Ní 2021, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi àti alábọ́dé èrè 139 gbòógì ti fẹnukò lórí àfojúsùn yìí, ṣùgbọ́n àwọn orílẹ̀ 56 kò ní ṣe àṣeyọrí àfojúsùn Ètò Ọmọ tuntun Kọ̀ọ̀kan ní 2030 bí àwọn ìgbìyànjú síwájú sí i kò bá jẹ́ ṣíṣe. Bí àwọn àṣà ìwòyí bá tẹ̀síwájú, àwọn ìkókó mílíọ̀nù 15.9 yóò jẹ́ àbíkú; ó sún mọ́ ìdajì àwọn wọ̀nyí (mílíọ̀nù 7.7, 48%) yóò ṣẹlẹ̀ ní agbègbè ẹkùn Sahara Afíríkà. Okùnfà: Pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú ìlera dídára káàkiri àsìkò ìlóyún àti ìbímọ, ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn àbíkú ṣe é dènà. Àwọn okùnfà àbíkú gbòógì ni àwọn ìṣòro oyún àti ìbímọ, oyún ọjọ́ pípẹ́, àìsàn ìyálọ́mọ bí ibà maléríà, sífílì àti KASA, àwọn ìṣòro ìyálọ́mọ pàápàá jùlọ ẹ̀jẹ̀ ríru, àti àtọ̀gbẹ, àti gbèdéke ìwọ̀n títóbi (nígbà tí ọmọ tí wọ́n kò tíì bí kò bá lè dàgbà bí ó ṣe tọ́ nítorí náà ó kéré jù bí ó ṣe yẹ lọ). Àwọn ìṣòro àsìkò ìbí ni wọ́n ṣokùnfà kò tó ìdá 10% àwọn àbíkú ni wọ́n jábọ̀ káàkiri àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ibùdó elérè pínnísín kò ní àkọsílẹ̀ nítorí àwọn gbèdéke inú àwọn àyẹ̀wò. Àwọn ìdí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ọjọ́ orí ìyá àti fífa nǹkan náà lè ṣe àgbéga ewu àìsàn ìyálọ́mọ àti àbíkú. Àwọn àbíkú lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ ètò ìfètòsọ́mọbíbí láti dènà oyún àìmọ̀dí, ìlera àti ìjẹunrekánú gidi síwájú àti lásìkò ìlóyún, dídára àti ìbọ̀wọ̀fún ìtọ́jú àsìkò ìlóyún àti ìbímọ tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn alámọ̀já òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera tí wọ́n tó dórí àwọn agbẹ̀bí. Ìtọ́jú sífílì lásìkò ìlóyún lè dènà ìṣirò àwọn àbíkú 200 000, nígbà tí àmójútó ìwọ̀n ìmí olẹ̀ àti àmójútó ìbímọ àti ìdásí àsìkò nígbà tí wọ́n nílò ṣe pàtàkì láti dènà àwọn àbíkú 832 000 lásìkò ìbí àti àdínkù àwọn ikú lẹ́yìn ìbí. Wọ́n ní àjọṣepọ̀ wà láàrin àbíkú pẹ̀lú àwọn aṣokùnfà àwùjọ àti ọrọ̀ ajé líle lórí ìlera. Ìdènà àti ìtọ́jú afèsì nílò láti jẹ́ ṣíṣe àmúlò káàkiri ìtẹ̀síwájú ìtọ́jú ìlera ìyálọ́mọ, àti jù bẹ́ẹ̀ lọ. Lára èyí ni ìtọ́jú alápọ̀n-ọ́nlé àti alátìlẹ́yìn lásìkò ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ikú. Fífi etí sílẹ̀ sí ìrírí àti ohùn àwọn obìnrin àti àwùjọ wọn ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún kíkojú àwọn ọ̀ràn ìdẹ́yẹsí tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àbíkú. Èsì WHO: Ohun púpọ̀ ní láti di ṣíṣe sí i láti ṣàmúlò ìdènà àbíkú láàrin ètò àgbáyé àti orílẹ̀ èdè fún ìtọ́jú ìlera ńlá fún àwọn obìnrin, ọ̀dọ́mọdé, àti ìkókó. Nípasẹ̀ ọgbọ́n Àgbáyé fún Ìlera àwọn Obìnrin, Ìkókó àti Ọ̀dọ́mọdé 2016-2030 àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn àbá Ètò Iṣẹ́ Ọmọ tuntun Kọ̀ọ̀kan (2014), Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ń ṣiṣẹ́ láti fòpin sí àwọn àbíkú tí wọ́n ṣe é dènà, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ara akitiyan láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera ìyálọ́mọ, ọmọ tuntun, ọmọdé àti ọ̀dọ́mọdé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àbíkú kì í gba ìwé ẹ̀rí ìbí bẹ́ẹ̀ sit ni wọn kì í forúkọ wọn sílẹ̀. Mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ètò ìjábọ̀ àwọn ìbí àti ikú ẹ̀yìn ìbímọ jẹ́ ọ̀rọ̀ ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn àti àmúyẹ fún mímú àdínkù bá àwọn àbíkú. Ní àfikún, ó nílò kí wọ́n mú ìrólágbára bá àwọn ètò ìròyìn ojoojúmọ́ láti sọ iye àti láti jábọ̀ àwọn àbíkú pérépéré fún àmójútó àti ìlọsíwájú ìtọ́jú ìlera. WHO pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti mú ìlọsíwájú ìwífun-àlàyé wọn lórí àwọn àbíkú àti àwọn ikú ìkókó. WHO tún ní ìtọ́nisọ́nà láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣàyẹ̀wò àti láti wádìí ikú ẹ̀dá kọ̀ọ̀kan kí wọ́n lè dábàá kí wọ́n sì ṣàmúlò àwọn ọ̀nà àbáyọ láti dènà irúfẹ́ àwọn nǹkan bẹ́ẹ̀ ní ọjọ́ iwájú àti ìpínsọ́wọ̀ọ́ ètò fún àwọn ikú àbíkú (ICD-PM). WHO àti UNICEF ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ìpè fún iṣẹ́ àjọṣe láti fòpin sí àwọn àbíkú tí wọ́n ṣe é dènà nípa:
translations.reference: Stillbirth Overview: A baby who dies after 28 weeks of pregnancy, but before or during birth, is classified as a stillbirth.There are nearly 2 million stillbirths every year – one every 16 seconds. Over 40% of all stillbirths occur during labour – a loss that could be avoided with improved quality and respectful care during childbirth including routine monitoring and timely access to emergency obstetric care when required. Experiencing a stillbirth during pregnancy or childbirth is a tragedy insufficiently addressed in global agendas, policies and funded programmes. There are psychological costs, especially to women and their families, such as maternal depression, financial consequences and economic repercussions, as well as stigma and taboo.In 2014, the World Health Assembly endorsed the Every Newborn Action Plan (ENAP), which includes a global target of 12 or fewer third trimester (late) stillbirths per 1,000 total births in every country by 2030. In 2021, 139 mainly high-income and upper middle-income countries had met this target, but 56 countries will not each the ENAP target by 2030 if further efforts are not made. If current trends continue, 15.9 million babies will be stillborn; nearly half of these (7.7 million, 48%) will occur in sub-Saharan Africa. Causes: With quality health care throughout pregnancy and childbirth, most stillbirths are preventable. The major causes of stillbirth include pregnancy and childbirth-related complications, prolonged pregnancy, maternal infections such as malaria, syphilis and HIV, maternal conditions especially hypertension, and diabetes, and fetal growth restriction (when an unborn baby is unable to achieve its growth potential and therefore smaller than it should be). Congenital abnormalities are responsible for less than 10% of stillbirths reported nationally in high-income countries and the exact proportion in low-income settings is unknown due to limitations in diagnostics. Factors related to age of the mother and practicing smoking can also increase the risk of maternal disease and stillbirth. Stillbirths can be prevented through family planning to avoid unwanted pregnancies, good health and nutrition prior to and during pregnancy, quality and respectful antenatal and childbirth care including adequate skilled health personnel including midwives. Syphilis treatment in pregnancy could also prevent an estimated 200 000 stillbirths, while fetal heart rate monitoring and labour surveillance and prompt interventions when needed are crucial for preventing 832 000 intrapartum stillbirths and reducing neonatal deaths. Stillbirths are strongly linked to adverse social and economic determinants of health. Prevention and responsive care need to be integrated across the continuum of maternal health care, and beyond. This includes respectful and supportive care in the event of a death. Listening to the experiences and voices of women and their communities is essential to help address issues of stigma associated with stillbirth. WHO response: More needs to be done to integrate stillbirth prevention within global and national agendas for high quality health care for women, adolescents, and babies. Through the Global Strategy for Women's, Children's and Adolescents Health 2016–2030 and implementing the recommendations of the Every Newborn Action Plan (2014), WHO is working to end preventable stillbirths, as part of efforts to improve maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health. Most stillbirths do not receive a birth certificate and are not registered. Improving systems for reporting births and neonatal deaths is a matter of human rights and a prerequisite for reducing stillbirths. In addition, there is a need to strengthen routine health information systems to accurately capture and report stillbirths for monitoring and improving the quality of care. WHO provides tools to help countries improve their data on both stillbirths and neonatal deaths. WHO also has guidance to help countries review and investigate individual deaths so they can recommend and implement solutions to prevent similar ones in the future and a standard classification system for perinatal deaths (ICD-PM). WHO and UNICEF are supporting a call for collective action to end preventable stillbirths by:
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Stillbirth: A Child Who Dies After Two Weeks of Gestation, But Before or After Birth, is Known as a Stillbirth.** Approximately 2 million stillbirths occur annually – each representing a devastating loss of 16 years of life. 40% of all stillbirths occur during pregnancy – a rate that is exacerbated by inadequate antenatal care and a lack of referral systems to specialized care when needed. In many stillbirth cases during pregnancy or childbirth, a lack of care is a contributing factor, which is not included in global programs, policies, and research. Factors are present, especially for women and their families, such as lack of access to quality maternal care, financial constraints and related economic hardships, and stigma and shame. In 2014, the World Health Assembly endorsed the Every Newborn Counts initiative, which aims to reduce the third (postnatal) week mortality rate to less than 20 per 1,000 live births by 2030. In 2021, 139 high-income and middle-income countries have committed to this goal, but 56 countries are unlikely to achieve the Every Newborn Counts target by 2030 without accelerated action. As mortality rates continue to rise, 15.9 million children will be stillborn; 7.7 million (48%) of these will occur in sub-Saharan Africa. Causes: Many stillbirths are preventable through quality healthcare throughout pregnancy and childbirth. The main causes of stillbirth are labor and delivery complications, prolonged labor, infections in the newborn, such as malaria, syphilis, and pneumonia, neonatal infections especially sepsis, birth asphyxia, and preterm birth (when a baby is born too early to survive). Birth-related complications account for less than 10% of stillbirths in high-income countries, but are more prevalent in low-income countries due to deficiencies in care and inadequate monitoring. Maternal health conditions and practices during labor and delivery can increase the risk of neonatal infections and stillbirth. Stillbirths can be prevented through comprehensive newborn care programs to address labor, illness, and genuine care before and after childbirth, quality and accessible antenatal and delivery care for healthcare workers, including midwives. Syphilis screening during pregnancy can prevent 200,000 stillbirths, while newborn resuscitation and delivery care and emergency care when needed can prevent 832,000 stillbirths during pregnancy and postnatal deaths. Partnerships exist between stillbirth prevention and social and economic health initiatives. Strengthening and expanding access to quality maternal and newborn health services are essential to address the stigma associated with stillbirth. WHO's Commitment: Significant action is needed to reduce stillbirth rates within global and national health programs for women, children, and adolescents. Through the Global Strategy for Women’s, Children’s and Adolescents’ Health 2016-2030 and the implementation of the Every Newborn Counts Action Plan (2014), the World Health Organization is working to address stillbirths, as a key priority to advance health for newborns, young children, and adolescents. Many stillbirths go unrecorded. Strengthening vital registration systems and death reporting is crucial for tracking and reducing stillbirths. Furthermore, it is necessary to strengthen media campaigns to raise awareness and report on the prevalence of stillbirths for care and health promotion. WHO provides guidance to countries to improve their data collection on stillbirths and neonatal deaths so that they can develop strategies to reduce these events in the future and standardize methods for classifying stillbirths (ICD-PM). WHO and UNICEF are advocating for investment in social services to identify stillbirths that can be prevented. WHO also has a mandate to support countries in reviewing and classifying each individual death so that they can develop appropriate strategies to prevent these events in the future and standardize methods for classifying stillbirths (ICD-PM).



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: mímú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìpolongo àti àdínkù ìdẹ́yẹsí ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn obìnrin àti mọ̀lẹ́bí tí ọ̀fọ́ sẹ̀ ìrólágbára àwọn ètò ìlera láti pèsè ìtọ́jú pípéye àmúlò àwọn àfojúsùn àbíkú ní orílẹ̀ èdè àti lábẹ́lé mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìdíwọ̀n àwọn àbíkú láti mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ẹ̀rí àti ìmọ̀.
translations.reference: increasing awareness and reducing stigma supporting bereaved women and families strengthening health systems to provide high-quality care nationalizing and localizing stillbirth targets improving measurement of stillbirths to enhance evidence and knowledge.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English, aiming for accuracy and clarity:

"The advancement of women and the empowerment of girls through the implementation and support of programs is crucial to reducing maternal mortality and improving the health of women and families within communities and at the grassroots level. Furthermore, the advancement of support for orphans is essential to fostering healing and knowledge."

**Explanation of Choices & Nuances:**

*   **mímú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìpolongo àti àdínkù ìdẹ́yẹsí ṣíṣe:** This is a complex phrase. I've broken it down as "the advancement of women and the empowerment of girls through the implementation and support of programs."  "Ìpolongo" implies a plan or policy, and "àdínkù ìdẹ́yẹsí ṣíṣe" means supporting the implementation of those plans.
*   **àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn obìnrin àti mọ̀lẹ́bí:** "Àtìlẹ́yìn" means support or well-being. "Àwọn obìnrin àti mọ̀lẹ́bí" refers to women and girls.
*   **tí ọ̀fọ́ sẹ̀ ìrólágbára àwọn ètò ìlera:** This translates to "whose focus is on strengthening health programs."
*   **láti pèsè ìtọ́jú pípéye àmúlò àwọn àfojúsùn àbíkú:** This means "to reduce the high rate of mortality of orphans."
*   **ní orílẹ̀ èdè àti lábẹ́lé:** This signifies "within communities and at the grassroots level."
*   **mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìdíwọ̀n àwọn àbíkú:** "The advancement of support for orphans."
*   **láti mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ẹ̀rí àti ìmọ̀:** "to foster healing and knowledge."



I hope this translation is helpful! Let me know if you have any other Yoruba texts you'd like me to translate.

translations.source: Àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ìsọnísókí: Ó tó bílíọ́nù méjì àwọn ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé ni wọn kò ní àǹfààní sí àwọn òògùn, abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára, irinṣẹ́ ìṣègùn tí wọ́n ṣe pàtàkì tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn irinṣẹ́ àyẹ̀wò àìsàn, àti àwọn ohun èlò ìlera mìíràn, èyí tí ó ń ṣẹ̀dá àlàfo tí ó máa ń sáábà jẹ́ dídí láti ọwọ́ àwọn ohun èlò ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ìṣòro yìí ń dàgbà sí i bí ẹ̀wọ́n ìpèsè káàkiri àgbáyé ṣe ń gbòòrò sí i, tí ó túmọ̀ sí pé àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n pèsè ní orílẹ̀ èdè kan lè jẹ́ dídì ní orílẹ̀ èdè kejì tí wọn yóò sì máa pín wọn káàkiri àwọn odi ìlú láti polówó tàbí tà fún swkmt oníbàárà ní ìkẹta. Ìdàgbàsókè okòwò orí ẹ̀rọ tún ń nípa lórí àṣà yìí nípa mímú kí ó rọrùn láti ra àwọn òògùn lórí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára, láti ọwọ́ àwọn orísun tí wọ́n kò ní àṣẹ ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà. WHO ti ṣe ìdámọ̀ ọ̀ràn yìí ọ̀kan nínú àwọn ìṣòro ìlera kíákíá fún ọdún mẹ́wàá tí ó ń bọ̀ nítorí pé ó lé ní ọ̀kan nínú mẹ́wàá àwọn òògùn ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè ni wọ́n ṣírò pé wọ́n jẹ́ ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Kò sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí kò tíì faragbá nínú ọ̀ràn yìí, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni WHO gba àwọn ìròyìn òògùn, abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti irinṣẹ́ àyẹ̀wò àìsàn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ ní gbogbo àwọn ẹkùn àgbáyé. Àti àwọn òògùn abéròjàjórúkọ àti àwọn òògùn àtọọ́dá ni wọ́n ṣe é gbélẹ̀rọ, láti orí orí àwọn ohun èlò olówó gọbọi fún jẹjẹrẹ dórí àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n kò wọ́n rárá fún ìtọ́jú ìrora. Àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé fẹnukò lórí ọgbọ́n gbòòrò káríayé tí ó fojúsun ìdènà, àyẹ̀wò àti ìfèsì láti tẹ̀síwájú lórí àṣeyọrí níní àǹfààní púpọ̀ sí ohùn èlò ìṣègùn dídára, aláàbò, tí ó múnádóko tí ó sì dára. Ipa: Àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ ṣẹ̀dá ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìṣòro fún ati awon ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń lo àwọn òògùn náà àti fún àwọn ẹbí, àwùjọ gbòòrò, ètò ìtọ́jú ilera àti ẹ̀wọ́n ìpèsè. Nígbà tí àwọn òògùn kò bá ṣiṣẹ́ bí ó ṣe yẹ kí wọ́n ṣiṣẹ́, wọ́n lè kùnà láti ṣèwòsàn tàbí dènà àìsàn, ń dá kún ìlòdìsí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò, ìfiṣòfò àwọn àlùmọ́ọ́nì iyebíye tí yóò sì yọrí sí àdánù ìgboyà nínú lílo àwọn òògùn àti àìfọkàntán àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera. Èyí lè ní ipa ajẹmọ́ àwùjọ òun ọrọ̀ ajé òdì, níní ipa lórí àdánù tí ó ní í ṣe nínú ìpèsè àti ṣíṣe àfikún àwọn owónàá fún ènìyàn náà àti ètò ìlera orílẹ̀ èdè. Ó ṣe é ṣe kí wọ́n kàn sí àwọn aláìsàn ní àwọn ipò níbi tí ìdènà àǹfààní ti wà sí àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn dídára àti aláàbò, ìdarí tí kò dára àti ipa ìmọ̀ tí kò lágbára. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ọ̀ràn yìí ń nípa lórí gbogbo orílẹ̀ èdè, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè níbi tí wọ́n kò ti ètò ìdarí aláìlágbára tàbí tí kò sí ti ń rú àjàgà púpọ̀ àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ní ayé tí ó ti lajú sí i, kò sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí ó ní àwọn èròjà tí ó tó àti agbára láti gbógun ti ọ̀ràn náà ní àdáwà. Nípasẹ̀ àwọn ètò àti ìgbésẹ̀ àgbáyé bí Ètò Àkóso àti Àmójútó Káríayé ẹgbẹ́ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé pẹ̀lú ọ̀ràn yìí ní àdáwà. Àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti dènà, ṣàyẹ̀wò àti fèsì sí àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ.
translations.reference: Substandard and falsified medical products Overview: Up to two billion people around the world lack access to necessary medicines, vaccines, medical devices including in vitro diagnostics, and other health products, which creates a vacuum that is too often filled by substandard and falsified products. This problem is growing as global supply chains become more complex, meaning products manufactured in one country may be packaged in a second country and distributed across borders to be marketed or sold to consumers in a third. The growth of e-commerce also contributes to this trend by making it easier to purchase medicines online, often from unauthorized sources. WHO has identified this issue one of the urgent health challenges for the next decade given that more than one in ten medicines in low- and middle-income countries are estimated to be substandard or falsified. No country remains untouched from this issue, and WHO has received reports of substandard or falsified medical medicines, vaccines and in vitro diagnostics from all regions of the world. Both generic and innovator medicines can be falsified, ranging from very expensive products for cancer to very inexpensive products for treatment of pain. WHO Member States have agreed on a comprehensive global strategy focused on prevention, detection and response to move towards achieving increased access to quality, safe, effective and quality medical products. Impact: Substandard and falsified medical products create many problems for both the individual taking the medicine and for families, the larger society, health care system and supply chain. When medicines do not work the way they should, they can fail to treat or prevent disease, contribute to antimicrobial resistance, waste precious resources and lead to a loss of confidence in medicines and distrust of healthcare providers. This can have a negative socioeconomic impact, contributing to related loss in productivity and adding expenses to the individual and national health system. They are most likely to reach patients in situations where there is constrained access to quality and safe medical products, poor governance and weak technical capacity. Although this issue impacts all countries, countries where there are weak or non-existent regulatory systems bear the greatest burden of substandard and falsified medical products. In an increasingly globalized world, no one country has sufficient resources and capacity to deal with this issue in isolation. Through global systems and processes such as the WHO Global Surveillance and Monitoring System and WHO Member State Mechanism, Member States are working together to prevent, detect and respond to substandard and falsified medical products.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Counterfeit and Substandard Medicines and Medical Products.**

**Background:** Approximately two billion people worldwide lack access to essential medicines, safe and effective medical products, and quality medical services, which are critical for diagnosing and treating diseases and other health conditions. This creates a vulnerability that can be exploited by counterfeit and substandard medical products. This problem is growing as global health needs increase, meaning that products considered safe in one country can be dangerous in another, and they are distributed across communities to profit or to deceive healthcare providers. The rise of online pharmacies also contributes to this issue, making it easier to purchase medicines from sources that are not properly regulated. The World Health Organization (WHO) has identified this as one of the most significant global health threats for the past decade, as an estimated one in ten medical products in low- and middle-income countries are falsely identified as being essential and safe. No country has been spared from this problem, and the WHO collects information on essential, safe, and effective medicines and medical products from all member states. This includes both branded and generic medicines, from those used for simple treatments to those used for critical care.

Member states of the World Health Assembly are concerned about the global trend focused on identifying, testing, and ensuring success in providing increased access to quality medical products, including essential, affordable, and trusted products.

**Impact:** Counterfeit and substandard medical products create significant problems for individuals who use them, their families, healthcare systems, and health services. When medicines do not work as they should, they can fail to treat or cure diseases, leading to antimicrobial resistance, undermining public trust in vaccines, and ultimately reducing the effectiveness of medicine use and hindering healthcare workers. This can have a devastating economic impact on communities, affecting the quality of care for individuals and the overall health system of a country. It can also lead to the neglect of patients in situations where access to essential medical products is limited, inadequate care is provided, and knowledge is lacking. While this problem affects all countries, those with weak regulatory systems or that are heavily flooded with counterfeit medical products are particularly vulnerable. Currently, no country has the resources and capacity to fully combat this problem alone. Through global initiatives and partnerships such as the Governance and Accountability Framework, the World Health Assembly is addressing this challenge. Member states are working together to identify, test, and ensure the quality of essential medical products.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ìdènà ìgbẹ́míaraẹni Ìsọnísókí: Ó lé ní àwọn ènìyàn 700 000 ni wọ́n kú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lọ́dọọdún. Fún ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan, ìṣirò àwọn ìgbìyànjú láti gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni tí ó ju 20 ló wà. Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lè wáyé ní èyíkéyìí ìpele ìgbésí ayé àti ní gbogbo ẹkùn àgbáyé. Ní 2019, ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni asíwájú okùnfà ikú kẹrin láàrin àwọn ọmọ ọdún 15-29 káàkiri àgbáyé, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni 77% gbogbo àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti àti elérè gọbọi. Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ẹ́ tó 20% àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lágbàáyé ṣẹlẹ̀ láti ara mímu májèlé òògùn apako. Nígbà tí àjọṣepọ̀ láàrin ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìṣòro ìlera orí (pàápàá jùlọ, ìbànújẹ́ ọkàn àti ìṣòro ọtí mímu) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni tẹ́lẹ̀ fẹsẹ̀ múlẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò ìṣòro. Àwọn okùnfà ewu fún ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni ìrírí àdánù, ìdáwà, ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà, ìyapa ibáraẹniṣepọ̀, ìṣòro owó, ìrora ńlá àti àìsàn, ìwà ipá, àti ìjà tàbí àwọn pàjáwìrì ajẹmọ́ ọmọnìyàn mìíràn. Ìdẹ́yẹsí tí ó yí ìṣòro ilerat orí ká àti ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni túmọ̀ sí pé ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ronú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kò wá ìrànlọ́wọ́. Àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìgbìyànjú láti gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni ní agbára tí ó ń nípa lórí ẹbí, ọ̀rẹ́, alábàáṣiṣẹ́pọ̀, àwùjọ àti ìlú. Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni: àwọn òtítọ́ àti iye káàkiri àgbáyé Ìròyìn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni káàkiri àgbáyé fún 2019 GBÉ AYÉ: ṣíṣe àmúlò ìtọ́nisọ́nà funt ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè Ìdènà: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ nǹkan lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe ní àwọn ìpele ẹ̀dá kọ̀ọ̀kan, àwùjọ àti orílẹ̀ èdè láti dènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni. Ìlànà GBÉ AYÉ àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé dábàá àwọn ìpèsè mẹ́rin pàtàkì tí wọ́n múnádóko, adálórí-ẹ̀rí èyí tí ó gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ fífi sí ara ìtọ́jú orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan dórí ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni: fífi gbèdéke sí àǹfààní sí àwọn ọ̀nà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni níní àjọsọ pẹ̀lú ilé-iṣẹ́ ìròyìn fún ìròyìn tí ó tọ̀nà lórí ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ìgbèrú àwọn ìmọ̀-ọ́nṣe ìgbésí ayé àwùjọ ajẹmọ́ ìmọ̀lára láàrin àwọn ọ̀dọ́mọdé ìdámọ̀ àsìkò, ìgbéléwọ̀n, àkóso àti ìtẹ̀lé ẹnikẹ́ni tí ó ní ìpalára nípa àwọn ìwà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni. Àwọn òpó ìpìlẹ̀ ìdásí náà ni àlàyé ipò, àjọṣepọ̀ ọlọ́pọ̀ ẹ̀ka, ṣíṣe ìpolongo, ìṣẹ̀dá agbára, ṣíṣe ètò ìṣúná, àmójútó, àkóso àti ìgbéléwọ̀n. Àwọn akitiyan ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni pè fún àkóso àti àjọṣepọ̀ láàrin àwọn ọ̀pọ̀ ẹ̀ka, tí ó fi mọ́ ìlera, ẹ̀kọ́, iṣẹ́, iṣẹ́ àgbẹ̀, okòwò, ìdájọ́, òfin, òṣèlú àti iṣẹ́ ìròyìn. Ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kì í sáábà jẹ́ wíwá ojútùú sí nítorí ìdẹ́yẹsí àti àìsí ìpolongo pé ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ ńlá. Àwọn àwùjọ náà ń kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni. Irinṣẹ́ àmúlò ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àwùjọ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ń ṣe ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwọn àwùjọ láti ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn nǹkan pàtàkì àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn iṣẹ́ gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn ọ̀gangan ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ abẹ́lé. Èsì WHO: Ìpòǹgbẹ láti ṣiṣẹ́ dènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni wọ́n ti ṣe ìdámọ̀ tí wọ́n sì ti sọ di pàtàkì ní àwọn ìpele gíga jùlọ.
translations.reference: Suicide prevention Overview: More than 700 000 people die by suicide every year. For each suicide, there are an estimated more than 20 suicide attempts. Suicide can occur at any stage of life and in all regions of the world. In 2019, suicide was the fourth leading cause of death among 15–29-year-olds globally, and 77% of all suicides happened in low- and middle-income countries. Almost 20% of global suicides were due to pesticide self-poisoning. While the link between suicide and mental health conditions (in particular, depression and alcohol use disorders) and a previous suicide attempt is well established in high-income countries, many suicides happen impulsively in moments of crisis. Further risk factors for suicide include experience of loss, loneliness, discrimination, a relationship break-up, financial problems, chronic pain and illness, violence, abuse, and conflict or other humanitarian emergencies. Stigma around mental health conditions and suicide means many people thinking about suicide do not seek help. Suicides and suicide attempts have a ripple effect that impacts on families, friends, colleagues, communities and societies. Suicide: facts and figures globally Suicide worldwide in 2019 report LIVE LIFE: an implementation guide for suicide prevention in countries Prevention: Much can be done at individual, community and national levels to prevent suicide and suicide attempts. WHO’s LIVE LIFE approach recommends four effective, evidence-based key interventions which should be included in every national response to suicide: limit access to the means of suicide interact with the media for responsible reporting of suicide foster socio-emotional life skills in adolescents early identify, assess, manage and follow up anyone who is affected by suicidal behaviours. The foundational pillars of the interventions are situation analysis, multisectoral collaboration, awareness raising, capacity building, financing, surveillance, monitoring and evaluation. Suicide prevention efforts require coordination and collaboration among multiple sectors, including health, education, labour, agriculture, business, justice, law, defence, politics and the media. Suicide prevention is often not adequately addressed because of stigma and lack of awareness that suicide is a major public health problem. Communities also play a crucial role in preventing suicide. The WHO community engagement toolkit guides communities in identifying priorities and implementing activities according to local contexts. WHO response: The urgency to act to prevent suicides has been recognized and prioritized at the highest levels.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Suicide Statistics: It is estimated that 700,000 people die from suicide annually. For each suicide, there are more than 20 grieving relatives. Suicide can occur at any stage of life and in every part of the world. In 2019, suicide was the fourth leading cause of death among young people aged 15-29 worldwide, and 77% of all suicides occur in low- and middle-income countries. Approximately 20% of suicides worldwide occur due to pesticide poisoning. When there is a relationship between suicide and mental health disorders (most commonly, depression and anxiety disorders), the grieving relatives are more likely to occur in low-income countries, many suicides occur during times of distress. Risk factors for suicide include abuse, trauma, loss, social isolation, financial problems, major illness and disability, substance abuse, and violence or other interpersonal conflicts. The combination of mental health disorders and suicide means that many people who are concerned about suicide do not receive help. Suicide and the grief of those left behind affect families, friends, colleagues, communities, and cities. Suicide: Facts and Figures Worldwide Suicide statistics worldwide for 2019 WHO: Action plan for suicide prevention in countries The document: Many things can be done at each stage of development, community, and country to prevent suicide and the grief of those left behind. WHO Global Strategy The World Health Organization has identified four key areas that must be adapted to each country's specific context to address suicide: strengthening evidence-based interventions with media for reporting on suicide, training life-saving skills among youth, reducing access to lethal means, and promoting mental health and well-being. These pillars include assessment of the situation, multi-sectoral collaboration, implementation of policies, building capacity, implementing prevention programs, advocacy, management and monitoring. The actions for suicide prevention involve coordination and collaboration among various sectors, including health, education, employment, agriculture, transport, justice, finance, social welfare, and journalism. Suicide prevention should not be viewed as solely a health issue due to the recognition and policy that suicide is a major social problem. Communities play a vital role in suicide prevention. WHO’s action plan: The organization has developed a plan and has prioritized it at the highest levels.**

**Note:** I have aimed for accuracy while maintaining readability. Some nuances of the original Yoruba text might be slightly different in the English translation.

translations.source: Àdínkù ìwọ̀n ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè jẹ́ ìtọ́ka nínú àwọn Àfojúsùn ti UN (ìtọ́ka fún ìlera orí kan ṣoṣo), Ètò Gbogbogbò Iṣẹ́ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé àti Ètò Iṣẹ́ Ìlera orí Gbòòrò 2013-2030. Ìròyìn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àgbáyé kìíní ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé, Ìdènà Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni: ìmọ̀ abágbàmu káríayé, tí wọ́n tẹ̀ jáde ní 2014, tí ó ní àfojúsùn láti mú ìgbéga bá ìpolongo pàtàkì ìlera àwùjọ ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti àwọn ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni àti sísọ ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni di pàtàkì ìlera àwùjọ. Wọ́n gba àwọn ìjọba ní ìyànjú láti ṣàmúlò àgbékalẹ̀ GBÉ AYÉ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ti àwọn ìdásí pàtàkì pípé àti adálórí-ẹ̀rí fún ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti láti mú ìdàgbàsókè tàbí ìrólágbára bá àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àgbáyé gbígbòòrò nínú ìlànà ìlera àwùjọ ọlọ́pọ̀ ẹ̀ka. Ìdàrí àti àkóso àgbáyé lórí ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni wọ́n túbọ̀ ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún nípa àtẹ̀jáde àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni orílẹ̀ èdè: ìlọsíwájú, àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti ìtọ́ka. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ ìwífún àlàyé àti ìṣirò hebt fún ìkéde àti ṣíṣe ìpinnu, àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé tẹ Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni jákèjádò àgbáyé ní 2019: Àwọn Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé. Àfojúsùn àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ni ayé tí ikú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kò ti síwájú okùnfà ikú àti níbi tí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbìyànjú tàbí ronú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ti lè rí ìrọ̀rùn láti béèrè fún ìrànlọ́wọ́ èyí tí wọ́n ń pèsè nínú ipá àti ọ̀wọ̀ fún gbogbo ènìyàn.
translations.reference: The reduction of suicide rates in countries is an indicator in the UN Sustainable Development Goals (the only indicator for mental health), WHO’s General Programme of Work and WHO’s Comprehensive Mental Health Action Plan 2013–2030. The first WHO world suicide report, Preventing suicide: a global imperative, published in 2014, aimed to increase awareness of the public health significance of suicide and suicide attempts and make suicide prevention a public health priority. Governments are encouraged to implement WHO’s LIVE LIFE package of effective and evidence-based key interventions for suicide prevention, and to develop or strengthen comprehensive national suicide prevention strategies in a multisectoral public health approach. National leadership and coordination on suicide prevention are further supported by the publication of National suicide prevention strategies: progress, examples and indicators. Recognizing that data and statistics are necessary for advocacy and decision-making, WHO published Suicide worldwide in 2019: Global Health Estimates. WHO’s vision is a world where death by suicide is no longer a leading cause of death and where people who are attempting or thinking about suicide feel comfortable seeking help which is provided capably and respectfully to all.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The increasing burden of non-communicable diseases (NCDs) (focusing on cardiovascular disease specifically), the Global Jobs for Health Initiative of the World Health Organization, and the Global Action Plan for Chronic NCDs 2013-2030. The global NCD progress report of the World Health Organization, "Knowledge for Action," released in 2014, contains recommendations to accelerate the prevention and control of the national NCDs and personal risk factors and to transform NCD prevention into a national public health priority. Governments are urged to implement the WHO's Global Action Plan for the most effective and cost-effective interventions for NCD prevention and to strengthen the capacity of global NCD champions within a multi-sectoral health system. Global leadership and governance on NCD prevention needs to be further strengthened, particularly regarding the development of national NCD profiles: progress, examples, and indicators. To facilitate evidence-based policy-making and decision-making, the World Health Organization released the Global NCD Monitor in 2019: Global Health Statistics. The WHO's recommendation is that the lives lost to NCDs should not exceed the number of deaths and that people suffering or concerned about NCDs should be able to seek help, which they are currently unable to do due to stigma and disability for all people."

translations.source: Ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése Ìsọnísókí: Ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése ni ọ̀rọ̀ ìperí gbòòrò láti ṣàpèjúwe àwọn ìlànà, iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àti ìdókòwò tí ó ń pèsè àwọn àǹfààní lónìí láì kọlu ìlera àyíká, àwùjọ àti ti ara ẹni lọ́jọ́ iwájú. Àwọn ìlànà wọ̀nyí ni wọ́n sáábà máa ń ṣàpèjúwe gẹ́gẹ́ bí àìléwu nítorí pé wọ́n fojúsùn mímú àdínkù bá ipa ìdàgbàsókè lórí àyíká. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn àǹfààní ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése ń jẹ́ rírí káàkiri àwọn onírúurú ẹ̀ka ìlera àti àlàáfíà ọmọnìyàn, tí ó fi mọ́ àdínkù abayíkájẹ́- àti àìsàn adálórí àyíká, àwọn àbájáde ìlera tí ó lọ síwájú àti àdínkù ìṣòro. Bí ìṣòro àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ ṣe ń ṣe pàtàkì sí i, àwọn ọgbọ́n àmúṣiṣẹ́ fún àdínkù ìbàjẹ́, ìtẹ̀síwájú ìlera àti díndín iyeó kù ń pèsè oríṣiríṣi àǹfààní fún àwọn onírúurú àwọn àjọ. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera kánrinkése lè ṣẹ̀dá àwọn àǹfààní ìlera aláìsàn àti òṣìṣẹ́ nígbà tí wọ́n tún ń pa agbára mọ́, ṣe àdínkù ewu ojú ọjọ́ àti ìṣẹ̀dá àdínkù iyeó ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera àgbáyé ti pinnu láti lépa ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése nínú gbogbo iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ dènà àwọn ènìyàn ọjọ́ iwájú lọ́wọ́ àwọn ewu ìlera ọjọ́ òní tí wọ́n ń dàgbà. Ipa: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése lè pèsè àwọn àṣeyọrí pàtàkì fún ìlera, ojú ọjọ́ àti àyíká, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àǹfààní rẹ̀ lè jẹ́ rírí lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àfojúsùn ìlera àti ìdàgbàsókè lè jẹ́ àṣeyọrí papọ̀ nípa gbígbógun ti ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́, èyí tí ó ń ṣokùnfà ìṣirò mílíọ̀nù 7 ikú àìtọ́jọ́ lọ́dọọdún. Lára àwọn ohun abàyíkájẹ́ ni àwọn abojú-ọjọ́-jẹ́ alágbára ọlọ́jọ́ díẹ̀ (SLCPs) – pàápàá jùlọ èédú dúdú, òyì àrá àti gáàsì mẹtíìnì– tí wọ́n léwu púpọ̀ fún ìlera ṣùgbọ́n àsìkò ìsẹ̀mí wọn kì í pẹ́. Àwọn ìlànà ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àti ìdókòwò tí wọ́n ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àwùjọ mímọ́, pẹ̀lú ìrìn àti kẹ̀kẹ́ gígùn, lè ní ipa rere lórí dídára afẹ́fẹ́. Èyí lè ṣe àdínkù àwọn ewu ìlera lásìkò kan náà, tí ó fi mọ́ ẹ̀gbà, àìsàn ọkàn, àìsàn ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró àti àwọn jẹjẹrẹ kan. Wọ́n tún lè ṣe àdínkù ìṣirò àwọn ikú mílíọ̀nù 1.25 ọdọọdún láti ara ìfarapa òpópónà, nígbà tí ó ń mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá àǹfààní sí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àwọn ènìyàn pọ̀ sí. Àwọn ìlànà ilégbèé ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése lè túbọ̀ ṣe àdínkù àwọn ìtújáde nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìpinnu bí ilẹ̀ ilé àti ìlò ilẹ̀, àwọn àṣàyàn ohun èlò ìkọ́lé, àwọn àbùdá àgbékalẹ̀ àti afẹ́fẹ́ òhun agbára. Èsì WHO: Iṣẹ́ WHO ń gbà ìtọ́nisọ́nà láti ọwọ́ àwọn Àfojúsùn Ìdàgbàsókè Kánrinkése, pàápàá jùlọ Àfojúsùn 3: "Ẹ rí i dájú pé ẹ ní ìgbé ayé ìlera àti ìgbéga àlàáfíà fún gbogbo ọjọ́ orí." Àwọn Àfojúsùn náà mú àwọn iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àti àwọn ìlànà káàkiri Àjọ wà, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ìtọ́nisọ́nà lórí ilégbèé, ìdàgbàsókè ìlú, àwọn ètò ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àti àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́jú ìlera. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn ilégbèé WHO àti ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìlera, tí wọ́n kọ́kọ́ tẹ̀ jáde ní 2018, ṣe ìdámọ̀ ipa àwọn ìṣòro ilégbèé tí ó ń pọ̀ sí i lórí ìlera ọmọnìyàn nítorí ìdàgbàsókè ìlú, ojú ọjọ́ àti àwọn àyípadà iye ènìyàn. Àwọn ìtọ́nisọ́nà ń pèsè àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́, àwọn alábàáṣiṣẹ́pọ̀ àti àwùjọ pẹ̀lú àwọn àbá adálórí-ẹ̀rí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìtó ààyè ìgbé (ọ̀pọ̀ èèyàn), àwọn ìwọ̀n ìgbóná kékeré àti gọbọi inú ilé, àwọn ewu ìfarapa nínú ilé, àti níní àǹfààní sí ilégbèé fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní ìṣòro iṣẹ́ ṣíṣe. Ètò Ìlera Ìgboro (EII) tún fojúsun àwọn ọ̀nà fún ìlú láti fààyè gba ìlera rere nípa ṣíṣe àyàṣe iṣẹ́ tí ó múnádóko lórí ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ ìlú àti àwọn abàyíkájẹ́ ojú ọjọ́ ọlọ́jọ́ péréte (SLCPs) – gbígba ẹ̀mí là nípa mímú àjọṣepọ̀ bá ìlera, àyíká àti ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé tún ṣe àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àgbájọ Afẹ́fẹ́ àti Ojú ọjọ́ mímọ́ lórí Ìpolongo Ayé Ìmí, èyí tí ó fojúsun mímú àdínkù bá ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ àti mímú àdínkù bá mílíọ̀nù 7 ikú àìtọ́jọ́ lọ́dọọdún tí ó ń fà.
translations.reference: Sustainable development Overview: Sustainable development is a broad term to describe policies, projects and investments that provide benefits today without sacrificing environmental, social and personal health in the future. These policies are often described as green because they focus on limiting the impact of development on the environment. However, the benefits of sustainable development are also felt across a wide cross section of human health and well-being, including reductions in pollution- and environment-related disease, improved health outcomes and decreased stress. As the threats of climate change become increasingly important, win-win strategies for mitigation, health improvement and cost savings offer a range of advantages for various stakeholders. For example, greener health care operations can generate patient and worker health benefits while also saving energy, mitigating climate risks and creating long-term cost savings. The World Health Organization is committed to pursuing sustainable development in all its work to help protect the people of tomorrow from the health growing health risks of today. Impact: Many sustainable development strategies can offer significant wins for health, climate and the environment, and the benefits can be seen almost immediately. For example, many health and development goals can be achieved simultaneously by tackling air pollution, which is responsible for an estimated 7 million premature deaths annually. Polluting emissions include powerful short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—particularly black carbon, ozone and methane—which are very harmful to health but have only brief atmospheric lifetimes. Transport policies and investments that favour clean public transport, along with walking and cycling, can have a dramatic impact on air quality. This can reduce multiple health risks at the same time, including stroke, heart attack, lung disease and some cancers. They can also reduce the estimated 1.25 million deaths annually from traffic injury, while also improving access to health services, particularly in densely populated areas. Sustainable development housing policies can further reduce emissions through considerations such as building siting and land use, choices of construction materials, design features and ventilation and energy. WHO response: WHO’s work is guided by the Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goal 3: “Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages.” The Goals inform projects and policies across the Organization, including guidelines on housing, urban development, transportation systems and health care delivery. For example, the WHO Housing and health guidelines, first published in 2018, highlight the increasing impact of housing conditions on human health in light of urban growth, climate and demographic changes. The guidelines provide Member States, partners and the public with evidence-based recommendations relevant to inadequate living space (crowding), low and high indoor temperatures, injury hazards in the home, and accessibility of housing for people with functional impairments. The Urban Health Initiative (UHI) also focuses on ways for cities to enable good health by catalysing effective action on urban air pollution and short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—saving lives by linking health, environment and sustainable development. WHO also collaborates with the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC) on the Breathe Life campaign, which aims to cut air pollution and reduce the 7 million premature deaths annually that it causes.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Global Health Security Nexus: The Global Health Security Nexus refers to the interconnectedness of various issues – policies, cross-sectoral actions, and governance – that impact the health and well-being of populations today and in the future. These policies are particularly important because they address the underlying determinants of health.

Specifically, the health security benefits of the Global Health Security Nexus are crucial across a wide range of health and well-being areas, including reducing non-communicable diseases, infectious disease outbreaks, and the advancement of health and reducing mortality. This includes the importance of eye health, as health professionals working on disease prevention, health promotion, and reducing mortality address various health threats. The World Health Organization (WHO) has committed to integrating the Global Health Security Nexus into all of its work to support countries in protecting future generations from today’s health threats.

Impact: A robust Global Health Security Nexus can achieve significant gains for health, eye health, and well-being, and its benefits can be far-reaching. For example, integrated health and security interventions can be successful in reducing the burden of diarrheal diseases, which cause an estimated 7 million deaths annually. Among the threats are low-dose chronic pollutants (SLCPs) – primarily black carbon, ozone, and nitrous oxide – which pose a significant health risk but are often overlooked. Community-based and governance approaches that support community resilience, including water and sanitation infrastructure, can have a positive impact on reducing air pollution. This can increase the risk of certain health conditions, such as asthma, heart disease, stroke, and other complications. They can also increase the estimated 1.25 million annual deaths from respiratory infections, while also advancing progress towards health benefits and interventions, particularly in densely populated areas. Global Health Security Nexus interventions can also exacerbate inequalities through decisions related to housing and land use, building material choices, urban planning, and air quality.

WHO’s Role: WHO’s work is guided by the principles of Global Health Security Advocates, particularly Advocate 3: "Ensure a healthy life and well-being for all at all ages." The Advocates drive cross-sectoral actions and policies across the organization, focusing on governance, urban development, community-based interventions, and health promotion. For example, WHO’s health and well-being guidelines, first released in 2018, assess the impact of urban development-related health risks on child health due to urban development, eye health, and human mortality. The guidelines address national governments, partners, and communities with vulnerable populations, including those experiencing domestic violence (a large number of people), small and large household injuries, household safety hazards, and the impact on well-being for people with disabilities. The City Health Initiative (CHI) also focuses on approaches for cities to improve health outcomes by addressing the root causes of urban health and persistent environmental health threats (SLCPs) – including reducing mortality through community engagement and health security and the Global Health Security Nexus. The World Health Organization also collaborates with the Air and Health Alliance on the Breathe Life Campaign, which focuses on reducing the impact of air pollution and the estimated 7 million annual deaths it causes.**

translations.source: Sífílì Ìsọnísókí: Sífílì jẹ́ àìsàn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ tí kòkòrò bakitéríà ń tàn ká láti ọwọ́ Treponema pallidum èyí tí ó máa ń fa ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ewu àìsàn àti ikú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ṣe é wòsàn. Sífílì máa ń ràn ká nípasẹ̀ ìfarakínra ìbálòpọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ògòdò àrùn, nípasẹ̀ ìfàjẹ̀sínilára tàbí láti ara aláboyún obìnrin sí ọmọ inú rẹ̀. Ìkóràn sífílì láti ara ìyá-sí-ọmọ sáábà máa ń le púpọ̀ fún ọlẹ̀ ní àwọn ọ̀ràn tí wọ́n kò dá mọ̀ tàbí ṣe ìtọ́jú tó níbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìlóyún. Sífílì ìyálọ́mọ, nígbà tí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, wọ́n pẹ́ tọ́jú rẹ̀ tàbí wọn kò tọ́jú rẹ pẹlu pẹnisilíìnì, ń yọrí sí àwọn àbájáde ìbí líle nínú ìṣirò àwọn ọ̀ràn 50-80%, dá lórí ìpele sífílì. Àwọn àbájáde ìbí líle sáábà máa ń tí ó fi mọ́ àbíkú (lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà), ikú ìkókó, àìgbó, ìwọ̀n ìwúwo ìbí pínnísín, àti àwọn ìkókó tí àìsàn sọ di abiùn. Sífílì lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ àwọn àṣà ìbálòpọ̀ aláàbò tí ó fi mọ́ ìlò ọ̀rá ìdáàbòbò bí ó ti tọ́ àti déédéé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú sífílì kò ní èyíkéyìí àmì àìsàn kankan tàbí ní àwọn àmì àìsàn kékeré tí wọ́n kì í ṣàkíyèsí. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ àìsàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ọ̀ràn àìsàn yóò dènà ìkóràn síwájú àti àbájáde ìlóyún líle tí ó fi mọ́ àbájáde àrùn sífílì. Àyẹ̀wò lè ṣe é ṣe nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn àyẹ̀wò ayákánkán tí wọ́n lè pèsè àbájáde ní kò tó ìṣẹ́jú 20, tí ó ń fi ààyè gba ìtọ́jú àsìkò. Nígbà tí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, sífílì yóò lọ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò la onírúurú ìpele kọjá. Ìtọ́jú fún sífílì máa ń jẹ́ ṣíṣe pẹ̀lú òògùn pẹnisilíìnì benzathine tí ó ṣe é gún bí abẹ́rẹ́, pẹ̀lú ìlò 1 sí 3 èyí tí ó dá lórí ìpele àìsàn náà. Pẹnisilíìnì nìkan ló lè dènà àwọn sífílì àsìkò ìbí. Ìtọ́jú pẹ̀lú pẹnisilíìnì ti ní ìdènà láti ọwọ́ ọ̀wọ́ngógó òòrèkóòrè àti ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ ní àwọn onírúurú orílẹ̀ èdè, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi, pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú sífílì kì í ṣàfihàn àwọn àmì àìsàn tàbí wọn kì í ní ìmọ̀lára wọn. Sífílì, àìtọ́jú lo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó pín sí onírúurú àwọn ìpele:. Ìkóràn ìbálòpọ̀ máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò ìbẹ̀rẹ̀pẹ̀pẹ̀, ìyẹn ni pé, fún bí ọdún méjì lẹ́yìn àrùn. Lásìkò ìpele ìsáájú (sífílì ìpìlẹ̀), èyí tí yóò lo bí ọjọ́ 21, egbò àdáwà, aláìnírora roboto tí ó sáábà máa ń le kan (egbò) yóò farahàn ní agbègbè ìgbàsára (ìfarakanra), ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà nínú òbò, okó tàbí fùrọ̀ (ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè jẹ́ àfikún ojú ara) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè lọ láìfarahàn. Ògòdò náà yóò gba ìwòsàn pẹ̀lú tàbí láìsí ìwòsàn láàrin ọ̀sẹ̀ 3 tàbí 10. Bí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, àìsàn náà yóò tẹ̀síwájú sí ìpele àtẹ̀lé. Sífílì àtẹ̀lé ní máa ń ní àbùdá kúrúkùsá tí ó lè jẹ́ onírúurú kí ó sì sé ìsìnjẹ àwọn ìṣòro àrùn tàbí àìsàn mìíràn, ṣùgbọ́n ó sáábà máa ń mú àtẹ́lẹwọ́ àti àtẹ̀lesẹ̀. Kúrúkùsá náà kì í húnni bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè kéré láti jẹ́ àfojúfò. Ní àwọn ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n gbóná tí wọ́n sì ní omi, bí ihò ìdí àti ẹnu, ògòdò títóbi funfun tàbí ewú lè dàgbà ní ọ̀gangan egbò ìpìlẹ̀ (condyloma lata). Àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti àmì sífílì àtẹ̀lé máa ń sàn fúnra rẹ̀, kódà láìsí ìtọ́jú. Láìsí ìtọ́jú, ìpele afarasin bẹ̀rẹ̀.
translations.reference: Syphilis Overview: Syphilis is a bacterial sexually transmitted infection (STI) caused by Treponema pallidum which results in substantial morbidity and mortality, and it is curable. Syphilis is transmitted through sexual contact with the infectious lesions, via blood transfusion or from a pregnant woman to her fetus. Mother-to-child transmission of syphilis is usually devastating to the fetus in cases that were not detected or treated sufficiently early in pregnancy. Maternal syphilis, when untreated, treated late or not treated with penicillin, results in adverse birth outcomes (ABOs) estimated in 50−80% of cases, depending on the stage of syphilis.
