translations.source: Ewé tábà Ìsọnísókí: Nikotíìnì tí ó wà nínú ewé tábà máa ń fà ìkúndùn púpọ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìlò ewé tábà jẹ́ ewu ńlá pàtàkì fún àwọn àìsàn ajẹmọ́ ọkàn àti òpónà ìmí, ó lé ní 20 onírúurú ẹ̀yà àti oríṣìí ẹ̀yà jẹjẹrẹ, àti àwọn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìṣòro ìlera tí wọ́n gbèrò mìíràn. Ní ọdọọdún, ó lé ní mílíọ̀nù 8 ènìyàn ń kú láti ara lílo ewé tábà. Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ikú tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìlò ewé tábà ní wọ́n ń wáyé ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè, tí wọ́n jẹ́ àfojúsùn ìkọlù ipa àti ọjà àwọn ilé iṣẹ́ tábà. Tábà tún lè léwu fún àwọn tí wọn kì í fa sìgá. Ìsíjúsí èéfín àmukù náà tún ti fa ọ̀ràn ní àwọn àbájáde ìlera, tí ó ń fa mílíọ̀nù 1.2 àwọn ikú lọ́dọọdún. Ó tó ìdajì gbogbo àwọn ọmọdé ni wọ́n ń mí àmísínú afẹ́fẹ́ tí èéfín tábà ti bàjẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọmọdé 65 000 ń kú lọ́dọọdún nítorí àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú èèfín àmukù. Fífa sìgá lásìkò ìlóyún lè yọrí sí àwọn ìṣòro ìlera ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ fún àwọn ìkókó. Àwọn èròjà tábà gbígbóná (HTPs) ní tábà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n máa ń síjú àwọn àwọn aṣàmúlò sí ìbújáde tí ó léwu, púpọ̀ àwọn èyí tí wọ́n ń fa jẹjẹrẹ tí wọ́n sì léwu fún ìlera. Àwọn ètò àgbékalẹ̀ nikotíìnì oníná (ENDS) àti àwọn ètò àgbékalẹ̀ tí kì í nikotíìnì oníná (ENNDS), tí àwọn ènìyàn mọ̀ sí sìgá oníná, kò ní tábà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè ní tàbí kí ó má ní nikotíìnì, ṣùgbọ́n wọ́n léwu fún ìlera bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó dájú pé wọn kò ní ààbò. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé, ó ti yá jù láti pèsè ìdáhùn pípé lórí ipa ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ ìlò HTPs àti/tàbí àwọn sìgá oníná. Ipa: Ìṣirò ènìyàn bílíọ́nù 1.3 káàkiri àgbáyé ń ṣàmúlò àwọn èròjà tábà, 80% àwọn tí wọ́n wà ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Ìlò tábà ń nípa lórí òsì nípa dídarí owónàá ilé kúrò lára àwọn ìlò abẹ́lé, bí oúnjẹ àti ilégbèé, sórí tábà. Ìwà ìnáwó yìí ṣòro láti yanjú nítorí pé tábà ní àbùdá àfẹ́sódì. Ó tún máa ń fa ikú àìtọ́jọ́ àti ààbọ̀ ara fún àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn wà ní ọjọ́ orí iṣẹ́ nínú ilé èyí tí ó ń ṣokùnfà àdínkù èrè àti iyeó ìtọ́jú ìlera. Ní àfikún sí ìpa ìparun tábà lórí ìlera, àpapọ̀ àléébù sìgá mímu lórí ọrọ̀ ajé (láti ara àwọn ìnáwó ìlera àti àwọn àdánù lápapọ̀) ní wọ́n ṣírò pé ó tó bí tírílíọ́nù US$ 1.4 lọ́dún, èyí tí ó jẹ́ àpapọ̀ 1.8% iye ọjà abẹ́lé gbogbo àgbáyé lọ́dọọdún (GDP). Ó tó ìdá 40% gbogbo àwọn iyeó yìí ni wọ́n wáyé ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ń gòkè àgbà, èyí tí ó ń tọ́ka sí àwọn àjàgà pàtàkì tí àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè wọ̀nyí ń kojú. Wọ́n máa ń ṣàmúlò àwọn owó orí tábà lápá kan láti gbógun ti ipa yìí bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n jẹ́ ọ̀nà tí ó dára jùlọ láti dáwọ́ ìlò tábà dúró, pàápàá jùlọ láàrin àwọn ọ̀dọ́ àti àwọn ènìyàn ní àwùjọ elérè pínnísín. Àlékún owó orí tí ó ń mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá àwọn iyeó tábà pẹ̀lú àdínkù 10% ìlò tábà láti ọwọ́ bí 4% ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi àti 5% ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Ìwọ̀n àjálù ènìyàn àti ọrọ̀ ajé yìí yanilẹ́nu, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún ṣe é dènà. Big Tobacco – pẹ̀lú gbogbo àwọn ilé iṣẹ́ olùpèsè ọjà tábà – ń gbìyànjú láti rí i pé àwọn ewu tí ó wà nínú ọjà wọn farasin, ṣùgbọ́n wọ́n ń gbógun ti èyí padà: Ní 2003, àwọn ìlú tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé fẹnukò láti ṣàmúlò Àpérò Ìhun Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé lórí Àkóso Tábà (WHO FCTC), àdéhùn ìlera àwùjọ kan ṣoṣo lábẹ́ ìdarí Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé). Nínú àṣẹ láti 2005, ó ní Ẹgbẹ́ 181 báyìí. Láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣe àmúlò WHO FCTC, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ MPOWER L, ètò àwọn òdiwọ̀n àti orísun ìmọ̀, ọ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan èyí tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ó kéré jù ìpèsè WHO FCTC kan. MPOWER kọ́ ipa àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣàmúlò àwọn òdiwọ̀n 6 láti ṣe àdínkù ìbéèrè fún àwọn ọjà tábà: ṣe àkóso ìlò tábà àti ìdènà àwọn ìlànà, dáàbò bo àwọn ènìyàn lọ́wọ́ ìlò tábà, pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti dáwọ́ ìlò tábà dúró, ṣe ìkìlọ̀ nípa àwọn ewu tábà, fi òfin de ìpolówó, ìgbéga àti ṣíṣe onígbọ̀wọ́ tábà àti ṣíṣe àlékún owó orí tábà.
translations.reference: Tobacco Overview: Nicotine contained in tobacco is highly addictive and tobacco use is a major risk factor for cardiovascular and respiratory diseases, over 20 different types or subtypes of cancer, and many other debilitating health conditions. Every year, more than 8 million people die from tobacco use. Most tobacco-related deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries, which are often targets of intensive tobacco industry interference and marketing. Tobacco can also be deadly for non-smokers. Second-hand smoke exposure has also been implicated in adverse health outcomes, causing 1.2 million deaths annually. Nearly half of all children breathe air polluted by tobacco smoke and 65 000 children die each year due to illnesses related to second-hand smoke. Smoking while pregnant can lead to several life-long health conditions for babies. Heated tobacco products (HTPs) contain tobacco and expose users to toxic emissions, many of which cause cancer and are harmful to health. Electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) and electronic non-nicotine delivery systems (ENNDS), commonly known as e-cigarettes, do not contain tobacco and may or may not contain nicotine, but are harmful to health and undoubtedly unsafe. However, it is too early to provide a clear answer on the long-term impact of HTPs and/or e-cigarette use. Impact: An estimated 1.3 billion people worldwide use tobacco products, 80% of whom are in low- and middle-income countries. Tobacco use contributes to poverty by diverting household spending from basic needs, such as food and shelter, to tobacco. This spending behaviour is difficult to curb because tobacco is so addictive. It also causes premature death and disability of productive age adults in households thus leading to reduced household income and increased healthcare costs. In addition to the detrimental impact of tobacco on health, the total economic cost of smoking (from health expenditures and productivity losses together) are estimated to be around US$ 1.4 trillion per year, equivalent in magnitude to 1.8% of the world's annual gross domestic product (GDP). Almost 40% of this cost occurred in developing countries, highlighting the substantial burden these countries suffer. Tobacco taxes are applied in part to combat this effect and are seen as the most cost-effective way of curbing tobacco use, particularly among youth and low-income populations. A tax increase that increases tobacco prices by 10% decreases tobacco consumption by about 4% in high-income countries and about 5% in low- and middle-income countries. WHO response: The scale of this human and economic tragedy is shocking, but it’s also preventable. Big Tobacco — along with all manufacturers of tobacco products — is fighting to ensure the dangers of their products are concealed, but we are fighting back: In 2003, WHO Member States unanimously adopted the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (WHO FCTC), the only public health treaty under the auspices of WHO. In force since 2005, it has currently 181 Parties. To help countries implement the WHO FCTC, WHO introduced MPOWER, a package of technical measures and resources, each of which corresponds to at least one provision of the WHO FCTC. MPOWER builds the capacity of countries to implement 6 measures to reduce the demand for tobacco products: monitor tobacco use and prevention policies, protect people from tobacco use, offer help to quit tobacco use, warn about the dangers of tobacco, enforce bans on tobacco advertising, promotion and sponsorship and raise taxes on tobacco.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Tobacco Epidemic: Nicotine and its Effects**

Nicotine contained in tobacco causes significant addiction, and tobacco use is a major risk factor for oral and respiratory diseases, with 20 different diseases and various other health problems associated with it. Annually, 8 million people die from tobacco use. Many of these deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries, which are vulnerable to tobacco industry manipulation and marketing of tobacco products. Tobacco can also be harmful to those who do not smoke. The effects of secondhand smoke also cause problems in healthcare systems, resulting in 1.2 million deaths annually. It exposes children to harmful substances, and 65,000 children die each year from illnesses related to secondhand smoke. Occasional smoking during adolescence can lead to chronic diseases in adulthood. Tobacco heating products (THPs) and nicotine-containing and non-nicotine electronic delivery systems (ENNDS), commonly known as e-cigarettes, do not contain tobacco but may or may not contain nicotine, but they are harmful to health and it is important that they are not considered safe. It has been difficult to establish a definitive impact on the long-term health effects of THPs and/or e-cigarettes.

**Impact:** 1.3 billion people worldwide use tobacco products, 80% of whom live in low- and middle-income countries. Tobacco use impacts households by diverting resources from essential expenses like food and education to tobacco. This habit is difficult to break because tobacco is addictive. It also causes premature death and disability for older adults who are in their working years, which significantly impacts the economy and healthcare costs. In addition to the health impact of tobacco, the total economic cost of smoking (from healthcare costs and lost productivity) is estimated at US$ 1.4 trillion annually, which is 1.8% of the global total expenditure (GDP). 40% of these costs occur in countries with aging populations, indicating the significant burden these countries face. Implementing tobacco taxes is a key strategy to combat this impact and is the most effective way to reduce tobacco use, especially among young people and those in low-income communities. Tax increases that accompany tobacco price increases have an average of 10% tobacco use reduction from a baseline of 4% in high-income countries and 5% in low- and middle-income countries and lower-income countries.

**World Health Organization’s Response:** This human and economic toll is alarming, but it is not insurmountable. "Big Tobacco" – with all the tobacco product marketing companies – is actively resisting efforts to mitigate the risks of their products, but they are being challenged: In 2003, countries that were members of the World Health Organization agreed to adopt the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (WHO FCTC), a legally binding public health treaty under the leadership of the World Health Organization. The treaty has 181 parties as of now. To assist countries in implementing the WHO FCTC, the World Health Organization launched MPOWER, a set of evidence-based policy measures, each addressing a key element of the WHO FCTC. MPOWER outlines actions for countries to take six measures to increase demand for tobacco products: control tobacco use and exposure to tobacco advertising, protect people from tobacco use, help people quit using tobacco, warn about the dangers of tobacco, raise taxes, and enforce bans on tobacco advertising and promotion and implement tobacco excise taxes.

translations.source: Tirakómà Ìsọnísókí: Tirakómà jẹ́ àìsàn ojú arit asíwájú àìsàn tí ó ń fa ìfọ́jú káàkiri àgbáyé. Bakitéríà inú hóró ara kan tí orúkọ rẹ̀ ń jẹ́ Chlamydia trachomatis ni ó ń fà á. Àrùn náà máa ń ràn láti ara ènìyàn-sí-ènìyàn nípa ìkóràn tààrà tàbí àlùmọ̀kọ́rọ́yí ipin ojú tàbí ikun imú ẹni tí ó ti faragbá; lára ìkóràn àlùmọ̀kọ́rọ́yí ni ìgbésára àwọn ẹ̀yà esinsin kan. Àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n wà ní ọjọ́ orí jéléósinmi ní àká ńlá àrùn náà. Àwọn àwòṣe fi hàn pé ènìyàn yóò nílò àrùn tí ó lé ní 150 ní ayé kan kí ó tó ní àwọn ìṣòro ìfọ́jú tirakómà. Tirakómà jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó wọ́pọ̀ ní àárín àwọn ènìyàn kan tí wọ́n tòṣì jùlọ lágbàáyé, tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè abúlé àti ìgbèríko tí wọn kò sì ní àǹfààní tí ó tó sí omi, ìmọ́tótó àti ìtọ́jú ìlera. Áfríkà ni ẹ̀yà tí ó faragbá jùlọ, ṣùgbọ́n òun náà ni ó ní àwọn akitiyan àkóso tí ó fọ́n ká jùlọ. Àìsàn náà tún wà ní Àárín gbùngbùn àti Gúúsù Amẹ́ríkà, Ésíà, Australia, àti Àárín gbùngbùn Ìlà oòrùn. Ní 2 Oṣù Kìíní 2020, orílẹ̀ èdè 13 jábọ̀ àṣeyọrí àwọn àfojúsùn ìfòpinsí. Ní 2019 nìkan, àwọn ènìyàn 92, 622 gba itojut iṣẹ́ abẹ fún tirakómà líle bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni mílíọ̀nù 95.2 ènìyàn gba ìtọ́jú pẹ̀lú òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò. Pẹ̀lú gbogbo àṣeyọrí wọ̀nyí, àìsàn náà sì jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ tí ó lè, pẹ̀lú ìṣirò àdánù bílíọ́nù US$8 bílíọ́nù lọ́dún nínú ìpèsè nítorí ìfọ́jú àti àléébù ojú. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Àrùn náà ni àṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn àyípadà ìrẹ̀dòdò nínú àwo inú ojú tí a mọ̀ sí "tirakómà tí ń ṣiṣẹ́". Ètò àjẹsára ẹ̀dá ènìyàn kan lè ṣe àmúkúrò ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àrùn kan ṣoṣo, ṣùgbọ́n ní àwọn àwùjọ tí àìsàn náà pọ̀ sí, àtúnkó kòkòrò naat máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ dáadáa. Lẹ́yìn ọ̀pọ̀ ọdún àṣetúnṣe àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsàn tirakómà, inú awọ ẹyinjú lè ní àwọn àpá ńlá (àpá tirakómà awọ ojú) tí yóò sì sẹ́po sínú èyí tí yóò mú kí irun ìpéǹpéjú máa nu ẹyinjú (tirakómà tirikíásì), èyí tí yóò máa fa ìrora lemọ́lemọ́ àti àìlefarada ìmọ́lẹ̀; èyí àti àyípadà ẹyinjú mìíràn lè fa àpá nínú ẹyinjú. Bí ó bá wà láìsí ìtọ́jú, ìṣòro yìí yóò yọrí sí ìkójọ àwọn ìwò bàlùbàlù tí kò ṣe é yí padà, èyí tí yóò yọrí sí àléébù ojú tàbí ìfọ́jú. Ọjọ́ orí tí èyí yóò ṣẹlẹ̀ dá lórí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìdí tí ó fi mọ́ agbára ìkóràn abẹ́lé. Ní àwọn àwùjọ tí ó wọ́pọ̀ sí gan-an, ó lè ṣẹlẹ̀ ní ọmọdé, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìṣòro ojú láàrin ọdún 30 sí 40 wọ́pọ̀ dáadáa. Àwọn obìnrin máa ń fojú ní ìlọ́po mẹ́rin bí àwọn ọkùnrin, bóyá nítorí ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àwọn àwọn ọmọ tí wọ́n ti lùgbàdì. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn àwùjọ olósì àti ìgbèríko Áfríkà, Ésíà, Australia, Àárín gbùngbùn Ìlà oòrùn, Àárín gbùngbùn àti Gúúsù Amẹ́ríkà àti àwọn Erékùṣù Pàsífíìkì wà nínú ewu. Ìtọ́jú: Tirakómà ṣe é dènà nípa mímú àdínkù bá àwọn ewu àyíká. Lára àwọn wọ̀nyí ni àìtó ìmọ́tótó, àwọn ilé tó kún fún ọ̀pọ̀ èrò, àìní àǹfààní tó tó sí omi àti àìní àǹfààní tó tó sí àti ìlò ìmọ́tótó. Àwọn àrùn sáábà máa ń bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ọjọ́ orí pínnísín bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n kì í dàgbà dáadáa àti pé àsìkò wọn kéré pẹ̀lú ọjọ́ orí tí ó ń lọ síwájú. Àwọn àrùn sáábà máa ń jẹ́ kíkó nípasẹ̀ ẹbí àti ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àìsàn náà. Àṣetúnṣe àwọn àrùn náà máa ń fa àwọn àmì àìsàn líle àrùn náà.
translations.reference: Trachoma Overview: Trachoma is a disease of the eye and the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. It is caused by an obligate intracellular bacterium called Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person by direct or indirect transfer of ocular and nasal discharges of infected people; indirect transfer includes carriage on the body of species of flies. Preschool-age children harbour the principal reservoir of infection. Models suggest that an individual requires more than 150 lifetime infections to develop the blinding complications of trachoma. Trachoma is endemic in some of the world’s poorest populations, who live in rural and remote areas and have highly inadequate access to water, sanitation and healthcare. Africa is the most affected continent, but it also has the most widespread control efforts. The disease is also found in Central and South America, Asia, Australia, and the Middle East. As of 2 January 2020, 13 countries had reported achieving elimination goals. In 2019 alone, 92 622 people received surgical treatment for advanced trachoma and 95.2 million were treated with antibiotics. Despite these successes, the disease remains a serious public health concern, with an estimated US$ 8 billion annual loss in productivity due to blindness and visual impairment. Symptoms: Infection is associated with inflammatory changes in the conjunctiva known as “active trachoma”. An individual’s immune system can clear a single episode of infection, but in endemic communities, re-acquisition of the organism occurs frequently. After years of repeated episodes of active trachoma, the inside of the eyelid can become so severely scarred (trachomatous conjunctival scarring) that it turns inwards and causes the eyelashes to rub against the eyeball (trachomatous trichiasis), resulting in constant pain and light intolerance; this and other alterations of the eye can lead to scarring of the cornea. Left untreated, this condition leads to the formation of irreversible opacities, with resulting visual impairment or blindness. The age at which this occurs depends on several factors including local transmission intensity. In very highly endemic communities, it can occur in childhood, though onset of visual impairment between the ages of 30 and 40 years is more typical. Women are blinded up to four times as often as men, probably due to more frequent contact with infected children. Individuals living in the poorest and most remote communities of Africa, Asia, Australia, the Middle East, Central and South America and the Pacific Islands are at risk. Treatment: Trachoma can be prevented by limiting environmental risk factors. These include inadequate hygiene, crowded households, inadequate access to water and inadequate access to and use of sanitation. Infections usually begin at an early age and grow less frequent and shorter in duration with increasing age. Infections are most often acquired through family and contact with others with active disease. Repeated infections cause the most serious symptoms of the disease.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Topic: Chlamydia**

Chlamydia is a sexually transmitted infection (STI) that is a leading cause of preventable infections worldwide. It is caused by a bacterium called *Chlamydia trachomatis*. The infection is transmitted from person to person through sexual contact, including vaginal, anal, or oral sex; among these contacts are the genitals of infants. Adolescents are particularly vulnerable to this infection. Studies show that a person needs to have 150 encounters in a lifetime to have chlamydia symptoms. Chlamydia is a common infection among sexually active young people worldwide, particularly those living in impoverished communities with limited access to clean water, education, and healthcare. Africa is the most affected region, but it also has the greatest control measures. The infection is also found in Central and South America, Asia, Australia, and Central Eastern Europe. As of June 2, 2020, 13 countries reported successful screening efforts. In 2019 alone, 92,622 people received syphilis prevention services for chlamydia, and 95.2 million people received treatment with antibiotics. Despite all this progress, the infection remains a major public health concern, with an estimated annual cost of US$8 billion in treatment for eye and reproductive health issues.

**Symptoms:**

The infection is associated with inflammation within the eye's inner lining, known as "active chlamydia." A person's immune system can sometimes clear the infection on its own, but in communities where the infection is prevalent, complications can occur. After years of chlamydia infection, the eyelids can develop large bumps (chlamydial eyelid bumps) that can spread into the eye, leading to scarring of the eyelids (chlamydial conjunctivitis), which can cause dryness and light sensitivity; this and other eyelid changes can cause bumps in the eyelids. If left untreated, this condition can lead to the formation of abnormal eyelashes that can cause eye irritation, which can lead to eye damage or blindness. This can occur at any age, but is more common in people with a history of multiple sexual partners. Women are more likely to experience eye problems than men, possibly due to sexual contact with infected children. People living in impoverished and rural communities in Africa, Asia, Australia, Central Eastern Europe, Central and South America, and the Pacific Islands are at risk.

**Prevention:**

Chlamydia can be prevented by reducing risk factors. These include lack of education, inadequate housing, limited access to clean water, and lack of access to and use of healthcare. Concurrent infections often begin within a few weeks of exposure and do not always improve and have a short duration. Concurrent infections are often spread through families and sexual contact with the infection. Treatment of these infections can lead to symptoms similar to the infection.

translations.source: Wọ́n lè fòpin sí tirakómà gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò ọgbọ́n SAFE, tí ó ní i: Iṣẹ́ abẹ láti ṣe ìtọ́jú tirakómà tirikíásì, ìpele ìfọ́jú àìsàn náà.; Òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò láti pa àrùn, pàápàá jùlọ lílo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò azithromycin, èyí tí olùpèsè rẹ̀ fi tọrẹ fún àwọn ètò orílẹ̀ èdè nípasẹ̀ Ètò Tirakómà ti Àgbáyé.; Ìmọ́tótó ojú; àti Ìdàgbàsókè àyíká, pàápàá jùlọ níní àǹfààní sí omi àti ìmọ́tótó. Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé lo ọgbọ́n SAFE ní 1993 bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n tẹ̀síwájú nínú ètò wọn láti pèsè ìdarí àti àkóso àwọn akitiyan àgbáyé láti fòpin sí tirakómà gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ.
translations.reference: Trachoma can be eliminated as a public health problem using the SAFE strategy, comprising: Surgery to treat trachomatous trichiasis, the blinding stage of the disease; Antibiotics to clear infection, particularly mass drug administration of the antibiotic azithromycin, which is donated by the manufacturer to national programmes through the International Trachoma Initiative; Facial cleanliness; and Environmental improvement, particularly better access to water and sanitation. WHO adopted the SAFE strategy in 1993 and continues in its mandate to provide leadership and coordination of international efforts to eliminate trachoma as a public health problem.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"It can be linked to a very serious epidemic of infectious diseases regarding the implementation of the SAFE (Study of Actionable Factors for Elimination) initiative, which includes: Free service to assess trachoma prevalence, the level of disease severity; Broad-spectrum antibiotics to treat the infection, especially the use of multiple broad-spectrum antibiotics like azithromycin, which its supplier has donated for national programs through the Global Trachoma Elimination Program; Eye examination; and Hygiene promotion, especially in relation to water and sanitation. The World Health Assembly adopted the SAFE initiative in 1993 and they have continued their program to manage and control global activities linked to a very serious epidemic of infectious diseases."

translations.source: Òògùn ìbílẹ̀, àfikún àti àṣepọ̀. Ìsọnísókí: Òògùn ìbílẹ̀ Òògùn ìbílẹ̀ ní ìtàn tí ó ti pẹ̀. Ó jẹ́ àpapọ̀ gbogbo ìmọ̀, ìmọ̀-ọ́nṣe, àti àwọn iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n da lórí àwọn ìlànà, ìgbàgbọ́, àti ìrírí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú onírúurú àṣà, yálà ó ṣe é ṣàlàyé tàbí bẹ́ẹ̀ kọ́, tí wọ́n ń lò fún àkóso ìlera àti fún ìdènà, àyẹ̀wò, ìlọsíwájú tàbí ìtọ́jú ìlera àgọ́ ara tàbí ìlera ọpọlọ. Òògùn àfikún Ọ̀rọ̀ ìperí "òògùn àfikún" tàbí "arọ́pò òògùn" ni àwọn onírúurú ètò iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ikera tí wọn kì í ṣe ara àṣà ti orílẹ̀ èdè yẹn tàbí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọn kò sè àmúlò wọn tán sínú ètò ìtọ́jú ìlera tí ó gbòde. Wọ́n máa ń lò wọ́n ní ìpààrọ̀ pẹ̀lú òògùn ìbílẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè kan. Àwọn òògùn egbòogi Lára àwọn òògùn egbòogi ni àgbo, èròjà egbòogi, àwọn ìpèsè egbòogi àti ọjà egbòogi tí wọ́n ti parí iṣẹ́ sí lára, tí ó ní àwọn èròjà ara àwọn ohun ọ̀gbìn, tàbí àwọn èròjà ohun ọ̀gbìn mìíràn, tàbí àwọn àpapọ̀. Ipa: Ìlànà Òògùn Ìbílẹ̀ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 2014-2023 ni wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ tí wọ́n sì fi lólẹ̀ ní ìfèsì sí ọ̀nà àbáyọ Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ (WHA62. 13). Ìlànà náà fojúsun ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìlànà ìjígìrì àti àmúlò ètò ìṣe tí yóò ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ipa tí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ ń kó nínú mímú kí àwọn ènìyàn wà ní àlàáfíà. Wíwá ojútùú sí àwọn ìṣòro, fífèsì sí àìní tí àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ṣe ìdámọ̀ àti ìtẹ̀síwájú lórí iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n ṣe lábẹ́ Ìlànà Òògùn abẹ́lé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: 2002-2005, ìlànà àmúdójú-ìwọ̀n fún àsìkò 2014-2023 fi àsìkò púpọ̀ sílẹ̀ ju aṣáájú rẹ̀ lọ fún mímú fífi ọwọ́ pàtàkì mú àwọn iṣẹ́ àti ètò ìlera, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn èròjà òògùn ìbílẹ̀ àti àfikún, àwọn àṣà àti aṣàmúlò. Àwọn ìlànà èròǹgbà ni: Láti ṣẹ̀dá ọgbọ́n àgbékalẹ̀ fún àkóso iṣẹ́ T & CM nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìlànà orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n tọ́.; Láti ṣe ìrónilágbára fún ìdánilójú dídára, ààbò, ìlò tí ó tọ́ àti ìmúnádóko T & CM nípa fífi òfin de àwọn ọjà, àṣà àti aṣàmúlò.; Láti ṣe àgbéga ìpèsè ìlera káríayé nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn iṣẹ́ T & CM nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera àti ìtọ́jú ìlera ara ẹni. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Ìròyìn àgbáyé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ àti àfikún 2019 ni wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ láti kojú àlàfo tí ó wà nínú ìṣeégbáralé, ìdára àti ojúlówó ìwífun-àlàyé láti ọwọ́ àwọn Orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ní agbègbè T & CM. Ìròyìn náà ṣàyẹ̀wò ìlọsíwájú káríayé T & CM láàrin àwọn ogún ọdún sẹ́yìn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó dá lórí àwọn ipa láti ọwọ́ àwọn Ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 179. Ó ń pèsè àlàyé gidi fún àwọn aṣòfin, alámọ̀já ìlera àti àwùjọ fún ṣíṣe àmúlò ipa ọjọ́ ọ̀la ìlera àti àlàáfíà T & CM.
translations.reference: Traditional, Complementary and Integrative Medicine Overview: Traditional medicine Traditional medicine has a long history. It is the sum total of the knowledge, skill, and practices based on the theories, beliefs, and experiences indigenous to different cultures, whether explicable or not, used in the maintenance of health as well as in the prevention, diagnosis, improvement or treatment of physical and mental illness. Complementary medicine The terms “complementary medicine” or “alternative medicine” refer to a broad set of health care practices that are not part of that country’s own tradition or conventional medicine and are not fully integrated into the dominant health-care system. They are used interchangeably with traditional medicine in some countries. Herbal medicines Herbal medicines include herbs, herbal materials, herbal preparations and finished herbal products, that contain as active ingredients parts of plants, or other plant materials, or combinations. Impact: The WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014–2023 was developed and launched in response to the World Health Assembly resolution on traditional medicine (WHA62.13). The strategy aims to support Member States in developing proactive policies and implementing action plans that will strengthen the role traditional medicine plays in keeping populations healthy. Addressing the challenges, responding to the needs identified by Member States and building on the work done under the WHO traditional medicine strategy: 2002–2005, the updated strategy for the period 2014–2023 devotes more attention than its predecessor to prioritizing health services and systems, including traditional and complementary medicine products, practices and practitioners. The strategic objectives are: To build the knowledge base for active management of T&CM through appropriate national policies; To strengthen the quality assurance, safety, proper use and effectiveness of T&CM by regulating products, practices and practitioners; To promote universal health coverage by integrating T&CM services into health care service delivery and self-health care. WHO response: The WHO global report on traditional and complementary medicine 2019 was developed to address the gap in reliable, credible and official data from Member States in the area of T&CM. This report reviews global progress in T&CM over the past two decades and is based on contributions from 179 WHO Member States. It provides valuable information for policy-makers, health professionals and the public for capitalizing on the potential contribution of T&CM to health and well-being.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Traditional Medicine, Complementary, and Alternative.**

**Topic:** Traditional Medicine

Traditional medicine has a long history. It is a repository of all knowledge, medical science, and practices based on the principles, beliefs, and values associated with various cultures, and it explains how they are used for disease prevention and treatment, diagnosis, promotion, or management of both individual and community illnesses.

**Complementary Medicine**

The term "complementary medicine" or "alternative medicine" refers to various healthcare service programs that are not part of the traditional practices of a particular country or traditional medicine. They are not fully integrated into the formal healthcare system. They are often used in conjunction with traditional medicine in some countries.

**Herbal Medicines**

Among the herbal medicines are roots, herbal powders, herbal preparations, and herbal products that have undergone processing, containing plant ingredients, or other plant ingredients, or combinations thereof.

**Impact:**

The World Health Organization's (WHO) Plan of Action 2014-2023 was launched and aligned with the Global Strategy on Traditional Medicine (WHA62. 13). The plan focuses on strengthening and supporting member states to develop national policies and programs that will support the role of traditional medicine in promoting health and well-being. It emphasizes addressing challenges, strengthening the monitoring and evaluation of work carried out under the WHO Plan of Action on Traditional Medicine: 2002-2005. The extended action plan for the period 2014-2023 has a longer timeframe than its predecessor for promoting the allocation of resources to health programs, including traditional and complementary medicines, practices, and regulations.

**Key Strategies:**

*   To create a knowledge base for the management of T&CM (Traditional and Complementary Medicine) services through national programs.
*   To strengthen the capacity for quality assurance, standardization, proper use, and safety of T&CM by regulating markets, practices, and regulations.
*   To strengthen global health services by integrating T&CM services into health service delivery and personal health care.

**WHO Report:**

The World Health Organization's Global Report on Traditional and Complementary Medicine 2019 was launched to address the gaps in evidence, data, and information sharing from Member States in the area of T&CM. The report reviews global T&CM progress over the past few years and is based on contributions from 179 Member States of the World Health Organization. It provides practical information for policymakers, health professionals, and communities to implement effective policies for the future of health and well-being of T&CM.

translations.source: Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara Ìsọnísókí: Ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró, ìṣù ara àti àwọn ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn. Ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró, ìṣù ara tàbí ẹ̀yà ara ń gba ẹ̀mí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ènìyàn là bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ń mú àwọn ojúṣe padà bọ̀ sípò níbi tí kò ti sí ìmúnádóko tí ó ṣe é fi wé. Ní ọdún 50, ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara ti di àṣeyọrí àṣà káàkiri àgbáyé. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé, àwọn ìyàtọ̀ ńlá wà láàrin àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè nínú àǹfààní wọn sí ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara tí ó bojúmu àti nínú ìpele ààbò, dídára, agbára ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n pèsè fún ìpààrọ̀ àti ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró, ìṣù ara àti ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn. Àwọn agbọn ẹ̀kọ́ ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara síwájú. Pàápàá jùlọ, àwọn àìní aláàárẹ̀ tí kò jẹ́ bíbá pàdé àti àìtó àwọn ẹ̀yà ara fún ìpààrọ̀ máa ń yọrí ìdánwò ìjíngbéyọ-ẹ̀yà ara fún ìpààrọ̀. Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn Àwọn hóró ara àti ìṣù ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún ìpààrọ̀ lè gba àwọn ẹ̀mí là tàbí kí ó mú ìpadàbọ̀sípò bá àwọn ojúṣe pàtàkì. Fún àpẹẹrẹ iṣẹ́ abẹ ìpààrọ̀ ìṣù ẹyinjú lè mú ìpadàbọ̀sípò bá ìríran fún ìfọ́jú inú ẹyinjú.; ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró ẹ̀jẹ̀ ìpìlẹ̀ aláìgbó lè ṣe ìwòsàn fún àwọn àìsàn àtìgbà èwe wá tàbí àìsàn tí ènìyàn kó bí jẹjẹrẹ inú ẹ̀jẹ̀. Ẹ rí àlàyé sí i lórí Hóró Ẹ̀jẹ̀ Ìpìlẹ̀ Aláìgbó. ìpààrọ̀ òpó ìdarí ọkàn ènìyàn sáábà máa ń ní àwọn ipò ìpààrọ̀ tí wọ́n dára jùlọ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn aláìsàn kò nílò ìwòsàn àìmẹ́jẹ̀dì ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara pẹ̀lú ti ẹranko Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara pẹ̀lú ti ẹranko, ẹranko sí ènìyàn, ni àwọn hóró alààyè, ìṣù ara tàbí ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n wá láti ara ẹranko àti àwọn omira ẹ̀dá ènìyàn, hóró, ìṣù ara tàbí ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n kò sí lára àwọn ẹ̀dá alààyè wọ̀nyí tẹ́lẹ̀, àwọn èròjà ẹ̀dá mìíràn, ní agbára láti jẹ́ arọ́pò fún àwọn èròjà tí wọ́n ti ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn wá kí wọ́n sì dí àlàfo tí ó wà nínú èròjà ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún ìpààrọ̀. Ìtọrẹ: Ìtọrẹ láti ara àwọn òkú gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ ṣíṣe ìdàgbàsókè fún dórí òpin agbára ìwòsàn wọn yàgò fún, nígbàkigbà tí ó bá ṣe é ṣe, àwọn ewu fún olùtọrẹ ààyè, gẹ́gẹ́ bí Ilanat Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 3 ṣe sọ ọ́. Ìdámọ̀ àmúyẹ kan wà fún àwọn àwùjọ, àti alámọ̀já ìlera, àti kẹ́kọ̀ọ́ daradaeat sí i nípa ìtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìyẹn jẹ́ pàtàkì fún àṣeyọrí àwọn ètò ìtọrẹ òkú. Àmọ́ sá, pẹ̀lú gbogbo òòrèkóòrè ìlò àwọn èròjà tí àwọn òkú fi tọrẹ, àwọn ìtọrẹ láti ọwọ́ àwọn alààyè olùtọrẹ pọn dandan fún àwọn oríṣìí ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara kan tàbí láti pèsè fún àwọn èròjà kan tí wọ́n kò sí ní ànító láti ọwọ́ àwọn òkú olùtọrẹ láti ṣe alábàápàdé àwọn àìní aláìsàn. Nítorí náà ni wọ́n ṣe máa ń ṣe àmúlò ìtọrẹ alààyè bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ó ní àwọn ewu tí wọ́n lè má ṣe é dijú sí fún olùtọrẹ. Bí gbígba ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún ìpààrọ̀ láti ara òkú tàbí alààyè olùtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ tí ó tẹ̀lé lè ní ewu ẹ̀kọ́ àti ààbò fún agbàtọrẹ àti olùtọrẹ, àwọn àkóso tí wọ́n dúró dáadáa àti àbójútó tí ó múnádóko gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ gbígbé kalẹ̀ láti ọwọ́ àwọn aláṣẹ ìlera láti dáàbò bò wọ́n. Àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà kàn ìtọ́jú tí ó dára jùlọ nípa fún àwọn olùtọrẹ àti agbàtọrẹ. Àbójútó tí ó hànde láti ọwọ́ àwọn aláṣẹ lórí ìtọrẹ àti àwọn iṣẹ́ ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara tún ṣe pàtàkì láti mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìgbẹ́kẹ̀lé àwùjọ nínú ètò náà. Ní àfikún, ìpinnu láti jẹ olùtọrẹ sáábà máa ń dá lórí ìlóye pé kíkó ipa nínú wíwà aowm èròjà ìpààrọ̀ lè jẹ́ àǹfààní fún àìní ìlera ẹbí olùtọrẹ ní ọjọ́ kan. Bí a bá gbáradì láti gba ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara nígbà tí s bá nílò ọ̀kan, nígbà náà ó yẹ ká ṣetán láti tọrẹ Àwọn Ẹ̀kọ́: Àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí Ìpààrọ̀ Hóró Ẹ̀dá Ènìyàn, Ìṣù ara àti Ẹ̀yà ara.
translations.reference: Transplantation Overview: Transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs Transplantation of human cells, tissues or organs saves many lives and restores essential functions where no alternatives of comparable effectiveness exist. In 50 years, transplantation has become a successful worldwide practice. However, there are large differences between countries in access to suitable transplantation and in the level of safety, quality, efficacy of donation and transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs. The ethical aspects of transplantation are at the forefront. In particular, the unmet patients’ needs and the shortage of transplants lead to the temptation of trafficking in human body components for transplantation. Human transplantation Human cells and tissues for transplantation can save lives or restore essential functions. For examplea corneal graft can restore sight in corneal blindness; the transplantation of haematopoietic stem cells can cure congenital or acquired diseases including some leukaemias See further information on Haematopoeitic Stem Cells the transplantation of a human heart valve often constitutes the best replacement situation and recipient patients do not require long term anti-coagulation therapy. Xenotransplantation Xenotransplantation, animal to human, defined as living cells, tissues or organs of animal origin and human body fluids, cells, tissues or organs that have ex vivo with these living, xenogeneic materials, has the potential to constitute an alternative to material of human origin and bridge the shortfall in human material for transplantation. Donation: Donations from deceased persons should be developed to their maximum therapeutic potential avoiding, whenever possible, the inherent risks to live donors, as stated in WHO Guiding Principle 3. There is a recognized need for communities, and health professionals, to become better educated about donation and transplantation and that is the key to the success of deceased donation programmes. However, despite the frequent use of materials donated from deceased donors, the donations from living donors are necessary for some types of transplants or to compensate for the limited supply of material available from deceased donors in order to meet patient needs. Living donation is thus practised despite the fact that it involves risks for the donor that may not be negligible. As the procurement of human material for transplantation from deceased or living donors and the subsequent allogeneic transplantation may entail ethical and safety risks for both the recipient and the donor, strict controls and effective oversight should be carried out by the health authorities to protect them. The Guiding Principles mandate optimal care for donors and recipients. The transparent oversight of the health authorities over donation and transplantation activities is also essential to increase the trust of the public in the system. In addition, the decision to be a donor is often based on the understanding that a contribution to the availability of transplant resources may someday benefit the health needs of the donor’s family. If we are prepared to receive a transplant should we need one, then we should be ready to give Ethics: WHO Guiding Principles on Human Cell, Tissue and Organ Transplantation
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Tissue Engineering: Tissue, Cells, and Human-Derived Tissues.** Tissue engineering, cell therapy, and tissue-derived human tissues are all beneficial. Cell therapy or tissue engineering receives a large amount of life force, and it also causes dimensions to return to normal where they were previously absent. In 50 years, tissue engineering has become a common practice worldwide. However, significant differences exist between countries in their adoption of tissue engineering, in terms of awareness, accessibility, and the level of tissue engineering expertise used to produce tissue for engineering and cell therapy. Advanced tissue engineering research continues. Most importantly, the lack of standardized protocols and the allocation of tissues for engineering can lead to flawed human tissue engineering testing. **Human Tissue Engineering** Human cells and human-derived tissues for engineering can receive life force or cause a reduction in critical dimensions. For example, burn wound tissue engineering can reduce scarring for wound healing; tissue engineering of skin stem cells can treat skin diseases or infections that occur in the skin. See details on Skin Stem Cells. Organ donation for organ transplant recipients will have the best possible tissue engineering positions, and patients will not need long-term immunosuppressive medication. **Tissue Engineering with Animal Tissue** Tissue engineering with animal tissue, animal to human, includes animal-derived tissues and human-derived tissues, tissues that do not exist among these animal tissues, other engineered products, with the potential to be a substitute for human-derived products to create a bridge within human tissue engineering. **Donation:** Donation from the deceased should be carried out in a way that maximizes their therapeutic potential, as the World Health Organization’s Guideline 3 states. There is a need for education for communities and healthcare professionals, and further training on donation and tissue engineering, as it is essential for the success of deceased organ donation programs. Moreover, with all the complexities of using donated products, donations from animal donors are essential for various tissue engineering applications or to supplement those that are not available from deceased donors to reduce health disparities. Therefore, they perform donor animal tissue testing even though it may not be safe for the recipient. As human-derived tissue for engineering from a deceased donor or animal donor and subsequent tissue-derived engineering can have risks of infection and rejection for the recipient and donor, robust and reliable regulatory frameworks must be in place by healthcare professionals to oversee them. Guidelines for ethical judgment provide the best information for donors and recipients. Collaboration from healthcare professionals on donation and tissue engineering work is also essential to promote community involvement in the program. In addition, the decision to become a donor should be based on the understanding that participation in the evaluation of tissue engineering products can be beneficial for the donor’s family’s health. If it is possible to receive tissue engineering when needed, it should be done to donate. **Guidelines:** World Health Organization Guidelines on Human Tissue Engineering, Cells, and Tissues.

translations.source: Ní 1987, Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé tí kò sí mọ́, lórí ìṣòwò fún èrè lórí ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn, ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ ìgbáradì àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé kìíní lórí Ìpààrọ̀ Ẹ̀yà ara, èyí tí Àjọ náà fọwọ́ sí ní 1991 nínú onat àbáyọ WHA44. 25. Àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà wọ̀nyí ti nípa ńlá lórí àwọn òfin àti ìṣe alámọ̀já àti ilé aṣòfin káàkiri àgbáyé fún bí ogún ọdún. Lẹ́yìn ìjùmọ̀sọ̀rọ̀ tí ó gba ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún, Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé Ìkẹtalélọ́gọ́ta ṣàmúlò ọ̀nà àbáyọ WHA463. 22 ní 21 Oṣù karùn-ún 2010, tí eomt sì fọwọ́ sí ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé àti ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn agbọn fún ìtẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe àmúdára àwọn àṣà ìtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara. Ìkéde Istanbul Àwọn alámọ̀já ìtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara láti gbogbo àgbègbè, nípasẹ̀ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àjọ àti ibùdó wọn, ń fọwọ́ sí Ìkéde Istanbul lórí ìjíngbéyọ-ẹ̀yà ara àti ìrìn àjò lọ sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí ẹ̀yà ara ti rọjú tí wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ lábẹ́ ìdarí Àjọ Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara àti Àjọ Àgbáyé lórí ìmọ̀ kíndìnrín ní Oṣù Karùn-ún 2008.
translations.reference: In 1987 the fortieth World Health Assembly, concerned at the trade for profit in human organs, initiated the preparation of the first WHO Guiding Principles on Transplantation, endorsed by the Assembly in 1991 in resolution WHA44.25. These Guiding Principles have greatly influenced professional codes and practices as well as legislation around the world during almost two decades. After a consultation process that took several years, the Sixty-third World Health Assembly adopted resolution WHA63.22 on 21 May 2010, endorsing the updated WHO Guiding Principles and identifying areas of progress to optimize donation and transplantation practices. The Declaration of Istanbul Professionals of donation and transplantation from all regions, through many of their organizations and institutions, are endorsing the Declaration of Istanbul on organ trafficking and transplant tourism developed under the leadership of the Transplantation Society and the International Society of Nephrology in May 2008.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"In 1987, the World Health Assembly, unaware of the funding for research on the subject of human genetic integrity, adopted Resolution 1 of the Guidelines for Ethical Considerations of the World Health Assembly on Genetic Engineering, which the Assembly approved in 1991 in agenda item WHA44.25. These Guidelines have had a significant impact on the policies and practices of medical and legal institutions worldwide for over a decade. Following extensive deliberations, the Second World Health Assembly adopted agenda item WHA46.3.22 on June 21, 2010, which also approved the modernization of the Guidelines for Ethical Considerations of the World Health Assembly and the implementation of actions to strengthen the protection of human rights and genetic integrity. The Istanbul Declaration: Medical and Genetic Integrity from All Regions, through various organizations and their branches, adopted the Istanbul Declaration on cross-border genetic services and the referral of cases of genetic disorders to countries with the necessary expertise under the leadership of the Committee on Genetic Engineering and the World Health Assembly on scientific knowledge in June 2008."

translations.source: Ìrìn àjò àti ìlera Ìsọnísókí: Ìrìn àjò àgbáyé lè mú onírúurú ewu bá ìlera, èyí dá lórí àwọn àbùdá arìnrìn-àjò àti ìrìnàjò náà. Àwọn arìnrìn-àjò lè ní àwọn àyípadà òjìji nínú ìrúkèrúdò, òtútù, kòkòrò àìfojúrí, àti ìgbóná, èyí tí ó lè yọrí sí àìlera. Ní àfikún, àwọn ewu ìlera líle lè dìde ní àwọn àgbègbè tí ilégbèé kò ti dára, ìmọ́tótó àti àyíká mímọ́ kò tó, àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera kò ní ìdàgbàsókè tó bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni kò sí omi tí ó mọ́. Ìrìnàjò àgbáyé ń pọ̀ sí i, bí ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ènìyàn ṣe ń rin ìrìn àjò fún ìdí iṣẹ́, àwùjọ, ìgbádùn àti ọ̀rọ̀ ọmọnìyàn lọ́dọọdún. Gbogbo àwọn arìnrìn-àjò gbọ́dọ̀ gbáradì fún onírúurú ewu tí wọ́n lè kojú ní àwọn àyíká tí wọn kò dé rí tẹ́lẹ̀, lásìkò àti lẹ́yìn tí wọ́n rin ìrìn àjò. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbèrò láti rin ìrìn àjò gbọ́dọ̀ gbàmọ̀ràn lórí ewu tí ó lè wà ní àwọn àgbègbè tí wọ́n ń lọ kí wọ́n sì lóye ọ̀nà tí ó dára jùlọ láti dáàbò bo ìlera wọn kí wọ́n sì mú àdínkù bá ewu kíkó àìsàn. Ìṣètò ọjọ́ iwájú, àwọn òté ìdáàbòbò tí wọ́n tọ́ àti ìsọ́ra tí ó yẹ lè dáàbò bo ìlera àwọn arìnrìn-àjò, kí ó sì ṣe àdínkù ewu ìjàm̀bá àti kíkó àìsàn. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ilé-iṣẹ́ iṣẹ́ ìṣègùn àti ìrìn àjò lè pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ gbígbòòrò àti ìmọ̀ràn ìjìnlẹ̀, ó sì jẹ́ ojúṣe arìnrìn-àjò láti wá ìròyìn, láti lóye àwọn ewu tí wọ́n ní í ṣe àti láti gbé ìgbésẹ̀ tí ó yẹ láti dáàbò bo ìlera wọn nígbà tí wọ́n bá ń rin ìrìn àjò. Ewu: Nígbà tí a bá ń ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn ewu ìlera fún ìrìnàjò àgbáyé, àwọn arìnrìn-àjò àti àwọn alámọ̀já ìlera gbọ́dọ̀ wo àwọn kókó pàtàkì wọ̀nyí: ọna ìrìnrìn-àjò (àwọn) ibi afẹ́dé àsìkò àti ìgbà ìrìnàjò ìdí ìrìnàjò ìkojúwọ̀n ilégbèé, ìmọ́tótó oúnjẹ àti àyíká mímọ́ ìwà arìnrìn-àjò ìlera àtẹ̀yìnwá arìnrìn-àjò Arìnrìn-àjò lè kojú àwọn ewu ìlera wọ̀nyí dá lórí ibi afẹ́dé wọn: ìgéjẹ ẹranko tàbí kòkòrò, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ẹ̀fọn, ìdun, àfòpiná, àti àwọn kòkòrò àfòká mìíràn, tí wọ́n lè tan àìsàn bí maléríà. ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú ẹ̀jẹ̀ tàbí àwọn omira mìíràn àyípadà àyíká, bí ìrúkèrúdò, òtútù, ìgbóná àwọn àìsàn àtara-oúnjẹsẹ̀ àti àtara-omisẹ̀ àìtó àwọn iṣẹ́ ìṣègùn ìfarapa, tí ó fi mọ́ láti ara ìjàm̀bá ojú pópó tàbí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìnajú ìlera ọpọlọ, tí ó fi mọ́ wàhálà àti ìyàlẹ́nu àṣà
translations.reference: Travel and health Overview: International travel can pose various risks to health, depending on the characteristics of both the traveller and the travel. Travellers may encounter sudden and significant changes in altitude, humidity, microbes, and temperature, which can result in ill-health. In addition, serious health risks may arise in areas where accommodation is of poor quality, hygiene and sanitation are inadequate, medical services are not well developed and clean water is unavailable. International travel is on the rise, as millions of people travel for professional, social, recreational and humanitarian purposes each year. All travellers must prepare for the variety of health risks they can be exposed to in unfamiliar environments before, during and after they travel. People planning to travel should seek advice on the potential hazards in their chosen destinations and understand how best to protect their health and minimize the risk of acquiring disease. Forward planning, appropriate preventive measures and careful precautions can protect the health of travellers, and minimize the risks of accident and of acquiring disease. Although the medical profession and the travel industry can provide extensive help and sound advice, it remains the traveller’s responsibility to seek information, to understand the risks involved and to take the necessary precautions to protect their health while travelling. Risk: When determining the health risks of international travel, travellers and health professionals should consider the following key factors: mode of transport destination(s) duration and season of travel purpose of travel standards of accommodation, food hygiene and sanitation behaviour of the traveller underlying health of the traveller Travellers may encounter the following health risks depending on their destination: animal and insect bites, including mosquitoes, ticks, fleas, and some flies, which can spread diseases such as malaria exposure to blood or other bodily fluids environmental changes, such as altitude, humidity, temperature foodborne and waterborne diseases inadequate medical services injuries, including from road traffic accidents or recreational activities psychological health, including stress and culture shock
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Travel and Health Advisory: Global Travel and Health Risks:** Global travel can expose travelers to various health risks, based on the specific travel health and travel-related factors. Travelers may experience symptoms such as fever, chills, cough, diarrhea, and vomiting, which can lead to illness. Additionally, health risks can arise in areas with poor sanitation, limited access to accurate information and clean water. Global travel is increasing, as more people travel for work, leisure, education, and social events. All travelers should be aware of the various risks they may encounter in unfamiliar environments, both before and after their travels. People planning to travel should be aware of the potential risks in the areas they are visiting and should understand the best ways to protect their health and minimize the risk of illness. Pre-travel consultations, appropriate vaccinations, and prophylactic medications can protect the health of travelers and reduce the risk of serious illness and complications. While healthcare and travel agencies can provide significant support and guidance, it is the traveler's responsibility to stay informed about the risks they face and to take appropriate steps to protect their health while traveling.

**Risks:** When assessing health risks for global travel, travelers and healthcare professionals should consider the following key factors:

*   Travel route (destination)
*   Duration and timing of travel
*   Reason for travel
*   Traveler's health status
*   Traveler's vaccination history
*   Traveler's access to healthcare

Travelers can contract the following health risks based on their destination:

*   Insect bites (mosquitoes), which transmit diseases like malaria, dengue fever, yellow fever, and other insect-borne diseases.
*   Exposure to heat or other fluids, leading to dehydration, diarrhea, and other gastrointestinal and urinary tract illnesses.
*   Lack of access to healthcare services, leading to complications from serious illnesses.
*   Eye infections or other health services that cause discomfort and distress.
*   Poor sanitation and hygiene practices.
