translations.source: Àwọn arìnrìn-àjò tún lè faragbá onírúurú iye àwọn àìsàn, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn wọ̀nyí ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn àrùn kán wà, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn àrùn tí wọ́n léwu jùlọ, èyí tí kò sí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára fún. Àwọn ibi afẹ́dé níbi tí ilégbèé, ìmọ́tótó àti àyíká mímọ́, ìtọ́jú ìṣègùn àti àbùdá omi ti dára púpọ̀ ní ewu líle níwọ̀n fún ìlera àwọn arìnrìn-àjò. Níbi tí àwọn iṣẹ́ wọ̀nyí kò ti jẹ́ pípèsè, àwọn arìnrìn-àjò gbọ́dọ̀ gbé ìgbésẹ̀ ìsọ́ra gidi láti yàgò fún àìsàn. Àwọn ikilọ ìrìnàjò láti ọwọ́ àwọn àjọ ìjọba gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ fífi ọwọ́ líle mú. Ìdáàbòbò: Síwájú ìrìnàjò: ẹ kàn sí ibùdó ìṣègùn fún ìrìnàjò tàbí oníṣègùn, bí ọ̀sẹ̀ 4-8 kí ẹ tó rin ìrìnàjò láti gba àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tí ó yẹ. ẹ ka nípa àìsàn abẹ́lé tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ibi afẹ́dé yín; ẹ béèrè fún ìròyìn lórí ewu maléríà àti ìdènà ẹ̀fọn jíjẹ. Èyí lè mọ́ ríra òògùn idenat tí ó tọ́ àti kíkó àwọ̀n ibùsùn àti òògùn ẹ̀fọn; ẹ ṣé àyẹ̀wò ìṣègùn àti eyín; ẹ gba àwọn àbá àkọsílẹ̀ fún òògùn gẹ́gẹ́ bí àsìkò tí ẹ máa lò kí ẹ sì ṣe àtòjọ irinṣẹ́ ìṣègùn tí ó yẹ; ẹ ra adójútòfo ìṣègùn tí ó yẹ pẹ̀lú ìpèsè tí ó tọ́ lẹ́yìn odi. Lásìkò ìrìnàjò: ẹ jẹ oúnjẹ tí wọ́n ṣe dáadáa àti ohun mímu tútù tí ó wà nínú ìgò tàbí ìdì nìkan. Ẹ ṣe omi mímu bí ẹ bá ń ṣiyèméjì nípa ààbò. ẹ ṣàkíyèsí àwọn ìjàm̀bá tàbí ìṣòro tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìjàm̀bá, ẹranko, èèwọ̀, òòrùn àti eré ìdárayá; ẹ mú ìwé pélébé tí ó ń ṣàfihàn ẹ̀yà ẹ̀jẹ̀ yín lọ́wọ́; ẹ wọ ẹ̀gbà ọwọ́ ìtanilólobó ìlera, bí ẹ bá ní àwọn èèwọ̀ tàbí àìsàn líle. Àwọn arìnrìn-àjò gbọ́dọ̀ ṣe àyẹ̀wò ìṣègùn nígbà tí wọ́n bá padà bí wọ́n ba: padà pẹ̀lú ibà láti orílẹ̀ èdè kan tí maléríà wà tàbí lè wà ní àìsàn líle ní àìsàn lẹ́yìn ọ̀sẹ̀ díẹ̀ tí wọ́n padà sí ilé gba ìtọ́jú maléríà nígbà tí wọ́n ń rin ìrìn àjò lè ti ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àrùn líle kan lò ju oṣù mẹ́ta lọ ní orílẹ̀ èdè tí ń gòkè àgbà
translations.reference: Travellers may also be exposed to a number of infectious diseases, and many of these can be prevented by vaccination. However, there are some infectious diseases, including some of the most dangerous, for which no vaccines exist. Destinations where accommodation, hygiene and sanitation, medical care and water quality are of a high standard pose relatively few serious risks to the health of travellers. Where these services are not provided, travellers should take stringent precautions to avoid illness. Travel warnings from governmental sources should be taken seriously. Prevention: Before travel: contact a travel medicine centre or a physician, preferably 4–8 weeks before departure to receive any required vaccinations; read up on local diseases related to your destination; request information on malaria risk and prevention of mosquito bites. This may include purchasing appropriate preventive medication and packing a bednet and insect repellent; get a medical and dental check-up; obtain prescriptions for medication according to length of stay and assemble a suitable medical kit; purchase medical insurance with appropriate cover abroad During travel: eat only thoroughly cooked food and drink only bottled or packaged cold drinks. Boil drinking-water if safety is doubtful. be aware of accidents or problems related to traffic, animals, allergies, sun and sport; carry a card showing your blood group; wear a medical alert bracelet, if you have allergies or a chronic disease Travellers should obtain a medical examination on their return if they: return with a fever from a country where malaria is or may be present suffer from a chronic disease experience illness in the weeks following their return home received treatment for malaria while travelling may have been exposed to a serious infectious disease spent more than 3 months in a developing country
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

Travelers can also prevent various diseases, and many of these are done through vaccinations. However, some diseases exist that have no vaccinations, and conditions such as poor sanitation, lack of clean water, inadequate nutrition, and poor hygiene pose a significant risk to travelers' health. Where these conditions are not ideal, travelers must take concrete preventative measures to protect against disease. Travel advisories from government agencies should be strictly adhered to.

**Recommendations: Before Travel:** Consult a health clinic or healthcare provider 4-8 weeks before travel to obtain necessary vaccinations.  Inquire about yellow fever and malaria prevention. This may involve obtaining the appropriate antimalarial medication and other preventative measures.  Check your vaccination status and receive any necessary boosters; obtain prescriptions for any necessary medications, and make a list of required vaccinations. Purchase appropriate travel health insurance with adequate coverage.

**During Travel:** Eat nutritious food and consume adequate fluids, preferably boiled or purified water. Be mindful of potential problems associated with diarrhea, parasites, worms, fever, and dehydration; carry a mosquito net; wear protective clothing, especially if you have any existing conditions or illnesses. Travelers should seek medical attention upon return if: they return with a fever from a country where malaria is present or could be present, or if they develop a fever within three weeks of returning home.  Malaria testing should be done during travel or within three months of returning from a country with high malaria prevalence.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ikọ́ọfe Ìsọnísókí: Ikọ́ọfe ni bakitéríà (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) máa ń fà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó daabat máa ń mú àwọn ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró. Ikọ́ọfe máa ń tàn ká nípa afẹ́fẹ́ nígbà tí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọn ní ikọ́ọfe ní ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró bá wúkọ́, sín tàbí tutọ́. Eniyan nílò láti fa àwọn kòkòrò àrùn díẹ̀ sínú láti ṣe àìsàn. Lọ́dọọdún, mílíọ̀nù 10 ènìyàn máa ń ṣàìsàn ikọ́ọfe (TB). Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ó jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó ṣe é dènà tí ó sì se é wò, mílíọ̀nù 1.5 ènìyàn kú nítorí TB ní ọdọọdún – tí ó mú kí ó jẹ́ àrùn apànìyàn tí ó lékè jùlọ lágbàáyé. Ikọ́ọfe jẹ́ okùnfà ikú àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní KASA àti okùnfà ìlòdì sí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò bakitéríà gbòógì. Púpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ṣàìsàn ikọ́ọfe ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè, ṣùgbọ́n ikọ́ọfe wà káàkiri gbogbo àgbáyé. Bí ìdajì àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé pẹ̀lú ikọ́ọfe lè wà ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè 8 : Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippinesàti South Africa. Bí ìdá kan nínú mẹ́rin àwọn ènìyàn àgbáyé ni wọ́n ṣírò pé wọ́n ní kòkòrò bakitéríà ikọ́ọfe, ṣùgbọ́n ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ènìyàn kò ní tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àrùn TB bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn kan yóò san nínú àrùn náà. Àwọn tí wọ́n ní àrùn náà ṣùgbọ́n tí wọn kò tíì ṣe àìsàn kò lè ṣe ìkóràn àìsàn náà. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní bakitéríà ikọ́ọfe ní ewu ìgbésí ayé 5-10% láti ṣàìsàn ikọ́ọfe. Àwọn tí wọ́n ní ìṣòro ètò àjẹsára, bí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé pẹ̀lú KASA, àìjẹunrekánú tàbí àtọ̀gbẹ, tàbí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń fa tábà, wà nínú ewu púpọ̀ láti ṣe àìsàn. Àmì àìsàn: Lára àwọn àmì àrùn ikọ́ọfe tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ni: Ikọ́ líle, Àyà dídùn, Rírẹ̀ tàbí ọ̀rẹ̀rẹ̀, RÍrù, Ibà, Òógùn òru Lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà, àwọn àmì àìsàn yìí yóò wà níwọ̀n fún ọ̀pọ̀ oṣù, èyí tí yóò yọrí sí àwọn ìdádúró láti wá ìtọ́jú bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò mú ìlọsíwájú bá ewu kíkó àrùn náà ran àwọn ẹlòmíràn. Bí oníṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera bá kẹ́fín pé aláìsàn kan ní àrùn ikọ́ọfe, wọn yóò rán aláìsàn náà lọ ṣe àyẹ̀wò. Ní ti ọ̀ràn ìkẹ́fín àrùn ikọ́ọfe ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró, wọn yóò sọ fún àwọn aláìsàn kí wọ́n pèsè kẹ̀lẹ̀bẹ̀ wọn fún àyẹ̀wò bakitéríà TB. Fún àwọn àrùn TB tí kò jẹ́ mọ̀ ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró, àwọn àpẹẹrẹ omira àti ìṣù ara tí ọ̀rọ̀ kan lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe àyẹ̀wò. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé dábàá àwọn àyẹ̀wò mòlíkúlà ayákánkán gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn àyẹ̀wò aṣáájú fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ṣàfihàn àmì àti àpẹẹrẹ TB. Àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìṣàyẹ̀wò mìíràn lè jẹ́ àyẹ̀wò fínnífínní kẹ̀lẹ̀bẹ̀ àti àwòrán onítànsàn-X àyà. Pẹ̀lú àrùn TB, ènìyàn lè ní àrùn bakitéríà TB tí yóò wà nínú ara láìṣe ọsẹ́. Àrùn yìí lè dàgbà di àrùn ikọ́ọfe bí ètò àjẹsára wọn bá káàárẹ̀. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní àrùn TB kì í ṣàfihàn èyíkéyìí àmì tàbí àpẹẹrẹ TB. Láti ṣe ìdámọ̀ àrùn TB, àwọn oníṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera yóò ṣàyẹ̀wò àwọn aláìsàn tí wọ́n nínú ewu láti wọ́gilé TB tí ó wà nínú ara, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè lo àyẹ̀wò awọ ara tàbí ẹ̀jẹ̀ láti ṣàyẹ̀wò àrùn TB. Ìtọ́jú: Àrùn ikọ́ọfe ṣe é wò.
translations.reference: Tuberculosis Overview: TB is caused by bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) and it most often affects the lungs. TB is spread through the air when people with lung TB cough, sneeze or spit. A person needs to inhale only a few germs to become infected. Every year, 10 million people fall ill with tuberculosis (TB). Despite being a preventable and curable disease, 1.5 million people die from TB each year – making it the world’s top infectious killer. TB is the leading cause of death of people with HIV and also a major contributor to antimicrobial resistance Most of the people who fall ill with TB live in low- and middle-income countries, but TB is present all over the world. About half of all people with TB can be found in 8 countries: Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines and South Africa. About a quarter of the global population is estimated to have been infected with TB bacteria, but most people will not go on to develop TB disease and some will clear the infection. Those who are infected but not (yet) ill with the disease cannot transmit it. People infected with TB bacteria have a 5–10% lifetime risk of falling ill with TB. Those with compromised immune systems, such as people living with HIV, malnutrition or diabetes, or people who use tobacco, have a higher risk of falling ill. Symptoms: Common symptoms of TB disease include: Prolonged cough, Chest pain, Weakness or fatigue, Weight loss, Fever, Night sweats Often, these symptoms will be mild for many months, thus leading to delays in seeking care and increasing the risk of spreading the infection to others. If the healthcare provider suspects a patient to have TB disease, they will send the patient for testing. In the case of suspected lung TB disease, patients will be asked to give a sputum sample for testing for TB bacteria. For non-lung TB disease, samples of affected body fluids and tissue can be tested. WHO recommends rapid molecular diagnostic tests as initial tests for people showing signs and symptoms of TB. Other diagnostic tools can include sputum smear microscopy and chest X-rays. With TB infection, a person gets infected with TB bacteria that lie inactive in the body. This infection can develop into TB disease if their immune system weakens. People with TB infection do not show any signs or symptoms of TB. To identify TB infection, healthcare providers will screen at-risk patients to rule out active TB, and they may use a skin or blood test to check for TB infection. Treatment: TB disease is curable.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Tuberculosis (TB) Information:** Tuberculosis is a disease caused by the bacterium *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* and it causes pulmonary lesions. Tuberculosis spreads through the air when people with pulmonary tuberculosis cough, sneeze, or speak. A person needs to expel some germs to cause infection. Annually, 10 million people get sick with tuberculosis (TB). Although it is a curable disease, 1.5 million people die from TB each year – making it the most deadly infectious disease worldwide. Tuberculosis is a major cause of death among people with HIV/AIDS and a major risk factor for severe bacterial drug resistance. Many of the people who get sick with tuberculosis live in poor and underdeveloped countries, but tuberculosis exists all over the world. The number of people living with tuberculosis can be found in 8 countries: Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines, and South Africa. One-fourth of the world's population is estimated to have latent tuberculosis bacteria, but many people do not progress to develop active TB disease, and some will reactivate the disease. People with active disease who have not been treated cannot transmit the disease. People with tuberculosis bacteria have a 5-10% lifetime risk of developing tuberculosis. Those at higher risk of developing the disease include people living with HIV/AIDS, immunocompromised individuals, elderly people, or people who smoke.

**Symptoms:** Common symptoms of tuberculosis include: persistent cough, fever, sweating or chills, chest pain, weakness, and fatigue. These symptoms can last for several months, which can lead to delays in seeking medical attention and increase the risk of spreading the disease. If a healthcare professional suspects that a patient has tuberculosis, they will refer the patient for testing. In the diagnosis of pulmonary tuberculosis, patients will be asked to provide sputum samples for tuberculosis bacteria testing. For non-pulmonary TB diseases, urine and blood samples can be tested. The World Health Organization recommends molecular tests as advanced tests for people presenting with TB symptoms and samples. Other diagnostic tests can be sputum smear microscopy and chest X-rays. With TB, a person can have bacterial TB in the body without symptoms. This disease can progress to active tuberculosis if their immune system weakens. People with TB may not show any symptoms or samples of TB. To detect TB, healthcare workers will screen high-risk patients to identify TB that is present in the body, and they may use skin tests or blood tests to diagnose TB.

**Treatment:** Tuberculosis is curable.

translations.source: Wọ́n máa ń tọ́jú rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú gbèdéke oṣù 6 plus àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò 4. Lára àwọn òògùn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ni rifampicin àti isoniazid. Ní ti àwọn ọ̀ràn kan bakitéríà TB kì í gbọ́ iwodsnt láti ara àwọn òògùn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ kan. Ní ti ọ̀ràn yìí, aláìsàn náà ni TB alòdìsí-òògùn. nítorí pé TB alòdìsí-òògùn máa ń pẹ́ ó sì ṣòro púpọ̀. Wọn yóò pèsè ìlò àwọn òògùn náà fún aláìsàn pẹ̀lú àlàyé, àmójútó àti àtìlẹ́yìn láti ọwọ́ òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera tàbí akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ afarajìn. Láìsí àtìlẹ́yìn yẹn, ìfarajìn fún ìtọ́jú lè ṣòro. Bí ìtọ́jú náà kò bá di ṣíṣe tan, àìsàn náà lè di alòdìsí-òògùn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè ràn ká. Ní ti ọ̀ràn àrùn TB (níbi tí aláìsàn ti ní àrùn TB ṣùgbọ́n tí kò ṣàìsàn), ìtọ́jú ìdènà TB lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe láti dáwọ́ ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ àrùn náà dúró. Ìtọ́jú yìí ń ṣàmúlò àwọn òògùn kan náà fún ìgbà díẹ̀. Àwọn àṣàyàn ìtọ́jú lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ ti kéré àsìkò fún ìtọ́jú sí oṣù 1 tàbí 3, ní àfiwé oṣù 6 ní ìgbà kan.
translations.reference: It is treated by standard 6 month course of 4 antibiotics. Common drugs include rifampicin and isoniazid. In some cases the TB bacteria does not respond to the standard drugs. In this case, the patient has drug-resistant TB. for drug-resistant TB is longer and more complex. The course of TB drugs is provided to the patient with information, supervision and support by a health worker or trained volunteer. Without such support, treatment adherence can be difficult. If the treatment is not properly completed, the disease can become drug-resistant and can spread. In the case of TB infection (where the patient is infected with TB bacteria but not ill), TB preventive treatment can be given to stop the onset of disease. This treatment uses the same drugs for a shorter time. Recent treatment options have shortened the duration to treatment to only 1 or 3 months, as compared to 6 months in the past.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Treatment is typically for 6 months plus 4 high-intensity drugs. Among the common drugs are rifampicin and isoniazid. In some cases of tuberculosis (TB) bacteria, the body does not respond to the common drugs. In this case, the patient has multi-drug resistant TB (MDR-TB) because MDR-TB is severe and difficult to treat. They will prescribe the drug regimen for the patient with explanation, support, and adherence from a healthcare worker or a community health worker. Without that support, adherence to treatment can be difficult. If treatment is not completed, the illness can become drug-resistant and potentially fatal. In cases of TB illness (where the patient has TB but is not drug-resistant), TB detection treatment can be effective to stop the spread of the disease. This treatment involves using certain drugs for a short period. Treatment options previously had shorter durations for treatment, typically 1 or 3 months, except for the 6-month regimen in some cases."

translations.source: Ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò Ìsọnísókí: Ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò ni ìwọ̀n gígùn ìgbì láti 100-400m, èyí tí ó jẹ́ ìpele ńlá bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó kéré ní ìwọ̀n gígùn ìgbì ju iná tí ó ṣe é rí lọ. Ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò máa ń wá láti ara òòrùn láìṣẹ̀sá, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè jẹ́ ṣíṣẹ̀dá láti ọwọ́ àwọn orísun àìtọọ́dá tí wọ́n ń lò ní ilé iṣẹ́, okòwò àti ìgbafẹ́. Agbègbè ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò ni ìwọ̀n gígùn ìgbì láti 100-400m bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó pín sí ẹ̀yà mẹ́ta: UVA (315-400 nm)UVB (280-315 nm)UVC (100-280 nm). Bí òòrùn bá ṣe ń kọjá nípasẹ̀ ojú sánmọ̀, gbogbo UVC àti bí 90% ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ UVB ni òsóònù, omi, ìkùukùu àti aléèédúléjì ọ́kísíjìn máa ń fà sínú. Ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ UVA kí í rí ìnira púpọ̀ láti ara òfurufú. Nítorí náà, ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò tí ó ń kan Ilé ayé máa ń ní UVA pẹ̀lú ẹ̀yà UVB kékeré. Iye ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ UV láti ara òòrùn tí ó ń dé Ilé Ayé dá lórí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn nǹkan, tí ó fi mọ́ gíga òòrùn lójú sánmọ̀, ìsọrínù, ìbòjú òfuurufú, gíga, ìnípọn awẹ́ òsóònù àti ìtànṣán ilẹ̀. Àdínkù nínú awẹ́ òsóònù nítorí ìbàyíkájẹ́ àtọọ́dá ẹ̀dá máa ń mú ìdàgbàsókè bá iye UVA àti UVB tí ó ń dé ojú náà. Èyí lè nípa lórí ìlera ènìyàn, ẹranko, àwọn ẹ̀dá inú omi àti ìgbésí ayé ohun ọ̀gbìn. Láàrin àwọn ènìyàn, ìfaragbá UV púpọ̀ lè fa jẹjẹrẹ awọ ara, owó ojú àti bíbá ètò àjẹsára jẹ́. Ìdènà: Bí iye àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ní jẹjẹrẹ awọ ara ṣe ń pọ̀ sí i láti bí ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn ní àṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú òòrùn lásìkò àwọn iṣẹ́ ìta gbangba àti àwọn orísun àtọọ́dá ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀ bí àtùpà atànbíòòrùn àti àwọn ibùsùn onífẹ̀ẹ́wẹ́lẹ́. Àpọ̀jù ìfaragbá tún ni okùnfà àwọn ipa burúkú lórí àwọn ojú àti ètò àjẹsára. Ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn ìsọ́ra kéékèèké díẹ̀ lè mú àdínkù bá ewu àwọn ìlera wọ̀nyí: Ẹ ṣe àdínkù àsìkò gbígba òòrùn ọ̀sán láti aago 10 àárọ̀ di 4 ìrọ̀lẹ́, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn ọjọ́ tí atọ́ka UV bá ti pọ̀ jù. Nígbà tí àwọn ìtànṣán UV bá lè jùlọ tí ẹ sì gbọ́dọ̀ wà níta, ẹ gbìyànjú láti wá ibòji kí ẹ sì wọ àwọn aṣọ aláàbò, tí ó fi mọ́ àkẹtẹ̀ tí yóò pèsè ààbò lọ́wọ́ òòrùn fún orí, ojú àti ọrùn yín. Ẹ lo ìpara agbòòrùn ọlọ́pọ̀ ìṣe ti ó kéré jù 15 SPF – ní gíga tó– kí ẹ sì máa tún lò ní wákàtí méjì méjì. Ẹ yàgò fún àtùpà atànbíòòrùn àti ilé onífẹ̀ẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ ara, nítorí pé wọ́n máa ń bá awọ ara àti àwọn ojú jẹ́. Ìfarakínra àwọn ọmọdé pẹ̀lú òòrùn gbọ́dọ̀ dín kù dáadáa nítorí pé wọ́n wà nínú ipò àyídáyidà ìdàgbàsókè nítorí náà ni wọ́n fi wà nínú ewu àyíká ju àwọn àgbàlagbà lọ. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ojúṣe pàtàkì bí ètò àjẹsára kì í dàgbà tán lásìkò ìbí, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn àyíká tí wọn kò ní ààbò lè ṣe ìdíwọ́ fún ìdàgbàsókè wọn bí ó ṣe yẹ. Èsì WHO: WHO ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ àti àwọn olùbádòwòpọ̀ láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlóye àwùjọ nípa àwọn ipa àpọ̀jù ìfaragbá ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò. Ètò INTERSUN jẹ́ àjọṣepọ̀ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé, United Nations Environment Programme, World Meteorological Organization, International Agency on Cancer Research àti International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection. Ó ń ṣe àgbéga àti ìgbéléwọ̀n ìwádìí lórí àwọn ipa ìlera ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ èsì tí ó tọ́ nípasẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà, àbá àti pínpín ìròyìn ká. Lára àwọn àfojúsùn ètò naa ni pípèsè ìmọ̀ràn tí ó ṣe mú lò àti ìmọ̀ràn dídára lórí àwọn ipa ìfaragbá ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò lórí ìlera àti àyíká, láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti gbé ìgbésẹ̀ láti se àdínkù àwọn ewu ìlera ajẹmọ́-ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò àti láti pèsè ìtọ́nisọ́nà fún àwọn aláṣẹ orílẹ̀ èdè nípa àwọn ètò ìpolongo òòrùn. Láti ṣe èyí, ètò náà ṣe àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn alámọ̀já àti àwọn àjọ pàtàkì láti ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ àwọn iṣẹ́ ìwádìí pàtàkì, ṣe ìdámọ̀ àti sọ iye ewu ìlera láti ara ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò, ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn àsọtẹ́lẹ̀ tí wọ́n ṣe é gbáralé nípa àwọn àbájáde ìlera àti àyíká lórí àwọn àyípadà sí ìfaragbá ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò pẹ̀lú àdínkù òsóònù ojú ọ̀run, àti ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ọ̀nà tí wọ́n ṣe é mú lò láti mójútó àyípadà àwọn ipa ìlera ajẹmọ́ ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò fún ìgbà pípẹ́. Ètò náà ń pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ìtọ́nisọ́nà tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú atọ́ka ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò, ìdáàbòbò lọ́wọ́ òòrùn fún àwọn ọmọdé, ibùsùn olóòrùn àtọọ́dá, ìgbafẹ́ àti ìlera iṣẹ́.
translations.reference: Ultraviolet radiation Overview: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation covers the wavelength range of 100–400 nm, which is a higher frequency and lower wavelength than visible light. UV radiation comes naturally from the sun, but it can also be created by artificial sources used in industry, commerce and recreation. The UV region covers the wavelength range 100-400 nm and is divided into three bands: UVA (315-400 nm)UVB (280-315 nm)UVC (100-280 nm). As sunlight passes through the atmosphere, all UVC and approximately 90% of UVB radiation is absorbed by ozone, water vapour, oxygen and carbon dioxide. UVA radiation is less affected by the atmosphere. Therefore, the UV radiation reaching the Earth’s surface is largely composed of UVA with a small UVB component. The amount of UV radiation from the sun that hits the Earth’s surface depends on several factors, including the sun’s height in the sky, latitude, cloud cover, altitude, the thickness of the ozone layer and ground reflection. Reductions in the ozone layer due to human-created pollution increase the amount of UVA and UVB that reaches the surface. This can impact human health, animals, marine organisms and plant life. In humans, increased UV exposure can cause skin cancers, cataracts and immune system damage. Prevention: The rise in the number of cases of skin cancer over the past decades is strongly related to increased exposure to the sun during outdoor activities and to artificial sources of UV radiation such as sunlamps and tanning beds. Overexposure is also the underlying cause of harmful effects on the eyes and immune system. Adopting a few simple precautions can greatly reduce the risk of these health conditions: Limit time in the midday sun from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m., particularly on days when the UV index is high When UV rays are most intense and you must be outside, try to find shade and wear protective clothing, including a brimmed hat that provides sun protection for your head, face and neck. Use a broad-spectrum sunscreen of at least 15 SPF – ideally higher– and reapply every 2 hours. Avoid sunlamps and tanning parlours, as they are known to damage the skin and eyes. Children’s UV exposure should be carefully limited because they are in a dynamic state of growth and therefore more susceptible to environmental threats than adults. Many vital functions such as the immune system are not fully developed at birth, and unsafe environments may interfere with their normal development. WHO Response: WHO works with Member States and partners to increase public understanding of the effects of overexposure to ultraviolet radiation. The INTERSUN Programme is a collaboration between WHO, the United Nations Environment Programme, the World Meteorological Organization, the International Agency on Cancer Research and the International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection. It promotes and evaluates research on the health effects of UV radiation, and develops an appropriate response through guidelines, recommendations and information dissemination. The goals of the programme including providing practical and sound advice on the health and environmental impacts of UV exposure, to encourage countries to take action to reduce UV-induced health risks and to provide guidance to national authorities about sun awareness programmes. To this end, the programme collaborates with experts and specialist agencies to implement key research activities, identifies and quantifies health risks from UV radiation, develops reliable predictions of health and environmental consequences of changes in UV exposure with stratospheric ozone depletion, and develops practical ways of monitoring change in UV-induced health effects over time. The programme provides tools and guidelines related to the UV index, sun protection for children, artificial sunbeds, tourism and occupational health.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Ultraviolet (UV) Radiation: Information**

Ultraviolet (UV) radiation has a wavelength range of 100-400m, which is a high level and smaller than the wavelength range of visible light. UV radiation usually comes from the east without exception, but it can also be generated from sources of artificial radiation used in industries, workshops, and beauty parlors. The area of UV radiation has a wavelength range of 100-400m and is divided into three categories: UVA (315-400 nm), UVB (280-315 nm), and UVC (100-280 nm). As sunlight passes through the atmosphere, all UVC and about 90% of UVB radiation are absorbed by the ozone layer, water vapor, and suspended particles. UVA radiation can penetrate deeply from the upper atmosphere. Therefore, sunlight reaching the Earth has UVA with a small proportion of UVB. The amount of UV radiation from the sun reaching the Earth affects many things, including increased sunlight through the atmosphere, sunburn, premature aging, high ozone levels, and climate change. Increased ozone depletion can lead to an increase in UVA and UVB radiation reaching the skin. This can affect human health, livestock, aquatic organisms, and ecosystems. Among humans, excessive UV exposure can cause skin cancer, eye damage, and cataracts.

**Facts:** The number of people with skin cancer has been increasing since the 1990s, along with the increase in sun exposure during outdoor work and the use of artificial UV radiation sources such as tanning beds and sunlamps. Further exposure also has negative effects on the eyes and cataracts. Regular eye examinations can help reduce the risk of these health problems: Increase the time of sun protection from 10 am to 4 pm, especially on days when the UV index is high. When UV radiation is likely to be high, it is advisable to seek shade and wear protective clothing that covers the body, which will shield the skin from sunlight for the head, eyes, and skin. Use a broad-spectrum sunscreen with a high SPF (at least 15) and apply it twice. Use tanning beds and sunlamps sparingly, as they can damage the skin and eyes. Children's sun exposure should be limited as much as possible because they are in a more vulnerable position due to their developing skin. Many critical systems, such as the immune system, do not fully develop until childhood, and the damage they do not have protection against can affect their development.

**WHO Initiative:** WHO works with member states and partners to promote the effective use of knowledge about the health effects of excessive ultraviolet (UV) radiation exposure. The INTERSUN program is a partnership between the World Health Organization, the United Nations Environment Programme, the World Meteorological Organization, the International Agency on Cancer Research, and the International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection. It strengthens and expands knowledge on the health effects of UV radiation, develops evidence-based policies through research, and disseminates information. Among the program's activities are clarifying the knowledge that promotes its use and specific knowledge about the health and mortality effects of UV radiation exposure to inform countries to take steps to prevent UV-related health risks and provide guidance to policymakers on sun protection programs. To do this, the program collaborates with experts and relevant organizations to conduct critical research, assess and quantify the health risks from UV radiation, develop interventions to reduce the health and mortality risks associated with UV radiation exposure with increased ozone depletion, and develop strategies to mitigate the long-term health effects of UV radiation exposure. The program also addresses misinformation and misconceptions about UV radiation, promotes sun protection for children, encourages the use of sunbeds, beauty parlors, and occupational health and safety.

translations.source: Iṣẹ́ ìlera Káríayé Ìsọnísókí: Iṣẹ́ ìlera káríayé (IIK) túmọ̀ sí pé gbogbo ènìyàn ní àǹfààní sí gbogbo agbègbè àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera pípéye tí wọ́n nílò, nígbà tàbí níbi tí wọ́n ti nílò wọn, láìsí ìṣòro owó. Ó pèsè fún gbogbo ìtẹ̀síwájú àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì, láti orí ìpolongo ìlera dórí ìdáàbòbò, ìtọ́jú, ìwòsàn àti ìrànwọ́ ìtọ́jú. Láti pèsè àdéhùn yìí, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè nílò láti ní àwọn ètò ìlera alágbára, tí wọ́n kájúẹ̀ àti aláìṣojúsàjú tí wọ́n ta egbò nínú àwọn àwùjọ tí wọ́n ń pèsè fún. Ìtọ́jú ìlera abẹ́lé (IIA) ni ọ̀nà tí ó kójúwọ̀n jùlọ tí kò sì gunpá láti dé ibẹ̀. Orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan ní ọ̀nà tí ó yàtọ̀ láti ṣe àṣeyọrí IIK àti láti pinnu ohun tí wọn yóò bò dá lórí àwọn àwọn àìní àwọn ènìyàn àti àwọn ohun èlò tí ó wà ní àrọ́wọ́tó. Ìdókòwò nínú IIK ń rí i dájú pé àwọn àìsàn wọ̀nyẹn ń jẹ́ ìdámọ̀, ní pàtàkì àti ṣíṣe àmójútó ní ọ̀nà àṣepọ̀; pé ikọ̀ òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti ìtọ́jú tí ó tóbi tí ó sì ní irinṣẹ́ wà; àti pé gbogbo ẹ̀ka àwùjọ ń kópa lórí kíkojú àwọn ìṣòro àyíká àti àwùjọ ọrọ̀ ajé tí wọ́n ń nípa lórí ìlera àti àlàáfíà, tí ó fi mọ́ gbígbáradì fún, ìfèsì sí àti ìwòsàn láti ara àwọn pàjáwìrì. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìpìlẹ̀ fún IIK, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé dábàá ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò ìlera nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò ìlànà ìtọ́jú ìlera abẹ́lé (IIA). Ṣíṣe àṣeyọrí IIK ni ó jẹ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé lógún jùlọ, pẹ̀lú àfojúsùn àwọn ènìyàn bílíọ́nù 1 sí i láti jẹ àǹfààní iṣẹ́ ìlera àgbáyé ní 2025. Ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí pẹ̀lú ètò ìlera tí kò lágbára, a ń fojúsun ìrànlọ́wọ́ ìmọ̀ láti kọ́ àwọn ibùdó orílẹ̀ èdè àti ìpèsè iṣẹ́ láti dí àwọn àlàfo pàtàkì ní àsìkò àwọn pàjáwìrì. Nínú àwọn ètò ìlera tí wọ́n tóbi, a ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ipa ìlera àwùjọ sí ìpèsè ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn nípasẹ̀ ìlànà ìtàkùrọ̀sọ fún àwọn ètò ọjọ́ iwájú àti àtìlẹ́yìn ọlọ́gbọ́n láti mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá iṣẹ́. Ìtọ́nisọ́nà àṣà àti àwọn àdéhùn, ìwífún-àlàyé, ìwádìí àti ìṣẹ̀dá ohun ọ̀tun; àti ìdarí níbi àwọn ìjíròrò, àgbàwí, ìdọ́gba takọtabo, ìlera àìṣojúsàjú àti àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn, ìṣe ọlọ́pọ̀ agbọn, àti ìṣúná ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún iṣẹ́ yìí. Àlàkalẹ̀ 2030 fún àwọn Àfojúsùn Ìdàgbàsókè Kánrinkése iṣẹ́ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ṣiṣẹ́ pọ̀ pẹ̀lú ìpinnu Àfojúsùn Ìdàgbàsókè Kánrinkése (AIK) 3.8, èyí tí ó fojúsun ṣíṣe àṣeyọrí iṣẹ́ ìlera káríayé, tí ó fi mọ́ ewu ààbò, àǹfààní sí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera pàtàkì tí wọ́n péye àti àǹfààní sí àwọn òògùn àti abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tí wọ́n ní ààbò, tí wọ́n múnádóko, tí wọ́n péye tí wọn kò sì gunpá fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Káríayé, àṣeyọrí nípa iṣẹ́ ìlera káríayé jẹ́ ìṣòro. Ìtẹ̀síwájú nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ti há nígbà tí iye àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń kojú ìnáwó ńlá àìlera ń pọ̀ sí i sá. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ń mójútó ìdàgbàsókè bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n ń ṣe àtẹ̀jáde àwọn ìròyìn káríayé láti ṣàfihàn ẹ̀rí tuntun ní ọdún méjì méjì. Ẹ wò àwọn ìròyìn náà níhìn-ín kí ẹ sì ṣe àbẹ̀wò sí ìwé òtítọ́ IIK fún àlàyé sí i.
translations.reference: Universal health coverage Overview: Universal health coverage (UHC) means that all people have access to the full range of quality health services they need, when and where they need them, without financial hardship. It covers the full continuum of essential health services, from health promotion to prevention, treatment, rehabilitation and palliative care. To deliver on this promise, countries need to have strong, efficient and equitable health systems that are rooted in the communities they serve. Primary health care (PHC) is the most effective and cost-efficient way to get there. Every country has a different path to achieving UHC and to decide what to cover based on the needs of their populations and the resources at hand. Investing in PHC ensures that all those needs are identified, prioritized and addressed in an integrated way; that there is a robust and equipped health and care workforce; and that all sectors of society contribute to confronting the environmental and socio-economic factors that affect health and well-being, including preparing for, responding to and recovering from emergencies. WHO response: As a foundation for UHC, WHO recommends reorienting health systems using a primary health care (PHC) approach. Achieving UHC is a WHO strategic priority, with the goal of 1 billion more people benefitting from universal health coverage by 2025. In countries with fragile health systems, we focus on technical assistance to build national institutions and service delivery to fill critical gaps in emergencies. In more robust health system settings, we drive public health impact towards health coverage for all through policy dialogue for the systems of the future and strategic support to improve performance. This work is supported by normative guidance and agreements; data, research and innovation; and leadership in the realms of diplomacy, advocacy, gender equality, health equity and human rights, multisectoral action, and finance. 2030 Agenda for SDGs WHO’s work is aligned with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) target 3.8, which focuses on achieving universal health coverage, including financial risk protection, access to quality essential health-care services and access to safe, effective, quality and affordable essential medicines and vaccines for all. Globally, progress towards universal health coverage is a challenge. Progress in service coverage has stalled while the proportion of the population facing catastrophic out-of-pocket health spending increases continuously. WHO monitors progress and publishes global reports to reveal the latest evidence every two years. See the reports here and visit the UHC fact sheet for more information.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Global Health Strategy: Universal Health Coverage**

Universal health coverage (UHC) means that everyone has access to a comprehensive range of quality health services, when and where they need them, without financial hardship. It encompasses all essential health services, from primary health care to prevention, treatment, rehabilitation, and palliative care. To achieve this, countries need to have strong health systems that are equitable and responsive to the needs of the communities they serve.  The Health Systems Strengthening (HSS) approach is the most effective and sustainable way to get there. Each country has its own way of achieving UHC and determining what priorities to focus on based on the needs of its population and available resources.  HSS recognizes that these illnesses are common, serious, and cause significant disability in a comparable way; that there are competent and motivated health workers and caregivers; and that all sectors of society are involved in reducing the final barriers and social determinants of health that lead to preventable, treatable, and curable diseases.

**World Health Organization’s Strategic Agenda:**

As a cornerstone for UHC, the World Health Organization (WHO) has prioritized the implementation of health systems strengthening (HSS). Achieving UHC is the WHO’s top priority, with the goal of ensuring that 1 billion more people benefit from universal health coverage by 2025. In countries with weak health systems, the focus is on knowledge transfer to identify country-specific challenges and priorities to address the most pressing health needs during emergencies. Within strong health systems, the social determinants of health are addressed to ensure access to quality health services for all through strategic planning for future programs and evidence-based expertise to drive progress. Cultural norms and values, beliefs, superstitions, innovation; and management in situations of conflict, disability, chronic diseases, neglected tropical diseases, and human rights, multiple burdens of disease, and stigma all support this work.

The World Health Organization’s Global Technical Strategy for Malaria 2016-2030 works in conjunction with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) 3.8, which focuses on achieving universal health coverage, including protection from health-related risks, access to essential health services and quality medicines and vaccines, and health coverage for everyone. Globally, achieving universal health coverage is a challenge. Progress in health service delivery is hampered by the increasing number of people needing significant health care. The World Health Organization is promoting innovation and developing global reports to highlight emerging trends every two years. Please refer to these reports and the full UHC document for more information.

translations.source: Ìlera ìlú ńlá Ìsọnísókí: Ìsọ̀gbẹ́dìgboro jẹ́ ọ̀kan nínú àwọn asíwájú ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àgbáyé ní ọ̀rúndún 21 tí ó ní ipa ńlá lórí ìlera. Ó lé ní ìdá 55% àwọn ènìyàn àgbáyé ni wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè ìgboro – iye tí ìrètí wà pé yóò tó 68% ní 2050. Gẹ́gẹ́ bí púpọ̀ ọjọ́ iwájú ìdàgbàsókè ìgboro yóò ṣe wáyé ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ń gòkè àgbà, ilé ayé lónìí ní àǹfààní tí ó yàtọ̀ láti ṣe ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìsọ̀gbẹ́dìgboro àti àwọn ìdàgbàsókè ìgboro abágbàmu mìíràn gbòógì ní ọ̀nà tí yóò dáàbò bo àti ṣe ìgbéga ìlera. Èyí ṣe pàtàkì, kì í ṣe nítorí pé ìlera àti àlàáfíà àwọn ará ìlú jẹ́ ọrọ̀ ikut tí ó ṣe pàtàkì jùlọ. Àmọ́ sá, púpọ̀ nínú àwọn ènìyàn bílíọ́nù 4.2 tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn ìlú ńlá ń kojú ilégbèé àti ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ tí kò tó, àìsí ìmọ́tótó àti àkóso ìdọ̀tí, àti àbùdá afẹ́fẹ́ tí ó kùnà àwọn àlàkalẹ̀ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé. Àwọn ẹ̀yà ìbàyíkájẹ́ mìíràn, bí ariwo, bíba omi àti ilẹ̀ jẹ́, àti èyí tí wọ́n ń pè ní àwọn erékùṣù gbígbóná ìgboro, àti àìsí ààyè fún ìrìn, kẹ̀kẹ́ gígùn àti ìgbé ayé iṣẹ́ túbọ̀ ń parapọ̀ láti mú kí àwọn ìlú ńlá jẹ́ ibùdó ìfọ́nkálé àìsàn tí kì í ràn àti àwọn awakọ̀ àyípadà ojú ọjọ́. Àká Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí Ìlera Ìlú ńlá Ipa: Bí 40% ìdàgbàsókè ìgboro wà nínú àwọn ibi èérí tí wọn kò ní omi àti ìmọ́tótó, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni 91% awomt ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní agbègbè ìgboro ń mí afẹ́fẹ́ burúkú. Nígbà ti ó bá di ọ̀rọ̀ oúnjẹ tí ó dára, ìsọ̀gbẹ́dìgboro máa ń mú ìgbéga bá kí ọ̀nà oko jìn fún jíjẹ, èyí tí ó ń mú ìgbéga bá àwọn oúnjẹ tí kò dára tí wọ́n ti tún ṣe. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ìlú ńlá tún wà nínú ewu àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ nítorí gbígbé ara lé àwọn epo rọ̀bì fún ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀, iná dídá àti ìmúlémóoru. Àwọn ìlú ńlá ni wọ́n ń lo ìdá méjì nínú mẹ́ta àwọn agbára àti yíyọ 60% èéfín àwọn gáàsì, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn torí ilẹ̀ lè ní ìrírí ìmóoru 3-5° C púpọ̀ ju àwọn àyíká agbègbè ìgbèríko nítorí ìgbòòrò kànnkéré àti àwọn ààyè koríko tí kò tó. Gbogbo èyí ni ó fi àwọn olùgbé ìlú ńlá sínú ewu ìlera. Púpọ̀ àwọn okùnfà ikú 10 ni wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìsọ̀gbẹ́dìgboro kankan àti aláìlétò, àti ìhun àti ètò ìgboro tí kò dára. Àwọn ìlú ńlá ń kojú ìlọ́po mẹ́ta àjàgà àwọn àrùn bí KASA/Ìsọdọ̀lẹ Àjẹsára, ikọ́ọfe, òtútù àyà, ibà oriìke àti ìgbẹ́ gbuuru; àwọn àìsàn tí kì í ràn bí àìsàn ọkàn, ẹ̀gbà, àìsàn sẹ́mìísẹ́mìí, jẹjẹrẹ, àtọ̀gbẹ àti ìbànújẹ́ ọkàn; àti ìwà ipá àti ìfarapa, tí ó fi mọ́ ìfarapa ojht pópó. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Pẹ̀lú gbogbo ìdojúkọ wọn, awomt ìlú ńlá lè ṣẹ̀dá àwọn àǹfààní fún ìlera dídára, àyíká mímọ́ àti iṣẹ́ ojú ọjọ́. Àwọn ìlànà ìlú ńlá tí wọ́n lágbára gbọ́dọ̀ pàtàkì ìlera, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ó ṣe ṣe pàtàkì fún ìdàgbàsókè igbe ayé dídára ní ìgbòòrò, ṣíṣe ẹ̀dá àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ tí wọ́n lè ṣiṣẹ́ dáadáa, ṣíṣe ẹ̀dá àwọn àwùjọ alágbára tí wọ́n sì dára, fífi ayé gba ìyanká, ìgbéga ìbánisọ̀rọ̀ ní àwùjọ àti ìdáàbòbò bo àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu. Ìlànà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé fún ìlera ìlú ńlá fojúsun afẹ́fẹ́ tí ó dára, omi àti ìmọ́tótó, àti àwọn atọ́ka àyíká mìíràn; ìṣètò ìlú ńlá tí ó ní ìlera; àwọn àyíká tí wọ́n ní ìlera tí wọn kò sì ní èéfín; ìyanká tí ó ní ààbò àti ìlera; ìdènà ìwà ipá àti àwọn ìfarapa; àgbéga ètò oúnjẹ àti oúnjẹ tí ó ní ètò; àkóso àyíká àwọn àìsàn tí ajọ̀fẹ́ ń fà; àti ìgbáradì ìgboro fún àwọn pàjáwìrì. Ṣíṣe iset káàkiri àwọn agbọn àti rírí i dájú pé àwọn ìlànà báramu káàkiri àwọn agbègbè jẹ́ pàtàkì fún ṣíṣẹ̀dá àtìlẹ́yìn àti àwọn àyíká tí ó dára fún ìlera, èyí tí yóò rí i dájú pé ìpinnu ìlera àti ìdọ́gba jẹ́ ṣíṣe àmúlò káàkiri ìgbésẹ̀ ètò, ìdókòwò àti àwọn ìpinnu ìlànà ní ìpele abẹ́lé. Láti ran àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ lọ́wọ́ láti kojú àwọn kókó pàtàkì òkè yìí, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ń ṣiṣẹ́ láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ibùdó ẹ̀rí láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn abẹ́núgan láti ṣe àwọn ìpinnu tí wọ́n dára nígbà tí wọ́n bá ń kojú ewu ìlera. Ó ń pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ìtọ́ni lórí ohun tí ó ṣiṣẹ́, àti àtìlẹ́yìn àkóso àwọn atọ́ka kókó ajẹmọ́ ìlera. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ń síwájú bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n ń ṣe iṣẹ́ láti ṣe àwọn ìpààrọ̀ ìlú-sí-ìlú kí wọ́n sì ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìhun àti ilé iṣẹ́ àti ìlànà fún ìdarí ìlú ńlá dáadáa fún ìlera àti àlàáfíà àwọn ìlú ńlá.
translations.reference: Urban health Overview: Urbanization is one of the leading global trends of the 21st century that has a significant impact on health. Over 55% of the world’s population live in urban areas – a proportion that is expected to increase to 68% by 2050. As most future urban growth will take place in developing countries, the world today has a unique opportunity to guide urbanization and other major urban development trends in a way that protects and promotes health. This is important, not least because the health and well-being of citizens is perhaps a city’s most important asset. However, most of the 4.2 billion people living in cities suffer inadequate housing and transport, poor sanitation and waste management, and air quality that fails WHO guidelines. Other forms of pollution, such as noise, water and soil contamination, so-called urban heat islands, and a lack of space for walking, cycling and active living further combine to make cities epicentres of a noncommunicable disease epidemic and drivers of climate change. WHO Repository on Urban Health Impact: Around 40% of urban growth is in slums that lack safe water and sanitation, and 91% of people in urban areas breathe polluted air. When it comes to healthy diets, urbanization increases the distance from farm to fork, driving demand for unhealthy, processed foods. Urban dwellers are also highly vulnerable to the effects of climate change because of their dependence on fossil fuels for transport, cooking and heating. Cities account for over two thirds of the world’s energy and emit 60% of greenhouse gases, and those inland may experience temperatures 3–5º C higher than surrounding rural areas because of their large expanses of concrete and limited open green spaces. All of this puts urban dwellers’ health at risk. Most the top 10 causes of death are closely related to rapid and unplanned urbanization, and poor urban design and planning. Cities face the triple health burden of infectious diseases like HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, pneumonia, dengue and diarrhoea; noncommunicable diseases like heart disease, stroke, asthma, cancer, diabetes and depression; and violence and injuries, including road traffic injuries. WHO response: Despite their challenges, cities can create opportunities for better health, cleaner environments and climate action. Strong urban policies must prioritize health, as it is essential for fostering good urban livelihoods, building a productive workforce, creating resilient and vibrant communities, enabling mobility, promoting social interaction and protecting vulnerable populations. WHO’s approach to urban health focuses on better air quality, water and sanitation, and other environmental determinants; healthy urban planning; healthier and smoke-free environments; safe and healthy mobility; preventing violence and injuries; promoting healthy food systems and diets; environmental management of vector-borne diseases; and urban preparedness for emergencies. Working across sectors and ensuring the coherence of policies across different areas is key to creating supportive and enabling environments for health, which ensures that health and equity considerations are integrated throughout the planning process, investments and policy decisions at the local level. To help Member States address the above priorities, WHO works to strengthen the evidence base to help policy-makers make informed decisions when addressing health risks. It provides tools and guidance on what works, and supports monitoring of key health-related indicators. WHO leads and engages in fostering city-to-city exchanges and helps develop institutional and policy frameworks for good urban governance for health and well-being in cities.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Urban Health Crisis: Urbanization as a Key Driver of Global Health Challenges**

Urbanization is one of the leading causes of global health crises in the 21st century, having a significant impact on health. It affects 55% of the world's population living in urban areas – a figure projected to reach 68% by 2050. As urbanization continues to grow, particularly in aging countries, the unique challenges and risks associated with urbanization and its impact on older populations need to be addressed in ways that will prevent disease and promote health. This is critical because the health and well-being of urban populations is a major concern. For example, over 4.2 billion people living in large cities are experiencing unhealthy diets and physical inactivity, a lack of accurate data and control of chronic diseases, and the burden of air pollution, which overwhelms the resources of the World Health Organization. Other factors, such as noise, water scarcity, and land degradation, and a lack of access to sanitation, hygiene, and adequate housing, are combining to make large cities hotspots for emerging infectious diseases and sources of future pandemics.

**The World Health Organization's Urban Health Initiative: Impact**

Approximately 40% of urban population growth occurs in areas lacking access to water and sanitation, and 91% of people living in urban areas experience air pollution. When it comes to adequate nutrition, urbanization can lead to a shift towards processed foods, which in turn promotes unhealthy diets. People living in large cities are also at risk of future pandemics due to the accumulation of fat deposits for transportation, industrial emissions, and deforestation. Large cities consume two-thirds of the world's energy and 60% of its greenhouse gas emissions, and urban areas can experience temperature increases of 3-5°C higher than surrounding rural areas due to the urban heat island effect and a lack of green spaces. All of this puts urban residents at risk of disease. Many of the top 10 causes of death are associated with urbanization and inequality, and poor housing and urban planning. Large cities are home to three-quarters of deaths from diseases like Cardiovascular Diseases/Stroke, cancer, diabetes, respiratory diseases, and injuries; diseases that are not as prevalent as infectious diseases, obesity, mental health disorders, substance abuse, and violence and injuries.

**The World Health Organization's Challenge:**

Despite these challenges, large cities can create opportunities for improved health, a healthier environment, and better quality of life. Urban policies must prioritize health, just as they prioritize sustainable urban development, creating a skilled workforce, building resilient communities, ensuring equity, promoting social cohesion, and protecting vulnerable populations. The World Health Organization's urban health initiative focuses on clean air, water, and sanitation, and other key environmental factors; urban planning with a health focus; health-promoting environments; equitable access to health services; violence prevention and control; improved food systems and nutrition; control of non-communicable diseases; and urban governance for health. Implementing comprehensive approaches across sectors and ensuring that local policies are aligned with national strategies are essential for creating and sustaining healthy and resilient urban populations. This will ensure that health and safety decisions are based on evidence-based data across the entire urban ecosystem, policy development, and policy decisions at the national level.

To support member states in addressing these critical issues, the World Health Organization is working to strengthen health data systems to help policymakers make informed decisions when addressing health risks. It is also providing guidance and evidence on what works, and supporting the monitoring of key health indicators. The World Health Organization is actively working to establish city-to-city partnerships to support the development of effective urban governance systems for health and well-being in large cities.

translations.source: Ìwà ipá sí àwọn Ọmọdé Ìsọnísókí: Lára àwọn ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé ni gbogbo ẹ̀yà ìwà ipá sí àwọn ènìyàn lábẹ́ ọdún 18. Fún àwọn ìkókó àti ọmọdé, ìwà ipá ní í ṣe fífi ìyà jẹ ọmọdé (ìyẹn ìyà ara, ìdàpọ̀ àti ìmọ̀lára àti àìbìkítà) ní ọwọ́ àwọn òbí àti àwọn aláṣẹ mìíràn. Àwọn ọmọkùnrin àti ọmọbìnrin wà nínú ewu adọ́gba ìdíyàjẹ ojúkojú àti ìmọ̀lára àti àìbìkítà, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọmọbìnrin wà nínú ewu ìdíyàjẹ nípa ìbálòpọ̀. Bí àwọn ọmọdé ṣe ń di àgùnbánirọ̀, ìwà ipá àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àti ìwà ipá alábàárìn, ní àfikún sí ìdíyàjẹ ọmọdé, pọ̀ sí i. Ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé lè ṣe é dènà. Ìdènà àti ìfèsì sí ìwà ipá tako àwọn ọmọdé pè fún kí àwọn akitiyan jẹ́ gbígbé kalẹ̀ láti kojú àwọn ewu àti àwọn okùnfà ààbò ní gbogbo àwọn ìpele ewu mẹ́rin tí wọ́n farakọ́ra (ènìyàn, ìbáṣepọ̀, ìlú, àwùjọ). Oṣù Karùn-ún 2016 ọ̀nà àbáyọ Àjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé fọwọ́ sí iṣẹ́ ètò káríayé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé àkọ́kọ́ irúu rẹ̀ lórí ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ojúṣe ètò ìlera láàrin ìfèsì ọlọ́pọ̀ ẹ̀ka orílẹ̀ èdè láti kojú ìwà ipá ibáraẹniṣepọ̀, pàápàá jùlọ sí àwọn obìnrin àti ọmọbìnrin, àti sí àwọn ọmọdé. A Gẹ́gẹ́ bí ètò yìí, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ àti olùbádòwòpọ̀ mìíràn, farajìn sí: Ṣíṣe àmójútó títóbi àti àwọn àbùdá ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé káríayé àti ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn akitiyan orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣe àkọsílẹ̀ àti òdiwọ̀n irúfẹ́ ìwà ipá bẹ́ẹ̀. Ṣíṣe àkóso àkóso àlàyé ètò orí ẹ̀rọ tí ó ṣe ìsọnísókí àwọn ìwífun-àlàyé lórí àjàgà náà, àwọn okùnfà ewu àti àbájáde ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé, àti ẹ̀rí fún ìdènà rẹ̀. Ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àti àpínká àwọn ìwé ìmọ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà adálórí-ẹ̀rí, àṣà àti òṣùwọ̀n fún ìdènà àti ìfèsì sí ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé. Ṣíṣe àtẹ̀jáde ìròyìn ipò káríayé lórí akitiyan orílẹ̀ èdè láti kojú ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìlànà orílẹ̀ èdè àti ètò iṣẹ́, òfin, àwọn ètò ìdènà àti àwọn iṣẹ́ ìfèsì. Ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè àti àwọn olùbádòwòpọ̀ nínú ṣíṣe àmúlò ìdènà adálórí-ẹ̀rí àti àwọn ọgbọ́n ìfèsì, bí àwọn tí wọ́n wà nínú INSPIRE: Ọgbọ́n méje fún fífi òpin sí ìwà ipá tako àwọn ọmọdé. Ṣíṣe àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn àjọ àti ilé-iṣẹ́ àgbáyé láti ṣe àdínkù àti ìfòpinsí ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé káàkiri àgbáyé, nípasẹ̀ àwọn ètò bí Àjọṣepọ̀ Káríayé lati fi òpin sí Ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé. àjọṣepọ̀ Káríayé lati fi òpin sí Ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé. Wíwà papọ̀ fún àwọn Ọmọbìnrin àti Ìdènà Ìwà ipá parapọ̀. Ìlòkulò ọmọdé Ìlòkulò ọmọdé ni ìlòkulò àti àìbìkítà tí ó ṣẹlẹ̀ sí ọmọdé lábẹ́ ọdún 18. Ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú gbogbo àwọn oríṣìí ìwà ojúkojú àti/tàbí ìlòkulò ìmọ̀lára, ìlòkulò ajẹmọ́-ìbálòpọ̀, àìbìkítà, ìdẹ́yẹsí àti okòwò tàbí ìyànjẹ mìíràn, èyí tí ó yọrí sí ìpalára tàbí ìpalára ọjọ́ iwájú fún ìlera, ìyè, ìdàgbàsókè tàbí iyì ọmọ náà nínú ọ̀gangan ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ojúṣe ìbáraẹniṣpọ̀, ìfọkàntán tàbí agbára. Ìfaragbá ìwà ipá ọ̀rẹ́ alábàárìn tún ṣe é kì ó wà lára ẹ̀yà ìlòkulò ọmọdé. Ìlòkulò ọmọdé ní àwọn àtúbọ̀tán ńlá yàjóyàjó àti ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Ju ikú, ìfarapa àti ààbọ̀ ara lọ, ìwà ipá lè yọrí sí wàhálà tí yóò nípa lórí ìdàgbàsókè ọpọlọ àti ìbàjẹ́ àwọn ètò ojúṣe ara àti àjẹsára. Èyí náà tún ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìdádúró ìdàgbàsókè ìlóye, ìṣe ilé ẹ̀kọ́ tí kò dára àti ààbọ̀ ẹ̀kọ́, àwọn ìṣòro ìlera ìròrí, ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀míaraẹni, àlékún àwọn ìwà tí wọ́n léwu fún ìlera, àṣetúnṣe ìbàjẹ́ ọkàn àti ṣíṣe ìwà ipá. Iroyin ayọ̀ ibẹ̀ ni pé lílo ọmọdé ní ìlòkulò lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìdásí tí wọ́n ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn òbí àti olùtọ́jú, ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn èrè àti ààbò ọrọ̀ ajé àwọn ẹbí, pèsè èsì dídára àti àwọn iṣẹ́ àtìlẹ́yìn, ṣẹ̀dá àti ṣàkóso àwọn àyíká aláàbò fún awomwt ọmọdé, atit ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àti àmúlò àwọn òfin tako ìlòkulò ọmọdé. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé, ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn olùbádòwòpọ̀:
translations.reference: Violence against children Overview: Violence against children includes all forms of violence against people under 18 years old. For infants and younger children, violence mainly involves child maltreatment (i.e. physical, sexual and emotional abuse and neglect) at the hands of parents and other authority figures. Boys and girls are at equal risk of physical and emotional abuse and neglect, and girls are at greater risk of sexual abuse. As children reach adolescence, peer violence and intimate partner violence, in addition to child maltreatment, become highly prevalent. Violence against children can be prevented. Preventing and responding to violence against children requires that efforts systematically address risk and protective factors at all four interrelated levels of risk (individual, relationship, community, society). May 2016 World Health Assembly resolution endorsed the first ever WHO Global plan of action on strengthening the role of the health system within a national multisectoral response to address interpersonal violence, in particular against women and girls, and against children. A According to this plan, WHO in collaboration with Member States and other partners, is committed to: Monitoring the global magnitude and characteristics of violence against children and supporting country efforts to document and measure such violence. Maintaining an electronic information system that summarizes the scientific data on the burden, risk factors and consequences of violence against children, and the evidence for its preventability. Developing and disseminating evidence-based technical guidance documents, norms and standards for preventing and responding to violence against children. Regularly publishing global status reports on country efforts to address violence against children through national policies and action plans, laws, prevention programmes and response services. Supporting countries and partners in implementing evidence-based prevention and response strategies, such as those included in INSPIRE: Seven strategies for ending violence against children. Collaborating with international agencies and organizations to reduce and eliminate violence against children globally, through initiatives such as the Global Partnership to End Violence against Children, the Global Partnership to End Violence against Children, Together for Girls and the Violence Prevention Alliance. Child maltreatment Child maltreatment is the abuse and neglect that occurs to children under 18 years of age. It includes all types of physical and/or emotional ill-treatment, sexual abuse, neglect, negligence and commercial or other exploitation, which results in actual or potential harm to the child’s health, survival, development or dignity in the context of a relationship of responsibility, trust or power. Exposure to intimate partner violence is also sometimes included as a form of child maltreatment. Child maltreatment has enormous immediate and long-term repercussions. Beyond death, physical injury and disability, violence can lead to stress that impairs brain development and damages the nervous and immune systems. This in turn is associated with delayed cognitive development, poor school performance and dropout, mental health problems, suicide attempts, increased health-risk behaviours, revictimization and the perpetration of violence. The good news is that child maltreatment can be prevented through interventions that support parents and caregivers, promote non-violent norms and values, provide education and life skills training, strengthen families’ income and economic security, offer high quality response and support services, create and sustain safe environments for children, and implement and enforce laws against child maltreatment. WHO, in collaboration with a number of partners:

**Violence Against Children: A Comprehensive Approach**

Violence against children encompasses all forms of violence against individuals under the age of 18. For infants and children, violence involves inflicting harm (i.e., physical abuse, emotional abuse, neglect, and exploitation) by parents and other caregivers. Both boys and girls are at risk of severe consequences from physical abuse, emotional abuse, neglect, and exploitation, and girls are particularly at risk of violence related to forced marriage. As children grow older, peer violence and intimate partner violence, in addition to child abuse, increase. Violence against children can be devastating. Prevention and response to violence are crucial to ensure that interventions are implemented effectively to address risks and harms across all four levels of risk (individual, family, community, society).

In June 2016, the World Health Organization endorsed the World Bank’s first global program on preventing violence against children, particularly targeting women and girls, and children. The World Health Organization, in partnership with member states and other stakeholders, is committed to:

*   Implementing evidence-based parenting and violence prevention programs for children worldwide and supporting national interventions to identify and address harmful norms related to violence against children.
*   Developing and implementing a digital platform to monitor data on violence against children, risks, and prevention interventions, and providing resources for prevention.
*   Developing and disseminating evidence-based guidelines, policies, and practices for preventing and responding to violence against children.
*   Strengthening global surveillance on violence against children through national data systems and programs, policies, prevention programs, and response services.
*   Supporting countries and partners in implementing evidence-based prevention and response programs, such as INSPIRE: A Seven-Point Framework to End Violence Against Children.
*   Partnering with global organizations and institutions to increase and scale up violence prevention for children worldwide, through initiatives such as the Global Partnership to End Violence Against Children.
*   Joint advocacy for girls and violence prevention.

**Child Abuse**

Child abuse is the harm or neglect experienced by a child under the age of 18. It includes all forms of emotional and/or physical abuse, neglect, sexual abuse, exploitation, and other forms of violence, which can lead to harm or future health problems, death, disability, or the child's involvement in the cycle of violence, trauma, or exploitation. Intimate partner violence also falls under the category of child abuse. Child abuse has significant short-term and long-term consequences.

Beyond death, injury, and self-harm, violence can lead to difficulties in development and damage to social and support systems. This can also result in poor school performance, reduced educational attainment, increased health problems, mental health issues, risky behaviors, and the need for psychological treatment and violence prevention programs.

Good news is that child abuse can be prevented through interventions that support parents and caregivers, provide financial assistance to families, especially vulnerable families, create and manage safe environments for children, and implement policies to protect children.

The World Health Organization, in partnership with numerous partners:

translations.source: pèsè ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìmọ̀ àti àṣà fún ìdènà ìlòkulò ọmọdé adálórí-ẹ̀rí; ṣíṣe agbẹnusọ fún ìlọsíwájú àtìlẹ́yìn káríayé fún ati ìdókòwò nínú ìdènà ìlòkulò ọmọdé adálórí-ẹ̀rí.; pípèsè àtìlẹ́yìn ìmọ̀ fún àwọn ètò ìdènà ìlòkulò ọmọdé adálórí-ẹ̀rí ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ Ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ káríayé. Ó ní àwọn onírúurú ìṣe láti orí ìpániláyà àti ìjà ojúkojú, dórí àwọn ìkọlù ìbálòpọ̀ àti ojúkojú dórí ìpànìyàn. Ìwà ipá àwọn ọ̀dọ́ ń mú kí iyeó ìlera, ìtọ́jú àti ìdájọ́ ọ̀daràn pọ̀ sí i; ń ṣe àdínkù iṣẹ́ ṣíṣe; bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ń ba ètò àwùjọ jẹ́. Yàtọ̀ sí àwọn ikú, ìfarapa àti ìpalára ọkàn, ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ lè yọrí sí kí àwọn ìwà tó léwu fún ìlera pọ̀ sí i bí fífa nǹkan, ìlòkulò òògùn olóró, ìbálòpọ̀ aláìláàbò, àti ìwà ipá síwájú sí i. Ìwọ̀nyí bákan náà ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn àìsàn òpónà ìmí líle, jẹjẹrẹ, àwọn àìsàn ọkàn, àtọ̀gbẹ, oyún oge àti awont àìsàn ìbálòpọ̀. Lára àwọn ìdènà tí ó tó àti àwọn ọgbọ́n èsì ni àwọn tí wọ́n ń ṣe àgbéga jíjẹ́ òbí àti ìdàgbàsókè ọmọdé láti kékeré.; ìdènà ìpániláyà ajẹmọ́-ilé-ẹ̀kọ́, akadá, àti ìgbésí ayé àti ìdàgbàsókè àwọn ètò ìmọ̀-ọ́nṣe àwùjọ; àwọn ìlànà ìwòsàn pẹ̀lú àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu púpọ̀, àti àwọn ìlànà adálórí-ìpele ìlú àti àwùjọ bí mímú àdínkù bá níní àǹfààní sí àti àsìlò ọtí líle àti ìbọn, àwọn àgbègbè ewu àti ṣíṣe àwọn ìlànà adálórí-ìṣòro, ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n ìgboro, àti àdínkù òsì. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé àti àwọn olùbádòwòpọ̀ ṣe àdínkù ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ nípasẹ̀ àwọn ètò tí wọ́n ń ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti ṣe ìdámọ̀, àpèjúwe àti fọ èsì sí ìṣòro náà, lára wọn ni: ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò fún àwọn ètò ìdènà ìwà ipá adálórí-ilé-ẹ̀kọ́; pípe àkíyèsí sí iye ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ àti ìdí tí wọ́n fi nílò ìdènà; ṣíṣẹ̀dá ẹ̀rí lórí ààlà àti àwọn oríṣiríṣi ìwà ipá ní àwọn onírúurú ibùdó; ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ àti àwọn ẹ̀ka tí ọ̀rọ̀ kan láti dènà ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ àti ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ńlá fún àwọn èsì sí i; ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn akitiyan orílẹ̀ èdè láti dènà ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́; àti ṣíṣe àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn àjọ àti ilé-iṣẹ́ àgbáyé láti dènà ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ káríayé.
translations.reference: provides technical and normative guidance for evidence-based child maltreatment prevention; advocates for increased international support for and investment in evidence-based child maltreatment prevention; provides technical support for evidence-based child maltreatment prevention programmes in several low- and middle-income countries. Youth violence Youth violence is a global public health problem. It includes a range of acts from bullying and physical fighting, to more severe sexual and physical assault to homicide. Youth violence dramatically increases health, welfare and criminal justice costs; reduces productivity; and generally undermines the fabric of society. Beyond deaths, injuries and psychological harm, youth violence can lead to increased health risk behaviours such as smoking, substance abuse, unsafe sex, and further violence. These in turn are associated with chronic respiratory diseases, cancer, cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, early pregnancy and sexually transmitted diseases. Effective prevention and response strategies include those that promote parenting and early childhood development; school-based bullying prevention, academic, and life and social skills development programmes; therapeutic approaches with high-risk individuals, and community- and society-level approaches like reducing access to and misuse of alcohol and firearms, hotspots and problem-oriented policing, urban upgrading, and poverty de-concentration. WHO and partners decrease youth violence through initiatives that help to identify, quantify and respond to the problem, these include: developing a package for schools-based violence prevention programmes; drawing attention to the magnitude of youth violence and the need for prevention; building evidence on the scope and types of violence in different settings; developing guidance for Member States and all relevant sectors to prevent youth violence and strengthen responses to it; supporting national efforts to prevent youth violence; and collaborating with international agencies and organizations to prevent youth violence globally.

**Guidance on the Science and Practice of Child Behavioral Disorder Management; Strengthening Support for Global Child Behavioral Disorder Management.**

**Guidance and Support for Child Behavioral Disorder Management Programs in Vulnerable and Marginalized Communities.**

Child behavior disorder is a major global public health concern. It manifests in various forms, ranging from disruptive and aggressive behaviors, through to bullying and violence. Child behavior disorders lead to increased rates of illness, injury, and developmental delays; they negatively impact productivity and burden social systems. Unlike death, disability, and mental illness, child behavior disorders can lead to the exacerbation of harmful behaviors such as substance abuse, drug use, reckless behavior, and aggressive tendencies. These are often accompanied by physical injuries, depression, mental health problems, learning difficulties, and behavioral disorders. Among the most significant and pressing interventions are those that focus on early childhood intervention and development; school-based prevention programs, life skills education, and the development of social science programs; healthcare strategies for people in high-risk situations, and community and social strategies such as increased awareness and prevention of violence, dangerous areas, and the implementation of prevention strategies, community empowerment, and increased resources.

The World Health Organization and its partners are strengthening child behavior disorder interventions through programs designed to identify, describe, and address the problem. These include: establishing programs to support child behavior disorder management in schools; paying attention to the prevalence of child behavior disorders and the need for intervention; creating resources on various behaviors and their manifestations in different contexts; establishing guidance for member states and specific groups to address child behavior disorders and providing significant support for interventions; implementing national strategies to address child behavior disorders; and collaborating with global organizations and businesses to address child behavior disorders worldwide.

translations.source: Ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin Ìsọnísókí: Ìwà ipá tako àwọn obìnrin – pàápàá jùlọ ìwà ipá ọ̀rẹ́ tímọ́tímọ́ àti ìwà ipá ìbálòpọ̀ – jẹ́ ìṣòro àwùjọ àti ìlera ìṣègùn gbòógì àti rírúfin àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn. Ó gbilẹ̀ nínú bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ń ṣàmúlò àìdọ́gba takọtabo. Káríayé 1 nínú àwọn obìnrin 3 ni wọ́n ní ìrírí ìwà ipá ara àti ìwà ipá ìbálòpọ̀ ní ayé wọn, lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà láti ọwọ́ alábàáṣepọ̀ tímọ́tímọ́. Èyí jẹ́ ìrántí gidi fún ìwọ̀n àìdọ́gba takọtabo àti ìyàsọ́tọ̀ sí àwọn obìnrin. Ìsọ̀kan àwọn Orílẹ̀ èdè Àgbáyé ki ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin gẹ́gẹ́ bí "èyíkéyìí ìwà ipá adálórí-ìjákọjábo tí ó yọrí sí, tàbí ṣe é ṣe kí ó yọrí sí, ìpalára ara, ìbálòpọ̀, tàbí ìròrí tàbí ìjìyà fún àwọn obìnrin, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ìpániláyà ìṣe bẹ́ẹ̀, ipá tàbí ìdènà òmìnira láìnídìí, yálà ó ṣẹlẹ̀ ní ìgbé ayé àwùjọ tàbí ìkọ̀kọ̀. Ìwà ipá alábàárìn tímọ́tímọ́ ni ìwà láti ọwọ́ alábàárìn tímọ́tímọ́ tàbí alábàárìn ìgbà kan tí ó ń fa ìpalára ara, ìbálòpọ̀ tàbí ìròrí, tí ó fi mọ́ ìbínú, ìwà ipá ìbálòpọ̀, ìlòkulò ajẹmọ́-ìròrí àti àwọn ìwà ìpàsẹwàá. Ìwà ipá ìbálòpọ̀ ni "èyíkéyìí ìṣe ìbálòpọ̀, ìgbìyànjú láti ṣe ní ìbálòpọ̀, tàbí ìṣe mìíràn tí ó lòdì sí ipò ìjákọ̀jábo ènìyàn nípa lílo ipá, láti ọwọ́ ẹnikẹ́ni bí ó ti wù kí àjọṣepọ̀ wọn rí sí afaragbá, ní ibùdó èyíkéyìí. Lára rẹ̀ ni ìfipábánilọpọ̀, tí wọ́n kì bí ipá ara tàbí nígbà mìíràn fífi ipa wọ inú òbò tàbí fùrọ̀ pẹ̀lú okó, ẹ̀yà ara tàbí nǹkan mìíràn. Ipa: Ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin jẹ́ ìṣòro káríayé bí àwọn ìwọ̀n àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn. Ó máa ń fa ìparun, ìpalára sí ayé àwọn obìnrin àti ti àwọn ọmọdé. Ó tún ń fa ìpalára fún ọrọ̀ ajé àti ìlera àwùjọ àwọn ẹbí, àwùjọ àti orílẹ̀ èdè wọn. Nígbà tí àwọn obìnrin kan wà nínú ewu ńlá ju àwọn mìíràn lọ, ìwà ipá lè ṣẹlẹ̀ sí obìnrin èyíkéyìí, ní èyíkéyìí orílẹ̀ èdè – láì fi àṣà, ẹ̀sìn tàbí ipò ọrọ̀ ajé ṣe. Lára àìdọ́gba ìjákọ̀jábo ni àwọn àṣà takọtabo tí wọ́n léwu jẹ́ adarí pàtàkì fún ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin. Ìwà ipá lè nípa burúkú lórí ìlera ara, ìròrí, ìbálòpọ̀, àti ìbísí àwọn obìnrin. Ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìlọsíwájú ewu àwọn ìfarapa, ìbànújẹ́ ọkàn, ìrújú ìpayà, oyún àìnídìí, àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀, KASA àti ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ìṣòro ìlera mìíràn. Àwọn ọmọdé máa ń dàgbà nínú àwọn ẹbí níbi tí ìwà ipá wọ́n sì lé ní ìrírí onírúurú àwọn ìdíwọ́ ìwà àti ìmọ̀lára. Àwọn wọ̀nyí lè yọrí sí ìṣòro orí àti ìdàgbàsókè bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n tún ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú wíwọ́lé kanlẹ̀ tàbí níní ìrírí ìwà ipá ní ìgbésí ayé ọjọ́ iwájú. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé sapá ńlá nínú pípé àkíyèsí sí àti fífèsì sí ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìlera àwùjọ àti ọ̀rọ̀ ìdọ́gba ìṣakọṣabo nípasẹ̀: Ìwádìí àti ìṣẹ̀dá ẹ̀rí láti sọ iye ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin, àwọn ewu àti àbájáde rẹ̀, àti láti ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn ìdásí pípéye fún ìdènà àti ìfèsì. Ṣíṣẹ̀dá àwọn ìlànà àti irinṣẹ́, àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn àṣà àti òṣùwọ̀n fún fífèsì sí ìlera pípéye sí ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin. Ṣíṣe ìrónilágbára fún ipa àwọn ètò orílẹ̀ èdè àti olùpèsè àti fífèsì sí ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin. Ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún adarí nínú ètò ìlera àti ṣíṣẹ̀dá ìfẹ́ òṣèlú láti kojú ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin nípasẹ̀ ṣíṣe agbẹnusọ àti àwọn ìbádòwòpọ̀. Lára àwọn Ètò Gbogbogbò fún Iṣẹ́ (EGbI) Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 13 ni ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin gẹ́gẹ́ bí ara ipa wọn fún àwọn Àfojúsùn Ìdàgbàsókè Kánrinkése (AIK) 5 lórí ìdọ́gba takọtabo àti àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn obìnrin (pàápàá jùlọ, AIK 5.2 lórí fífi òpin sí ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin àti ọmọbìnrin). Ní Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé ní Oṣù Karùn-ún 2016, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ fọwọ́ sí ètò káríayé lórí ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ojúṣe àwọn ètò ìlera nínú wíwá ojútùú sí ìwà ipá ara ẹni, pàápàá jùlọ sí àwọn obìnrin àti ọmọbìnrin àti sí àwọn ọmọdé.
translations.reference: Violence against women Overview: Violence against women – particularly intimate partner violence and sexual violence – is a major public and clinical health problem and a violation of women's human rights. It is rooted in and perpetuates gender inequalities.Globally 1 in 3 women experience physical and/or sexual violence in their lifetime, mostly by an intimate partner. This is a stark reminder of the scale of gender inequality and discrimination against women. The United Nations defines violence against women as "any act of gender-based violence that results in, or is likely to result in, physical, sexual, or mental harm or suffering to women, including threats of such acts, coercion or arbitrary deprivation of liberty, whether occurring in public or in private life. Intimate partner violence refers to behaviour by an intimate partner or ex-partner that causes physical, sexual or psychological harm, including physical aggression, sexual coercion, psychological abuse and controlling behaviours. Sexual violence is "any sexual act, attempt to obtain a sexual act, or other act directed against a person’s sexuality using coercion, by any person regardless of their relationship to the victim, in any setting. It includes rape, defined as the physically forced or otherwise coerced penetration of the vulva or anus with a penis, other body part or object." Impact: Violence against women is a global problem of pandemic proportions. It causes devastating, harm to women’s lives and that of their children. It also hurts the economic and social health of their families, communities, and countries. While some women are more at risk than others, violence can happen to any woman, in any country – regardless of culture, religion or economic status. Gender inequality including harmful gender norms are key drivers of violence against women. Violence can negatively affect women’s physical, mental, sexual, and reproductive health. It is associated with increased risk of injuries, depression, anxiety disorders, unplanned pregnancies, sexually transmitted infections, HIV and many other health problems. Children who grow up in families where there is violence may suffer a range of behavioural and emotional disturbances. These can lead to mental and development problems and also be associated with perpetrating or experiencing violence later in life. WHO response: WHO plays a key role in bringing attention to and responding to violenceagainst women as a public health and gender equality issue through: Research and evidence-building to highlight the magnitude of violence against women, its risk factors and consequences, and to identify effective interventions for prevention and response. Developing guidelines and tools, setting norms and standards for an effective health response to violence against women. Strengthening country capacity of health systems and providers to prevent and respond to violence against women. Encouraging leadership in health systems and building political will to address violence against women through advocacy and partnerships. The WHO 13th General Programme of Work (GPW) includes violence against women as part of its contribution to Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 5 on gender equality and women’s empowerment (specifically, SDG 5.2 on the elimination of violence against women and girls). At the World Health Assembly in May 2016, Member States endorsed a global plan of action on strengthening the role of the health systems in addressing interpersonal violence, in particular against women and girls and against children.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Violence Against Women: A Global Health Issue**

Violence against women – particularly intimate partner violence and sexual violence – is a major public health problem and a violation of human rights. It is a leading cause of injury and disability worldwide. One in three women globally experiences physical or sexual violence, most often from an intimate partner. This is a significant cause of injury and death for women. The United Nations recognizes violence against women as "any act of violence against women, including physical, sexual, psychological, or economic violence, occurring in public or private life."  Intimate partner violence is violence perpetrated by an intimate partner or former partner, causing harm, violence, or fear, including emotional abuse, sexual violence, coercion, and controlling behavior. Sexual violence is "any sexual act performed without free, specific, and informed consent," including rape, sexual assault, and harassment.

Violence: Violence against women is a global health problem like infectious diseases. It causes suffering, violence against women and children. It also causes violence related to the financial and health problems of families, communities, and countries. When women are in greater danger than others, violence can occur against those women, within a specific country – regardless of customs, laws, or financial status.  Harmful traditional practices are a major risk factor for violence against women. Violence can be related to poor health, sexual violence, intimate partner violence, and female genital mutilation. It is associated with the spread of HIV, depression, substance abuse, unwanted pregnancies, sexually transmitted infections, KASA (likely a local term), and many other health problems. Children grow up in families where violence occurs, and they are at risk of various behavioral and learning problems. These can lead to mental health issues and a high risk of violence in their future lives.

**World Health Organization:** The World Health Organization is strongly committed to increasing attention to and addressing violence against women as a major public health problem and a priority health issue through:

*   Collecting and analyzing data to determine the prevalence of violence against women, its consequences, and to identify effective interventions for prevention and response.
*   Developing policies and guidelines, strengthening laws and social norms to address violence against women.
*   Strengthening national and local programs and addressing violence against women.
*   Advocating for leadership in health programs and creating a supportive environment to address violence against women through awareness campaigns and partnerships.

Violence against women is one of the six Global Priority Areas for Action within the World Health Organization’s Framework for Action on Health (Framework 13) for the Comprehensive Implementation of the Global Action Plan for Prevention and Control of Non-Communicable Diseases (specifically, Framework 13.2 on addressing violence against women and girls). In the 2016 Global Health Agenda in June 2016, member states committed to action and support for increasing the scope of health programs in reducing the burden of violence, particularly against women and girls and children.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Iná igbó aláìlákòóso Ìsọnísókí: Iná igbó aláìlákòóso ń pọ̀ sí i káàkiri àgbáyé ní iye, líle àti àsìkò, tí ó ń mú kí ìdí láti lóye àwọn ipa ìlera ìfaragbá iná igbó aláìlákòóso pọ̀ sí i. Ewu iná igbó aláìlákòóso pọ̀ ní àwọn àsìkò gbígbẹ, bí ọ̀dà, àwọn ìgbì ooru ari lásìkò afẹ́fẹ́ ńlá. Èéfín iná igbó aláìlákòóso jẹ́ àpòpọ̀ àwọn èròjà abafẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ tí wọ́n léwu, bí PM2. 5, NO2, òsóònù, àwọn èédú-áídírò aromátíìkì, tàbí òjé. Ní àfikún sí bíbá afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn abafẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ burúkú, iná igbó aláìlákòóso tunt ń yára nípa lórí ojú ọjọ́ nípa ṣíṣe ìtújáde ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn èédú àti afẹ́fẹ́ oníbejì àti àwọn afẹ́fẹ́ adènà ooru mìíràn sínú afẹ́fẹ́. Pẹ̀lú àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ tí ó ń yọrí sí àwọn ìgbóná ojú ọjọ́ àti àwọn ọ̀gbẹlẹ̀ pẹ̀lú ìsọ̀gbẹ́dìgboro àwọn ìgbèríko tí ó ń tẹ̀síwájú, àsìkò iná náà ń tètè bẹ̀rẹ̀, ó sì ń pẹ́ parí. Àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ iná igbó aláìlákòóso ń le sí i ní agbègbè sarè ilẹ̀ tí ó ń jó, àsìkò àti agbára, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè ṣe ìdíwọ́ ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀, ìbáraẹnisọ̀rọ̀, ìpèsè omi, àti àwọn iṣẹ́ iná mọ̀nàmọ́ná àti gáàsì. Ipa: Iná igbó aláìlákòóso tí ó jó sún mọ́ àwọn àgbègbè tí ènìyàn pọ̀ sí lè nípa ńlá lórí àyíká, dúkìá, ẹran ọ̀sìn àti ikú ẹ̀dá ènìyàn àti ikú èyí dá lórí ìtóbi, ìyára àti agbègbè sí iná náà, àti bóyá àwọn ènìyàn náà gba ìkìlọ̀ siwaju láti kúrò. Èéfín iná igbó aláìlákòóso jẹ́ àpòpọ̀ àwọn èròjà abafẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ èyí tí ìdọ̀tí afẹ́fẹ́ (IA) jẹ́ olórí ewu ìlera àwùjọ. PM2.5 láti ara èéfín iná igbó aláìlákòóso ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ikú àìtọ́jọ́ nínú iye ènìyàn gbogbogbò, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè fa àti kí ó fẹjú àwọn àìsàn ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró, ọkàn, ètò ọpọlọ/ara, awọ ara, òpónà oúnjẹ, kíndìnrín, ojú, inú àti ẹ̀dọ̀. Wọ́n tunt tí fìdí rẹ̀ múlẹ̀ pé ó ń yọrí sí idenat ìlóye àti àdánù ìrántí. Àwọn panápaná àti òṣìṣẹ́ afèsìsí pàjáwìrì tún máa ń ní àwọn ìfarapa, àpá iná àti fífa èèfún sínú, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn ìwọ̀n líle. Ìwádìí ajẹmọ́ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ẹ̀ka ìwádìí ni ó dájú pé yóò lóye àwọn ipa ìfaragbá iná igbó aláìlákòóso afarasin àti ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ lórí ìlera lórí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu (àwọn ọmọdé, àgbàlagbà, àwọn aláboyún, àwọn tí wọ́n ń ṣe àìsàn ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́), pàápàá jùlọ fún àwọn agbègbè tí wọn ń faradà ìfaragbá àṣetúnṣe àti olóbìrípo sí awokt ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ iná igbó aláìlákòóso wọ̀nyí. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Títóbi ipá ìnáwó ara àti ẹ̀dá ènìyàn láti àwọn iná igbó aláìlákòóso lè ṣe é dínkù bí ìdènà, ìgbáradì, ìfèsì sí pàjáwìrì tí ó tó àti àwọn òṣùwọ̀n fún imúpàdàbọ̀sípò bá jẹ́ ṣíṣe àmúlò ní ọ̀nà kánrinkése àti lójú ọjọ́. Ṣíṣe iṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti ṣẹ̀dá àwọn ètò ìlera alágbára tí ó sì ní ìdarí tí ó lè fojúsun àwọn àìní àti ìṣòro lásìkò àwọn pàjáwìrì, kí ó lè ṣe é ṣe fún wa láti ṣe àdínkù àwọn ewu àti ìfèsì pípé lásìkò tí ó nílò.
