This year, one of the awardees, Ms. Sprina Robi Chacha, a female farmer from Kenya, was recognized for her transition from tobacco cultivation to high-protein crops, but also for mentoring nearly a hundred farmers on how to achieve this.

182 parties to the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control have committed to "...support alternative livelihoods for workers and those involved in tobacco cultivation." One way countries can achieve this is by redirecting tobacco subsidies and providing support for nutritious crops. With the shift to growing food instead of tobacco, we can improve nutrition, reduce poverty, and increase food security.

translations.source: Ìjábọ̀ tó lápẹẹrẹ ya ojú ọ̀nà fún ṣíṣe àtúntò àwọn ọrọ̀ ajé láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn Nínú ìjábọ̀ àkọ́kọ́-irú-rẹ̀, Ìgbìmọ WHO lórí Àwọn ọrọ̀-ajéÌlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn ti ṣe àlàkalẹ̀ ọ̀nà tuntun láti ṣe àtúntò àwọn ọrọ̀-ajéláti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn nǹkan tó ṣe kókó - ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Ìgbìmọ̀ náà, tí Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní oṣù Bẹlu ọdún 2020 ní ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, tí Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Mariana Mazzucato jẹ́ alaga rẹ̀, ti lo ọdún méjì sẹ́yìn láti gbèrò ọrọ̀-ajénáà láti ojú ìwòye ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn, tí ó sì ń fi gbogbo agbára ti ìpìlẹ̀-òye pé ìlera ènìyàn àti àgbáyé gbọ́dọ̀ wà ní àárín gbùngbùn bí a bá ṣe ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò àwùjọ, ìlera àti ọrọ̀-ajéwa àti àwọn òfin. Ìgbìmọ̀ náà ti ṣe ìtìsíwájú àlàyé tuntun tó fojú hàn tó fẹsẹ̀múlẹ̀ nínú ọgbọ́n ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun láti ṣe àtúntò àwọn ọrọ̀-ajé láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn kárí àwọn kókó ọ̀rọ̀ mẹ́rin tó jọra: Iye - mímọ iye àti wíwọn àwọn nǹkan tó ṣe pàtàkì nípasẹ̀ àwọn mẹ́tíríkì ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun; Ìpèsè owó - bí a ṣe lè pèsè owó fún ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìdókówò ọlọ́jọ́-pípẹ́, tí kìí ṣe iye owó onígbà-díẹ̀; ìmọ̀-ọ̀tun- bí a ṣe lè mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunìlera fún dáadáa gbogbo ènìyàn; Ìkápá - bí a ṣe ró oríṣiríṣi ìkápá àwọn ẹ̀ka ìjọba lágbára láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. "Ní ọdún méjì sẹ́yìn, mo ní kí ikọ̀ àwọn onímọ̀ nípa ọrọ̀-ajé tó ń léwájú lágbàyé àti àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìlera àwùjọ - gbogbo wọn jẹ́ obìnrin - láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àyípadà iṣẹ́. Báyìí, dípò kí wọ́n rí ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ẹrú fún ìdàgbàsókè ọrọ̀-ajé, a ní ìlànà-iṣẹ́ fún àtúntò àwọn igbéṣẹ̀ ọrọ̀-ajé ní ọ̀nà tí yóò mú wa débi èròngbà rírí gbogbo ènìyàn pẹ̀lú ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì tó yára pẹ̀lú àwọn àbájáde tó dára," Dr Tedros ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Láti bí ọdún méjì sẹ́yìn, Ìgbìmọ̀ WHO lórí Àwọn Ọrọ̀-ajé Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn ti ṣiṣẹ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àlàyé ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun - èyí tí ó lè mú àyípadà bá ìpèsè owó fún ìlera láti ìnáwó sí ìdókówò," Alága Ìgbìmọ̀ náà, Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Mariana Mazzucato ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "A ti ṣe àyẹ̀wò àwọn àyípadà tó nílò - tó fi mọ́ sí àgbékalẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀, àwọn àjọṣepọ̀ ìjọba-aládàni, àti àwọn ètò ìṣúná - láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọrọ̀-ajé tí yóò pèsè ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Nínú ìjábọ̀ ìgbẹ̀yin wa, a pè fún òfin ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun tí kìí ṣe nípa ìṣàkóso ọjà ṣùgbọ́n nípa ṣíṣe àto àwọn ọjà lọ́nà tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ àti ní àjọṣepọ̀ tí ó mú ìlera àwọn ènìyàn àti àgbáyé ní ọ̀kùkúdùn." Wọ́n ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní oní ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin, ìjábọ̀ náà, tí àkòrí rẹ̀ jẹ́ Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn: Mímú Àyípadà Bá Àwọn Ọrọ̀-ajé láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn nǹkan tó ṣe pàtàkì (https://www.who.int/groups/who-council-on-the-economics-of-health-for-all), pèsè ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tuntun tí wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ lórí àwọn òpó mẹ́rin tó wà lókè, pẹ̀lú àwọn àbá kan pàtó lábẹ́ ìkọ̀ọ̀kan - tó mú látara iṣẹ́ àtẹ̀yìnwá Ìgbìmọ̀ náà. Lára àwọn àbá pàtàkì ni: A nílò láti mọ iyì àti òdiwọn àwọn nǹkan tó ṣe pàtàkì tòótọ́ - gbígbèrú àwọn ènìyàn àti àgbáyé - yàtọ̀ sí lílé ìdagbàsókè ọrọ̀-ajé àti jíjẹ èrè GDP lọ́pọ̀ láì ní ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn àbájáde rẹ̀. Láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn, àwọn ìjọba gbọ́dọ̀ gbèrò iye kí wọ́n sì ṣe àtúntò àti ìdarí ọrọ̀-ajé tó dá lórí ìgbáyégbádùn àwùjọ àti àgbáyé, tí mẹ́tíríkì tuntun ń darí. Àtúnṣe ìpìlẹ̀ fún àwọn ètò orílẹ̀-èdè àti ilẹ̀ òkèèrè fún ìpèsè owó fún ìlera ni a nílò, kí a lè ṣe ìnáwó lórí ìlera gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìdókówò ọlọ́jọ́-pípẹ́. Ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn máa nílò ọ̀pọ̀ owó, àti ojúlówó ìpèsè owó tó pọ̀. ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunnílò òye àjọni - Kìí ṣe èso ilé-iṣẹ́ kan ṣoṣo tàbí ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba. Ṣùgbọ́n àyàfi tí wọ́n bá darí ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunfún dáadáa gbogbo ènìyàn, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn ni ó máa yọ kúrò nínú àwọn ànfààní rẹ̀. Àwùjọ ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunìlera ìbẹ̀rẹ̀-dé-òpin tuntun tí ó mú dáadáa gbogbo ènìyàn ní ọ̀kùkúdùn ni a nílò. Gẹ́gẹ́ bí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 ṣe ti fi hàn, ojúlówó àti ìkápá ìjọba ṣe pàtàkì. Àwọn ìjọba tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ kọ́ ni wọ́n kéré jùlọ, ṣùgbọ́n àwọn tí wọ́n ní àgbékalẹ̀ tó dára tí wọ́n sì ṣe ìpèsè fún dáadáa, pẹ̀lú ìpèsè owó àti èyí tó níṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn ènìyàn wọn àti ohun amáyédẹrùn. Ṣíṣe ìdókówò nínú ìkápá ìjọba ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn. Ìjábọ̀ náà tún pèsè àmọ̀ràn lórí ohun tí ó lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe nínú ìfikọ́ra láti ṣàmúlò àwọn àyípadà tí wọ́n nílò láti ṣe àtúntò àwọn ìlànà fún iye ọrọ̀-ajé, ìpèsè owó fún ìlera, ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunàti ṣíṣe ìdagbàsókè ìkápá ẹ̀ka àwùjọ nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn.
translations.reference: Landmark report charts route for reorienting economies to deliver health for all In the first-ever report of its kind, the WHO Council on the Economics of Health for All has outlined a bold new path to reorient economies to deliver what matters - health for all. The Council, created by WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus in November 2020 in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, and chaired by Professor Mariana Mazzucato, has spent that last two years rethinking the economy from a health for all perspective, and pushing forcefully the principle that human and planetary health must be at the heart of how we design our social, health and economic systems and policies. The Council has put forward a bold new narrative grounded in new economic wisdom to reorient economies to deliver health for all across four interrelated themes: Value - valuing and measuring what matters through new economic metrics; Finance - how to finance health for all as a long-term investment, not a short-term cost; Innovation - how to advance health innovation for the common good; Capacity - how to strengthen dynamic public sector capacity to achieve health for all. “Two years ago, I asked a team of the world’s leading economists and public health experts – all women – to create a paradigm shift. Now, instead of health for all being seen as the servant of economic growth, we have a roadmap for structuring economic activity in a way that will allow us to reach the goal of seeing all people with access to essential health services faster with better results,” said Dr Tedros. “Over the past two years, the WHO Council on the Economics of Health for All has worked to craft a new economic narrative – one that transforms financing for health from an expenditure to an investment,” said the Council’s Chair, Professor Mariana Mazzucato. “We have examined the changes needed – including to the structure of patents, public-private partnerships, and budgets – to design an economy that delivers Health for All. In our final report, we call for new economic policy that is not about market fixing but about proactively and collaboratively shaping markets that prioritize human and planetary health.” Launched today in conjunction with the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly, the report, entitled Health for All: Transforming economies to deliver what matters (https://www.who.int/groups/who-council-on-the-economics-of-health-for-all), provides a new framework built on the above four pillars, with specific recommendations under each – drawing from the Council’s previous work. Key recommendations include: We need to value and measure the things that truly matter - human and planetary flourishing - rather than pursuing economic growth and GDP maximization regardless of the consequences. To achieve health for all, governments must rethink value and reshape and redirect the economy based on social and planetary well-being, guided by new metrics. A fundamental overhaul of national and international systems for financing health is needed, so that spending on health is treated as a long-term investment. Delivering Health for All will require both more money, and higher quality financing.Innovation requires collective intelligence—it is never the fruit of just one company or government agency. But unless innovation is governed for the common good, many people remain excluded from its benefits. A new end-to-end health innovation ecosystem that prioritises the common good is needed. As the COVID-19 pandemic has made clear, the quality and capacity of government matters. Effective governments are not the smallest, but those that are well-designed and properly resourced, both financially and in terms of their people and infrastructure. Re-investing in government capacity is crucial to delivering Health for All. The report also provides suggestions on what can be done in practice to implement the changes needed to reorient measures of economic value, the financing of health, innovation and building public sector capacity in the service of health for all.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Report on a Roadmap for Economic Reforms to Achieve Health for All**

Within this report, the WHO Council on the Economics of Health for All has established a new approach to economic reforms to achieve health for all. The Council, established by WHO Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus in May 2020 in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, with economist Mariana Mazzucato as chair, has spent two years exploring the economic narrative from the perspective of health for all. It emphasizes that the health of people and the world must be at the center of social, health, and economic policies and regulations.

The Council has advanced a new analysis that emerged within the new economic thinking to guide economic reforms to achieve health for all, focusing on four interconnected pillars:

1.  **Value:** Meaningful value and assessment of essential goods through new economic metrics.
2.  **Investment:** How to invest in health for all sustainably, not as a short-term expenditure but as a long-term investment.
3.  **Innovation:** How to drive health innovation for the benefit of all people.
4.  **Governance:** How to leverage the power of different government sectors to achieve health for all.

"Over the past two years, I have seen the leading experts on the global economy and social determinants of health – all of whom are women – come together to drive change. Instead of viewing health for all as a burden on economic progress, we have a framework for reforming economic structures in a way that will bring us closer to the realization of everyone's potential with a focus on essential health priorities and positive outcomes," said Dr. Tedros.

"For the past two years, the WHO Council on the Economics of Health for All has been working to develop a new economic narrative – one that can drive investment in health from cradle to grave," said Council Chair, economist Mariana Mazzucato. "We have examined the transformations needed – focusing on building foundations, public-private partnerships, and financing mechanisms – to create an economy that delivers health for all. In our final report, we call for new economic policies that are not based on market control but on actively shaping markets and in partnerships that prioritize the health of people and the planet."

Released in partnership with the Global Health Initiative, the report, titled *Health for All: Charting a Course for Economic Transformation to Achieve Essential Goods* (https://www.who.int/groups/who-council-on-the-economics-of-health-for-all), outlines a new approach built on the four pillars, with specific actions under each – driven by the Council’s intensive work. Key actions include:

We need to know the true value and cost of essential goods – exceeding the human and planetary costs of economic growth and GDP growth without considering their consequences. To achieve health for all, governments must prioritize and manage economic resources based on social and planetary well-being, guided by new metrics.

Reform national and regional funding structures for health investment to ensure sustainable financing from cradle to grave.

Achieving Health for All will require significant investment and a substantial shift in investment priorities.

We need a knowledge ecosystem – not just a business or government sector. But unless they drive innovation for the benefit of all people, many of the benefits will be lost. A new, integrated health innovation knowledge ecosystem that benefits all people is needed.

As the COVID-19 pandemic has shown, government action and governance are essential. Governments that act quickly and invest in solutions, engaging with their citizens and leveraging data, have been more successful. Strengthening governance within government is essential to achieve Health for All.

The report also highlights the potential for collaboration to mobilize the transformations needed to shape approaches to value, investment in health, innovation, and strengthening governance within the health sector for all.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Láàrín àwọn wọ̀nyìí, ìjábọ̀ náà mẹ́nu ba oríṣiríṣi àpẹẹrẹ, tó fi mọ́: Ilé-ìwòsàn ibùdó ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ mRNA ní South Africa: ètò adálórí-iye tí ó ń gbìyànjú láti gba ẹ̀tọ́ ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun, ìpèsè owó àti ìkápá lórí-àfojúsùn; Ìdókòwò àwùjọ orílẹ̀-ède Brazil nínú ilé ìtàjà ilé-iṣẹ́-ìlera tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún dáadáa gbogbo eniyan; Àwọn ilé-ìfowópamọ́ onídágbàsókè ní ẹkùn agbègbè gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn aṣokùnfa àyípadà ní Gúúsù Àgbáyé; Ìṣọ̀kan Ìgbáyégbádùn Ọrọ̀-ajé - ìṣọ̀kan oríṣiríṣi àwọn ìjọba àti àwọn àjọ tó lé ní 600 mìíràn tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá àwọn ètò ọrọ̀-ajé nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún ẹ̀mí; Àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ fún ìpèsè owó fún ètò amúṣẹ́ṣe (NAPs) lórí ẹ̀yàwuuru jáàgùn nípasẹ̀ ètò ìṣúná àjọṣe ti oríṣiríṣi ẹ̀ka, pẹ̀lú pé ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn NAP ni wọn ò rí ìpèsè owó. Àwọn àbá tí wọ́n fi sínú ìjábọ̀ náà lè mú àyípadà bá ọ̀nà tí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ń gbà wò tí wọ́n sì ń pèsè owó fún ìlera. WHO ń pè àwọn aṣòfin, àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ, àti àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ ìlera àti ọrọ̀-ajé láti gbèrò kíkún nípa àwọn àbá kí wọ́n sì lò wọ́n gẹ́gẹ́ bí atọ́ka-ọ̀nà láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn òfin ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun àti àwọn àgbékalẹ̀ tó lè mú wa bọ́ sí ojú ọ̀na láti sọ ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn di tòótọ́.
translations.reference: Among these, the report mentions several examples, including: The mRNA technology hub facility in South Africa: a values-driven system that tries to get the innovation, financing and capacity right ex-ante; Brazil’s public investment in a health-industrial complex that serves the common good; Regional development banks as enablers of change in the Global South; The Wellbeing Economy Alliance – an alliance of several governments and over 600 other organizations working together to transform economic systems in the service of life; Approaches to financing national action plans (NAPs) on antimicrobial resistance through multi sector joint budgeting, given that most NAPs remain unfunded. The recommendations included in the report could change the way countries view and finance health. WHO calls on policy-makers, civil society, and members of the health and economics communities to give full consideration to the recommendations and use them as a compass to develop new economic policies and structures that can move us along the road to making health for all a reality.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Among others, the report highlights several examples, including: A rural knowledge of mRNA technology center in South Africa: an initiative aimed at acquiring knowledge rights, funding, and addressing sustainability; The social impact bond of the Brazilian state within the healthcare sector operating for the benefit of all citizens; Elderly care homes in various regions as catalysts for development, like the Global Ageing Fund; The Solidarity for Economic Recovery – a collaboration of various governments and organizations numbering 600, working together to drive development within the job creation pillar for livelihoods; Steps for funding commitments for national action plans (NAPs) on antimicrobial resistance through the market-based innovation mechanism of various sectors, with many NAPs not having received funding commitments. The findings within the report can drive development in the way countries access health services and commit funding for health. WHO is calling on parliamentarians, civil society groups, and members of health and economic organizations to raise awareness about the findings and to use them effectively as a roadmap to develop new financial mechanisms and initiatives that can lead us closer to achieving universal health coverage."

translations.source: Wọ́n ti yan àwọn gbajúgbajà òṣèré Renée Fleming àti Pretty Yende gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Aṣojú Onínúure fún Iṣẹ́-ọnà àti Ìlera Inú WHO dùn láti kéde ìyànsípò àwọn ìlú-mọ̀-ọ́-ká òṣèré nì Renée Fleming àti Pretty Yende gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn Aṣojú Onínúure fún Iṣẹ́-ọnà àti Ìlera. Ìkéde àmì-ẹ̀yẹ yìí ni wọ́n ṣe gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀kan lára ìrántí ayẹyẹ Àjọ̀dún 75th WHO lásíkò Àpèjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin ní ọjọ́ 22 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023. Renée Fleming, ọkàn nínú àwọn olórin àsìkò wa tó gba àmì-ẹ̀yẹ jùlọ, àti Pretty Yende, olóhùn tínrín aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ tí a mọ̀ fún orin rẹ̀ tó ń dáni lọrùn , ti wọ́n ti yàn fún àṣeyọrí iṣẹ́-ọnà wọn àti ìgbẹnusọ fún àyípadà àbájáde ìlera nípasẹ̀ àwọn ọ̀nà àtinúdá. Ní ọjọ́ 8 oṣù Igbe ọdún 2023, Fleming àti Yende ṣeré fún eré-orin aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ kan tí wọ́n pe àkòrí rẹ̀ ní Healing Arts, tí wọ́n fi sọrí àjọ̀dún 75th WHO ní Geneva, Switzerland. Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, sọ wí pé, "Iyì ńlá ni ó jẹ́ fún WHO pé Renée Fleming àti Pretty Yende dárapọ̀ mọ́ wa gẹ́gẹ́ bíí Aṣojú Onínúure fún Iṣẹ́-ọnà àti Ìlera. Ẹ̀bùn aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ wọn, ìfọkànsìn wọn sí iṣẹ́-ọnà náà, àti ìfọkànsìn wọn láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera àti ìgbáyégbádùn ló sọ wọ́n di aṣojú tó yẹ fún ìrìnàjò pàtàkì yìí. Ìdásí wọn máa ṣe ìwúrí fún àwọn ènìyàn, àwọn àwùjọ àti àwọn aṣòfin láti mọ rírì àti láti ṣàmúlò agbára àyípadà tí Iṣẹ́-ọnà ní nínú ìlépa ìlera tó dára fún gbogbo ènìyàn. *Fleming máa mú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe bọ̀ wá sí ojúṣe rẹ̀ tuntun. Ó fa ojú àwọn olùgbọ́ rẹ̀ jákèjádò àgbáyé mọ́ra pẹ̀lú òhun tínrín rẹ̀ tó rẹwà àti Iṣẹ́-ọnà rẹ̀ tó fànimọ́ra. Yàtọ̀ sí ẹ̀bùn ohùn rẹ̀ tó lápẹrẹ, Fleming ti jẹ́ agbẹnusọ tọkàn-tọkàn fún ìwádìí ní ìdábùú àwọn Iṣẹ́-ọnà àti ìlera. Àwọn àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ jákèjádò àgbáyé pẹ̀lú àwọn onímọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera, ipò asíwájú rẹ̀ nínú àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ tó jẹ́ ojúlówó, àti àjọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú WHO ṣàpèjúwe ìfọkànsìn rẹ̀ sí ṣíṣe àmúlò agbára àyípadà tí Iṣẹ́-ọnà ní fún àwùjọ tó dára. "Inú mi dùn pé àjọ elétò ìlera àgbáyé tó olókìkí nì ń mọ rírì ipa alágbára tí iṣẹ́-ọnà ní nínú ìlera, iyì ni ó sì jẹ́ fún mi pé wọ́n fi ìgbàgbọ́ sínú mi láti jẹ́ iṣẹ́ yìí," Fleming ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Ní ojoojúmọ́, ìtẹ̀síwájú nínú ìwádìí sáyẹnsì-ọpọlọ àti ajẹmọ́-ìtọ́jú ń ṣàfihàn ọ̀pọ̀ nǹkan nípa àwọn ànfààní ìtọ́jú ajẹmọ́ iṣẹ́-ọnà, àwọn ìrírí, àti ètò-ẹ̀kọ́, pẹ̀lú àwọn ìpadàbọ̀ rẹ̀ fún ìgbáyégbádùn ní gbogbo ìṣẹ̀mí ènìyàn. Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, a tún ṣẹ̀ṣẹ̀ bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí mọ agbára ìyanu iṣẹ́ yìí ni, bẹ́ẹ̀ ni mo sì ń fojú sọ́nà láti fẹ ìpolongo àti ànfààní náà lójú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Aṣojú Onínúure WHO. "Pretty Yende ti gba ọkàn àwọn olùgbọ́ rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú ohùn alárinrin rẹ̀ àti àwọn eré rẹ̀ tó yanilẹ́nu. Gẹ́gẹ́ bí olóhun tínrín tí ó ní òye ìjìnlẹ̀ nípa agbára orin, ó ti ṣàpèjúwe ìfọkànsìn rẹ̀ sí lílo ẹ̀bun rẹ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá àwùjọ. Ìfẹ́ Yende fún sísọ àwọn abala ìwòsàn orin àti ìfọkànsìn rẹ̀ sí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àwùjọ dọ́gba gidi gan-an pẹ̀lú ètò Iṣẹ́-ọnà àti Ìlera WHO. Yende sọ wí pé, "Inú mi dùn pé wọ́n yàn mí gẹ́gẹ́ bí Asojú Onínúure WHO fún Iṣẹ́-ọnà àti Ìlera. Mo nírètí láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún ìdásílẹ̀ ìpìlẹ̀-òye kan fún ìtẹ̀síwájú ìwádìí nínú àwọn iṣẹ́ ọnà láti jẹ́ ohun èlò ìpìlẹ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá ìrísí, ọpọlọ, àti ìgbáyégbádùn àwùjọ wa. Mo dúpẹ́ púpọ̀ lọ́wọ́ WHO fún fífi ààyè sílẹ̀ fún ìwádìí tuntun àti ìpèsè ìkànnì àgbáyé wọn láti kéde ìhìnrere náà àti láti ṣe ìwádìí lórí àjọṣepọ̀ iṣẹ́-ọnà àti ìlera. "Mò ń fojú sọ́nà fún gbogbo aṣeéṣe láti ṣe iṣẹ́ pàtàkì ní ìlú mi orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa àti ní àgbáyé fún ọjọ́ ọ̀la tó dára tó sì ní àlàáfíà," Yende fi kún-un. Ìyànsípò Fleming àti Yende tọ́ka sí àjọṣepọ̀ tó fẹsẹ̀múlẹ̀ láàrín iṣẹ́-ọnà àti ìlera. Kíkópa nínú àwọn iṣẹ́ àtinúdá, bíi orin, iṣẹ́-ọnà, àti ijó, máa ní ipa rere lára ìrísí, ìlera ọpọlọ, ìgbáyégbádùn àwùjọ, àti àpapọ ojúlówó fún ìṣẹ̀mí. Nípasẹ̀ àwọn ojúṣe wọn gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Aṣojú Onínúure, Fleming àti Yende máa ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àfibọ̀ iṣẹ́-ọnà sínú ètò ìlera, ṣe agbẹnusọ fún ànfààní sí àwọn ìtọ́jú iṣẹ́-ọnà àtinúdá, kí wọ́n tún léwájú pàtàkì àwọn ìṣesí iṣẹ́-ọnà láti mú àyípadà bá àwọn àbájáde ìlera káàkiri àgbáyé.
translations.reference: Renowned artists Renée Fleming and Pretty Yende appointed as WHO Goodwill Ambassadors for Arts and Health WHO is delighted to announce the appointment of internationally acclaimed artists Renée Fleming and Pretty Yende as Goodwill Ambassadors for Arts and Health. This prestigious announcement was made as part of WHO's 75th Anniversary commemoration during the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly on 22 May 2023. Renée Fleming, one of the most celebrated singers of our time, and Pretty Yende, an extraordinary soprano known for her dazzling performances, have been selected for their artistic achievement and advocacy for improved health outcomes through creative means. On 8 April 2023, Fleming and Yende performed for a special concert titled Healing Arts, dedicated to WHO's 75th anniversary in Geneva, Switzerland. Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General, said, "WHO is honoured to have Renée Fleming and Pretty Yende join as Goodwill Ambassadors for Arts and Health. Their unparalleled talent, dedication to the arts, and commitment to advancing health and well-being make them ideal ambassadors for this important cause. Their involvement will inspire individuals, communities and policy-makers to recognize and harness the transformative power of the arts in the pursuit of better health for all. "Fleming brings a wealth of expertise to her new role. She has captivated audiences worldwide with her luminous soprano voice and compelling artistry. Beyond her remarkable vocal talent, Fleming has been a fervent advocate for research at the intersection of the arts and health. Her presentations worldwide with scientists and practitioners, her leading role in major initiatives, and her association with WHO demonstrate her commitment to harnessing the transformative power of the arts to better society. "I am elated that the preeminent global health agency is recognizing the powerful impact of the arts in health, and I am deeply honoured to be entrusted to share this message," said Fleming. “Every day, advances in neuroscience and clinical research reveal more about the benefits of arts therapies, experiences, and education, with implications for well-being throughout the human life span. However, we have only just begun to realize the amazing potential of this work, and I look forward to expanding awareness and access as a WHO Goodwill Ambassador. "Pretty Yende has mesmerized audiences with her exquisite voice and stunning performances. As a soprano with a deep understanding of the power of music, she has demonstrated her commitment to using her talent to improve society. Yende's passion for sharing the healing aspects of music and her dedication to social causes align perfectly with WHO’s Arts and Health agenda. Yende said, "I am delighted to be appointed a WHO Goodwill Ambassador for Arts and Health. I hope to help establish a foundation for continued investigation into the arts being a fundamental ingredient in improving our physical, mental, and social well-being. I am grateful to WHO for paving the way for new research and providing their global platform to spread the word and generate research on the connection of arts and health. "I look forward to all the possibilities to do essential work in my native South Africa and globally for a better and healthier tomorrow," added Yende. The appointments of Fleming and Yende underscore the profound link between arts and health. Engagement in creative activities, such as music, art, and dance, positively impacts physical, mental health, social well-being, and overall quality of life. Through their roles as Goodwill Ambassadors, Fleming and Yende will promote the integration of arts into health-care systems, advocate for access to creative arts therapies, and champion the importance of artistic expression in improving health outcomes globally.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Renée Fleming and Pretty Yende Appointed WHO Goodwill Ambassadors for Arts and Health**

Renowned opera singers Renée Fleming and Pretty Yende have been appointed Goodwill Ambassadors for Arts and Health by the World Health Organization (WHO). This appointment is part of the organization's 75th-anniversary celebrations during the World Health Assembly held on April 22, 2023. Renée Fleming, one of the most decorated singers of our time, and Pretty Yende, a celebrated coloratura soprano known for her captivating voice, were selected for their outstanding work in the arts and their commitment to improving health outcomes through creative approaches.

On August 8, 2023, Fleming and Yende performed in a special concert titled "Healing Arts" to commemorate the WHO's 75th anniversary in Geneva, Switzerland. Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General, stated, "It is a great honor for WHO to welcome Renée Fleming and Pretty Yende as Goodwill Ambassadors for Arts and Health. Their exceptional talent, their dedication to the arts, and their commitment to advancing health and well-being make them ideal ambassadors for this critical mission. Their work will inspire people, communities, and policymakers to recognize and harness the transformative power of the arts within a healthy and equitable world for all."

Fleming will bring extensive knowledge to the forefront. She has captivated audiences worldwide with her voice and her artistic endeavors. In addition to her prestigious awards, Fleming has been a steadfast advocate for the integration of the arts into health and wellness initiatives. Her global network of scientists and healthcare professionals, her leadership role in pioneering projects, and her partnership with WHO demonstrate her commitment to maximizing the potential of the arts for a healthier society.

"I am delighted that the global health community, which understands the powerful role of the arts in health, has entrusted me with this important work," Fleming said. "Every day, advances in scientific and technological innovation reveal more about the benefits of creative engagement, including resilience, well-being, and education, with positive impacts on all aspects of human life. However, we are only beginning to understand the immense power of this field, and I am eager to champion and realize its potential as a WHO Goodwill Ambassador."

Pretty Yende has captivated audiences with her radiant voice and captivating performances. As a soprano with a deep understanding of the power of music, she has demonstrated her commitment to using her talent to improve society. Yende’s passion for promoting the arts and her dedication to addressing social issues through the WHO’s Arts and Health program are commendable.

Yende stated, "I am thrilled to be appointed a WHO Goodwill Ambassador for Arts and Health. I am committed to building a foundation for advancing the integration of the arts into health initiatives to serve as a catalyst for improving resilience, well-being, and the health of our communities. I am grateful to WHO for prioritizing innovation and their global commitment to addressing challenges and advancing arts and health collaborations."

"I look forward to working with all stakeholders to make a significant difference in my home country of South Africa and around the world for a healthier and peaceful future," Yende concluded.

Fleming and Yende’s appointments highlight the growing partnership between the arts and health. Creative fields, such as music, arts, and dance, can have a positive impact on resilience, health, community well-being, and overall social development. Through their roles as Goodwill Ambassadors, Fleming and Yende will advocate for the integration of the arts into health programs, promote the benefits of creative arts interventions, and advance solutions to address health challenges worldwide.

translations.source: WHO ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ojú-òpóàgbáyé láti mọ̀ àti láti dènà àwọn ewu ààrùn àkóràn WHO àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ń ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ojú-òpóàgbáyé láti pèsè ààbò fún àwọn ènìyàn kúrò níbi àwọn ewu ààrùn àkóràn nípasẹ agbára apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn. Ojú-òpó ìtọpinpin Afàìsàn Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè (IPSN) máa pèsè ìkànnì láti so àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè pọ̀, mímú àyípadà bá àwọn ètò fún gbígbà àti ṣíṣe ìyànnàná ohun àyẹ̀wò péréte, lílo àwọn dátà yìí láti ṣètò àwọn ìpinnu ìlera àwujọ ṣíṣe, àti àti pínpín àwọn ìròyìn yẹn káàkiri lọ́pọ̀. Apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn máa ń yànnàná Kóódù jínnì àwọn kòkòrò ààrùn, batéríà àti àwọn abẹ̀mí afàìsàn mìíràn láti ní ìmọ̀ nípa bí wọ́n ṣe lè ràn sí, bí wọ́n ṣe lè ṣe okùnfà ikú sí, àti bí wọ́n ṣe ń ràn. Pẹ̀lú ìròyìn yìí, àwọn onímọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera láwùjọ lè ṣe ìdámò àti ìtọpa ààrùn láti dènà àti láti dáhùn sí ìbẹ́sílẹ̀ ààrùn gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀kan lára ètò ìtọpinpin ààrùn tó gbòòrò, àti láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìtọ́jú àti àjẹsára. IPSN náà, pẹ̀lú Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tí Ibùdó WHO gba àlejò rẹ̀ fún Ọgbọ́n Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé, ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ jákèjádò àgbáyé ní ibi ìyànnàná dátà apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran tó ń léwájú, láti ọ̀dọ̀ àwọn ìjọba, àwọn ẹlẹ́yinjú àánú, àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ lóriṣiríṣi, àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ, àwọn ilé-ẹ̀kọ́ àti ẹ̀ka aládàni. Gbogbo wọn ni wọ́n ní èròngbà kan náà: láti ṣàwárí àti láti dáhùn sí àwọn ewu ààrùn ṣíwájú kí wọ́n tó di àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé, àti láti ṣàmúlò ìlànà-iṣẹ́ ìtọpinpin ààrùn. Èròngbà ojú-òpó tuntun yìí ní àfojúsùn, ṣùgbọ́n ó kàn lè kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ààbò ìlera: láti fún gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní ànfààní sí tẹ̀lé-ǹ-tẹ̀lé apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn àti ìyànnàná gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀kan lára ètò ìlera àwùjọ rẹ̀," Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Gẹ́gẹ́ bí wọ́n ti ṣe ṣàpèjúwe rẹ̀ kedere fún wa ní àsìkò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, gbogbo àgbáyé ní agbára nígbà tí a bá jọ dúró papọ̀ láti gbógun ti àwọn ewu ìlera àjọni." COVID-19 ṣàfihàn ipa pàtàkì tí apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn ń kó nínú ìdáhùn sí àwọn ewu àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn. Láì sí tẹ̀lé-ǹ-tẹ̀lé ojú ẹsẹ̀ fún apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran SARS-COV-2, àjẹsára kì bá tí ṣiṣẹ́ bó ṣe yẹ, tàbí bí ó wà nílẹ̀ kíákíá báyẹn. Tuntun, ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn àdàmọ̀dì àkóràn kòkòrò ààrùn náà kì bá tí di mímọ̀ kíákíá. Apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran wà ní àárín gbùngbùn ìgbaradì àti ìdáhùn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé tó ń ṣiṣẹ́, bákan náà ni gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀kan lára ìtọpinpin àwọn oríṣiríṣi ààrùn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́, látara àwọn ààrùn inú oúnjẹ àti ọ̀fìnkìn sí ikọ́-ife àti KASA. Lílò rẹ̀ nínú àmójútó ìtànkálẹ̀ jáàgùn KASA, fún àpẹẹrẹ, ti yọrí sí àwọn àkókò oògùn apa KASA tí ó ti dóólà àìmọye ẹ̀mí. "Àjọṣepọ̀ Àgbáyé nínú ìtọpinpin apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn ti ṣe pàtàkì gan-an pẹ̀lú bí gbogbo ayé ṣe ń gbógun ti COVID-19 lápapọ̀," Dr Rajiv J. Shan, Ààrẹ fún The Rockfeller Foundation ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "IPSN gùn lórí ìrírí yìí pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkànnì tó lágbára fún àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ káàkiri àwọn ẹ̀ka àti ẹnu-bodè láti sọ ọ̀rọ̀ ọlọgbọ́n, àwọn irinṣẹ́, àti àwọn ìṣesí láti rí i dájú pé ìdènà àti ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn di ìmọ̀-ọ̀tun tó sì pọ̀ ní ọjọ́ iwájú." Pẹ̀lú ìfẹ̀lójú àìpẹ́ yìí nínú ìkápá apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè nítorí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, ọ̀pọ̀ ni ó sì ń ṣàfẹ́kù àwọn ètò tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ yìí fún gbígbà àti ìyànnàná ohun àyẹ̀wò péréte tàbí lílo àwọn dátà wọ̀nyẹn láti ṣe àwọn ìpinnu ìlera àwujọ. Kò sí pínpín dátà, àwọn ìṣesí, àti àwọn ìmọ̀-ọ̀tun tí ó tó láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwòṣe ìtọpinpin ìlera àgbáyé tó lágbára. Àwọn ètò ìṣúná tí ó lọ sókè lásìkò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà, tó fi ààyè gba ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìkápá kíákíá, ni wọ́n ti ń rẹ́bùú báyìí, kódà ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n lọ́rọ̀ jùlọ. "Orílẹ̀-èdè Argentina ń náwó gidi gan-an nínú ṣíṣe àfikún sí ìkápá orílẹ̀-èdè tiwa nínú apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn àti ìkápá àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè mìíràn," Josefina Campos, Olùdarí Ààyè fún Apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran àti Òye dátà Ìbí Orílẹ̀-èdè ní ANLIS Malbrán, orílẹ̀-èdè Argentina. "Àwọn ààrùn kìí bọ̀wọ̀ fún ẹnu-bodè: ewu àìsàn ní orílẹ̀-èdè kan tún jẹ́ ewu fún òmíràn. A ń fojú sọ́nà sí àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ IPSN láti ṣe àṣeyọrí èrò àjọni fún dídènà ààrùn àti dídóólà àwọn ẹ̀mí." IPSN máa kojú àwọn ìpèníjà wọ̀nyìí nípasẹ̀ ojú-òpó àgbáyé, síso àwọn ojú-òpó agbègbè àti ààrùn-pàtọ́ papọ̀, láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò àjọṣepọ̀ láti túnbọ̀ ṣàwárí, dènà àti dáhùn sí àwọn ewu ààrùn dáadáa. Àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ máa ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ ní àwọn ìsọ̀rí àkànṣe láti gbájúmọ́ àwọn ìpèníjà pàtó, tí wọn yóò ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún pẹ̀lú ìpèsè owó nípasẹ̀ IPSN láti fẹ àwọn èrò àti àwọn àkànṣe iṣẹ́ nínú apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn lójú.
translations.reference: WHO launches global network to detect and prevent infectious disease threats WHO and partners are launching a global network to help protect people from infectious disease threats through the power of pathogen genomics. The International Pathogen Surveillance Network (IPSN) will provide a platform to connect countries and regions, improving systems for collecting and analyzing samples, using these data to drive public health decision-making, and sharing that information more broadly. Pathogen genomics analyzes the genetic code of viruses, bacteria and other disease-causing organisms to understand how infectious they are, how deadly they are, and how they spread. With this information, scientists and public health officials can identify and track diseases to prevent and respond to outbreaks as part of a broader disease surveillance system, and to develop treatments and vaccines. The IPSN, with a Secretariat hosted by the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence, brings together experts worldwide at the cutting-edge of genomics and data analytics, from governments, philanthropic foundations, multilateral organizations, civil society, academia and the private sector. All share a common goal: to detect and respond to disease threats before they become epidemics and pandemics, and to optimize routine disease surveillance. The goal of this new network is ambitious, but it can also play a vital role in health security: to give every country access to pathogen genomic sequencing and analytics as part of its public health system,” said WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “As was so clearly demonstrated to us during the COVID-19 pandemic, the world is stronger when it stands together to fight shared health threats.” COVID-19 highlighted the critical role pathogen genomics plays in responding to pandemic threats. Without the rapid sequencing of the SARS-COV-2 genome, vaccines would not have been as effective, or have been made available so quickly. New, more transmissible variants of the virus would not have been as quickly identified. Genomics lies at the heart of effective epidemic and pandemic preparedness and response, as well as part of the ongoing surveillance of a vast range of diseases, from foodborne diseases and influenza to tuberculosis and HIV. Its use in monitoring the spread of HIV drug resistance, for example, has led to antiretroviral regimes that have saved countless lives. “Global collaboration in pathogen genomic surveillance has been critical as the world fights COVID-19 together,” said Dr Rajiv J. Shah, President of The Rockefeller Foundation. “IPSN builds upon this experience by creating a strong platform for partners across sectors and borders to share knowledge, tools, and practices to ensure that pandemic prevention and response is innovative and robust in the future.” Despite recent scale-up in genomics capacity in countries as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, many still lack effective systems for collecting and analyzing samples or using those data to drive public health decision-making. There is not enough sharing of data, practices, and innovations to build a robust global health surveillance architecture. Budgets that soared during the pandemic, allowing a rapid build-up of capabilities, are now being slashed, even in the wealthiest countries. “Argentina is deeply invested in building our own country capacity in pathogen genomics and the capacity of other countries,” said Josefina Campos, Director of the National Genomics and Bioinformatics Center at ANLIS Malbrán, Argentina. “Diseases do not respect borders: a disease threat in one country is also a threat to others. We look forward to collaborating with IPSN members to achieve our common goal of preventing illness and saving lives.” The IPSN will tackle these challenges through a global network, connecting geographies and disease-specific networks, to build a collaborative system to better detect, prevent and respond to disease threats. Members will work together in dedicated groups focusing on specific challenges, supported by funding through the IPSN to scale-up ideas and projects in pathogen genomics.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**WHO Establishes Global Network to Monitor and Prevent Infectious Disease Threats**

The World Health Organization (WHO) and its partners are establishing a global network to provide a buffer for people displaced from areas affected by infectious disease emergencies through the power of genomic sequencing. The Global Outbreak and Response Network (GOARN) will facilitate the connection of countries and regional hubs, strengthening preparedness for rapid response and disease investigation, utilizing this data to inform public health decision-making, and widely disseminating this information. Genomic sequencing will identify variants of disease-causing pathogens, batteries, and other disease biomarkers to understand how they spread, how they can cause death, and how they behave. With this information, scientists and healthcare workers can assess and mitigate disease risks to prevent and control outbreaks, as part of a robust disease surveillance program, and to establish interventions and safeguards.

GOARN, along with the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence, convenes global experts in data-driven genomic sequencing analysis from governments, philanthropic organizations, various industries, civil society organizations, educational institutions, and national laboratories. They all share a common goal: to anticipate and prevent disease threats before they become outbreaks and pandemics, and to strengthen disease surveillance systems. This new global network is promising, but it can play a crucial role in public health: to provide all countries with access to genomic sequencing and outbreak response as part of their public health programs," said WHO Under-Secretary-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "Just as it has proven invaluable to us during the COVID-19 pandemic, the entire world has the power to work together to defeat today's health threats."

COVID-19 highlighted the critical role of genomic sequencing in responding to emerging disease threats. Without genomic sequencing for SARS-CoV-2, interventions would not have been effective, or would have been implemented much later. Many variants of concern were not identified until they had already spread widely. Genomic sequencing was at the heart of outbreak detection and pandemic response, and is essential for monitoring various diseases, including foodborne illnesses and those transmitted from animals to humans. Its use in the investigation of monkeypox outbreaks, for example, has led to the identification of previously unknown clades.

"The Global Outbreak and Response Network has been instrumental in how the entire world has fought against COVID-19 together," said Dr. Rajiv J. Shah, President of The Rockefeller Foundation. "GOARN leverages this strength with a powerful mechanism for connecting partners across regions and countries to share knowledge, insights, and best practices to ensure that prevention and response to outbreaks become a priority for the future."

With this innovation in genomic sequencing across countries due to the COVID-19 pandemic, many are now expanding programs for rapid response and disease investigation. Data, insights, and foresight that are essential for building a robust global health surveillance system are lacking. Programs that were scaled back during the pandemic, which enabled the establishment of robust capacities, are now being revived, even in the most developed countries.

"Argentina is truly committed to expanding its national genomic sequencing capacity and the capacity of other countries," said Josefina Campos, Director of Global Affairs for Genomic Sequencing and Data Governance at ANLIS Malbrán, Argentina. "Diseases do not respect borders: a health threat in one country is a threat to another. We are dedicated to partnering with GOARN members to achieve a common goal for disease prevention and saving lives."

GOARN facilitates these connections through a global network, linking regional and disease-specific hubs to establish collaborative programs to enhance foresight, prevent, and respond to emerging health threats. Members work together in working groups to address specific challenges, which they will support with financial resources through GOARN to implement projects and activities in genomic sequencing.

translations.source: Pẹ̀lú síso àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè, àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè, àti ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ papọ̀, IPSN máa ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti mú àlékún bá ìkápá pàtàkì, gbé àwọn ohùn ní ìpele ẹkùn agbègbè àti orílẹ̀-èdè sókè, kí ó sì ró àwọn kàn-án-pá wọn lágbára. Nípa ìfilọ́lẹ̀ IPSN Wọ́n máa ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ IPSN bó ṣe yẹ ní ìta ìpàdé Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé ní Geneva. Ayẹyẹ ìfilọ́lẹ̀ náà máa wáyé ní 20 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023, 17:00 – 19:30h (CET) ní InterContinental Geneva, ó sì ṣí sílẹ̀ fún àwọn oníròyìn. Nínú rẹ̀ ni a ti rí àwọn kókó ọ̀rọ̀ láti ẹnu: Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé; Dr Mike Ryan, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ, Èto Pàjáwírì Ìlera WHO; àti Dr Chikwe Ihekweazu, Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé, Ibùdó fún Òye Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé. Ìgbìmò kan yóò tún wà pẹ̀lú àwọn olùdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ onípele-gíga láti Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ àti àwọn tó ń pèsè owó fún IPSN, tó fi mọ́; Dame Jenny Harries, Olórí Aláṣẹ, Àjọ tó ń rí sí Ààbò Ìlera UK; Dr Jean Kaseya, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀, Àwọn Ààyè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ fún Ìdíwọ́ àti Ìdènà àti Ààrùn; Dr Naveen Rao, Igbákejì Ààrẹ Àgbà, Rockfeller Foundation; Dr Alexander Pym, Olùdarí fún Ààrùn Àkóràn, Wellcome Trust; Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Tulio de Oliveira, Olùdarí, Ààyè fún Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn ní Stellenbosch University, South Africa; Björn Kümmel, Igbákejì Olórí Ẹ̀ka, Ìlera Àgbáyé, Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba Àpapọ̀ tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera, Germany; Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Leo Yee Sin, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ fún Ààyè Orílẹ̀-èdè fún Àwọn Ààrùn Àkóràn; singapore; Dr Mario Moreira, Ààrẹ, Fiocruz, Brazil; àti Dr Josefina Campos, Olùdarí Ààyè fún Apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran àti Òye dátà Ìbí Orílẹ̀-èdè ní ANLIS Malbrán, orílẹ̀-èdè Argentina. Ayẹyẹ náà máa wáyé ní ìlànà alárànbarà, tó ń pèsè ànfààní fún àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ IPSN àti àwọn olùgbọ́ tí ó kù láti jíròrò pẹ̀lú IPSN àti àwọn iṣẹ́ rẹ̀. Nípa IPSN IPSN jẹ́ ojú-òpó àgbáyé tuntun fún àwọn amúṣẹ́ṣe apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn, tí Ibùdó WHO gba àlejò rẹ̀ fún Ọgbọ́n Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé, láti mú kí ìlọsíwájú lórí ìfiránṣẹ́ apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn yá àti láti mú àyípadà bá ṣíṣe ìpinnu ìlera àwujọ. IPSN ní àfojúsùn àgbáyé kan níbi tí gbogbo orílẹ̀-èdè ní ànfààní tó dọ́gba sí ìkápá alálòpẹ́ fún tẹ̀lé-ǹ-tẹ̀lé jínnì àti ìyànnàná gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ọ̀kan lára ètò ìtọpinpin ìlera àwùjọ. Ó bẹ́rẹ̀ ìrìnàjò yìí láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ojú-òpó àgbáyé aṣàtìlẹyìn ànfààní fún àwọn amúṣẹ́ṣe ìtọpinpin apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran tó ń sọ di ńlá tó sì ń mú kí iṣẹ́ àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ rẹ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá ànfààní àti ìdọ́gba. IPSN ní àpapọ àwọn ẹgbẹ́ òṣìṣẹ́ mẹ́ta pàtàkì tí ó ń ṣe àkójọpọ̀ oríṣiríṣi ẹ̀yà àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀, tí Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ní Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO. Àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ń ní àjọṣepọ̀ ní Àwọn Àwùjọ Àwọn Òṣìṣẹ́ Ìlera (COPs) láti yanjú àwọn ìpèníjà àjọni, tí wọ́n sì ń gbèrò láti mú àlékún bá ìṣọ̀kan àti ìmọ̀-ọ̀tun nínú apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn. Nínú Amúṣẹ́yá Ìfẹ̀lójú Orílẹ̀-èdè (CSUA), àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti sọ àwọn ìgbìyànjú di ọ̀kan kí wọ́n sì lè jẹ́ kí àwọn Gúúsù-Gúúsù ó ṣe ìṣípòrọpò láti fẹ ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkápá orílẹ̀-èdè lójú.
translations.reference: By connecting countries, regions, and wider stakeholders, the IPSN will help to increase critical capacity, amplify regional and country-level voices, and strengthen their priorities. About the IPSN launch The IPSN will be formally launched on the sidelines of the World Health Assembly in Geneva. The launch event takes place on 20 May 2023, 17:00 – 19:30h (CET) in the InterContinental Geneva, and is open to media. It includes keynote speeches from: Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General, World Health Organization; Dr Mike Ryan, Executive Director, WHO Health Emergencies Programme; and Dr Chikwe Ihekweazu, Assistant Director-General, World Health Organization, Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence. There will also be a panel with high-level speakers from Member State and IPSN funders, including; Dame Jenny Harries, Chief Executive, UK Health Security Agency; Dr Jean Kaseya, Director-General, Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention; Dr Naveen Rao, Senior Vice President, Rockefeller Foundation; Dr Alexander Pym, Director of Infectious Disease, Wellcome Trust; Professor Tulio de Oliveira, Director, Centre for Epidemiology at Stellenbosch University, South Africa; Björn Kümmel, Deputy Head of Unit, Global Health, Federal Ministry of Health, Germany; Professor Leo Yee Sin, Executive Director of the National Centre for Infectious Diseases; Singapore; Dr Mario Moreira, President, Fiocruz, Brazil; and Dr Josefina Campos, Director of the National Genomics and Bioinformatics Center at ANLIS Malbrán, Argentina. The event will take place in a hybrid format, providing an opportunity for IPSN members and broader audiences to engage with the IPSN and its activities. About the IPSNThe IPSN is a new global network of pathogen genomic actors, hosted by the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence, to accelerate progress on the deployment of pathogen genomics and improve public health decision-making. The IPSN envisions a world where every country has equitable access to sustained capacity for genomic sequencing and analytics as part of its public health surveillance system. It sets out to create a mutually supportive global network of genomic surveillance actors that amplifies and accelerates the work of its members to improve access and equity. The IPSN consists of three main operational bodies that bring together different sets of stakeholders, supported by a Secretariat at the WHO Pandemic Hub. Partners collaborate in Communities of Practice (COPs) to solve common challenges, aiming to increase harmonization and innovation in pathogen genomics. In the Country Scale-Up Accelerator (CSUA), stakeholders work together to align efforts and enable South-South exchange to scale-up country capacity building.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"With countries, regional organizations, and numerous scientific experts coming together, IPSN will work to bring solutions to critical challenges, elevate expertise at the country and regional levels, and strengthen their capacities. The IPSN launch event will be held as part of the World Health Assembly in Geneva. The launch event will take place on June 20, 2023, at 17:00 – 19:30h (CET) at the InterContinental Geneva and will be open to journalists. Speakers at the event include: Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the World Health Organization; Dr Mike Ryan, Executive Director, WHO Health Emergencies Programme; and Dr Chikwe Ihekweazu, Deputy Director-General, World Health Organization, Hub for Pandemic Intelligence and Global Infectious Disease Surveillance. There will also be a panel discussion with high-level data scientists from Member States and those funding IPSN, including: Dame Jenny Harries, Chief Medical Officer, UK Health Security Agency; Dr Jean Kaseya, Director-General, Global Fund for Infectious Diseases and Epidemics; Dr Naveen Rao, Senior Vice President, Rockefeller Foundation; Dr Alexander Pym, Head of Infectious Diseases, Wellcome Trust; Professor Tulio de Oliveira, Director, Centre for Viral Genomics at Stellenbosch University, South Africa; Björn Kümmel, Deputy Head of Unit, Global Health, German Federal Ministry of Health; Professor Leo Yee Sin, Chief Medical Officer for National Infectious Diseases, Singapore; Dr Mario Moreira, Director, Fiocruz, Brazil; and Dr Josefina Campos, Director of the National Reference Laboratory for Viral and Data Science, ANLIS Malbrán, Argentina. The event will be held virtually, offering benefits for IPSN members and those interested in engaging with IPSN and its work.

About IPSN: IPSN is a new global hub for genomic sequencing experts, endorsed by the WHO for Pandemic and Global Infectious Disease Surveillance, to accelerate genomic sequencing and inform public health decision-making. IPSN has a global mandate to ensure that all countries benefit from a robust response to emerging and re-emerging threats as part of a strengthened public health emergency preparedness and response system. It began this journey by establishing a sustainable global network for genomic sequencing experts and enabling the work of its members to bring benefits and resilience. IPSN has three core working groups that bring together a diverse range of experts, supported by the WHO Hub for Pandemic Intelligence. Partners collaborate within Communities of Practice (COPs) to address technical challenges, and they work to promote harmonization and best practices in genomic sequencing. Within the Country Strengthening Alliance (CSUA), experts work together to align priorities so that governments can develop country-level response plans."

translations.source: COP àti CSUA ń ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ láti ẹlẹ́ka-jẹ̀ka, àwọn ìpele ìpawówọlé, àti àwọn agbègbè, pẹ̀lú ìfọkànsìn sí ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ ilẹ̀-òkèèrè àti ìdọ́gba, àti ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe tó jinlẹ̀ nínú yálà apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran tàbí fífẹ àwọn ètò ìtọpinpin orílẹ̀-èdè lójú. Ẹgbẹ́ kẹta ni Àpèjọ Àwọn Olùpèsè-Owó láti darí àwọn olùpèsè-owó onínúure, ẹlẹ́gbẹ́-jẹgbẹ́ àti ìjọba tí wọ́n wà ní àyíká mú àlékún bá àkíyèsí òṣèlú àti ìpèsè owó tí ó tó fún ìtọpinpin apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn. Àpẹ̀jọ Àwọn Olùpèsè-owó tún pèsè àfikún ìpèsè owó ìrànwọ́ fún àwọn iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ IPSN. Ìdásílẹ̀ IPSN náà ni ìpèsè owó ìjọba orílẹ̀-èdè Germany ti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún sí Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO. Nípa Ibùdó WHO fún Ọgbọ́n Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé Ó jẹ́ ọ̀kan lára Ètò Pàjáwírì Ìlera WHO, Ibùdó WHO fún Ọgbọ́n Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé (Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO náà), ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àjọṣepọ̀ àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ àgbáyé láti oríṣiríṣi ẹ̀ka tí ó ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ láti kojú àwọn ewu àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé lọ́jọ́ iwájú pẹ̀lú ànfààní tó dára sí dátà, àwọn ìkápá ìyànnàná tó dára, àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ojú ìwòye tó dára fún ìpinnu-ṣíṣe . Pẹ̀lú àtìlẹyìn láti ọ̀dọ Ìjọba Federal Republic of Germany, wọ́n ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO ní oṣù Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2021 ní Berlin, ní ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, tí ó ṣàfihàn àìkájú-òṣùwọ̀n káàkiri àgbáyé pẹ̀lú bí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣe ń ṣàwárí, ṣàmójútó àti àkóso àwọn ewu ìlera àwujọ. Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ àti àwọn ọ́fíísì ẹkùn agbègbè àti orílẹ̀-èdè WHO láti ró àwọn ìkápá pínpín-dátà wọn lágbára àti láti mú kí àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ káàkiri àgbáyé ní àjọṣepọ̀ kí wọ́n sì pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti ṣe àkójọ àti láti yànnàná dátà fún ìtọpinpin ìkìlò kíákíá. Pẹ̀lú wíwà rẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tó ju 150 lọ, àwọn ọ́fíísì ẹkùn agbègbè mẹ́fà, àti olú-ilé-iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ ní Geneva, arọ́wọ́tó WHO fún wa ní ànfààní láti ṣe ìtọ́jú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn, àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé àti àwọn ewu ìlera àwujọ pẹ̀lú kánjú-kánjú àti àìṣèmẹ́lẹ́ tó dọ́gba káàkiri àgbáyé. Pẹ̀lú síso àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ abẹ́lẹ́, ẹkùn agbègbè, àti àgbáyé pọ̀, Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwùjọ àjọṣepọ̀ fún àwọn onímọ̀-ọ̀tun, àwọn onímọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ káàkiri oríṣiríṣi ẹ̀ka, tí ó ń fún wa ní ànfààní láti ṣàmúlò àti láti pín àwọn ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ tó ń léwájú àti dídarí àwọn iṣẹ́ wa fún ohun tí àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ káàkiri àgbáyé nílò. Gígùn lé àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ káàkiri àwọn ẹ̀ka-ẹ̀kọ́, àwọn ẹ̀ka àti àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè, ó máa ṣàmúlò àkójọ agbára WHO láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ọ̀nà àbáyọ àgbáyé tí wọ́n gbé sórí àgbékalẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ àgbáyé àti ìgbẹkẹ̀lé.
translations.reference: The COP and CSUA bring together organizations from across sectors, income levels, and geographies, with a commitment to international cooperation and equity, and deep expertise in either genomics or country scale-up of surveillance systems. The third body is the Funders Forum to coordinate philanthropic, multi-lateral and governmental donors around increased political attention and financing efficiency of pathogen genomic surveillance. The Funders Forum also catalyses additional grant funding for projects of IPSN members. The establishment of the IPSN has been supported by German government funding to the WHO Pandemic Hub. About the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence Forming part of the WHO Health Emergencies Programme, the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence (the WHO Pandemic Hub), facilitates a global collaboration of partners from multiple sectors that supports countries and stakeholders to address future pandemic and epidemic risks with better access to data, better analytical capacities, and better tools and insights for decision-making. With support from the Government of the Federal Republic of Germany, the WHO Pandemic Hub was established in September 2021 in Berlin, in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, which demonstrated weaknesses around the world in how countries detect, monitor and manage public health threats. The WHO Pandemic Hub works closely with Member States and WHO regional and country offices to strengthen their data-sharing capacities and enable partners from around the world to collaborate and co-create tools to gather and analyse data for early warning surveillance. With a presence in more than 150 countries, six regional offices, and its Geneva headquarters, WHO’s reach gives us the ability to treat pandemic, epidemic and public health risks with equal urgency and diligence around the globe. By linking local, regional, and global initiatives, the WHO Pandemic Hub fosters a collaborative environment for innovators, scientists and experts from across a wide spectrum of disciplines, allowing us to leverage and share cutting-edge technology and anchoring our work in the needs of stakeholders around the world. Building on expertise across disciplines, sectors, and regions, it will leverage WHO’s convening power to foster global solutions built on an architecture of global collaboration and trust.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

COP and CSUA are bringing together health facilities from local governments, funding levels, and regions, with a focus on rural health and resilience, and expertise embedded in the year's national health action plan or emergency response. The third pillar is the Assembly of Taxpayers to lead taxpayers, community members, and the government who are jointly responsible for tax revenue and tax payments that are sufficient for national emergency funding. The Assembly of Taxpayers also provides additional tax credits for projects of IPSN members. The foundation of IPSN was supported by a tax credit from the German Federal Government to the WHO Hub for Health Emergencies. Through the WHO Hub for Global Health Emergencies and Pandemic Preparedness (the WHO Hub for Health Emergencies), it supports a network of global partners from various sectors that support countries and communities to mitigate the risks of health emergencies and pandemics in the future with good access to data, effective response measures, and good policies and decision-making. With support from the Federal Republic of Germany, the WHO Hub for Health Emergencies was established in Berlin in November 2021, in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, which exposed the global vulnerability with how countries are prepared, respond and control public health threats. The WHO Hub for Health Emergencies works closely with Member States and regional and country WHO offices to strengthen their data-driven response capacity and to enable global partners to collaborate and provide advice to collect and analyze data for evidence-based decision-making. With a presence in over 150 countries, six regional offices, and its headquarters in Geneva, WHO’s capacity provides us with the ability to assess health emergencies, pandemics, and public health threats promptly and effectively across the globe. With the expansion of regional and global partnerships, the WHO Hub for Health Emergencies supports a collaborative network for researchers, scientists, and policymakers across various sectors, providing us with the ability to innovate and tailor our work to the needs of communities worldwide. Leveraging experts across academic disciplines, sectors, and regional offices, it strengthens WHO’s collective capacity to support global solutions that promote global collaboration and resilience.

translations.source: Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin máa gbájúmọ́ "dídóólà ẹ̀mí, mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn" bí WHO ṣe pé 75 Pẹ̀lu bí gbogbo àgbáyé ṣe ń kojú àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera àti ọmọnìyàn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́, Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin máa gbájúmọ́ mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Ìjókòó Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé ọdún yìí máa ṣe ìpinnu lórí ọjọ́ iwájú ojú ẹsẹ̀ àti onígbà-pípẹ́ WHO, bẹ̀rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú ètò ìṣúná fún ọdún méjì tó ń bọ̀, àwọn ìpinnu tó ṣe pàtàkì nípa ìpèsè owó alálòpẹ́ fún Ilé-iṣẹ́ náà àti àwọn àyípadà tí wọ́n ti ṣe láti mú àyípadà bá àwọn ìlànà àti ìṣirò WHO. Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba tún máa jíròrò nípa ipa pàtàkì tí WHO ní nínú Àgbékalẹ̀ Pàjáwírì Ìlera Àgbáyé. Ìṣíṣọ lójú àwọn ìṣòro wọ̀nyìí ni ayẹyẹ ọdún 75th àjọ náà, pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe àfihàn àwọn àṣeyọrí ìlera àgbáyé àjọni rẹ̀ láti ìgbà tí wọ́n ti dá WHO sílẹ̀ ní ọdún 1948. Ó máa ṣe àtúnyẹwò ìlọsíwájú ọdún tí ó kọjá, àwọn àṣeyọrí àti àwọn ìpèníjà pẹ̀lú àwọn kàn-án-pá ọjọ́ iwájú káàkiri àwọn kókó ìpìlẹ iṣẹ́ WHO: Kíkárí Ìlera Lágbàyé, Àwọn Pàjáwírì, Ṣíṣe Ìpolongo Ìlera àti Ìgbáyégbádùn. WHA76 máa wáyé ní 21-30 oṣù Èbìbí ní Palais des Nations ní Geneva. "Bí a ṣe ń ṣe àjọ̀dun ọdún 75th WHO, a lè ṣe ìyanràn pẹ̀lú àwọn àṣeyọrí àtẹ̀yìnwá wa, ṣùgbọ́n a gbọ́dọ̀ tún fi ọkàn sí àwọn ẹ̀kọ́ tí a ti kọ́, pẹ̀lú bí a ṣe ń ṣe ìṣípòrọpò láti abala pàjáwírì COVID-19, kí á sì pèsè ọjọ́ iwájú níbi tí gbogbo ènìyàn máa ní ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera tí wọ́n nílò," Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. Èyí túnmọ̀ sí ríró àgbékalẹ̀ àgbáyé fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún pàjáwírì ìlera, ìdáhùn àti ìfaradà, ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní ojú ọ̀nà sí kíkárí ìlera lágbàyé, àti ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ WHO tó lágbára, ìpèsè owó alálòpẹ́ àti ìṣirò. Láti bẹ̀rẹ WHA76 ní ọjọ́ Àìkú 21 oṣù Èbìbí, àtúnṣe kẹrin Walk the Talk: Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn máa bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní Palais des Nations ní 8:30 am CEST (ìmúrasílẹ̀ máa bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní 7:30 am CEST), láti ṣe ìpolongo ìlera ara àti ọpọlọ àti láti ṣe ayẹyẹ pàtàkì àjọṣepọ̀ lásìkò àjọ̀dún ọdún 75th WHO. Ní bíi 2:00pm CEST, lẹ́yìn fọ́tò ẹbí pẹ̀lú Àwọn Olórí Aṣojú Ìjọba, ìkíni káàbọ̀ onípele-gíga Àpéjọ náà ní Palais des Nations máa ṣàfihàn àwọn ọ̀rọ̀ àkọ́sọ láti ẹnu Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, Àwọn Olórí Orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn àlejò pàtàkì. Àkójọpọ̀ àwọn olùdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ wà ní ìsàlẹ̀). Ní àkókò ìjókòó yìí, DG máa gbé àmì-ẹ̀yẹ àṣeyọrí ìgbésí-ayé fún àwọn olórí ìlera àgbáyé méjì. Ní ọjọ́ Ajé 22 oṣù Èbìbí, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ náà máa ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọ̀rọ rẹ̀ gangan ní déédé àkókò 10:00am. Ní ọjọ́ Ẹtì, 26 oṣù Èbìbí, àwọn aṣojú àwọn ìpìlẹ̀ àgbáyé márààrún máa darapọ̀ mọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, láti pín àmì-ẹ̀yẹ ìlera àwujọ márùn-ún àti àwọn ẹ̀bùn fún àwọn ẹni iyì méje fún ọdún 2023. Wọ́n wá láti Bahrain, China, Guatemala, Lebanon, Nepal, Thailand àti Philippines. Àwọn ìṣòro tó jẹ́ kókó Àwọn ìjírórò tó ṣe kókó àti àwọn ìpinnu ni wọ́n ń retí lórí: Àtúnyẹwò iṣẹ́ WHO nínú pàjáwírì ìlera, tó fi mọ́ Àwọn Òfin Ìlera Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè àti ṣíṣe ìrólágbára fún ìgbaradì WHO fún àti ìdáhùn sí àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera; Àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àti ìgbésẹ̀ àgbáyé lórí àwọn agbègbè bíi ìlera àwọn obìnrin, àwọn ọmọde àti ọ̀dọ́, ìtúnirọ, kíkárí ìlera lágbàyé àti ètò ìlera alábọ́dé, ìṣègùn ìbílẹ̀, ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà àkóràn, oògùn tí kìí ṣe ojúlówó àti ayédèrú, ìlera àwọn asásàálà àti aṣíkiri, àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn, ìlera ọpọlọ, àwọn aṣokùnfà àwùjọ, èròjà aṣara lóore àti àwọn ìpèníjà ara; Ìbuwọ́lù Ètò Ìṣúná WHO fún 2024-2025, tó fi mọ́ ìpinnu láti mú àlékún bá owó àjọ tí wọ́n yẹ̀wò rẹ̀ àti àwọn ọ̀rọ̀ mìíràn tó ń wá láti ọ̀dọ Ẹgbẹ́ Òṣìṣẹ́ lórí Ìpèsè owó Alálòpẹ́. Ìjírórò Orí-tábílì Ojoojúmọ́ máa wáyé ní àkókò oúnjẹ ọ̀sán láti ọjọ́ Ajé sí ọjọ́ Àbámẹ́ta ní Yàrá XVIII. Àwọn ìjókòó yìí máa ní àwọn ìjírórò láàrín àwọn aṣojú ìjọba fún àpéjọ, àwọn àjọ tó jẹ́ alájọṣepọ̀, àwọn aṣojú àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ àti àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ WHO lórí àwọn kàn-án-pá ìlera àwujọ. Àwọn ìjókòó náà máa bẹ̀rẹ̀ láti 22-27 oṣù Èbìbí, láti 13:00 sí 14:15 CEST ó sì ṣe é tẹ̀lé lórí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára. Gẹ́gẹ́ bí ẹgbẹ́ tí ń ṣe ìpinnu WHO tó ga jùlọ, Àpéjọ Ìlera náà ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn òfin Àjọ náà ó sì ń buwọ́lù ètò ìṣúná rẹ̀. Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba láti 149 Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ ló máa ń wá sí WHA.
translations.reference: Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly to focus on “saving lives, driving health for all” as WHO turns 75 As the world faces ongoing health and humanitarian emergencies, the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly will focus on driving forward health for all. This year’s session of the World Health Assembly will determine the immediate and longer-term future of WHO, starting with the program budget for the next two years, key decisions about the sustainable financing of the Organization and changes put in place to improve WHO’s processes and accountability. Delegates will also deliberate about the critical role that WHO has in the Global Health Emergency Architecture. The backdrop to these issues is the organization’s 75th anniversary, with the highlights of its collective global health achievements since WHO’s creation in 1948. It will review last year’s progress, accomplishments and challenges along with future priorities across the key pillars of WHO’s work: Universal Health Coverage, Emergencies, Promoting Health and Well-being. WHA76 will convene 21-30 May at the Palais des Nations in Geneva. “As we mark WHO’s 75th anniversary, we can be proud of our past achievements, but we must be mindful of lessons learned, as we transition from the emergency phase of COVID-19, and create a future where every person has access to the health services they need,” said Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “This means strengthening the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response and resilience, supporting countries on the road to universal health coverage, and building a stronger, sustainably-funded and accountable WHO. "To kickstart WHA76 on Sunday 21 May, the fourth edition of Walk the Talk: Health for All challenge will start at Place des Nations at 8:30 am CEST (warm-up starts at 7:30 am CEST), to promote physical and mental health and celebrate the importance of partnership during the WHO’s 75th anniversary. At about 2:00 pm CEST, following a family photo with Heads of Delegations, the Assembly high-level welcome at Palais des Nations will feature speeches from the WHO Director-General, Heads of State and special guests. List of speakers below). During this session, the DG will present life-time achievement awards to two global health leaders. On Monday 22 May, the Director-General will give his main address at approximately 10:00 am. On Friday, 26 May, the WHO Director-General will be joined by representatives of five global foundations, to present five public health awards and prizes to the seven laureates for 2023. They come from Bahrain, China, Guatemala, Lebanon, Nepal, Thailand and the Philippines. Key issues Key discussions and decisions are expected on: A review of WHOs work in health emergencies, including the International Health Regulations and strengthening WHO preparedness for and response to health emergencies; Strategies and global action on areas such as women’s, children’s and adolescents’ health, rehabilitation, universal health coverage and primary health care, traditional medicine, infection prevention and control, substandard and falsified medicines, health of refugees and migrants, non-communicable diseases, mental health, social determinants, nutrition and disabilities; Approval of the WHO Programme Budget for 2024-2025, including the decision to increase assessed contributions and other matters emanating from the Working Group on Sustainable Financing. Daily Strategic roundtables will take place at lunch time from Monday to Saturday in Room XVIII. These events will host discussions among assembly delegates, partner agencies, representatives of civil society and WHO experts on public health priorities. Sessions run from 22-27 May, from 13:00 to 14:15 CEST and can be followed online. As WHO’s highest decision-making body, the Health Assembly sets out the Organization’s policy and approves its budget. WHA is attended by delegations from all WHO 194 Member States.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**World Health Assembly to Convene, Focusing on "A Healthy World, Progress for All"**

The 76th World Health Assembly (WHA) will convene with the theme "A Healthy World, Progress for All," as stated by WHO.  Alongside the global community's efforts to address health challenges and disease outbreaks, the 76th World Health Assembly will focus on progress for all. This year's Assembly agenda will include decisions regarding the future of the WHO's budget and long-term priorities, starting with the two-year budget plan, key decisions regarding the organization's funding, and reforms implemented to strengthen WHO policies and governance. Government representatives will also discuss the critical role of WHO in Global Health Emergency Preparedness. The 75th anniversary celebration of the Assembly will highlight the organization's health successes over the years since WHO was established in 1948. It will review progress over the past years, successes, and challenges, alongside the future priorities across the WHO's core areas of work: Global Health Security, Emergencies, Health Promotion and Wellbeing. WHA76 will take place from May 21-30 at the Palais des Nations in Geneva.

"As we celebrate WHO’s 75th anniversary, we can build on our proven successes, but we must also learn from our experiences, including how we responded to the COVID-19 pandemic, to ensure a future where everyone has access to the health services they need," said Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. This also calls for global action to strengthen health systems, prevention and control of diseases, providing support to countries on the path to global health security, and strengthening the WHO’s capacity, funding, and governance. To kick off WHA76 on Monday, May 21, the fourth installment of "Walk the Talk: Health for All" will begin at the Palais des Nations at 8:30 am CEST (broadcast will begin at 7:30 am CEST), to promote health and well-being and celebrate the important partnership during WHO’s 75th anniversary year. At approximately 2:00 pm CEST, following a family photo with Heads of State, the high-level opening of the Assembly at the Palais des Nations will feature remarks from the WHO Director-General, Heads of State, and other key guests. (List of speakers follows). During this session, the DG will present lifetime achievement awards to two global health leaders. On Wednesday, May 22, the Director-General will deliver his address in detail at 10:00 am. On Friday, May 26, representatives from all six regions will join the WHO Director-General to award twelve social impact awards and seven awards for the year 2023. They are from Bahrain, China, Guatemala, Lebanon, Nepal, Thailand, and the Philippines.

**Key Issues and Decisions**

The key issues and decisions to be addressed include:

*   Review of WHO’s work in health emergencies, aligning with the Health Regulations and strengthening WHO’s preparedness and response to health emergencies;
*   Global health issues and progress on areas such as women’s and children’s health, disability, global health security, essential medicines and health products, health systems, prevention and control of noncommunicable diseases, social determinants of health, mental health and substance use, nutrition, and environmental health;
*   Approval of the WHO’s budget for 2024-2025, including decisions regarding the review of the organization's budget and other matters from the Independent Oversight Advisory Committee;
*   The Committee on Health’s agenda will be held during lunch hours from Monday to Wednesday in Yàrá XVIII. These sessions will include discussions between government representatives for the Assembly, partner agencies, representatives of civil society organizations, and WHO experts on social issues. The sessions will begin from May 22-27, from 13:00 to 14:15 CEST and will be streamed online. As the highest decision-making body of WHO, the Health Assembly sets the organization’s agenda and approves its budget.

Representatives from 149 Member States will attend WHA.

translations.source: Ètò Àpéjọ Ìlera náà ni ìpàdé 30th fún Ètò náà, Ìgbìmọ̀ fún Ìṣúná àti Alámòjútó ti Ìgbìmọ̀ Alàkóso máa ṣíwájú rẹ̀, tí wọ́n máa pàdé ní 17-19 oṣù Èbìbí, ọdún 2023. Lẹ́yìn Àpéjọ náà, ìpàdé 153th ti Ìgbìmọ̀ Alàkóso máa wáyé láti ọjọ́ 31 oṣù Èbìbí sí 1 Òkudù. Àpéjọ Ìlera náà wà fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ tí wọ́n forúkọsílẹ̀ tí wọ́n sì gba àṣẹ, Àwọn Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ Alájọṣepọ̀, Àwọn Alákíyèsí, àwọn aṣojú tí wọ́n fi ìwé pè láti UN àti àwọn àjọ ìjọba-sí-ìjọba àti àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ tí kìí ṣe ti ìjọba tí wọ́n ń kópa. WHA76 náà ni wọn yóò gbé sórí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára bó ṣe ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lórí ìbùdó-ìtàkun WHO. Ètò tí wọ́n ti ṣe, àtẹ-iṣẹ́ àkọ́kọ́ ṣe, àwọn ìwé Àpéjọ àti ìwé ìròyìn ojoojúmọ́ lè di wíwò níbi. Fífún àwọn oníròyìn ní àṣẹ àti àwọn ìfitóni fún Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Àjọ Iṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé ní Geneva ló máa ṣàkóso fínfún àwọn oníròyìn ní àṣẹ fún WHA76. Forúkọsílẹ̀ nípasẹ̀ Indico. Láti gba àkọmọ̀nà rẹ: Ní ọjọ́ Ẹtì 19 oṣù Èbìbí (àkókò iṣẹ́: 9:00 am sí 16:30 pm CEST) ní Villa Feuillantines, Place des Nations . Àlàyé àti àdírẹ́sì : https://unece.org/important-information-how-obtain-your-badge Ní ọjọ́ Àbámẹ́ta 20 oṣù Èbìbí: UN Palais des Nations ní Pregny Gate (àdojúkọ ICRC Building) láti 13:00 sí 17:00 CEST. Ní ọjọ́ Àìkú 21 oṣù Èbìbí, wà á gba àkọmọ̀nà oníròyìn rẹ ní UN Palais des Nations ní Pregny Gate (àdojúkọ ICRC Building) láti 9:00am sí 16:00 pm CEST Ní ọ̀sẹ̀ tó ń bọ̀ - 22-26 oṣù Èbìbí- láti gba àwọn àkọmọ̀nà oníròyìn: Villa Les Feuillantines at Place des Nations. Ìfitóni lori Àpéjọ Ìlera ni wọ́n máa fi sí orí ibùdó ìtàkùn WHO wọn ó sì fi àwọn ohun èlò tó ṣe pàtàkì náà ránṣé sí àkójọpọ̀ àwọn oníròyìn àgbáyé. Forúkọsílẹ̀ fún ìfitóni. Àwọn fọ́tò àti fídíò Àwọn fọ́tò WHO wà nílẹ̀ fún lílò àwọn oníròyìn nípasẹ àká fọ́tò WHO. Forúkọsílẹ̀ ní ẹ̀ẹ̀kan fún ànfàní sí ìròyìn sí àká àwọn fọ́tò WHO pẹ̀lú títẹ "media registration" lórí ìkànnì yìí. Fún àwọn ìbéèrè tó jọ mọ́ àwọn fọ́tò WHO tẹ ímeèlì sí [ímeèlì tí wọ́n dáàbòbò] Àwọn ohun èlò fídíò orí afẹ́fẹ́ tí wọ́n ká sílẹ̀ àti àwọn àkóónú ìròyìn máa wà nílẹ̀ fún àwọn oníròyìn láti gbà á sílẹ̀ lórí Canto. Fún èyíkéyìí ìbéèrè tó jọ mọ́ àgbéká fídíò bó ṣe ń ṣẹlẹ̀ jọ̀wọ́ tẹ ímeèlì sí [ímeèlì tí wọ́n dáàbòbò]. Ìkànnì ìbánidọ́rẹ̀ẹ́ WHO máa pèsè ìfitóni àtìgbàdégbà láti ibi Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé nípasẹ Twitter -tẹ̀lé @WHO àti ìsàmìááṣì #WHA76. Tẹ̀lé àwọn àkántì ìkànnì ìbánidọ́rẹ̀ẹ́ WHO tókù fún ìfitóni láti#WHA76: Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, YouTube

After the Assembly, the 153th meeting of the Executive Board will take place from 31 May to 1 June. The Health Assembly is open to registered and accredited Member States, Associate Members, Observers, invited representatives of the UN and other participating inter-governmental organizations and non-State actors.
