Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba document:

The World Health Assembly's (WHA) 76th session will be held from May 17-19, 2023, with the meeting being advanced by the Executive Board. Following the Assembly, the 133rd session of the Executive Board will take place from May 31st to June 1st. The World Health Assembly is for Member States that are registered and have accreditation, Associate Members, Observers, journalists with press credentials from the UN, and government, intergovernmental organizations and non-governmental staff. WHA76 will focus on the impact of digital health on the WHO’s emergency response. Documents related to the Assembly, agenda items, and daily press briefings will be available on-site. Press accreditation for the World Health Assembly of the World Health Organization in Geneva will control the distribution of press credentials for WHA76. Registration is through Indico.

To collect your badge: On Tuesday, May 19th (working hours: 9:00 am to 16:30 pm CEST) at Villa Feuillantines, Place des Nations. For details and address: https://unece.org/important-information-how-obtain-your-badge On Wednesday, May 20th: UN Palais des Nations at Pregny Gate (adjacent to the ICRC Building) from 13:00 to 17:00 CEST. On Thursday, May 21st, badges will be collected at UN Palais des Nations at Pregny Gate (adjacent to the ICRC Building) from 9:00 am to 16:00 pm CEST. During the week of - May 22-26 - to collect press badges: Villa Les Feuillantines at Place des Nations. Briefings on the Health Assembly will be posted on the WHO website and essential information will be sent to the global press pool. Register for briefings. Photos and Videos WHO photos are available for journalists' use through the WHO photo library. Register separately for access to the WHO photo library with the title "media registration" on this link. For any questions regarding WHO photos, please email [redacted email address]. Video assets, including b-roll and soundbites, will be available for journalists to download on Canto. For any inquiries regarding the video library, please email [redacted email address]. WHO's official Twitter account will provide briefings and updates from the World Health Assembly through @WHO and hashtag #WHA76. Follow the WHO's dedicated event links for briefings from #WHA76: Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, YouTube.

translations.source: A nílò ìgbẹ́sẹ̀ ojú ẹsẹ̀ láti kojú ìlọsíwájú tó ti dúró lórí àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ ajẹmọ́-ìlera WHO ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò ọdún 2023 fún ìjábọ̀ àwọn Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé ọlọ́dọọdún rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ònkà tuntun lórí ipa àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 àti àwọn ìṣirò tuntun lórí ìlọsíwájú fún Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ (SDG) ajẹmọ́-ìlera. Ìjábọ̀ náà pẹ̀lú dátà tí ó dé ọdún 2022 ṣàfihàn ìdúróṣinṣin ìlọsíwájú ìlera lórí àwọn atọ́ka ìlera pàtàkì láti bíi ọdún díẹ̀ sẹ́yìn yàtọ̀ sí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tí a rí ní àkókò ọdún 2000-2015. Ó tún pe àkíyèsí wa sí ewu àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn (NCDs) tó ń lọ sókè àti àyípadà ojú ọjọ́, ó sì pè fún ìdáhùn àjọṣe àti ìrólágbára. Iye tí COVID-19 kó nínú ẹ̀mí tí ó sọnù àti ìlọsíwájú ìlera Àwọn ìwé ìjábọ̀ náà ṣe ìsọdọ̀tun ìṣirò lórí aburú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà lórí ìlera àgbáyé, tó sì ń ṣokùnfà ìfàsẹ́yìn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ nínú ìlọsíwájú láti dé SDGs. Ní àkókò 2020-2021, COVID-19 yọrí sí 336.8 mílíọ̀nù ọdún ẹ̀mí tí ó sọnù káàkiri àgbáyé tó sì yanilẹ́nu. Èyí dọ́gba pẹ̀lú ìdajì ọdún 22 ẹ̀mí tí ó sọnù fún àpọ̀ju ikú kọ̀ọ̀kan, tí ó gẹ́ ẹ̀mí mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn kéré ní òjijì pẹ̀lú ìbànújẹ́. Láti ọdún 2000, a rí àyípadà tó lápẹrẹ nínú ìlera ìyá àti ọmọ pẹ̀lú ikú tí ó wálẹ̀ ní ìdá-kan-nínú-mẹ́ta àti ìdajì-kan. Ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àwọn ààrùn àkóràn bíi KASA, ikọ́-ife (TB) àti àìsàn ibà náà tún dínkù, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àdínkù ewu ikú àìtọ́jọ́ látara NCDs àti àwọn ìfarapa. Lápapọ̀, àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà ti ṣe àfikún sí àlékún nínú ìrètí ìṣẹ̀mí lágbàyé láti ọdún 67 ní ọdún 2000 sí ọdún 73 ní ọdún 2019. Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà ti fi mú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn atọ́ka ajẹmọ́-ìlera kúrò ní ojú ọ̀nà síwájú sí i ó sì ti yọrí sí àìdọ́gba nínú ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú ìlera tó jẹ́ ojúlówó-ńlá, ìlàna-iṣẹ́ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti ààbò ìpèsè owó. Nítorí ìdí èyí, àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ìlọsíwájú nínú àìsàn ibà àti TB ti padà sẹ́yìn, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ènìyàn díẹ̀ ni wọ́n tọ́jú fún àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì (NTDs). Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé jẹ́ àyẹ̀wò ọlọ́dọọdún WHO lórí ipò ìlera àgbáyé. Ìjábọ̀ náà fi ìkìlọ̀ alágbára ránṣẹ́ lórí ewu àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn, tí ó ní ọ̀pọ̀ àti àlékún aburú lórí àwọn ẹ̀mí, ọ̀nà ìgbà rí ìjẹ-ìmu, àwọn ètò ìlera, àwọn àwùjọ, àwọn ọrọ̀-ajé àti àwọn ẹgbẹ́," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. Ìjábọ̀ náà pè fún àlékún nínú àwọn ìdókówò fún ìlera àti àwọn ètò ìlera láti padà sí ojú ònà lọ sí Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́." NCDs-ewu ìlera tó máa ń lékún ní gbogbo ìgbà fún àwọn ìran ọjọ́ iwájú Pẹ̀lú àpapọ ìlọsíwájú ìlera, ìpín iye ikú tí NCDs ń ṣe okùnfà rẹ̀ lọ́dọọdún ti lọ sókè léra-léra ó sì ti wá ń gbà tó ìdá-mẹ́ta-nínú-mẹ́rin gbogbo àwọn ẹ̀mí tó ń sọnù ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan. Tí ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ yìí bá tẹ̀síwájú, NCDs ni wọ́n fojú sùn pé yóò ṣe okùnfà fún bíi 86% nínú 90 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ikú ọdọọdún ní ìdajì-ọgọ́rùn ọdún; fún ìdí èyí, 77 mílíọ̀nù àwọn wọ̀nyìí ni ó máa jẹ́ nítorí NCDs - àlékún tó fẹ́rẹ̀ súnmọ́ 90% nínú iye ònkà láti ọdún 2019. Ìdúróṣinṣin ìlọsíwájú pè fún amúṣẹ́yá Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tuntun ṣe àfihàn ìfàsẹ́yìn nínú iye àdínkù ọdọọdún (ARR) fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn atọ́ka. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, iye ikú ìyá lágbàyé nílò láti dínkù pẹ̀lú 11.6% ní ọdún kan láàrín ọdún 2021 àti 2030 láti dọ́gba pẹ̀lú àfojúsùn SDG. Bákan náà, àpapọ̀ àdínkù nínú ìṣẹ̀lẹ TB láti ọdún 2015 sí 2021 jẹ́ ìdá-kan-nínú-márùn ọ̀nà láti débi àṣeyọrí 2025 ti Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ Fi Òpin sí TB WHO. Pẹ̀lú àdínkù nínú ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú oríṣiríṣi ewu ìlera - bíi lílo tábà, ọtí mímu, rògbòdìyàn, omi tó léwu àti ìmọ́tótó, àti àìdàgbà sókè àwọn ọmọ - ìtẹ̀síwájú náà ò péye bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àwọn ewu kọ̀ọ̀kan bíi bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ sì pọ̀ gan-an. Eyí tó banilẹ́rù ni pé, ọ̀nkà àpapọ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsan ìsanra ń lékún láì sí ààmì ìdápadà ojú ẹsẹ̀. Síwájú sí i, ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì ti fà sẹ́yìn ní àfiwé àwọn èrè ṣáájú-2015, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àìsí ìtẹ̀síwájú tó lápẹrẹ nínú àdínkù ìnira ìpèsè owó nítorí iye owó ètò ìlera. Èyí máa mú àdínkù tó lápẹrẹ bá agbára wa láti ṣe àṣeyọrí Ìkárí Ìlera Àgbáyé ní ọdún 2030. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 jẹ́ ìrántí pàtàkì pé ìtẹ̀síwájú ò lọ tààrà kò sì ní ìdánilójú," Dr Samira Asma, Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Dátà, Ìyànnàná àti Àgbékalẹ̀ fún Ipa WHO kílọ̀.
translations.reference: Urgent action needed to tackle stalled progress on health-related Sustainable Development Goals WHO is releasing the 2023 edition of its annual World Health Statistics report with new figures on the impact of COVID-19 pandemic and the latest statistics on progress towards the health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). The report with data up to 2022 underscores a stagnation of health progress on key health indicators in recent years compared with trends seen during 2000-2015. It also alerts us to the growing threat of noncommunicable diseases (NCDs) and climate change, and calls for a coordinated and strengthened response. COVID-19 cost in lost lives and health progress The report documents updated statistics on the toll of the pandemic on global health, contributing to the ongoing decline in progress towards the SDGs. During 2020-2021, COVID-19 resulted in a staggering 336.8 million years of life lost globally. This equates to an average of 22 years of life lost for every excess death, abruptly and tragically cutting short the lives of millions of people. Since 2000, we saw significant improvements in maternal and child health with deaths falling by one-third and one-half, respectively. The incidence of infectious diseases such as HIV, tuberculosis (TB) and malaria also declined, along with a lowered risk of premature deaths from NCDs and injuries. Together, these contributed to an increase in global life expectancy from 67 years in 2000 to 73 years in 2019. However, the pandemic has put many health-related indicators further off-track and contributed to inequalities in access to high-quality health care, routine immunizations and financial protection. As a result, improving trends in malaria and TB have been reversed, and fewer people were treated for neglected tropical diseases (NTDs). “The World Health Statistics is WHO’s annual check-up on the state of the world’s health. The report sends a stark message on the threat of noncommunicable diseases, which take an immense and increasing toll on lives, livelihoods, health systems, communities, economies and societies,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “The report calls for a substantial increase in investments in health and health systems to get back on track towards the Sustainable Development Goals.” NCDs ̶ an ever-increasing health threat for future generationsDespite overall health progress, the share of deaths caused annually by NCDs has grown consistently and is now claiming nearly three quarters of all lives lost each year. If this trend continues, NCDs are projected to account for about 86% of the 90 million annual deaths by mid-century; consequently, 77 million of these will be due to NCDs – a nearly 90% increase in absolute numbers since 2019. Stagnating progress calls for acceleration More recent trends show signs of slowdown in the annual rate of reduction (ARR) for many indicators. For example, the global maternal mortality ratio needs to decline by 11.6% per year between 2021 and 2030 to meet the SDG target. Similarly, the net reduction in TB incidence from 2015 to 2021 was only one-fifth of the way to the 2025 milestone of WHO’s End TB Strategy. Despite a reduction in exposure to many health risks – such as tobacco use, alcohol consumption, violence, unsafe water and sanitation, and child stunting – progress was inadequate and exposure to some risks such as air pollution remains high. Alarmingly, the prevalence of obesity is rising with no immediate sign of reversal. Furthermore, expanded access to essential health services has slowed compared to pre-2015 gains, coupled with no significant progress in reducing financial hardship due to health-care costs. This drastically limits our ability to achieve Universal Health Coverage by 2030. “The COVID-19 pandemic is an important reminder that progress is neither linear nor guaranteed,” warns Dr Samira Asma, WHO Assistant Director-General for Data, Analytics and Delivery for Impact.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"There is a need for urgent action to reverse the progress stalled on the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) of the WHO’s Global Health Milestones (GHS) for 2023, reporting on the Global Health Statistics of its annual report with new data on the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic and new progress on the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) of the Global Health Milestones (GHS). The report, with data up to 2022, reveals a stagnation in health progress on key health indicators compared to the gains made during the period 2000-2015. It also highlights the rising threat of non-communicable diseases (NCDs) and the stagnation of life expectancy, and calls for increased investment and capacity building. The impact of COVID-19 on mortality and health progress is assessed in the report, which analyzes the impact of the pandemic on global health, and confirms the setback in progress towards the SDGs. During 2020-2021, COVID-19 was responsible for 336.8 million lost years of life globally, equivalent to an additional 22 years of life lost per premature death, severely impacting the lives of young children in particular with disruptions. Since 2000, there has been a significant decline in child and maternal mortality, with deaths falling by more than half. Incidence of diseases like HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis (TB), and malaria have also decreased, along with an increase in the risk of death from NCDs and injuries. Overall, the pandemic has added to the setback in global health life expectancy from 67 years in 2000 to 73 years in 2019. However, the pandemic has pushed many health-related SDG indicators off track and has led to reversals in significant gains in essential health services, immunization coverage, and access to financial resources. As a result, progress in tuberculosis and malaria has been reversed, and fewer people are being screened for neglected tropical diseases (NTDs). The World Health Statistics is the WHO’s annual monitoring of the global health situation. “The report sends a strong warning about the threat of non-communicable diseases, which have a significant impact on mortality, morbidity, health systems, economies, and societies,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. The report calls for increased investment in health and health programs to get back on track towards the Sustainable Development Goals. NCDs – a persistent and growing threat for the future – account for the proportion of deaths that have increased over the years, and now account for nearly three-quarters of all deaths each year. If this trend continues, NCDs are projected to account for as much as 86% of the 90 million deaths annually by 2030; for this reason, 77 million of these will be due to NCDs – a figure close to 90% of the projected death toll from 2019. Stagnant progress reveals a reversal in the annual rate of increase (ARR) for many indicators. For example, the global maternal mortality rate needs to decline by 11.6% per year between 2021 and 2030 to meet the SDG target. Furthermore, the overall increase in TB incidence from 2015 to 2021 was only one-sixth of the target for the WHO’s End TB Strategy 2025. With increased exposure to various health risks – such as tobacco use, alcohol consumption, sugary drinks, polluted water, and misinformation – and declining investment in prevention, progress on these risks has not been sufficient, and progress on each of these risks, such as preventing pneumonia, has been particularly slow. This is exacerbated by the lack of progress in strengthening health systems to address the impact of the pandemic. Previously, gains in essential health services have been reversed due to pre-2015 challenges, along with the slow progress in increasing financial resources for health programs. This makes it more difficult to achieve the Global Health Goals for 2030. “The COVID-19 pandemic is a stark reminder that progress has not been fast enough and that there is no room for complacency,” said Dr. Samira Asma, Deputy Director-General for Data, Analytics and Global Health Impact at WHO."

translations.source: "Láti wà lójú ọ̀nà fún ètò SDG ọdún 2030, a gbọ́dọ̀ gbé ìgbésẹ̀ ìpinnu àti lápapọ̀ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ipa tó ṣe é wọ̀n ní gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè." Lára ìjábọ̀ ọdún yìí ni a ti rí fún ìgbà àkọ́kọ́ ìsọ̀rí àkànṣe lórí àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ àti ìlera, a sì ń fojú sọ́nà pé èyí máa tún ṣe pàtàkì nínú ìjábọ̀ náà láti àsìkò yìí lọ. Fún ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ yìí àti àwọn agbègbè mìíràn, dátà ní àsìkò, aṣeégbáralé tí wọ́n yà sọ́tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ ṣe pàtàkì láti tọpa ìlọsíwájú àti láti mú àyípadà bá àwọn òfin ìlera orílẹ̀-èdè èdè àti àgbáyé. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ Olóòtú: Ìjábọ̀ Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé ni àkójọpọ̀ ọdọọdún Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) fún àwọn dátà tuntun tí ó wà lórí ìlera àti àwọn atọ́ka ajẹmọ́-ìlera. Fún ìbéèrè, kàn sí [ímeèlì tí wọ́n dáàbòbò]
translations.reference: “To stay on track towards the 2030 SDG agenda, we must act decisively and collectively to deliver a measurable impact in all countries.” This year’s report includes for the first time a dedicated section on climate change and health, and we anticipate that this will be of more relevance in the report going forward. For this issue and all other areas timely, reliable and disaggregated data are critical to track progress and improve national and global health policies. Editor’s note: The World Health Statistics report is the World Health Organization’s (WHO) annual compilation of the most recent available data on health and health-related indicators. For inquiries, contact [email protected]
