translations.source: Àbájáde ìwádìí tuntun fi hàn pé àwọn ètò ìlera ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí padàbọ̀sípò lẹ́yìn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn Lẹ́yìn ọdún mẹ́ta àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, àwọn ètò ìlera ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ṣàfihàn àwọn ààmì pàtàkì àkọ́kọ́ fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò ètò ìlera, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìjábọ̀ fìdíhẹẹ́ WHO lórí "Àtúnṣe kẹrin fún ìwádìí ìsinmi àgbáyé lórí ìtẹ̀síwájú àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19: oṣù Bẹlu ọdún 2022-oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023". Ní ìbẹ̀rẹ ọdún 2023, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè jábọ̀ kíkojú àdínkù àwọn ìdíwọ́ nínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ ìlera, ṣùgbọ́n ṣàfihàn ìdí láti dókówò nínú ìpadàbọ̀sípò àti ìfaradà tó lágbára fún ọjọ́ iwájú. Láàrín àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 139 tí wọ́n fésì sí àtúnṣe kẹrin fún ìwádìí ìsinmi WHO, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè jábọ̀ ìtẹ̀síwájú àwọn ìdíwọ́ nínú bíi ìdá kan nínú mẹ́rin ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ní òdiwọn. Ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 84 níbi tí àlàyé ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ti ṣe é ṣe, ìdá àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ tó ní ìdíwọ́ dínkù ní òdiwọ̀n láti 56% ní oṣù Agẹmọ-Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2020 sí 23% ní oṣù Bẹlu ọdún 2022- oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023. Àwọn ìdíwọ́ tó ń tẹ̀síwájú jẹ́ nítorí àwọn aṣokùnfà ìbéèrè- àti apá-ìdáhùn, tó fi mọ́ àwọn ìpele kékeré wíwá-ètò ìlera ní àwọn àwùjọ bákan náà ni àìtó àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti àwọn ohun èlò ìtọ́jú mìíràn bíi àwọn ilé ìwòsàn tó ṣí sílẹ̀ tàbí àwọn ìfipamọ́ oògùn àti àwọn ohun èlò tí ó wà. "Ó jẹ́ ìròyìn ayọ̀ pé àwọn ètò ìlera ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ṣe ìdápadà àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì fún mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ṣe àfẹ́rí wọn ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà," Dr Rudi Eggers, Olùdarí fún Ìpèsè iṣẹ́ Ìlera Aláfibọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Ṣùgbọ́n a nílò láti rí i dájú pé gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tẹ̀síwájú láti dí àlàfo yìí láti ṣe àdápadà àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera, àti láti ṣàmúlò àwọn ẹ̀kọ́ tí wọ́n kọ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò ìlera tó múra sílẹ̀ tó sì ní àfaradà gidi gan-an fún ọjọ́ iwájú". Àwọn ààmì ìdánimọ̀ àkọ́kọ́ fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò Nínú ìjábọ̀ tuntun yìí, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè díẹ̀ jábọ̀ mí mọ̀ọ́mọ̀ ṣe ìgbéléwọ̀n ànfààní padà jákèjádò gbogbo àwọn ìkànnì fún ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ iṣẹ́ àti àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera àwùjọ tó ṣe pàtàkì láti ìjábọ̀ ọdún 2020-2021, tó ń ṣàfihàn ìgbésẹ̀ pàtàkì láti padà sí àwọn ìpele ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ iṣẹ́ ṣíwájú-àjàkálẹ̀-ààrùn àti ìṣiṣẹ́ ètò tó fẹjú. Ní ìparí ọdún 2022, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ni wọ́n jábọ̀ àwọn ààmì díẹ̀ fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò ìpèsè iṣẹ́, tó fi mọ́ nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún ìlera ìbálòpọ̀, ìbímọ, ìyá, ọmọtuntun, ọmọ àti ọ̀dọ́; aṣaralóore; àjẹsára; àwọn ààrùn tó máa ń ràn (tó fi mọ́ àìsàn ibà, KASA TB, àti àwọn ààrùn látara ìbálòpọ̀ mìíràn); àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì; àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn; àmójútó ọpọlọpọ, àwọn ààrùn ajẹmọ́-ọpọlọ àti ìlòkulò oògùn; ìtọ́jú fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti darúgbó; àti ìtọ́jú ìbílẹ̀ àti/tàbí ìbádọ́gba. Iye àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ń jábọ̀ ìdíwọ́ fún ètò orílẹ̀-èdè wọn dínkù láti bíi ìdajì (29 nínú 59 àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ń fèsì) sí bíi ìdámẹ́rin (18 nínú 66 àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tó ń fèsì) láàrín ọdún tí ó kọjá. Pẹ̀lú àwọn ààmì ìpadàbọ̀sípò, àwọn ìdíwọ́ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ṣì ń wáyé jákèjádò àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní gbogbo àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè àti àwọn ìpele ìpawówọlé, àti jákèjádò ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ààyè ìṣe àgbékalẹ̀ iṣẹ́ àti àwọn ààyè iṣẹ́ ìtọpinpin. Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tún kojú àlékún àṣepatì iṣẹ́ - ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìgbà níní ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún àyẹ̀wò, àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ ààrùn àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn - tí ó lè yọrí sí nǹkan tí ò dára pẹ̀lu bí àwọn ènìyàn bá ṣe ń ní ìdíwọ́ ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú lásìkò. Ìpadàbọ̀sípò àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì ṣe pàtàkì nítorí àwọn ìdíwọ́ - tó fi mọ́ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún ìpolongo ìlera, ìdènà ààrùn, àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ ààrùn, ìtọ́jú, ìtúnirọ àti ẹ̀rọ̀ - lè ṣe àkóbá tó burú púpò fún ìlera lára àwọn ènìyàn àti ìpele ẹnìkan ju àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn fúnra rẹ̀ lọ, pàápàá jùlọ láàrín àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kángun séwu. Ṣíṣe àfikún àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ COVID-19 sínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì Nínú ìgbésẹ̀ pàtàkì mìíràn fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò ètò àti ìṣípòrọpò, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti ní ìlọsíwájú nínú àfikún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ COVID-19 sínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àgbékalẹ̀ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera. Bíi 80-90% àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ni wọ́n ti ṣe àfikún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára COVID-19, àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ ààrùn àti àmójútó ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ pátápátá bákan náà ni ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ lẹ́yin COVID-19 sínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́. Síbẹ̀, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè (80% nínú 83 àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ń fèsì) jábọ̀ pé ó kéré jù ìdíwọ́ kan fún ṣíṣe ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí àwọn irinṣẹ́ COVID-19 tó ṣe pàtàkì (b.a. àyẹ̀wò fún ìdámọ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, ìtọ́jú, àjẹsára àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìdáàbòbò àdáni - PPE), pẹ̀lú Ìṣòro iye àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti àìsí ìpèsè owó tó dúró fún àwọn ìdíwọ́ tó wọ́pọ̀ júlọ. A nílò ìtẹ̀síwájú àtìlẹyìn fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò, ìfaradà àti ìmúrasílẹ̀ Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ṣàmúlò nǹkan tí wọ́n kọ́ ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, tó fi mọ́ nípa ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìdíwọ́ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun tó ń ṣe àkóbá fún àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ sínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera. Nínú èyí ni ìṣàmúlò àwọn ìlànà ìṣẹ̀gùn orí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára, ìpolongo ìtọ́jú ajẹmọ́-ilé tàbí àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú-ara-ẹni, àwọn ìlànà fún ríró wíwà níkálẹ̀ àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera lágbára, àwọn ìkápá àti àwọn ètò àtìlẹ́yìn, àwọn ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun fún rírà àti ṣíṣe ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn oògùn àti àwọn ohun èlò, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìlàna-iṣẹ́ ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ, àti àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn olùpèsè ní ẹ̀ka aládàni. Ìdá mẹ́ta nínú mẹ́rin àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ni wọ́n jábọ̀ àfikún ìpèsè owó fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò, ìfaradà àti ìmúrasílẹ̀ ètò ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣàlàyé ìdí fún àtìlẹ́yìn WHO láti kojú àwọn ìpèníjà tí ó kù ní ọ̀gangan ipò COVID-19 àti jù bẹ́ẹ̀ lọ, ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìgbà tí ó ní ṣe pẹ̀lú ìrólágbára àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera, ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìkápá àmójútó fún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera, ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò ìlera alábọ́dé -- àwọn àwòṣe ìtọ́jú tó dọ́gba, ìṣèjọba, òfin àti ètò ṣíṣe àti ṣíṣe ètò owó àti ìpèsè owó. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún àwọn olóòtú: Nínú abala kẹrin ìwádìí ìsinmi àgbáyé WHO, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 222, àwọn agbègbè àti àwọn ààyè ni wọ́n fi ìwé pè láti fèsì sí ìwádìí orí-ibùdó-ìtàkùn tó jẹ́ ojúlówó láàrín oṣù Bẹlu àti oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023. Ìwádìí kẹrin ṣe ìtẹ̀lé lórí àwọn ìwádìí ìsinmi àtẹ̀yìnwá ọdún 2020 àti 2021 WHO: Abala 1 (oṣù Èbìbí-Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2020), Abala 2 (oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ-Èrenà ọdún 2021), àti Abala 3 (oṣù Bẹlu-Ọ̀pẹ ọdún 2021) tí ó ṣàfihàn bí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà ṣe ń kóbá ìtẹ̀síwájú àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì àti bí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣe ń gbé ìgbésẹ̀.
translations.reference: New survey results show health systems starting to recover from pandemic After three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, health systems in countries have started showing the first major signs of health system recovery, according to the WHO interim report on the “Fourth round of the global pulse survey on continuity of essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: November 2022–January 2023”. By early 2023, countries reported experiencing reduced disruptions in the delivery of routine health services, but highlighted the need to invest in recovery and stronger resilience for the future. Among the 139 countries that responded to the fourth round of WHO’s pulse survey, countries reported continued disruptions in almost one-quarter of services on average. In 84 countries where trend analysis is possible, the percentage of disrupted services declined on average from 56% in July-September 2020 to 23% in November 2022- January 2023. Persisting disruptions are due to both demand- and supply-side factors, including low levels of health care-seeking in communities as well as limited availability of health workers and other health-care resources such as open clinics or available stocks of medicines and products. “It is welcome news that health systems in the majority of countries are starting to restore essential health services for millions of people who missed them during the pandemic,” said Dr Rudi Eggers, WHO Director for Integrated Health Services. “But we need to ensure that all countries continue to close this gap to recover health services, and apply lessons learnt to build more prepared and resilient health systems for the future”. First notable signs of recovery In this new survey, fewer countries reported intentionally scaling back access across all service delivery platforms and essential public health functions since 2020-2021 reporting, showing an important step to return to pre-pandemic levels of service delivery and broader system functioning. By the end of 2022, most countries reported partial signs of service recovery, including in services for sexual, reproductive, maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health; nutrition; immunization; communicable diseases (including malaria, HIV, TB, and other sexually-transmitted infections); neglected tropical diseases; noncommunicable diseases; management of mental, neurological and substance use disorders; care for older people; and traditional and/or complementary care. The number of countries reporting disruption to their national supply chain system reduced from nearly half (29 of 59 responding countries) to about a quarter (18 of 66 responding countries) within the last year. Despite signs of recovery, service disruptions persist across countries in all regions and income levels, and across most service delivery settings and tracer service areas. Countries are also dealing with increasing service backlogs – most frequently in services for screening, diagnosis and treatment of noncommunicable diseases – which can lead to negative consequences as people are delayed access to timely care. Recovering essential health service delivery is critical because disruptions – including to services for health promotion, disease prevention, diagnosis, treatment, rehabilitation and palliation – may have even greater adverse health effects at population and individual level than the pandemic itself, especially among vulnerable populations. Integrating COVID-19 services into essential health services In another important step towards system recovery and transition, most countries have made progress in integrating COVID-19 services into routine health service delivery. About 80-90% of countries have fully integrated COVID-19 vaccination, diagnostic and case management services as well as services for post COVID-19 condition into routine service delivery. Still, most countries (80% of 83 responding countries) reported at least one bottleneck to scaling up access to essential COVID-19 tools (e.g. COVID-19 diagnostics, therapeutics, vaccines and personal protective equipment - PPE), with health workforce issues and lack of funding representing the most common barriers. Further support needed for recovery, resilience and preparedness Most countries have started to apply what they have learnt during the COVID-19 pandemic, including through the institutionalization of a number of innovative service disruption mitigation strategies into routine health service delivery. These include deployment of telemedicine approaches, promotion of home-based care or self-care interventions, approaches for strengthening health workers availability, capacities and support mechanisms, innovations in procuring and delivering medicines and supplies, more routine community communications, and partnerships with private sector providers. Three quarters of countries reported additional funding allocation towards longer term system recovery, resilience and preparedness. Countries expressed need for WHO support to address remaining challenges in the COVID-19 context and beyond, most frequently related to health workforce strengthening, building the monitoring capacities of health services, designing primary health care -- oriented models of care, governance, policy and planning and financial planning and funding. Note to editors: In the fourth round of WHO’s global pulse survey, 222 countries, territories and areas were invited to respond to a standardized web-based survey between November 2022 and January 2023. The fourth survey followed up on WHO’s previous 2020 and 2021 pulse surveys: Round 1 (May-September 2020), Round 2 (January-March 2021), and Round 3 (November-December 2021) which showed the extent to which the pandemic was affecting the continuity of essential health services and how countries are taking action.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**New Report Shows Health Programs Beginning to Recover After COVID-19 Pandemic**

A new report indicates that health programs have begun to recover following the COVID-19 pandemic. After three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, health programs in countries have started to restore essential services for program recovery, as detailed in the WHO's "Fourth Update on Global Health Security Progress on Sustaining Essential Health Services During the COVID-19 Pandemic: March 2022-March 2023." In early 2023, countries reported increased resource allocation within health sector plans, but also identified challenges in sustaining recovery and the resilience needed for the future.

Among the 139 countries that responded to the fourth WHO health security update, countries reported progress in resource allocation within approximately one in four essential health services. In 84 countries where data is available, the proportion of essential health services with increased resources decreased from 56% in August 2020 to 23% in March-April 2023. Progress is hampered by supply chain issues and disruptions, which have impacted the availability of essential health services in communities due to shortages of health workers and other essential supplies like medical facilities, medicine, and equipment.

"It is encouraging that health programs in many countries have begun to restore essential health services for millions of people who were affected during the pandemic," said Dr. Rudi Eggers, Director for Health Security at the WHO. "But we need to ensure that all countries continue to prioritize this effort to restore essential health services and to learn lessons to build more resilient health systems that are truly sustainable for the future."

**Key Indicators for Recovery**

In this new report, countries also reported a decline in the overall implementation of all activities for the delivery of essential health services and community health services that were highlighted in the 2020-2021 reports, which showed significant backsliding from pre-pandemic service delivery levels and program performance. By the end of 2022, many countries reported some setbacks in essential service recovery, including in services for maternal and newborn health, immunization, nutrition, children and adolescents, non-communicable diseases (including diabetes, TB, and other chronic diseases), neglected tropical diseases, emergency care, and access to essential medicines and vaccines; and community and/or outreach services.

The proportion of countries reporting increased resources for their national programs ranged from slightly more than half (29 of 59 countries that responded) to slightly less than two-thirds (18 of 66 countries that responded) over the past year. Alongside service delivery, progress in health workforce investments continued across all regions and levels of income, and across most areas of health sector planning and service delivery. Countries also reported challenges with service integration – often in areas of testing, diagnosis of diseases, and treatment of communicable diseases – that could be linked to limited access to time-sensitive care.

Essential health service recovery is critical because disruptions – including in services for health promotion, disease prevention, diagnosis, treatment, rehabilitation, and care – can have devastating consequences for health outcomes and the population as a whole, even more so than the pandemic itself, especially among vulnerable populations.

**Integrating COVID-19 Services into Essential Health Services**

In another significant step towards program recovery and resilience, many countries have advanced in integrating COVID-19 services into essential health service delivery plans. Approximately 80-90% of countries have incorporated COVID-19 vaccination, testing, and case management into essential health service plans, including post-COVID-19 condition management. However, many countries (80% of the 83 respondents) reported limited resources for implementing essential COVID-19 services (e.g., COVID-19 testing, treatment, vaccination, and PPE), partly due to workforce shortages and insufficient funding for these resources.

**Continued Investment in Recovery, Resilience, and Sustainability is Needed**

Many countries have begun to learn lessons from the COVID-19 pandemic, including the importance of integrating innovative digital health solutions into essential health service delivery. This includes the use of telemedicine platforms, remote patient monitoring, or digital diagnostic tools, strategies for strengthening the capacity of health workers, training and support systems, and digital and support technologies, innovative approaches for rapid procurement and distribution of medicines and supplies, integrated health information systems, and partnerships with the private sector. Approximately three in four countries reported increased funding for program recovery, resilience, and sustainability. Countries explained the need for WHO support to address the gaps in COVID-19 response and beyond, often related to health workforce capacity, implementation of supportive supervision for essential health services, implementation of robust health systems – including referral networks, governance, and financial and human resource management.

**For the Record:**

In the fourth WHO global health security update, 222 countries, areas, and territories responded to the priority health security assessment between March and April 2023. The fourth update follows the WHO’s 2020 and 2021 annual health security progress reports: Update 1 (June 2020), Update 2 (September 2021), and Update 3 (March 2021) which highlighted how the pandemic impacted progress on essential health services and how countries were addressing the backsliding.

translations.source: Nígbà tí ìwádìí ìsinmi ní àwọn kùdìẹ̀-kudiẹ bíi ìjábọ̀ aṣègbè àti ṣíṣe aṣojú, agbára ìgbìyànjú yìí ni wí pé ó kún fọ́fọ́ ó sì ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìròyìn kíákíá. Ọ̀rọ̀ "orílẹ̀-èdè" ni a gbọ́dọ̀ ní òye rẹ̀ pé ó ní àfikún gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè, àwọn agbègbè àti àwọn ààyè. Wọ́n parí àlàyé ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ náà pẹ̀lú ìdáhùn láti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 84, àwọn agbègbè àti ààyè tí wọ́n dáhùn sí ó kéré jù abala ìwádìí kan nínú gbogbo àwọn àṣetẹ̀léra ìwádìí ìsinmi mẹ́rin náà.
translations.reference: While pulse surveys have limitations such as reporting bias and representativeness, the strength of this effort is that it is comprehensive and delivers information rapidly. The term “country” should be understood to include all countries, territories and areas. Trend analyses was completed based on responses from 84 countries, territories, or areas that responded to at least one survey section in all four pulse survey rounds.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"While the summary of news focuses on local reports and official statements, this intelligence power states that it is constantly monitoring and gathering news. The term "country" should be understood to encompass all countries, regions, and cultures. They described the incident with information from 84 countries, regions, and cultures, with the information received from them being less than one of the four major news agencies."

**Explanation of some choices:**

*   **"Intelligence power"** -  "Agbára ìgbìyànjú" is a bit nuanced. It implies a powerful entity involved in gathering and analyzing information, so "intelligence power" seemed fitting.
*   **"Constantly monitoring and gathering news"** - This captures the sense of "ó kún fọ́fọ́ ó sì ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìròyìn kíákíá" which suggests continuous and extensive news collection.
*   **"Major news agencies"** - "Àṣetẹ̀léra ìwádìí ìsinmi" refers to news agencies, and "mẹ́rin" means four, so "four major news agencies" is a reasonable interpretation.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Àlàyé ti ayẹyẹ fún àjọ̀dún 50th HRP: Sweden àti United Kingdom of Great Britain àti Northern Ireland Láti ṣàlàyé ìdùnnú àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Ìgbìmọ̀ fún Òfin àti Ìdarí (PCC) tí wọ́n ṣe ayẹyẹ àjọ̀dún 50th HRP àti àwọn àṣeyọrí rẹ̀ lóríṣiríṣi, Sweden àti United Kingdom of Great Britain àti Northern Ireland ka ìwé ìfi ẹ̀mí ìmoore hàn àti ìkíni kú orí-ire níbi ìpàdé 36th HRP PPC. Ìran wa ti rí ìlọsíwájú tó pọ̀ nínú ìmọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ ju gbogbo àwọn ìran tí wọ́n ti gbawájú lápapọ̀ lọ a sì ti rí oríṣiríṣi àyípadà ọgbọ́n, ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ, ìṣègùn àti àwùjọ tí àyípadà àwọn ohun adèna oyún léwájú rẹ̀ tí àwọn ìlọsíwájú nínú ìlera ìbímọ, àwọn ìlera ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun náà sì tẹ̀lé e bákan náà ni àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ àti ìdọ́gba takọ-tabo. Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, àwọn ìpèníjà tó pọ̀ ní ẹ̀ka SRHR ń tẹ̀síwájú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni, ó ṣe é ṣe, àwọn ìlọsíwájú kọ̀ọ̀kan tún wà nínú ìbéèrè lónì. A gbóríyìn fún àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ HRP, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ àti àwọn alátílẹyìn lórí àìmọye àṣeyọrí títí di òní a sì mọ rírì àwọn ìgbéṣẹ̀ ńlá tí wọ́n ti gbé ní àkókò ìdajì ọgọ́run ọdún nínú ìpèsè ẹ̀rí tó ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn kíkún àti ànfààní tó dọ́gba sí ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìbímọ àti àwọn ẹ̀tọ́. Yóò wùn wá láti ṣàlàyé àwọn ìsọ̀rí àṣeyọrí pàtàkì mẹ́ta: Akọ́kọ́, ìwádìí ojúlówó ńlá tí HRP ṣe ní ipa pàtàkì lórí òfin ìlera àwujọ, àwọn òdiwọ̀n, àti ìfikọ́ra jákèjádò àgbáyé tó ń kojú àwọn ìdí fún oríṣiríṣi àwọn ènìyàn tó ń yí padà. Ẹ̀kejì, HRP, ní pàtàkì jùlọ nípasẹ ìṣọ̀kan HRP, ti ṣe ìrólágbára fún àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ ìwádìí lábẹ́lé àti káàkiri àgbáyé, ó sì ti, jẹ́ kí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn oníwádìí ní àwọn ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe ìwádìí tí ó ń rí i dájú pé ìwádìí SRHR ojúlówó-ńlá lè tẹ̀síwájú, àti pẹ̀lu bẹ́ẹ̀ náà lékún, lọ sí ọjọ́ iwájú. Ẹ̀kẹ́ta, ní ilé ayé tí ìdààmú, rògbòdìyàn àti àlékún àtakò fún ìdọ́gba takọ-tabo ń dojúkọ, HRP ti ṣe ìgbédìde àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ pàtàkì fún àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ọmọbìnrin àti àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kángun séwu. HRP ṣì dúró gẹ́gẹ́ bíi irinṣẹ́ pàtó láàrín ètò Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé fún ìwádìí nínú ìbímọ ènìyàn ó sì ń tẹ̀síwájú láti jẹ́ olórí aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ nínú ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìbímọ àgbáyé. HRP àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ ti mú wa súnmọ́ ìlérí Àpérò Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè ti ọdún 1994 lórí Iye àwọn Ènìyàn àti Ìdàgbàsókè àti ìpe rẹ̀ láti fi àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ àwọn ènìyàn, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ọmọbìnrin, sí àárín gbùngbùn ìdagbàsókè alálòpẹ́. A fẹ́ fi ẹ̀mí ìmoore wa hàn àti ìdúpẹ́ fún ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba HRP, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀, àwọn onígbọ̀wọ́-àjọṣe rẹ̀ àti ojú òpó àgbáyé ti àwọn ààyè àjọṣepọ̀ àti àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ tí wọ́n ń ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti rí i dájú pé àwọn ìgbìyànjú rẹ̀ yọrí sí mímú àyípadà bá àwọn obìnrin, ọkùnrin, ọmọdé, ọ̀dọ́ àti àwọn oríṣiríṣi àwùjọ tí HRP ní àfọkànsìn láti ṣiṣẹ́ fún. Ní orúkọ àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ PCC, ẹ jẹ́ kí á tún ṣe ìfirinlẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ wa pẹ̀lu yín láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn àṣeyọrí HRP, lórí èyí tó ṣe wí pé àṣeyọrí Èròngbà ọdún 2030 gbára lé.
translations.reference: Statement of celebration for HRP’s 50th anniversary: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland To capture the spirit of Policy and Coordination Committee (PCC) members celebrating HRP’s 50th anniversary and its numerous achievements, Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland read the following note of appreciation and congratulations at the 36th meeting of the HRP PCC. Our generation has seen more progress in science and technology than all preceding generations together and witnessed several intellectual, technological, medical and social transformation spearheaded by the contraceptive revolution and followed by advances in reproductive health, health innovations as well as rights and gender equality. However, considerable challenges in the field of SRHR continue, and, arguably, some advances are in question again today. We applaud HRP-staff, partners and supporters on the numerous achievements to date and recognise the enormous strides accomplished during half a century in producing the evidence that supports full and equal access to sexual and reproductive health and rights. We would like to outline three major categories of achievements: Firstly, the high-quality research by HRP has had tangible impact on public health policy, standards, and practice worldwide addressing the evolving needs of diverse populations. Secondly, HRP, notably through the HRP Alliance, has strengthened research institutions locally and globally, and, enabled numerous researchers to acquire the research skills that ensure that high-quality SRHR research can continue, and indeed increase, into the future. Thirdly, in a world beset by crisis, conflict and increasing resistance to gender equality, HRP has facilitated the upholding of the basic rights of women and girls and vulnerable populations. HRP remains the main instrument within the United Nations system for research in human reproduction and continues to be an exceptional leader in global sexual and reproductive health. HRP and its partners have brought us closer to the promise of the 1994 International Conference on Population and Development and its call to put the rights of individuals, especially women and girls, at the heart of sustainable development. We want to express our appreciation and gratitude to the HRP secretariat, its partners, co-sponsors and the global network of collaborating centres and experts helping to ensure its efforts contribute to making a difference to the women, men, children, adolescents and various communities HRP is committed to serve. On behalf of PCC members, allow us to reaffirm our partnership with you to support the achievements of HRP, upon which the success of the 2030 Agenda depends.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Celebration of the 50th Anniversary of the HRP: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland**

To celebrate the 50th anniversary of the Human Rights Programme (HRP) and its various achievements, Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland presented a commemorative publication and a message of congratulations at the 36th HRP Programme Coordination Committee (PCC) meeting. We have witnessed significant progress in science and technology exceeding all previous collective efforts and have seen a variety of innovations in technology, science, support, and social welfare that have advanced the lives of vulnerable populations, with advancements in maternal health and reproductive health following closely, along with related rights and guarantees. However, significant challenges remain in the SRHR (Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights) sector, and, indeed, each progress area is still a matter of concern today.

We commend the HRP staff, partners, and supporters for the successes achieved to date and acknowledge the significant shortcomings that have been identified over the past eight decades in the health sector that supports comprehensive and sustainable reproductive health and rights. It will be important to discuss three key areas of success. First, HRP has played a crucial role in social welfare policies, advocacy, and global efforts to combat harmful practices affecting various populations. Second, through the HRP coalition, HRP has strengthened the capacity of welfare institutions both within and outside the country, ensuring that many welfare practitioners recognize that comprehensive SRHR can continue and, consequently, move forward. Third, in a world where marginalization, discrimination, and violence threaten equal rights, HRP has championed fundamental rights for women and girls and vulnerable people.

HRP remains a vital and specific pillar within the United Nations Framework for Action on Reproductive Health and Rights and continues to lead in global reproductive health and rights. HRP and its partners have brought us closer to the spirit of the 1994 International Conference on Population and Development (ICPD) in Cairo and its call to prioritize human rights, especially those of women and girls. We extend our heartfelt gratitude to the HRP government agency, its partners, its donors, and the global network of partners and experts who are working to ensure that its mandates benefit women, men, children, youth, and the diverse communities that HRP is committed to serving.

On behalf of the PCC members, we renew our partnership with you to support the successes of HRP, especially in achieving the 2030 Agenda.

translations.source: WHO ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ tuntun láti mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn Láti ran àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lọ́wọ́ láti múra sílẹ̀ dáadáa fún àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn lọ́jọ́ iwájú, WHO ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ tuntun lónì tí yóò pèsè ìtọ́nisọ́nà lórí ṣíṣe ètò aláfibọ̀ fún ìdáhùn sí èyíkéyìí afàìsàn èémí bíi ọ̀fìnkì tàbí kòkòrò ààrùn kòrónà. Ìmúrasílẹ̀ tuntun náà àti Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ìfaradà fún Àwọn Ewu Tó Ń Jẹyọ tàbí PRET, ṣe àmúlò àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ìlànà ìgbàlódé fún ẹ̀kọ́ àjọkọ́ àti iṣẹ́ àjùmọ̀ṣe tí wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 àti àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera àwùjọ mìíràn láìpẹ́ yìí. Nípasẹ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ náà, WHO máa lo àkóràn kan láti tọ́ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè sọ́na nínú ètò ṣíṣe fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn, pẹ̀lú bó ṣe jẹ́ wí pé ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìkápá àti ìkáátò ni ó wọ́pọ̀ láàrín àwọn ìpín afàìsàn. PRET dáhùn ìpè fún ìtọ́nisọ́nà onímọ̀ ẹ̀rọ àti àtìlẹ́yìn fún ṣíṣe ìpolongo àti ìrólágbára ìmúrasílẹ̀ aláfibọ̀ àti ìdáhùn, gẹ́gẹ́ bí wọ́n ṣe là á kalẹ̀ nínú àwọn ìfẹnukò Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 àti àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera mìíràn ti fi hàn pé àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè nílò láti ní ìgbaradì fún iṣẹ́ láti dáhùn sí àwọn ewu ààrùn àkóràn, pẹ̀lú àwọn ètò ìmúrasílẹ̀ àkànṣe lọ́wọ́ wọn àti ìdarí àti àjọṣepọ̀ tó dára pẹ̀lú àwọn ẹ̀ka mìíràn bíi iṣẹ́ àgbẹ̀. "Àwọn iṣẹ́ ìmúrasílẹ̀, ìdènà, àti ìdáhùn ò gbọdọ̀ jẹ́ agbègbè ẹ̀ka ètò ìlera nìkan," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera ṣe ní ipa káàkiri ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ẹ̀ka, bẹ́ẹ̀ náà ni àwọn ìgbìyànjú ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti ìdáhùn lọ káàkiri àwọn ẹ̀ka, àwọn iṣẹ́ àti àwọn afàìsàn. Ó ṣe pàtàkì, bákan náà, pé kí ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ àti ìdọ́gba jẹ́ àfojúsùn àwọn ìgbìyànjú wa, pàápàá jùlọ fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n dẹ́yẹ sí tí wọ́n sì kángun séwu jùlọ." PRET jẹ́ àyípadà kan nínú ìgbésẹ̀ WHO fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn nípasẹ ìṣàmúlò lẹ́nsì ìlànà àkóràn, yàtọ̀ sí gbígbájú mọ́ àwọn ààrùn kan pàtó. WHO máa tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àti àgbéjáde ìtọ́nisọ́nà lórí àwọn ààrùn kọ̀ọ̀kan bó ṣe yẹ. Ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ náà máa wáyé ní ọjọ́ tó gbẹ́yìn nínú ìpàdé náà pẹ̀lú 120 lé àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ṣojú Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀, àti Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba WHO, láti wá ojú ọ̀nà fún ìtẹ̀síwájú fún PRET, tó fi mọ́ àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ amúṣẹ́ṣe àgbáyé fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn afàìsàn èémí. Ẹ̀kọ́ àkọ́kọ́ Ìpilèṣẹ PRET gbájúmọ́ àwọn afàìsàn èémí, tó fi mọ́ ọ̀fìnkì, kòkòrò ààrùn kòrónà, àti àwọn kòkòrò ajẹmọ́-hóró èémí. Pẹ̀lú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ àti ewu ọ̀fìnkì látara ẹyẹ tó ṣe é ṣe, ẹ̀kọ́ yìí máa jẹ́ kí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lè ṣe àtúnyẹ́wò, àyẹ̀wò, àti ìsọdituntun àwọn ìgbìyànjú ètò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn èémí wọn gidi gan-an láti rí i dájú pé wọ́n ní ìkápá àti ìkáátò tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ nílẹ̀. Ìlànà kan ń lọ lọ́wọ́ láti ṣe ìdánimọ̀ àwọn ìpín afàìsàn tí ó kàn, bíi àwọn ẹ̀fọn, tí wọn yóò kojú lábẹ́ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ yìí. Èyí máa tẹ̀lé àwọn kàn-án-pá tí wọ́n ṣe ìdánimọ̀ wọn nípasẹ àwọn àbá mẹ́wàá láti ró àgbékalẹ̀ àgbáyé fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ pàjáwírì ìlera, ìdáhùn, àti ìfaradà (HEPR) lágbára. Ìpilèṣẹ PRET náà ṣe àfihàn ìgbà ọ̀tun fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ó sì dúró fún àyípadà nínú àwọn ojúlówó iṣẹ́ WHO láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti ró ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún I, ìdènà, àti ìkápá àti ìkáátò ìdáhùn wọn lágbára. Ìpilèṣẹ̀ náà tún lè ṣiṣẹ́ láti sọ àwọn èròngbà àti ìpèsè Ìsọ̀kan Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn di ṣíṣe, tí àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ ń jíròrò lé lórí lọ́wọ́. Lẹ́yìn àwọn àfikún àti ìjírórò láàrín àwọn akópa nínú ìpàdé náà, WHO pe àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ sí ìpè láti ṣiṣẹ́ gbogbo àgbáyé yìí láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn afàìsàn èémí. Ìpè láti Ṣiṣẹ́ WHO, Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́, àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe ìdánimọ̀ àti láti ṣàmúlò àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ kàn-án-pá láti ró ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àwọn ewu ààrùn àkóràn tó ń jẹyọ ní orílẹ̀-èdè, ẹkùn agbègbè, àti àgbáyé lágbára. A mọ rírì ipa àwọn àwùjọ àti gbogbo àwọn ẹ̀ka, àti ìdí fún ìtẹ̀síwájú ní ìṣọ̀kan àti alálòpẹ́ láti fi òpin sí ìṣẹ̀lẹ ẹ̀rù àti ìdẹ́yẹsí gbogbo ìgbà tí ó wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ nínú ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn. A rántí àwọn ẹ̀kọ́ láti inú àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé àtẹ̀yìnwá, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn tí afàìsàn èémí ń fà. Àwọn afàìsàn wọ̀nyìí ti wọn ó sì tẹ̀síwájú láti jẹ́ ewu àgbáyé tó lápẹẹrẹ pẹ̀lú agbára láti fa ààrùn tó pọ̀ àti ikú, pọ̀jù fún àwọn ètò ìlera, da ọrọ̀ ajé àgbáyé rú, kí ó sì mú kí àìṣedéédé ó burú síi, èyí tí ó wà nínú ànfààní sí àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti dènà àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti ètò ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Èyí jẹ́ Ìpè láti Ṣiṣẹ́ láti mú kí ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àwọn ewu tó ń jẹyọ jákèjádò àgbáyé yá.
translations.reference: WHO launches new initiative to improve pandemic preparedness To help countries better prepare for future pandemics, WHO launched a new initiative today that provides guidance on integrated planning for responding to any respiratory pathogen such as influenza or coronaviruses. The new Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative, or PRET, incorporates the latest tools and approaches for shared learning and collective action established during the COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health emergencies. Through the initiative, WHO will use a mode of transmission approach to guide countries in pandemic planning, given that many capacities and capabilities are common among groups of pathogens. PRET answers the call for technical guidance and support for promoting and strengthening integrated preparedness and response, as outlined in World Health Assembly resolutions. The COVID-19 pandemic and other health emergencies have shown that countries need to be operationally ready to respond to infectious disease threats, with tailored preparedness plans in hand and better coordination and collaboration with other sectors such as agriculture. “Preparedness, prevention, and response activities must not be the province of the health sector alone,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Just as health emergencies have impacts across many sectors, so must our preparedness and response efforts span sectors, disciplines and pathogens. It is critical, too, that community engagement and equity are the centre of our efforts, especially for those populations that are marginalized and most at risk.” PRET is an evolution in WHO’s approach to pandemic preparedness through the application of a mode of transmission lens, rather than a focus on specific diseases. WHO will continue to develop and disseminate guidance on specific diseases as needed. The launch of the initiative is taking place on the last day of meetings with over 120 individuals representing Member States, partners, and the WHO secretariat, to define the path forward for PRET, including the development of a global implementation roadmap for respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. The PRET Initiative’s first module focuses on respiratory pathogens, including influenza, coronaviruses, and respiratory syncytial virus. Given the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and the possible threat of avian influenza, this module will enable countries to critically review, test, and update their respiratory pandemic planning efforts to ensure they have the functional capacities and capabilities in place. A process is underway to identify the next group of pathogens, such as arboviruses, to be addressed under this initiative. This will follow priorities identified through the ten proposals to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience (HEPR). The PRET Initiative ushers in a new era for pandemic preparedness and represents an evolution of WHO’s core activities to support all Member States in strengthening health emergency preparedness, prevention, and response capacities and capabilities. The initiative can also serve to operationalize the objectives and provisions of the Pandemic Accord, which is currently being negotiated by WHO Member States. Following inputs and discussions among meeting participants, WHO invites stakeholders to this global call to action to advance respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. Call to Action WHO, Member States, and partners are working together to identify and implement priority actions to strengthen national, regional, and global preparedness for pandemics and emerging infectious disease threats. We recognize the role of communities and all sectors, and the need for harmonized and sustained momentum to end the cycle of panic and neglect that is all too common in pandemic preparedness and response. We recall lessons from previous epidemics and pandemics, especially those caused by respiratory pathogens. These pathogens have and will continue to pose a significant global threat with the potential to cause tremendous morbidity and mortality, overwhelm health systems, de-stabilize the global economy, and exacerbate inequities, which exist in the access to both the tools to prevent pandemics and health care for all people. This is a Call to Action to accelerate preparedness for pandemics and emerging threats globally.
