Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**WHO Launches New Initiatives to Strengthen Surveillance for Disease Outbreaks to Support Countries in Preparedness for Future Disease Outbreaks**

The World Health Organization (WHO) has launched new initiatives to strengthen disease surveillance and preparedness for future outbreaks. This includes a new framework for evidence-based decision-making regarding the severity of health emergencies, such as whether to declare a public health emergency of international concern. The new surveillance and Preparedness Response to Emerging Threats (PRET) framework will guide actions and strategies for pandemic preparedness and response, as well as other public health emergencies that have occurred recently. Through this initiative, WHO will use a structured approach to support countries in implementing disease outbreak response programs, even though many health systems face significant challenges and gaps.

PRET provides a framework for evidence-based decision-making for technology and digital solutions to strengthen surveillance and preparedness, as they are implemented within the Global Health Security Agenda. The COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health emergencies have demonstrated the need for countries to have the capacity to respond to infectious disease threats, with robust surveillance systems and effective leadership and coordination with other sectors, such as agriculture.

"Surveillance, detection, and preparedness should not be limited to the health sector alone," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. "Because health emergencies have a far-reaching impact on many sectors, surveillance and preparedness efforts must also extend across sectors, services, and populations. It is essential that community engagement and trust are fundamental to our efforts, especially for vulnerable populations who are most at risk."

PRET is part of WHO’s broader effort to strengthen disease outbreak surveillance through a structured approach, rather than focusing on specific diseases. WHO will continue to develop and refine evidence on each disease as needed. The launch of the initiative will take place during a meeting with 120 Member States, partners, and WHO technical experts to chart a path forward for PRET, which will inform the development of a global action plan for preparedness for health emergencies. The first phase of the PRET initiative focuses on these diseases, including smallpox, measles, and rubella. With the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and the threat of new variants, this training will enable countries to thoroughly review, assess, and refine their disease outbreak response programs to ensure they have effective prevention and control measures in place.

A process is underway to identify high-risk populations, such as refugees, who will be prioritized under this initiative. This will support the strengthening of global capacity for health emergency preparedness, detection, and response (HEPR). The PRET initiative provides a critical opportunity to strengthen disease outbreak surveillance and serves as a catalyst for WHO’s work to support Member States in strengthening their health emergency prevention, detection, and control capabilities. The initiative can also address gaps and challenges in the International Health Regulations. Following discussions and feedback during the meeting, WHO called on all stakeholders to work together globally to accelerate progress in strengthening preparedness for health emergencies.

**Call to Action**

WHO, Member States, and partners are working together to identify and prioritize steps to strengthen disease outbreak preparedness and response to emerging health threats at the country, regional, and global levels. We recognize the importance of communities and all sectors and the need for collaboration and solidarity to address the most pressing challenges in disease surveillance and preparedness. We draw lessons from past disease outbreaks and the recent global pandemic, especially those that have disproportionately affected vulnerable populations. These diseases continue to pose a significant global threat with the potential to cause widespread illness and death, disrupt economies, and exacerbate inequalities, hindering efforts to control disease outbreaks and strengthen health systems for all. This Call to Action aims to make disease outbreak preparedness and response a global priority.

translations.source: Ìmúrasílẹ̀ tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ gbára lé ètò ṣíṣe tó tóbi àti ìgbésẹ̀ tó ní àmójútó. Ìfọkànsìn àjọni lórí àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ wọ̀nyìí máa jẹ́ kí á ní ìlọsíwájú tí yóò bá fi di oṣù Ọ̀pẹ ọdún 2025 gẹ́gẹ́ bí wọn ó ṣe ṣàpèjúwe rẹ̀ nínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àmójútó (Ìgbaradì àti Ìfaradà fún Àwọn Ewu Tó ń Jẹyọ (PRET): Ṣe ìsọdituntun àwọn ètò ìmúrasílẹ̀ tí ó fọwọ́ sí àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ kàn-án-pá tí wọ́n sì ti gbèro kíkọ́ ẹ̀kọ́ látara àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àtẹ̀yìnwá. Mímọ àwọn ewu tí àwọn afàìsàn èémí lè fà, ṣíṣe ètò fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn afàìsàn èémí pẹ̀lú àwọn kókó ọ̀rọ̀ tí wọ́n di ṣe ìdánimọ̀ wọn nínú Ẹ̀kọ́ PRET #1: Ṣíṣe ètò fún Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Afàìsàn Èémí jẹ́ kàn-án-pá Àlékún nínú àjọṣepọ̀ láàrín àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ nínú ṣíṣe ètò ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn nípasẹ ìdarí àti ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ tó létò. Lára èyí ni ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò adọ́gba; ṣíṣe àwọn iṣẹ́ àjọṣe; àti pínpín ìròyìn lórí àwọn ìṣesí tó dára, àwọn ìpèníjà, àti àwọn ànfààní. Ṣe ìyàsọ́tọ̀ àwọn ìdókówò, ìpèsè owó àti àmójútó fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn pẹ̀lú àfojúsùn pàtó lórí kíkojú àwọn àlàfo tí wọ́n rí ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé tó kọjá. Ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 náà ti ṣàpèjúwe nǹkan tí ó ṣe é ṣe pẹ̀lú ìfọkànsìn òṣèlú, ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ àti ìpèsè owó. Ní àárín gbùngbùn iṣẹ́ yìí ni láti rí i dájú pé ìdọ́gba ti wà nílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn tó ń bọ̀ lápapọ̀. A nílò ìgbésẹ̀-gbogbo-àwùjọ láti jẹ́ kí ìlọsíwájú tí wọ́n là kalẹ̀ nínú Ìpè láti Ṣiṣẹ́ yìí di ṣíṣe. Nítorí náà ṣíṣe àmúlò gbọ́dọ̀ ró ìfaradà àwọn àwùjọ lágbára; ṣe ìtẹ̀síwájú, àlòpẹ́, kí ó sì gùn lé àwọn ètò ìlànà-iṣẹ́; kí ó sì ṣàmúlò àwọn ìkápá tó gbòòrò fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti ìdáhùn sí pàjáwírì. Ìpè láti Ṣiṣẹ́ yìí ni wọ́n gbé jáde níbi Ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti Ìfaradà fún Àwọn Ewu Tó ń Jẹyọ (PRET): Ìpàdé Àgbáyé fún Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Afàìsàn Èémí Ọjọ́ Iwájú tó wáyé ní 24-26 oṣù Igbe ọdún 2023 ní Geneva, Switzerland.
translations.reference: Effective preparedness relies on robust planning and coordinated action. A collective commitment on the following actions will see progress achieved by December 2025 as will be described in the (Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET) monitoring framework: Update preparedness plans that affirm priority actions and that have considered learnings from past events. Recognizing the risk posed by respiratory pathogens, planning for a respiratory pathogen pandemic based on the themes identified in the PRET Module #1: Planning for Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics is a priority Increase connectivity among stakeholders in pandemic preparedness planning through systematic coordination and cooperation. This includes building equitable systems; conducting joint exercises; and sharing information on good practices, challenges, and opportunities. Dedicate sustained investments, financing and monitoring of pandemic preparedness with a particular focus on addressing the gaps identified during past pandemics and epidemics. The COVID-19 pandemic response has demonstrated what can be achieved with political commitment, community engagement and funding. At the heart of this work is to ensure equity to be ready for the next pandemic together. Whole-of-society action is needed to make the progress outlined in this Call to Action. Implementation should therefore strengthen the resilience of communities; maintain, sustain, and build on routine systems; and leverage broader capacities for emergency preparedness and response. This Call to Action was released at the Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET): Global Meeting for Future Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics held on 24-26 April 2023 in Geneva, Switzerland.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The health sector is working hard on a large program and a plan with a roadmap. Daily focus on these steps should lead to progress by October 2025, as described in the roadmap (Preparedness and Response for Emerging Threats (PRET): Addressing the adaptation of health programs to the second-order effects of pandemics and the expansion of educational opportunities from pandemic events. Considering the risks these events can cause, the program involves preparing for pandemic-related illnesses with key messages to identify them in PRET Education #1: Preparing for Pandemic-Related Illnesses is a priority in the partnership among health professionals in implementing health programs for pandemic prevention through leadership and collaboration. This includes establishing sub-programs; implementing project activities; and disseminating information on successful interventions, challenges, and lessons learned. Addressing vulnerabilities, financial resources, and the roadmap for health preparedness with specific focus on the termination of misinformation during a pandemic and global pandemics that have occurred. The experience of the COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the importance of political commitment, community engagement, and financial resources. The core of this work is to ensure that a foundation exists for future pandemics. A whole-of-society approach is needed to ensure that the progress made in this Plan of Action becomes a reality. Therefore, the program must prioritize community engagement; accelerate, scale up, and strengthen program strategies; and address the challenges for health preparedness and response to emergencies. This Plan of Action was launched at the Preparedness and Response for Emerging Threats (PRET): Global Meeting on Pandemic-Related Illnesses, Future Perspectives, held in Geneva, Switzerland, on 24-26 November 2023."

translations.source: WHO pè fún àlékún ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn irinṣẹ́ tí wọ́n dábàá láti kojú àìsàn ibà Láti sààmì Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Àìsàn Ibà Lágbàyé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) ń fi ìpè síta fún àlékún ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn ìdásí tuntun àti àwọn tó ti wà tẹ́lẹ̀ láti dóólà àwọn ẹ̀mí kúrò lọ́wọ́ àìsàn ibà. Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ tó 1.5 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ńlá fún àìlera àti ikú látara àìsàn ibà ní orílẹ̀-èdè Ghana, kenya àti Malawi ni wọ́n ti gba oògùn àkọ́kọ́ fún àjẹsára àìsàn ibà àkọ́kọ́ báyìí, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), ọpẹ́ lọ́wọ́ ètò alábẹ ṣékélé kan tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ tí WHO ṣàkóso rẹ̀. Àjẹsára alábẹ ṣékélé àìsàn ibà náà, tí wọ́n ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2019, ń mú àlékún bá ìdọ́gba nínú ànfààní sí ìdènà àìsàn ibà fún àwọn tó kángun séwu jùlọ ó sì ń dóólà ẹ̀mí. Tí wọ́n bá ṣàmúlò rẹ̀ lọ́nà tó gbòòrò, WHO ṣírò pé àjẹsára àìsàn ibà lè dóólà ẹ̀mí ẹgbẹ̀rún lọ́nà ẹ̀gbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ọmọdé ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan. A ní àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti dín àìsàn ibà kù, àpapọ àwọn ìdásí tí ó ní ìdènà àwọn alákòóba ààrùn, àwọn oògùn ìdèna àìsàn, àyẹ̀wò, àti ìtọ́jú," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Àwọn wọ̀nyìí ni àjẹsára àìsàn ibà tí ò léwu tó sì ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú, tí ó lè dóólà ẹ̀mí ẹgbẹ̀rún lọ́nà ẹ̀gbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ọmọdé ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan. Pẹ̀lú ìdókówò alálòpẹ́ àti fífẹ àwọn ìgbìyànjú lójú láti dé ọ̀dọ̀ àwọn tó kángun séwu jùlọ, ìṣẹ́gun àìsàn ibà ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè wà ní àrọ́wọ́tó. "Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Àìsàn Ibà ọdún 2023 ni wọ́n ń sààmì lábẹ́ àkòrí “Àkókò láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àìsàn ibà òdo: dókówò, ní ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun, ṣàmúlò”. Nínú àkòrí yìí, WHO ń pè fún ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ dáadáa láti dènà, ṣe àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ àti ìtọ́jú àìsàn ibà, pàápàá jùlọ láàrín àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti dẹ́yẹ sí. Gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìjábọ̀ àìsàn ibà Lágbàyé tuntun, tí wọ́n gbé jáde ní oṣù Ọ̀pẹ ọdún 2022, ìṣirò 247 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ àìsàn ibà tuntun ni ó wà ní ọdún 2021. Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ru ẹrù àìsàn náà tó pọ̀ jùlọ - tó dúró fún àpapọ 95% gbogbo àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ àìsàn ibà (234 mílíọ̀nù) àti 96% gbogbo ìṣẹ̀lẹ ikú (593 000) ní ọdún 2021. Bíi 80% àwọn ikú àìsàn ibà ní Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ ló wà láàrin àwọn ọmọdé tí ọjọ́ orí wọn wà lábẹ́ ọdún márùn-ún. Àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ tuntun Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti ní ìlọsíwájú nínú ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ àìsàn ibà fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ. Pẹ̀lú àwọn ìlọsíwájú kọ̀ọ̀kan yìí, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí wà nínú ewu ńlá fún àìsàn ibà ò tíì ní ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ tí ó lè dènà, ṣàwarí kí ó sì tọ́jú àìsàn náà. Àwọn ìpèníjà nínú ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ àìsàn ibà ti kenú, pàápàá jùlọ ní Ìwọ̀-oòrùn Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, pẹ̀lú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, àkójọ àwọn rògbòdìyàn ọmọnìyàn, àìsí ìpèsè owó, àwọn ètò ìtọpinpin tó lẹ, àti àdínkù nínú ìwúlò àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìgbógun ti àìsàn ibà tòótọ́. Láti kojú àwọn ewu wọ̀nyìí àti láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò àìsàn ibà tó ní àfaradà gidi gan-an, WHO láìpé yìí ṣe àtẹ̀jáde àwọn ìlàna-iṣẹ́ tuntun àti àwòṣe, tó fi mọ́: ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tuntun láti kojú jáàgùn apa-àìsàn-ibà ní ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀; ìpilèṣẹ̀ tuntun láti dáwọ́ ìtànkálẹ̀ Anopheles stephensi dúró ní àwọn agbègbè ìlú; ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tuntun, tí WHO àti UN-Habitat jọ ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀, láti tọ́ àwọn adarí ìlú sọ́nà nínú ìdènà àìsàn ibà ní ìlú. irinṣẹ́ tuntun láti ran àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lọ́wọ́ láti ṣe àyẹ̀wò ètò ìtọpinpin àìsàn ibà wọn àti láti mọ àwọn agbègbè fún ìdókówò WHO tún ti ṣe àfikún sí kíkó àkóyawọ́, àyípadà àti ànfààní sí àwọn àbá lórí àìsàn ibà rẹ̀. Àwọn Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Ìṣọ̀kan WHO fún àìsàn ibà ti wà lórí àwọn ìkànnì àkósódù méjì: MAGICapp àti ohun èlò ìmúṣiṣẹ́ “Malaria Toolkit”. WHO ṣe ìwúrí fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣe àwọn àbá náà ní ìbámu pẹ̀lú àwọn ààyè ààrùn lábẹ́lé fún ipa tó pọ̀.
translations.reference: WHO urges increased implementation of recommended tools to combat malaria Marking World Malaria Day, the World Health Organization (WHO) is issuing a call for increased implementation of new and existing interventions to save lives from malaria. Nearly 1.5 million children at high risk of illness and death from malaria in Ghana, Kenya and Malawi have now received their first dose of the first malaria vaccine, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), thanks to an ongoing pilot programme coordinated by WHO. The malaria vaccine pilots, launched in 2019, are increasing equity in access to malaria prevention for the most vulnerable and are saving lives. If implemented broadly, WHO estimates that malaria vaccines could save the lives of tens of thousands of children each year. “We have the tools to drive down malaria, a package of interventions that includes vector control, preventive medicines, testing, and treatment,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. “These are joined by a safe and effective malaria vaccine, which could save the lives of tens of thousands children every year. With sustained investment and scaled-up efforts to reach those most at risk, malaria elimination in many countries is in reach. "World Malaria Day 2023 is being marked under the theme “Time to deliver zero malaria: invest, innovate, implement”. Within this theme, WHO is urging more effective implementation of available tools and strategies to prevent, diagnose and treat malaria, particularly among marginalized populations. According to the latest World malaria report, published in December 2022, there were an estimated 247 million new cases of malaria in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to shoulder the heaviest burden of the disease – accounting for an estimated 95% of all malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of all deaths (593 000) in 2021. Nearly 80% of malaria deaths in the African Region were among children under the age of five. New strategies and tools Countries have made some progress in expanding access to malaria services for most-at-risk populations. Despite some progress, many people at high risk of malaria still lack access to services that can prevent, detect and treat the disease. Challenges in expanding access to malaria services have been compounded, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, by the COVID-19 pandemic, converging humanitarian crises, restricted funding, weak surveillance systems, and declines in the effectiveness of core malaria-fighting tools. To address these threats and support countries in building more resilient malaria programmes, WHO recently published new strategies and frameworks, including: a new strategy to contain antimalarial drug resistance in Africa; a new initiative to stop the spread of Anopheles stephensi in urban environments; a new framework, developed jointly by WHO and UN-Habitat, to guide city leaders in urban malaria control. a new toolkit to help countries assess their malaria surveillance systems and identify areas for investment WHO has also increased the transparency, flexibility and access to its malaria recommendations. The consolidated WHO Guidelines for malaria are now available through two digital platforms: MAGICapp and the “Malaria Toolkit” app. WHO encourages countries to tailor the recommendations to local disease settings for maximum impact.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the provided Yoruba document into English:

**WHO Urges Accelerated Rollout of Malaria Interventions to Combat Disease**

The World Health Organization (WHO) is urging an accelerated rollout of interventions to combat malaria, following the World Malaria Day and the Global Fund’s Sixth Replenishment Conference. The WHO is issuing a call for accelerated implementation of new and existing interventions to protect people from malaria. Approximately 1.5 million children in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi have now received the first dose of the most effective malaria vaccine to date, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), thanks to a pilot programme managed by WHO. This vaccine, piloted in 2019, has demonstrated a significant reduction in malaria infection rates among at-risk children and saves lives. If rolled out widely, WHO estimates the vaccine could save an additional 600,000 children’s lives each year.

“We have tools to reduce malaria, including diagnostics for detecting parasite carriers, malaria medicines, vector control, and treatment,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “The malaria vaccine is a powerful tool that can save an additional 600,000 children’s lives each year. With sustained investment and commitment from partners to reach the most at-risk populations, malaria elimination in many countries is achievable.”

The 2023 World Malaria Day is being held under the theme “Accelerating towards Zero Malaria: Doubling our efforts, tripling our impact.” Within this theme, WHO is calling for the implementation of effective interventions and strategies to prevent, detect, and treat malaria, particularly among vulnerable populations. According to the latest World Malaria Report, released in October 2022, 247 million malaria cases were reported in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to bear the brunt of the disease – accounting for 95% of all malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of all malaria deaths (593 000) in 2021. Eighty percent of malaria deaths in the African Region occurred among children under five years of age.

**New Strategies and Tools**

Countries have made progress in strengthening surveillance systems for malaria interventions among at-risk populations. However, many people at high risk of malaria have not benefited from preventive interventions or received timely treatment. Progress in strengthening surveillance systems for malaria interventions has been hampered, particularly in the African Region, by the COVID-19 pandemic, disruptions to routine immunization, supply chain challenges, and increased vector resistance to existing malaria control tools.

To address these challenges and to support countries in building sustainable malaria programmes, WHO has developed new tools and approaches, including: a new strategy to combat insecticide resistance in the African Region; a new approach to control Anopheles stephensi mosquito populations in urban areas; a new strategy, co-developed by WHO and UN-Habitat, to engage city leaders in malaria prevention in urban settings; and new guidance to support countries in assessing their malaria control programmes and identifying areas for WHO support. WHO has also expanded its digital tools, including the Malaria Dashboard and the “Malaria Toolkit” digital tool. WHO encourages countries to use these tools to inform their malaria programmes with data and evidence.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Àwọn àfojúsọ́nà fún àwọn ìdásí tuntun Ìdókòwò onítẹ̀síwájú nínú àgbékalẹ̀ àti ìṣàmúlò àwọn àjẹsára àìsàn ibà tuntun àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìran-tó-ń-bọ̀ máa ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àwọn àfojúsùn àìsàn ibà àgbáyé ọdún 2030. Àjẹsára àìsàn ibà kejì, àjẹsára R21/Matrix-M (R21), tí ó bá gba àṣẹ, lè ṣe ìrànwọ́ láti dí àlàfo ńlá tó wà láàrin ìfiránṣẹ́ àti ìbéèrè kí ó sì tẹ̀síwájú láti mú àdínkù bá àìlera àwọn ọmọdé àti ikú látara àìsàn ibà. Kàn-án-pá ló jẹ́ fún WHO láti tẹ̀síwájú àtúnyẹwò fínífíní àti ti akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ tó péye fún àjẹsára àìsàn ibà R21 lọ́gán tí àlékún dátà ààbò pàtàkì àti ipa láti abala 3 ìdánwò R21 tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ bá ti wà nílẹ̀ tí wọ́n sì pèsè rẹ̀ fún WHO. Ìpín sí ìsòrí àfihàn àjẹsára àìsàn ibà RTS,S ní àfikún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ ni ìrètí wà pé yóò bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ìbẹ̀rẹ ọdún 2024. Ní ààyè ìdènà alákòóba ààrùn, àwọn ohun èlò 28 tuntun ló wà nínú ojú òpó R&D. Àwọn irinṣẹ́ tó wà lábẹ́ ìgbéléwọ̀n ni, fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn ẹ̀yà tuntun àwọ̀n apẹ̀fọn, àwọn ìjẹ aṣàfojúsùn tí ó máa ń pe ẹ̀fọn, àwọn apanirun ní ààyè kan, àwọn atanipa ilé tó lágbára (àwọn túúbù Eaves) àti ṣíṣe àyípadà jínnì àwọn ẹ̀fọn. Àwọn oníwádìí ń mú ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìtọ́jú aláìní-ACT ní ọ̀kùkúdùn ní ẹ̀ka àwọn oògùn apa-àìsàn-ibà nítorí ìjẹyọ àti ìtànkálẹ̀ àwọn jáàgùn artemisinin. Ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn oògùn ìran-tó-ń-bọ̀, bíi "triple-ACTs," tí ó ń lo àpapọ artemisinin àti àwọn oògùn alájọṣepọ̀ méjì, ti wà lójú ọ̀nà láti mú àdínkù bá ewu jáàgùn. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún olóòtú: Fún àlàyé síwájú sí i nípa ìpolongo Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Àìsàn Ibà Lágbàyé WHO, kàn sí https://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023
translations.reference: Prospects for new interventions Continued investment in the development and deployment of new malaria vaccines and next-generation tools will be key to achieving the 2030 global malaria targets. A second malaria vaccine, the R21/Matrix-M (R21) vaccine, if approved, could help close the sizable gap between supply and demand and further reduce child illness and death from malaria. It is a priority for WHO to continue the thorough and efficient expert review of the R21 malaria vaccine once additional key safety and efficacy data from the ongoing R21 phase 3 trial are available and provided to WHO. Phased introductions of the RTS,S malaria vaccine in additional countries in Africa are expected to begin in early 2024. In the vector control space, there are 28 new products in the R&D pipeline. Tools under evaluation include, for example, new types of insecticide-treated nets, targeted baits that attract mosquitoes, spatial repellents, lethal house lures (eaves tubes) and genetic engineering of mosquitoes. Researchers are prioritizing the development of non-ACT treatments in the field of antimalarial medicines due to the emergence and spread of partial artemisinin resistance. The development of next-generation medicines, such as "triple ACTs," which use a combination of artemisinin and two partner drugs, is underway to reduce the risk of drug resistance. Notes to the editor:For more information on the WHO World Malaria Day campaign, visit: https://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Innovative tools for new malaria cases, including advanced diagnostics in the development and deployment of new antimalarial drugs and rapid diagnostic tests, will be crucial to achieving the 2030 global malaria elimination goals. The second malaria vaccine, R21/Matrix-M (R21), which is expected to be approved, could help fill a significant gap between supply and demand and further reduce child illness and deaths from malaria. The WHO is working to accelerate rigorous and comprehensive evaluation for the malaria vaccine R21, following the release of critical and impact data from Phase 3 of the R21 trial, which was submitted to the WHO. The rollout of the malaria vaccine RTS,S, with additional countries in the African region, is expected to begin in early 2024. In the era of coordinated research, 28 new products are in the R&D pipeline. These include, for example, new mosquito repellents, drugs that attract mosquitoes, single-dose treatments, potent household insecticides (Eaves tubes), and the production of sterile male mosquitoes. Researchers are accelerating the development of intra-ACT combinations in the eastern region of the malaria drug pipeline due to the scarcity and cost of artemisinin derivatives. The development of rapid diagnostic tests, such as "triple-ACTs," which use artemisinin derivatives and two partner drugs, is being explored to combat drug resistance. For more information: For further information about the WHO’s World Malaria Day campaign, visit https://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023"

translations.source: Ètò Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA sún sí ìpele tí ó kàn nínú ìdàgbàsókè rẹ̀ Ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ilé ìwòsan Ibùdó ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ní Afrigen, ní orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa. Àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ kórajọ sí Cape Town láti jíròrò lórí ìpele tí ó kàn nínú ìdàgbàsókè àjẹsára mRNA àti ìgbaraẹni nímọ̀ràn. Ó lé ní 200 àwọn akópa ilẹ̀-òkèèrè tí wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú Ètò Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ni wọ́n pàdé ní Cape Town ní ọ̀sẹ̀ yìí fún ìpàdé ìfojúrinjú àkọ́kọ́ wọn. Olùdarí Àpapọ̀ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO), Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus àti Dr Joe Phaahla, Mínísítà fún Ètò Ìlera àti Mínísítà fún Kátà-kárà àti Ilé-iṣẹ́, Mr Ebrahim Patel ti orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ onípele-gíga láti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ń pèsè owó ni wọ́n darapọ̀ mọ́ wọn, ìpàdé aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ yìí ṣe àtúnyẹ́wò ìlọsíwájú láti ìgbà tí WHO àti Medicine Patent Pool (MPP) ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní oṣù Òkudù ní ọdún 2021. Inú mi dùn láti wà ní Cape Town níbi pẹ̀lú àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ wa láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwòṣe alálòpẹ́ fún ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA láti fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré àti agbede-méjì ní ànfààní adọ́gba sí àwọn àjẹsára àti àwọn ohun èlò ìlera adóólà-ẹ̀mí mìíràn," Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Ìwúrí ńlá gidi gan-an ni àṣeyọrí gbogbo àwọn tí wọ́n lọ́wọ́ sí iṣẹ́-àkànṣe yìí jẹ́ fún mi; ní nǹkan bí ọdún méjì dín a ti fi hàn pé a lè jọ ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀, a ṣe àṣeyọrí lápapọ̀." Àwọn tí wọ́n kópa nínú ìpàdé náà ni àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ tí wọ́n ń ṣe ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí láti orílẹ̀-èdè 15 nínú ètò náà, àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ tó ń léwájú, ilé-iṣẹ́, àwọn aṣojú ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ, àti àwọn olùpèsè owó. Ní àkókò ìpàdé ọlọ́jọ́-márùn náà, àwọn akópa máa sọ nípa ìlọsíwájú wọn ó sì jíròrò lórí àwọn aṣokùnfà pàtàkì fún ìtẹ̀síwájú Ètò náà bíi àwọn ìṣòro ohun-ìní ọlọgbọ́n àti àwọn tó jẹ́ ti òfin, bákan náà ni sáyẹ́nsì fún ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA àti àwọn ìṣàmúlò pàtàkì tó níṣe pẹ̀lú LMICs ní agbègbè àwọn ààrùn mìíràn bíi KASA àti ikọ́fe. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 náà ti fi hàn pé àìdọ́gba tó pọ̀ wà nínú ànfààní sí àwọn ohun èlò ìlera, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn àjẹsára. Títí di oṣù Èrenà ọdún 2023, ọdún mẹ́ta lé lẹ́yìn tí WHO kéde COVID-19 gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Pàjáwírì Ìlera Àwùjọ fún Ẹ̀dun Ọkàn Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè (PHEIC), 69.7% àwọn ènìyàn lágbàyé ti gba ó kéré jù oògùn àjẹsára COVID-19 kan. Pàtàkì jùlọ, ìpín yìí ṣì wà lábẹ́ 30% ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré (LCIs). Ètò yìí gbèrò láti ṣe àfikún sí ànfààní adọ́gba sí àjẹsára mRNA pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe àfikún sí pínpín ìkápá ìpèsè alálòpẹ́ jákèjádò LMICs, ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àjọṣepọ̀ ní ẹkùn agbègbè àti ẹlẹ́kùnjẹkùn, àti ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àti Ìrónilágbára fún àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ lábẹ́lé nípasẹ ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àkànṣe àti aláfibọ̀ àti ìrànlọ́wọ́ àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́. Nǹkan tó dá yátọ̀ nínú Àwòṣe Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ni ìgbésẹ̀ oríṣiríṣi tí yóò fi ààyè sílẹ̀ fún pínpín àwọn ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ fún oríṣiríṣi àwọn tí yóò gbà á kí ó ba lè dé ọ̀dọ àwọn tó nílò rẹ̀ nípasẹ àwọn ìpèsè lábẹ́lé àti ẹkùn agbègbè kíákíá. Dr Phaahla, Mínísítà fún Ètò Ìlera ní orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa, sọ wí pé: "Nǹkan tí a rí níbi lónì, jẹ́ àkókò kan nínú ìtàn, Ètò kan tí wọ́n gbèrò láti ró àwọn LMIC lágbára nípasẹ ojú òpó àjọṣepọ̀ àgbáyé. Inú mi dùn láti rí ìtẹ̀síwájú tí ó ti di ṣíṣe láàrín àkókò díẹ̀ mo sì gba àtìlẹ́yìn láti ọ̀dọ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lóríṣiríṣi - àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè bíi South Africa tí ó ní ìkápá ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ dáadáa tó sì lágbára tí wọ́n sì ti ṣetán láti ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀, kẹ́kọ̀ọ́ láti ọ̀dọ ara wọn kí wọ́n sì tún sọ ọ́ fún ará wọn." Mínísítà Blade Nzimande fún Ètò-ẹ̀kọ́ Ilé-ìwé Gíga àti Ìmọ̀ Sáyẹ́nsì, sọ wí pé: Tiwa ni àfojúsùn Ibùdó Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA tayọ COVID-19 nìkan. Àwọn ìkápá tí à ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ ń wá láti ró wa lágbára láti kojú àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ọjọ́ iwájú mìíràn tí àjẹsára wọn ò lè lo àwọn ìkànnì ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA kan náà. Ó jẹ́ àfojúsùn kan tí ó fi tẹjú rẹ̀ mọ́ àwọn ààrùn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ní àwọn agbègbè wa bíi àìsàn ibà, ikọ́fe àti KASA." Ìjókòó náà tún sààmì àṣeyọrí pàtàkì fún ètò náà pẹ̀lú ìfilọ́lẹ̀ Ilé Ìwòsàn Ibùdó Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ní Afrigen ní Cape Town ní ojú Dr Tedros àti Dr Matshidoso Moeti Olùdarí Ẹkùn Agbègbè WHO fún Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, Charles Gore Olùdarí Aláṣẹ fún MPP àti Ọ̀narébù Mínísítà tí ó kópa nínú ayẹyẹ gígé ríbínì ní Afrigen. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Petro Terblanche, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ fún Afrigen sọ wí pé: "Inú gbogbo ikọ̀ Afrigen dùn láti ṣe àṣeyọrí pàtàkì yìí pẹ̀lú píparí ìkànnì ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA náà. Ìkànnì yìí wà nínú ìdàgbàsókè àjẹsára mRNA láti ìbẹ̀rẹ̀-dé-òpin àti ìpèsè ilé ìwòsàn níbi tí àwọn olùdíje Ibùdó mRNA àjẹsára COVID-19 AfriVac 2121 ti wà ní abala ìfẹ̀lójú. Láti bíi oṣù 18 sẹ́yìn, Afrigen ti la àyípadà tó lápẹẹrẹ kọjá pẹ̀lú àtìlẹyìn ojú òpó àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ àti àwọn olùbánidámọ́ràn tí Ètò yìí fi ààyè gbà. A ti fi kún ìkápá àti ìkáátò wa láti dọ́gba pẹ̀lú àwọn òdiwọ̀n ojúlówó tó ga jùlọ fún àgbékalẹ̀ àjẹsára mRNA, tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún èròngbà láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkápá alálòpẹ́ nínú LMICs láti pèsè àwọn àjẹsára mRNA ". Afrigen ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà ìpèsè àjẹsára COVID-19 ní àṣeyọrí pẹ̀lú òdiwọn ààyè fún ìmọ̀ ìjìnlẹ̀ tí ó sì ń ṣe ìfẹ̀lójú ìlànà yẹn dé ìpele tí ó ṣe é lò fún ìgbà pípẹ́ fún ìpèsè àwọn abala àjẹsára tí yóò jẹ́ lílo nínú Abala I/II àyẹ̀wò ajẹmọ́-ìtọ́jú sí àwọn òdiwọ̀n GMP.
translations.reference: mRNA Technology Transfer Programme moves to the next phase of its development Inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen, South Africa. Partners gather in Cape Town to discuss the next phase of the mRNA vaccine development and knowledge sharing. Over 200 international participants working with the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme met in Cape Town this week for their first face-to-face meeting. Joined by World Health Organization (WHO) Director General, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus and Dr. Joe Phaahla, Minister of Health and Minister of Trade and Industry, Mr. Ebrahim Patel of South Africa together with high-level officials from funding countries, this unique meeting reviewed the progress since WHO and Medicines Patent Pool (MPP) launched it in June 2021. “I am delighted to be here in Cape Town with our partners to support a sustainable model for mRNA technology transfer to give low- and middle-income countries equitable access to vaccines and other lifesaving health products,” said WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “I am immensely proud of the achievement of all those involved in this project; in less than two years we have shown that when we work collaboratively, we succeed collectively.” The meeting participants include biomanufacturing partners from 15 countries in the Programme, leading experts, industry, civil society representatives, and funders. During the five-day meeting, participants will share progress and discuss critical enablers for the sustainability of the Programme such as intellectual property issues and regulatory aspects, as well as the science of mRNA technologies and key applications relevant to LMICs in other disease areas such as HIV and tuberculosis. The COVID-19 pandemic has underscored that gross inequity exists in access to health products, especially vaccines. As of March 2023, more than three years after WHO declared COVID-19 a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), 69.7% of the global population had received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. Notably, this proportion still remains below 30% in low-income countries (LICs) . This Programme aims to contribute to equitable access to mRNA vaccines by increasing the distribution of sustainable manufacturing capacity across LMICs, enhancing regional and inter-regional collaborations, and developing and empowering a local workforce through tailored and inclusive training and expert support. What is unique in the mRNA Technology Transfer model is the multilateral process that allows sharing of technologies to multiple recipients so that through local and regional production those in need can be reached rapidly. Dr Phaahla, Minister of Health South Africa, said: “What we see here today, is a moment in history, a Programme that is aimed at empowering LMICs through a global collaborative network. I am thrilled to see the progress made in such a relatively short time and welcome the support from so many different countries - countries like South Africa that have a strong vibrant biomanufacturing capacity and that are willing to work together, learn from and share with each other.” Minister Blade Nzimande of Higher Education and Science, said: “Ours is the vision of the mRNA Technology Transfer Hub beyond just COVID-19. The capabilities we are building are looking to empower us to deal with other future pandemics whose vaccines could use the same mRNA technology platform. It is a vision that has set its eyes on diseases that are prevalent in our environments such as Malaria, Tuberculosis and HIV and AIDS.” This occasion also marks an important milestone for the Programme with the inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen in Cape Town in the presence of Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti WHO Regional Director for Africa, Charles Gore Executive Director of MPP and the Honorable Ministers who took part in a ribbon cutting ceremony at Afrigen. Professor Petro Terblanche, Executive Director of Afrigen said: “The entire Afrigen team are thrilled to reach this important milestone with the completion of the mRNA technology platform. This platform is housed within the end-to-end mRNA vaccine development and production facility where the mRNA Hub Covid 19 vaccine candidate AfriVac 2121 is currently in scale-up phase. Over the last 18 months, Afrigen has undergone an incredible transformation with the support of a network of partners and mentors enabled by this Programme. We have grown our capability and capacity to meet the highest quality standards of mRNA vaccine development, serving the objective to build sustainable capacity in LMICs to produce mRNA vaccines”. Afrigen has successfully established a COVID-19 vaccine manufacturing process at a laboratory scale and is currently scaling up that process to a level suitable for manufacturing vaccine batches to be used in Phase I/ II clinical trials to GMP standards.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**mRNA Vaccine Manufacturing Initiative Advances in Significant Step Within Its Development**

The regional hub of the mRNA Vaccine Technology Transfer Institute was inaugurated in Afrigen, Cape Town, South Africa. Partners traveled to Cape Town to discuss the progress of mRNA vaccine and therapeutics development. Approximately 200 individuals working within the mRNA Vaccine Technology Transfer Initiative met in Cape Town this week for their first in-person meeting. The Director-General of the World Health Organization (WHO), Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, and Dr. Joe Phaahla, Minister of Health and Minister of Employment and Labour of South Africa, together with high-level officials from countries contributing funding, joined the meeting, which reviewed progress since the WHO and Medicine Patent Pool (MPP) launched it in July 2021.

"I am delighted to be in Cape Town with our partners to champion a sustainable model for mRNA vaccine manufacturing for low- and middle-income countries facing vaccine and other health product shortages," said WHO Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "The success of all those involved in this endeavor is truly remarkable; in just about two years, we have demonstrated that we can work together and achieve success together."

Participants in the meeting included partners conducting fill-and-finish operations from 15 countries within the initiative, regulatory authorities, industry, civil society groups, and funders. During the meeting, hubs reported on their progress and discussed key challenges for advancing the initiative, such as intellectual property issues and those related to regulations, as well as support for mRNA technology and critical supplies needed by Low- and Middle-Income Countries (LMICs) in areas like Tuberculosis (TB) and malaria.

The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the significant inequities in access to health products, particularly vaccines. As of August 2023, three years after the WHO declared COVID-19 a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), only 69.7% of the global population had received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. Most notably, this figure remains below 30% in low-income countries (LCIs).

The initiative aims to enhance equitable access to mRNA vaccines by expanding local manufacturing capacity, strengthening partnerships across regions and continents, and building and empowering local workforce through training, mentorship, and support of regulatory authorities. A key feature of the mRNA Vaccine Technology Transfer Initiative is the phased approach that will allow for the transfer of technologies for various products to reach those in need through local and regional manufacturing, ensuring that it can be used for a long time for vaccine production in GMP-compliant facilities.

Dr. Phaahla, Minister of Health in South Africa, stated: "What we are witnessing today is a moment in history, an initiative that aims to empower LMICs through a global partnership. I am pleased to see the progress that has been made in a short period and the support from many countries – countries like South Africa that have a well-functioning fill-and-finish ecosystem and are ready to work together, learning from each other and sharing their expertise."

Minister Blade Nzimande of Higher Education, Science and Innovation, said: "The African Union’s mRNA Vaccine Technology Transfer Initiative is not limited to COVID-19 alone. The capabilities we are building will enable us to address future disease outbreaks for which vaccines are needed and mRNA technology cannot be utilized. It is a capability that will benefit us in addressing diseases prevalent in our regions, such as tuberculosis, malaria, and HIV/AIDS."

The event also marked a significant milestone for the inauguration of the mRNA Vaccine Technology Transfer Regional Hub in Afrigen in Cape Town, with Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti, Regional Director of the WHO for Africa, Charles Gore, CEO of MPP, and the Minister of Health participating in the grand opening at Afrigen. Dr. Petro Terblanche, CEO of Afrigen, said: "The entire Afrigen team is delighted to achieve this significant milestone with the transfer of mRNA technology. This technology is at the heart of mRNA vaccine development from inception to fill-and-finish operations within the vaccine manufacturing hub where the AfriVac 2121 COVID-19 vaccine candidates have been in the development phase. Since mid-August, Afrigen has been working with the support of partners and funders of the initiative on technology transfer. We have leveraged our capacity and expertise to partner with leading technology providers to establish a robust mRNA vaccine manufacturing capacity in LMICs to address vaccine shortages." Afrigen has successfully established a fill-and-finish vaccine manufacturing process with a high level of technology readiness and is advancing the process to a stage suitable for long-term use in fill-and-finish operations for vaccine products in Phase I/II clinical trial GMP facilities.

translations.source: Ní ìlànà adọ́gba, Afrigen máa tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àti ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ fún àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ojú òpó náà. Àwọn olùdásílẹ̀ náà kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún Ètò náà pẹ̀lú ìpèsè owó lápapọ̀ títí di òní ní US$ 177 mílíọ̀nù tí orílẹ̀-èdè France sì jẹ́ àkọ́kọ́ láti pèsè owó fún iṣẹ́ ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA náà. Àwọn tó péjú síbi ìpàdé náà ni àwọn aṣojú láti Àjọ Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù, Belgium, Germany bákan náà ni Norway, Canada, Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, South Africa, àti ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delaney, Olùdarí Àpapọ̀ GlobalAffairs Canada fún Gúúsù àti Ìlà-oòrùn Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ sọ wí pé: "Orílẹ̀-èdè Canada ń ṣe ìfirinlẹ̀ ìtẹ̀síwájú àtìlẹyìn rẹ̀ fún Ètò Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA inú wa sì dùn láti kéde ìtẹ̀síwájú àfikún 15 mílíọ̀nù dọ́là owó orílẹ̀-èdè Canada níbi ìpàdé náà lónì. Èyí mú gbogbo owó tí orílẹ̀-èdè Canada ti fi dásí Ètò náà jẹ́ 45 mílíọ̀nù dólà owó orílẹ̀-èdè Canada. Àwọn owó wọ̀nyìí ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún iṣẹ́ ṣíṣe ní Ibùdó náà ní orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ojú òpó káàkiri àgbáyé. A ní ìgbàgbọ́ tó rinlẹ̀ nínú pàtàkì ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkápá ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè àti ẹkùn agbègbè ". Mr Martin Seychell, Igbákejì Olùdarí Aláṣẹ Àjọ Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù, ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "EC ti dá €40 mílíọ̀nù tẹ́lẹ̀ fún ìdásílẹ̀ Ibùdó Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ó sì ti buwọ́lù owó ìrànwọ́ láìpẹ́ yìí pẹ̀lu EIB fún €15,5 mílíọ̀nù láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìfẹ̀lójú ìkápá ìpèsè àwọn àjẹsára. Èyí jẹ́ lára àpapọ ìdókówò lábẹ́ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ EU Global Gateway, níbi tí wọ́n ti ṣe àkójọ €1 bílíọ̀nù lé lábẹ́ Ikọ̀ Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù lórí Ìpèsè àwọn ohun èlò ìlera. Ó ti wá ṣe pàtàkì pàápàá báyìí láti kojú àwọn ìfúnpinpin òfin àti ìbéèrè tó ṣe é ṣe, láti rí i dájú pé kìí ṣe ìpèsè àjẹsára tí wọ́n jẹ́ ojúlówó tó dára jùlọ ní gbogbo ẹkùn agbègbè àgbáyé nìkan, ṣùgbọ́n pẹ̀lu ànfààní ojú ẹsẹ̀ àti adọ́gba sí àwọn àjẹsára wọ̀nyẹn."
translations.reference: In a parallel process, Afrigen will continue to carry out training and technology transfer to the network partners. The funders play a crucial role in supporting the Programme with total funding to date at US$ 117 million and France being the first to fund the mRNA technology transfer work. Present at the meeting were representatives from the European Commission, Belgium, Germany, as well as Norway, Canada, African Union, South Africa, and the ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delany, GlobalAffairs Canada’s Director General for Southern and Eastern Africa said: “Canada reaffirms its continued support of the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme and we are delighted to announce a further contribution of 15 million Canadian dollars at the meeting today. This brings Canada’s total contribution to the Programme up to 45 million Canadian dollars. These funds support the running of the Hub in South Africa as well as network partners around the world. We firmly believe in the importance of building capacity at local and regional level”. Mr Martin Seychell, Deputy Executive Director European Commission, said. “The EC has already contributed €40 million to the establishment of the mRNA technology transfer Hub and has recently signed another grant with the EIB for €15,5 million to facilitate the expansion of vaccines manufacturing capacity. This is part of the overall investment under the EU Global Gateway strategy, where more than €1 Billion have been mobilised under the Team Europe Initiative on Manufacturing health products. It is now particularly important to also address possible regulatory and demand bottlenecks, to ensure not only production of vaccines of the highest standard in all regions of the world, but also rapid and equitable access to those vaccines.”
translations.hypothesis:

"Under the Team Europe approach, Afrigen will continue to manufacture and distribute mRNA technology for its partners worldwide. The founders played a crucial role in establishing and supporting the program with a total financial commitment of US$177 million to date, with France being the first to contribute funding for the mRNA technology transfer initiative. Other participants in the meeting included representatives from the European Union, Belgium, Germany, Norway, Canada, the African Union, South Africa, and the ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delaney, Senior Global Affairs Canada Advisor for Sub-Saharan Africa, stated: "Canada is committed to advancing and supporting our mRNA Technology Transfer Program and we are delighted to announce an additional C$15 million in funding from Canada at today's meeting. This brings the total funding from Canada to the program to C$45 million. These funds are supporting manufacturing operations in the hub in South Africa and global partners. We have great confidence in the importance of scaling up production at the national and regional level." said Mr. Martin Seychell, Deputy Director-General of the European Commission. "The EC has already allocated €40 million for the establishment of the mRNA Technology Transfer Hub and has recently mobilized additional funding with the EIB for €15.5 million to support the development of vaccine production capacity. This is part of a broader investment package under the EU Global Gateway strategy, where €1 billion has been mobilized under the European Pillar of Health for the Global Good. It has been particularly important to address the supply chain and demand challenges to ensure that it is not a vaccine that is only accessible to high-income countries, but also with equitable and affordable access for these vaccines."

translations.source: Àwọn ìyàn sípò aṣíwájú pàtàkì tí wọ́n ṣe láti darí ìlànà-iṣẹ́ ìdarí àti ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ WHO Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) ti ṣe ìyànsípò àwọn lọ́gà-lọ́gà márùn-ún tuntun sí ikò adarí olú ilé-iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ ní Geneva. Àwọn ìyànsípò tuntun náà tẹ̀lé ìtúnni yàn sípò Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus sí sáà ọlọ́dún-márùn-ún kejì gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀. Àpapọ ikọ̀ àwọn olùdarí náà ni wọ́n ti ṣe láti dọ́gba pẹ̀lú àwọn ohun pàtàkì Àjọ náà fún ọdún márùn-ún tó ń bọ̀ tí wọn ó sì ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ láti mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá àwọn nǹkan pàtàkì wọ̀nyìí àti èròngbà àyípadà àlá WHO. Láti mú kí ìlọsíwájú yá lórí ìṣàmúlò Ètò Àpapọ̀ fún Iṣẹ́ WHO 13th (GPW13), àti àṣeyọrí àfojúsùn "bílíọ̀nù mẹ́ta" rẹ̀ àti Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ ajẹmọ́-ìlera náà, iṣẹ́ WHO fún ọdún márùn-ún tó ń bọ̀ dá lórí àwọn nǹkan pàtàkì márùn-ún: ṣíṣe àyípadà iṣẹ́ lọ sí ibi ìpolongo ìlera àti ìdènà ààrùn pẹ̀lú kíkojú àwọn aṣokùnfà ìpìlẹ̀ rẹ̀; ṣíṣe àtúntò àwọn ètò ìlera fún ètò ìlera alábọ́dé láti ìpìlẹ̀; Ríró àwọn ètò náà lágbára fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti ìdáhùn sí pàjáwírì ìlera; ṣíṣe àmúlò agbára sáyẹ́nsì, ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun, dátà àti àwọn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ilera; àti ríró WHO lágbára gẹ́gẹ́ bíi aláṣẹ tó ń léwájú lórí ìlera àgbáyé àti láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ipa ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè. Ikọ̀ adarí olú ilé-iṣẹ́ náà, tó jẹ́ àpapọ iye àwọn ọkùnrin àti àwọn obìnrin kan náà, máa ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ọ́fíísì ẹkùn agbègbè àti orílẹ̀-èdè. Àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ tuntun nínú ikọ̀ adarí olú ilé-iṣẹ́ náà náà: Dr Jeremy Farrar máa di Olórí Onímọ̀ Sáyẹ́nsì WHO láti 8 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023. Ìyànsípò Dr Farrar ni wọ́n ti kọ́kọ́ kéde ní oṣù Ọ̀pẹ ọdún 2022. Nínú ipò yìí Dr Farrar máa ṣàmójútó Ẹ̀ka Ìmọ Sáyẹ́nsì, tí yóò ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ àti àwọn ojú òpó tó dára jùlọ àti nínú ìmọ sáyẹ́nsì àti ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun jákèjádò àgbáyé láti ṣe ìtọ́nisọ́nà, àgbédìde àti àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ojúlówó òfin ìlera àti ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀ jùlọ. Ṣíwájú kí ó tó darapọ̀ mọ́ WHO Dr Farrar ni Olùdarí fún Wellcome Trust. Kí ó tó darapọ̀ mọ́ Wellcome ní ọdún 2013 Dr Farrar lo ọdún 17 gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Olùdarí fún Ẹ̀ka Ìwádìí Ajẹmọ́-ìlera ní Ilé Ìwòsàn fún Àwọn Ààrùn Ìgbóná ní Vietnam níbi tí àwọn àfojúsùn ìwádìí ti dá lórí ìlera àgbáyé pẹ̀lú àdojúkọ àwọn ààrùn àkóràn tó ń jẹyọ. Dr Farrar jẹ́ oníwádìí ajẹmọ́-ìlera pẹ̀lú ìwé ẹ̀rí ìṣègùn àti PhD. Dr Ailan Li máa di Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Kíkárí Ìlera Àgbáyé, Àwọn Ènìyàn Tó Ní Àlàáfíà láti 8 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023. Nínú ipò yìí Dr Li máa ṣàmójútó àwọn ìgbìyànjú Àjò náà láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìlera tó dára àti ìgbáyégbádùn nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìdásí tó níṣe pẹ̀lú agbègbè, àwùjọ, àti àwọn aṣokùnfà ọrọ̀ ajé fún ìlera, tó fi mọ́ àyípadà ojú ọjọ́, ìdarí tábà, ààbò kẹ́míkà, ààbò ojú pópó, àwọn ètò oúnjẹ àti èròjà aṣara lóore, àwọn iṣẹ́ àfojúrí, bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ àti ìtànkálẹ̀, nípasẹ ìlànà Ìlera Kan. Dr Li ti ń ṣiṣẹ́ gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Aṣojú WHO ní Kingdom of Cambodia láti 15 oṣù Agẹmọ ọdún 2019. Ṣíwájú ipò yìí, Dr Li ti ṣiṣẹ́ gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Olùdarí Pàjáwírì Ẹkùn Agbègbè fún Ètò Àwọn Pàjáwírì Ìlera WHO ní Ọ́fíísì Ẹkùn Agbègbè WHO fún Western Pacific. Dr Li ní ìwé-ẹ̀rí ìṣẹ̀gun àti ìwé-ẹ̀rí nínú sáyẹ́nsì ìlera àwùjọ. Dr Yukiko Nakatani máa di Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Ànfààní sí Àwọn Oògùn àti Àwọn Ohun Èlò Ìlera láti 2 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023. Nínú ipò yìí Dr Nakatani máa ṣàmójútó àgbékalẹ̀ àti àmúlò àwọn ìṣe àti òfin WHO láti ṣe àrídájú ànfààní sí adọ́gba sí àwọn ojúlówó oògùn, àwọn àjẹsára àti àyẹ̀wò fún ààrùn fún gbogbo àwọn ènìyàn ní ibi gbogbo, tó fi mọ́ ìdènà àti ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn. Dr Nakatani ti ń ṣiṣẹ́ gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Olùdarí fún Ẹ̀ka Ààrùn Jẹjẹrẹ àti Ìdènà Ààrùn ti Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tó ń Rí Sí Ètò Ìlera, Òṣìṣẹ́ àti Ìgbáyégbádùn ní orílẹ̀-èdè Japan àti gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Olùbánidámọ́ràn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ fún Mínísítà fún Ìpínlè fún Àwọn Òṣìṣẹ́ Abẹ́rẹ́ Àjẹsára COVID-19 ní orílẹ̀-èdè Japan.
translations.reference: Key leadership appointments made to drive WHO strategic direction and initiatives The World Health Organization (WHO) has appointed five new senior figures to its headquarters leadership team in Geneva. The new appointments follow the reappointment of Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus to his second five-year term as Director-General. The overall leadership team has been consolidated to align with the Organization’s priorities for the next five years and will work closely with the Director-General to drive forward these priorities and WHO’s ambitious transformation agenda. To accelerate progress on implementation of WHO’s 13th General Programme of Work (GPW13), and achievement of its “triple billion” targets and the health-related Sustainable Development Goals, WHO’s work for the coming five years is focused on five priorities: making a paradigm shift towards promoting health and preventing disease by addressing its root causes; radically reorienting health systems towards primary health care; strengthening the systems for health emergency preparedness and response; harnessing the power of science, innovation, data and technologies to advance health; and strengthening WHO as the leading authority on global health and to achieve impact in countries. The headquarters leadership team, comprising equal numbers of women and men, will work jointly with WHO regional and country offices. New members of the headquarters leadership team: Dr Jeremy Farrar will become WHO’s Chief Scientist as of 8 May 2023. The appointment of Dr Farrar was previously announced in December 2022. In this role Dr Farrar will oversee the Science Division, bringing together the best experts and networks in science and innovation from around the world to guide, develop and deliver high quality health policies and services to the people who need them most. Prior to joining WHO Dr Farrar was Director of the Wellcome Trust. Before joining Wellcome in 2013 Dr Farrar spent 17 years as Director of the Clinical Research Unit at the Hospital for Tropical Diseases in Vietnam where his research interests were in global health with a focus on emerging infectious diseases. Dr Farrar is a clinician researcher with a medical degree and a PhD. Dr Ailan Li will become Assistant Director-General for Universal Health Coverage, Healthier Populations as of 8 May 2023. In this role Dr Li will oversee the Organization’s efforts to promote better health and well-being through interventions relating to the environmental, social, and economic determinants of health, including climate change, tobacco control, chemical safety, road safety, food systems and nutrition, physical activity, air pollution and radiation, through a One Health approach. Dr Li has been serving as the WHO Representative to the Kingdom of Cambodia since 15 July 2019. Prior to this role, Dr Li served as Regional Emergency Director for the WHO Health Emergencies Programme in the WHO Regional Office for the Western Pacific. Dr Li holds a medical degree and degree in health social sciences. Dr Yukiko Nakatani will become Assistant Director-General for Access to Medicines and Health Products as of 2 May 2023. In this role Dr Nakatani will oversee the development and implementation of WHO’s norms and policies to ensure equitable access to quality medicines, vaccines and diagnostics for all populations everywhere, including for preventing and responding to epidemics. Dr Nakatani has been serving as the Director of the Cancer and Disease Control Division of the Japanese Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare and as the Technical Advisor to the Minister of State for the COVID-19 Vaccination Task Force in Japan.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the provided Yoruba document into English:

**Appointments to Key Leadership Positions at the World Health Organization (WHO)**

The World Health Organization (WHO), the global health authority, has appointed seven new senior officials to the office of the Director-General in Geneva. These new appointments follow the reappointment of Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus for a second consecutive term as Director-General. The appointments are intended to support the Organization’s key priorities for the coming five years and to work closely with the Director-General to advance these priorities and the WHO’s strategic agenda.

To ensure progress on the implementation of the WHO’s 13th General Programme of Work (GPW13), and the success of its “billion lives” ambition and the related Global Accelerator on Health and Wellbeing, WHO’s work for the coming five years is based on six key priorities: strengthening health service delivery and disease prevention through strengthening its core functions; developing health programs from the ground up; making programs robust for resilience and response to health emergencies; leveraging digital, data, and technology to advance health; and making WHO a stronger, more agile leader in global health and achieving impact in countries.

The Director-General’s office, which comprises a team of men and women, will work closely with regional and country offices. The new members of the Director-General’s office are:

Dr. Jeremy Farrar will become the WHO’s Chief Scientist from 8 June 2023. Dr. Farrar’s appointment was first announced in October 2022. In this role, Dr. Farrar will lead the Science Council, bringing together the best scientists and thought leaders in science and data globally to provide evidence, guidance, and solutions for the most pressing health challenges and the needs of populations. Prior to joining WHO, Dr. Farrar was Director of the Wellcome Trust. Before joining Wellcome in 2013, Dr. Farrar spent 17 years as Director of the Clinical Infection Research Unit at the Institute for Tropical Medicine in Vietnam, where research focused on global health with a focus on infectious diseases. Dr. Farrar is a clinical scientist with an MD and a PhD.

Dr. Ailan Li will become Deputy Director-General for Universal Health Coverage, Healthier Populations from 8 June 2023. In this role, Dr. Li will lead the Organization’s efforts to advance health and well-being through interventions that work with communities, civil society, and financial partners for health, addressing non-communicable diseases, tobacco control, chemical hazards, eye health, nutrition and food safety, immunization, and pandemic preparedness, through the One Health approach. Dr. Li has been working as a WHO Representative in the Kingdom of Cambodia since 15 August 2019. Prior to this role, Dr. Li served as Regional Team Lead for Health Emergencies at the WHO Regional Office for the Western Pacific. Dr. Li holds an MD and a PhD in social medicine.

Dr. Yukiko Nakatani will become Deputy Director-General for Access to Medicines and Health Products from 2 February 2023. In this role, Dr. Nakatani will lead the development and implementation of WHO activities to accelerate access to essential medicines, vaccines, and diagnostics for all people everywhere, contributing to prevention and response to disease. Dr. Nakatani has been working as Director for Infectious Disease and Health Security at the Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare of Japan, and also as a technology policy advisor for the Minister for Health for the COVID-19 Vaccination Program in Japan.

translations.source: Dr Nakatani ní ìwé-ẹ̀rí ìṣẹ̀gun àti PhD nínú ìlera àwùjọ. Dr Razia Pendse máa di Chef de Cabinet láti 4 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023. Nínú ipò yìí Dr Pendse máa di olórí Ọ́fíísì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀, tí yóò máa ṣe ìrànwọ́ láti darí àwọn nǹkan pàtàkì Àjọ náà àti àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀, yóó sì ṣe àrídájú ìdọ́gba láàrín ikọ̀ àwọn olùdarí WHO àti jákèjádò àwọn ìpele mẹ́tẹ̀ẹ̀ta WHO. Dr Pendse ti ń ṣiṣẹ́ gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Olùdarí fún Àwọn Ènìyàn Tó Ní Àlàáfíà àti Àwọn Ààrùn tí kìí Ràn ní Ọ́fíísì Ẹkùn Agbègbè WHO fún Gúúsù Ìlà-oòrùn Asia tí ó sì jẹ́ Aṣojú WHO ní Sri Lanka tẹ́lẹ̀. Dr Pendse ní ìwé-ẹ̀rí ìṣẹ̀gun àti ìwé-ẹ̀rí másítà nínú ìlera àwùjọ. Dr Jérôme Salomon máa di Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Kíkárí Ìlera Lágbàyé, Ààrùn Àkóràn àti Ààrùn tí kìí Ràn láti 17 oṣù Igbe ọdún 2023. Nínú ipò yìí Dr Salomon máa ṣàmójútó àwọn ipò àwọn ètò ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ tó fẹjú tó kárí KASA, àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn jẹ̀dọ̀jẹ̀dọ̀, àwọn ààrùn látara ìbálòpọ̀, ikọ́fe, àìsàn ibà, àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì, ìlera ọpọlọ, ààrùn ìlòkulò oògùn, àti àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn bíi ààrùn ọkàn, àtọgbẹ, ààrùn ọ̀fìnkì tó lágbára àti ààrùn jẹjẹrẹ. Dr Solomon ti ń ṣiṣẹ́ gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Ìlera ní Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera àti Ìdènà fún orílẹ̀-èdè France. Ó jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Ìgbìmọ̀ Aláṣẹ WHO ṣíwájú ìyànsípò rẹ̀ ó sì ní ìrírí tó pọ̀ nínú àmójútó àwọn ètò ìlera, àwọn ààrùn àkóràn àti ìlera àwujọ ilẹ̀-òkèèrè. Dr Solomon ní ìwé-ẹ̀rí ìṣẹ̀gun, ìwé-ẹ̀rí másítà nínú ìlera àwùjọ àti PhD nínú ẹ̀kọ́ nípa àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn. Àwọn ipò àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ tó ṣì wà nínú ikọ̀ olórí olú ilé-iṣẹ́ náà ni: Dr Samira Asma máa tẹ̀síwájú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Dátà, Ìyànnàná àti Àgbékalẹ̀ fún Ipa. DR Asma ń ṣàmójútó ipò kan tó ń rí i dájú pé àwọn dátà ìlera ṣe é gbáralé ó sì wà fún lílò, wọ́n sì ń lò ó láti mú àyípadà bá àbájáde ìlera jákèjádò àgbáyé, tó fi mọ́ ìtọpinpin àti mímú kí ìtẹ̀síwájú yá fún àwọn àfojúsùn bílíọ̀nù mẹ́ta àti àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ ajẹmọ́-ìlera. Ṣíwájú kí ó tó darapọ̀ mọ́ WHO ní ọdún 2018, Dr Asma wà ní ipò olórí kan ní Ààyè fún Ìdarí Ààrùn àti Ìdènà (CDC) ní U.S. fún ogún ọdún lé. Ó ní ìwé-ẹ̀rí ọ̀mọ̀wé nínú iṣẹ́ abẹ eyín àti ìwé-ẹ̀rí másítà nínú ìlera àwùjọ. Dr Bruce Aylward ni wọ́n ti yàn sípò gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Kíkárí Ìlera Lágbàyé, Ẹ̀ka Iṣẹ́ Títí Láéláé láti 4 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023. Dr Aylward ti ń darí Ètò Àyípadà WHO àti iṣẹ́ Àjọ náà lórí Ànfààní oríṣiríṣi-àjọ sí Ibùdó àwọn Amúṣiṣẹ́ Irinṣẹ́ COVID-19 (ACT-A). Nínú ipò tuntun rẹ̀, Dr Aylward máa darí ètò Àjọ náà láti mú àyípadà bá àyípadà bá ètò ìlera alábọ́dé gẹ́gẹ́ bíi àárín gbùngbùn fún kíkárí ìlera lágbàyé, bákan náà ni ṣíṣe àmójútó iṣẹ́ WHO lórí àwọn ètò ìlera, abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti ìlera ìbímọ, ìyá àti ọmọ. Dr Aylward ní ìwé-ẹ̀rí ìṣẹ̀gun àti ìwé-ẹ̀rí másítà nínú ìlera àwùjọ, Dr Hanan Balkhy máa tẹ̀síwájú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún ẹ̀yàwuuru Jáàgùn. Dr Balkhy ń pèsè ìdarí ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ àti òṣèlú láti dènà àwọn ìṣòro ìlera àti ọrọ̀ ajé fún jáàgùn, tó fi mọ́ nípasẹ àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú ẹlẹ́ka-jẹ̀ka pẹ̀lú Ilé-iṣẹ́ Oúnjẹ àti Iṣẹ́ Àgbẹ̀, Àjọ Àgbáyé fún Ìlera Ẹranko, àti Ètò Àwùjọ Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé. Ṣíwájú kí ó dó darapọ̀ mọ́ WHO, Dr Balkhy jẹ́ Olùdarí Aláṣẹ fún Ìdènà àti Ìdarí Àkóràn ní Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tó ń rí sí Ààbò Orílẹ̀-èdè ní orílẹ̀-èdè Saudi Arabia. Dr Balkhy ní ìwé-ẹ̀rí ìṣẹ̀gun pẹ̀lú àmọ̀já nínú àwọn ààrùn àkóràn ìkókó. Dr Catherina Boehme máa di Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀, Àwọn Àjọṣepọ̀ Láti Ìta àti Ìṣèjọba láti 4 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023. Nínú ipò tuntun yìí Dr Boehme máa léwájú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ ìbáraẹnisọ̀rọ̀ WHO ní àwọn agbègbè ìṣèjọba, ṣíṣe àkójọpọ̀ ohun èlò àti àjọṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn alájọṣepọ̀. Nínú ipò rẹ̀ ni a ti máa rí àwọn ìlànà àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ tó lágbára, bíi ìdúnàdúrà wọn fún ìṣọ̀kan àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn, àtúnṣe fún ìṣèjọba WHO àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn àbá lórí ìpèsè owó alálòpẹ́. Dr Boehme ti ń ṣiṣẹ́ gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ Chef de Cabinet ní àkókò tó ṣe wí pé ó darí àfojúsùn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ adarí, ṣíṣe àrídájú ìdọ́gba jákèjádò Àjọ náà àti pẹ̀lú àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀.
translations.reference: Dr Nakatani holds a medical degree and a PhD in public health. Dr Razia Pendse will become the Chef de Cabinet as of 4 May 2023. In this role Dr Pendse will head the Director-General’s Office, helping to drive the Organization’s priorities and initiatives, and will ensure alignment within the WHO leadership team and across the three levels of WHO. Dr Pendse has been serving as the Director of Healthier Populations and Noncommunicable Diseases in the WHO Regional Office for South-East Asia and was previously the WHO Representative to Sri Lanka. Dr Pendse holds a medical degree and master's degree in public health. Dr Jérôme Salomon will become Assistant Director-General for Universal Health Coverage, Communicable and Noncommunicable Diseases as of 17 April 2023. In this role Dr Salomon will oversee a broad portfolio of technical programmes covering HIV, viral hepatitis, sexually-transmitted infections, tuberculosis, malaria, neglected tropical diseases, mental health, substance use disorders, and noncommunicable diseases such as cardiovascular disease, diabetes, chronic respiratory diseases and cancer. Dr Salomon has been serving as the Director-General for Health at the Ministry of Health and Prevention of France. He was a Member of the WHO Executive Board prior to his appointment and has extensive experience in health systems management, communicable diseases and international public health. Dr Salomon holds a medical degree, a master’s degree in public health and a PhD in epidemiology. The portfolios of existing members of the headquarters leadership team: Dr Samira Asma will continue as the Assistant Director-General for Data, Analytics and Delivery for Impact. Dr Asma oversees a portfolio which ensures that health data are reliable and accessible, and are used to improve health outcomes worldwide, including tracking and accelerating progress towards the triple billion targets and the health-related Sustainable Development Goals. Prior to joining WHO in 2018, Dr Asma held leadership positions at the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) for over two decades. She holds a doctorate degree in dental surgery and a master's degree in public health. Dr Bruce Aylward has been appointed Assistant Director-General of the Universal Health Coverage, Life Course Division as of 4 May 2023. Dr Aylward has been leading WHO’s Transformation Agenda and the Organization’s work on the multi-agency Access to COVID-19 Tools Accelerator (ACT-A) Hub. In his new role, Dr Aylward will drive the Organization’s agenda to transform primary health care as central to universal health coverage, as well as overseeing WHO’s work on health systems, immunization and reproductive, maternal and child health. Dr Aylward holds a medical degree and a master’s degree in public health.Dr Hanan Balkhy will continue as Assistant Director-General for Antimicrobial Resistance. Dr Balkhy provides technical and political leadership to curb the health and economic burdens of drug resistance, including through multi-sectoral collaboration with the Food and Agriculture Organization, the World Organization for Animal Health, and the United Nations Environment Programme. Prior to joining WHO, Dr Balkhy was Executive Director for Infection Prevention and Control at Saudi Arabia’s Ministry of National Guard. Dr Balkhy holds a medical degree with specialization in paediatric infectious diseases. Dr Catharina Boehme will become Assistant Director-General, External Relations and Governance as of 4 May 2023. In this role Dr Boehme will lead WHO’s strategic engagement in the areas of governance, resource mobilization and partner relations. Her portfolio will include critical Member States processes, such as their negotiation of a pandemic accord, the reform of WHO’s governance and the implementation of recommendations on sustainable financing. Dr Boehme has been serving as the Director-General’s Chef de Cabinet during which time she drove the leadership’s strategic vision, ensuring alignment across the Organization and with Member States and partners.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba document:

Dr. Nakatani holds a medical degree and a PhD in public health. Dr. Razia Pendse will become Chef de Cabinet starting April 4, 2023. In this role, Dr. Pendse will lead the Executive Office, responsible for driving the Organization’s key priorities and initiatives, facilitating dialogue between WHO leadership and the two WHO level secretariats. Dr. Pendse has previously served as Director for Noncommunicable Diseases and Mental Health at the WHO Regional Office for South-East Asia and as WHO Focal Point in Sri Lanka. Dr. Pendse holds a medical degree and a master’s degree in public health. Dr. Jérôme Salomon will become Deputy Director-General for Global Health Security, Infectious Diseases and Emergencies starting November 17, 2023. In this role, Dr. Salomon will oversee the portfolio of science-based programmes covering global health security, disease outbreaks, neglected diseases, immunization, maternal and child health, reproductive health, and diseases such as cancer, diabetes, neurological disorders and rare diseases. Dr. Salomon previously served as Director-General for Health in the Government Agency overseeing the Health and Solidarity Fund for France. He is a member of the WHO Strategic Advisory Group and has extensive experience in health programme management, infectious diseases and public health in low-resource settings. Dr. Salomon holds a medical degree, a master’s degree in public health and a PhD in epidemiology. The following appointments are also made within the leadership team: Dr. Samira Asma will continue as Deputy Director-General for Data, Analytics and Impact. Dr. Asma champions the critical role of health data and its availability for use to accelerate global health progress, informing strategic decision-making and mobilizing resources for the three billion people who lack access to health services and the Sustainable Development Goals. Prior to joining WHO in 2018, Dr. Asma served as Chief of Staff at the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) for several years. She holds a bachelor’s degree in nursing and a master’s degree in public health. Dr. Bruce Aylward has been appointed as Deputy Director-General for Global Health Security, Emergency Preparedness from April 4, 2023. Dr. Aylward has led the WHO’s COVID-19 response and the Organization’s work on various cross-cutting issues to the Access to COVID-19 Tools (ACT-A) initiative. In his new role, Dr. Aylward will lead the Organization’s efforts to accelerate the acceleration of the global health programme, serving as a central hub for global health security, as well as overseeing WHO’s work on health programmes, immunization, maternal and child health, and tuberculosis and neglected tropical diseases. Dr. Aylward holds a medical degree and a master’s degree in public health. Dr. Hanan Balkhy will continue as Deputy Director-General for Antimicrobial Resistance. Dr. Balkhy leverages scientific and technological leadership to address the challenges of antimicrobial resistance and poverty for AMR, through partnerships with the Food and Agriculture Organization, the World Animal Health Organization, and the One Health Joint Plan. Prior to joining WHO, Dr. Balkhy was Chief Medical Officer for Infection Control and Patient Safety in the Government Agency overseeing Hospital Administration in Saudi Arabia. Dr. Balkhy holds a medical degree with a specialization in infectious diseases. Dr. Catherina Boehme will become Deputy Director-General, External and Government Relations starting April 4, 2023. In this new role, Dr. Boehme will strengthen WHO’s engagement with government partners, facilitate collaboration with partners, and mobilize resources. In this role, she will also represent Member States, advocating for solidarity in disease outbreak response, reform of the WHO governance and mobilizing resources on philanthropic funding. Dr. Boehme previously served as Chef de Cabinet, where she led strategic management, facilitated dialogue across the Organization and with Member States and partners.

translations.source: Ó darapọ̀ mọ́ WHO ní ọdún 2021, lẹ́yìn tí ó ti jẹ́ Olórí Àwọn Òṣìṣẹ́ fún FIND, ìṣọ̀kan ilẹ̀-òkèèrè fún àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ ààrùn. Dr Boehme ní ìwé-ẹ̀rí ìṣẹ̀gun àti dípúlómà nínú ìlera àwùjọ àti àmójútó. Dr Chikwe Ihekweazu máa tẹ̀síwájú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Ẹ̀ka fún Àwọn Ètò Ìmọ̀ Ìlera Pàjáwírì àti Ìtọpinpin nínú Àwọn Èto Pàjáwírì. Gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀kan lára ipò rẹ̀, Dr Ihekweazu máa léwájú Ibùdó WHO fún Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Ìmọ̀ Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé, ṣíṣe àkójọpọ̀ àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ láti kojú àwọn ewu àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé lọ́jọ́ iwájú pẹ̀lú ànfààní tó dára sí dátà, àwọn ìkápá ìyànnàná, àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ fún ìpinnu-ṣíṣe. Ṣíwájú kí ó tó darapọ̀ mọ́ WHO, Dr Ihekweazu ni Olùdarí Àpapọ̀ àkọ́kọ́ fún Ààyè fún Ìdènà Ààrùn ní orílẹ̀-èdè Nigeria (NCDC), láti ọdún 2016 sí 2021. Dr Ihekweazu ní ìwé-ẹ̀rí ìṣẹ̀gun, pẹ̀lú àmọ̀já gẹ́gẹ́ bíi onímọ̀ nípa àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ààrùn àkóràn, àti ìwé-ẹ̀rí másítà nínú ìlera àwùjọ. Dr Michael Ryan máa tẹ̀síwájú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Olùdarí Aláṣẹ fún Ètò Pàjáwírì Ìlera WHO. Dr Ryan ti ń ṣàmójútó àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera ní WHO láti bíi ọdún 25 sẹ́yìn àti ti àìpẹ́ yìí tó léwájú ìdáhùn pàjáwírì WHO sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19. Ó léwájú ìdáhùn WHO sí ìtànkálẹ̀ ààrùn, àwọn rògbòdìyàn ọmọnìyàn àti àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera àwùjọ mìíràn. Dr Ryan jẹ́ ọ̀kan lára àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ olùdásílẹ̀ Ìfitóni Ìtànkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Àgbáyé àti Ojú Òpó Ìdáhùn (GORAN), tí ó ti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún ìdáhùn sí ọgọ́run lé ìtànkálẹ̀ ààrùn jákèjádò àgbáyé. Dr Ryan ní ìwé-ẹ̀rí nínú ìṣẹ̀gun àti ìwé-ẹ̀rí másítà nínú ìlera àwùjọ. Mr Raul Thomas máa tẹ̀síwájú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Àwọn Iṣẹ́ Òkòwò. Mr Thomas ní àpapọ ojúṣe fún àwọn iṣẹ́ okòwò WHO, tó fi mọ́ ètò ìṣúná, ìpèsè owó, àwọn òṣìṣẹ́, ìṣàkóso, àyẹ̀wò owó, àmójútó ewu, ìṣirò àti ìtẹ̀lé, àti ìṣàmójútó lápapọ̀. Òun ló ṣokùnfà fún ṣíṣe àmúlò ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ lábẹ́ ètò àyípadà Àjọ náà àti pípèsè ibi-iṣẹ́ tí ọ̀wọ́ wà, àti aláfibọ̀. Ní àkókò 20 ọdún iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú WHO, Mr Thomas ti ṣiṣẹ́ ní àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, àwọn America, Eastern Mediterranean àti Western Pacific, bákan náà ni Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè fún Ìwádìí àti ààrùn Jẹjẹrẹ (IARC). Mr Thomas ní ìwé-ẹ̀rí másítà nínú ìṣàkóso ilé-iṣẹ́ àti ìwé-ẹ̀rí báṣẹ́lọ̀ nínú ìṣàkóso okòwò.
translations.reference: She joined WHO in 2021, having been the Chief Executive Officer of FIND, the international alliance for diagnostics. Dr Boehme holds a medical degree and diplomas in public health and management. Dr Chikwe Ihekweazu will continue as Assistant Director-General for the Division of Health Emergency Intelligence and Surveillance Systems in the Emergencies Programme. As part of his portfolio, Dr Ihekweazu leads the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence, bringing together partners to address future pandemic and epidemic risks with better access to data, analytical capacities, and tools for decision-making. Prior to joining WHO, Dr Ihekweazu was the first Director General of the Nigeria Centre for Disease Control (NCDC), from 2016 to 2021. Dr Ihekweazu holds a medical degree, with specialization as an infectious disease epidemiologist, and a master’s degree in public health. Dr Michael Ryan will continue as Executive Director of WHO’s Health Emergencies Programme. Dr Ryan has been managing health emergencies in WHO for the past 25 years and most recently led WHO’s emergency response to the COVID-19 pandemic. He leads WHO’s response to disease outbreaks, humanitarian crises and other public health emergencies. Dr Ryan is a founding member of the Global Outbreak Alert and Response Network (GOARN), which has aided the response to hundreds of disease outbreaks around the world. Dr Ryan holds a degree in medicine and a master’s degree in public health. Mr Raul Thomas will continue as Assistant Director-General for Business Operations. Mr Thomas has overall responsibility for WHO’s business functions, including budget, finance, human resources, administration, audit, risk management, accountability and compliance, and general management. He is responsible for implementing many of the initiatives under the Organization’s transformation agenda and creating a respectful, inclusive workplace. During his 20 years of employment with WHO, Mr Thomas has served in the regions of Africa, the Americas, the Eastern Mediterranean and the Western Pacific, as well as with the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC). Mr Thomas holds a master’s degree in organizational management and a bachelor’s degree in business administration.
